Using these promoters, researchers developed Sex Distorter Male Drive (SDMD) technology that generates male-biased progeny spreading through super-Mendelian inheritance. SDMD simplifies previous genetic construct designs and potentially improves genetic stability and resilience against target site resistance development. Authors propose SDMD as a promising, resistance-resilient tool for population suppression of Anopheles mosquitoes in malaria-endemic regions.
articles.notable_reason: Novel genetic drive with improved resistance-resilience mechanism

articles.headline: Severe malaria referral patterns in Northeast Ethiopia
articles.title_original: "Referral patterns and associated factors among adult patients admitted with Severe Malaria at Shedeho Meket Primary Hospital, Northeast Ethiopia".
articles.details: Retrospective study of 165 adult patients admitted with severe malaria (April 2023–April 2025) in Northeast Ethiopia found 91.5% (151/165) recovered, 13.94% (23/165) were referred, and no deaths. Patients were 57% male (94/165), mean age 34.44 years. Multivariable logistic regression identified severe malarial anemia (AOR=3.052, 95% CI 2.066–13.232), significant bleeding (AOR=8.012, 95% CI 2.066–21.232), and hyperparasitemia (AOR=2.083, 95% CI 0.019–7.803) as significantly associated with referral decisions. Authors emphasize importance of early detection and intervention for severe malaria presentations.

articles.headline: Editorial on malaria protective immunity from infection and vaccination
articles.title_original: Editorial: Dissecting malaria protective immunity: acquired by natural infection and/or vaccination.
articles.details: Editorial discussing malaria protective immunity acquired through natural infection and/or vaccination. (No specific quantitative findings provided.)

articles.headline: Spleen retains live malaria sporozoites during liver transit
articles.title_original: Retention and survival of Plasmodium sporozoites in the spleen.
articles.details: Study injected P. yoelii and P. berghei sporozoites intravenously in mice and observed them using GFP microscopy. Live sporozoites were present in the splenic parenchyma for at least 3 hours post-inoculation, suggesting the spleen has roles beyond simply destroying sporozoites during their journey to the liver.

articles.headline: Malaria-induced lung injury linked to dendritic cell autophagy pathway
articles.title_original: Plasmodium yoelii infection induces lung injury by modulating type 2 conventional dendritic cells autophagy via the STAT3-IRF4 signaling.
articles.details: Plasmodium yoelii-infected mice developed lung injury through accumulation of type 2 conventional dendritic cells (cDC2) in lungs, with reduced phagocytosis and enhanced Th1 cell differentiation. The mechanism involves the JAK/STAT3-IRF4 signaling pathway regulating cDC2 autophagy; JAK inhibitor JSI-124 almost completely reversed this effect, providing a potential therapeutic target.

articles.headline: Malaria drives clonal expansion of cytotoxic Vδ2+ T cells
articles.title_original: Cytotoxic Vδ2+ T cell subsets expand in response to malaria in human tonsil and spleen organoids.
articles.details: Researchers exposed human tonsil and spleen organoids to P. falciparum-infected red blood cells, observing significant increases in Vδ2+ γδ T cell frequencies with clonal expansion and upregulated cytotoxic markers (granzymes, interferon genes). This was validated in Ugandan children with asymptomatic parasitemia showing expanded Vδ2+ T cells with enhanced cytotoxic potential compared to uninfected controls.

articles.headline: Novel PRMT inhibitors show activity across Plasmodium falciparum stages
articles.title_original: Protein Arginine Methyltransferase Inhibitors Target Multiple Stages of Plasmodium falciparum Parasites In Vitro.
articles.details: Researchers screened protein arginine methyltransferase (PRMT) inhibitors against P. falciparum and identified TC-E 5003 as a potent compound with activity across multiple parasite developmental stages. The compound likely targets PfPRMT1; structure-activity relationship studies defined a clear pharmacophore, suggesting a promising new drug target mechanism.

articles.headline: Plasmodium malariae and ovale widely misdiagnosed as vivax in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: The hidden malaria: Misidentification of Plasmodium malariae and Plasmodium ovale in low-transmission setting targeted for elimination in Ethiopia.
articles.details: Among 245 P. vivax-positive samples from Ethiopian health facilities (September 2019–July 2022) re-tested by qPCR, only 87.7% (95% CI 82.98–91.58%) were actually P. vivax; 3.7% were P. falciparum, 3.3% P. malariae, 2.4% P. ovale, and 2.8% had mixed infections. Neither routine microscopy nor RDTs detected P. malariae or P. ovale, highlighting a major diagnostic gap in elimination efforts.
articles.notable_reason: Reveals systematic diagnostic failure undermining elimination

articles.headline: AL and ASAQ remain highly efficacious in Sierra Leone
articles.title_original: Efficacy and safety of artemether-lumefantrine (AL) and artesunate-amodiaquine (ASAQ) for the treatment of uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria among children 6-59 months in three sentinel sites of Sierra Leone, 2021-2022.
articles.details: Among 496 children with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria in Sierra Leone (2021–2022), artemether-lumefantrine (AL) showed uncorrected efficacy of 89.5–96.1% and artesunate-amodiaquine (ASAQ) 92.0–94.5%; PCR-corrected efficacy reached nearly 100% at all sites. No Pfk13 mutations or evidence of artemisinin resistance were detected, confirming continued drug efficacy for first- and second-line treatments.

articles.headline: Malaria microscopy quality highly variable across rural Kenyan clinics
articles.title_original: Malaria microscopy evaluation and quality assurance in rural clinics of Rarieda and Alego Usonga sub-counties of Siaya County, western Kenya.
articles.details: Assessment of 29 health facilities in western Kenya examined 1,494 blood smears for malaria. Overall concordance between routine and expert microscopy was 91% (range 55–100% by facility), with sensitivity 76% and specificity 92% versus gold-standard readings. Accuracy was significantly lower at parasite densities 1–100 p/μL (OR 0.12, 95% CI 0.05–0.29) and higher at >10,000 p/μL, and 21% of facilities had poor-quality slides.

articles.headline: Socioeconomic factors and location drive malaria treatment delays
articles.title_original: Determinants of late malaria treatment among under-five children in West Africa.
articles.authors: Shittu SB, Shittu RO, Adedini SA
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of Malaria Indicator Survey data from Mali, Niger, Nigeria, and Senegal identified country-specific determinants of late malaria treatment in children under-5: maternal wealth and education were protective against delays (Nigeria AOR 0.54 for wealth, AOR 0.49 for education), while remote residence increased delays in some countries, suggesting need for targeted interventions.

articles.headline: PfVPS4 ESCRT protein essential for parasite growth and transmission
articles.title_original: PfVPS4, an ESCRT AAA-ATPase, is essential for asexual proliferation and gametocyte sexual conversion in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.details: Conditional knockdown of PfVPS4 (ESCRT complex ATPase) in P. falciparum reduced asexual progeny by 84% and gametocyte conversion by 46%, without affecting gametocyte maturation. Biochemical studies showed optimal activity at pH 7.5 and 37°C with Km 0.086 mM; key residues (D213, E214, T161, I288) and cofactor PfVta1 are essential, identifying PfVPS4 as an attractive antimalarial drug target.
articles.notable_reason: Novel drug target combining blood-stage and transmission blocking

articles.headline: WWII posters and cartoons boosted soldier malaria compliance
articles.title_original: Attention for Compliance: Visual Media in Second World War Anti-Malaria Efforts.
articles.details: During World War II, extensive media campaigns including posters and films (with contributions from artists like Dr. Seuss) were conducted to encourage US soldiers' compliance with antimalarial measures and medications. These efforts, combined with medicines and engineering improvements, contributed to malaria control in the military despite tens of thousands of initial casualties from inadequate mosquito control.

articles.headline: Thai-Myanmar border malaria persistence driven by systemic barriers
articles.title_original: Challenges and responses of malaria elimination in a high-endemic area along the Thai-Myanmar border: A health systems perspective.
articles.details: Mixed-methods evaluation of malaria elimination in Tha Song Yang District assessed 388 households and surveillance data (2018–2022). Despite high community awareness (75.77% knowledge, 94.59% positive attitudes), adherence to the 1-3-7 elimination strategy declined sharply to 69% for Day 7 activities by 2022. Key barriers included short-term migrant populations, workforce shortages, and reduced funding, suggesting transmission persistence reflects systemic challenges rather than knowledge gaps.

articles.headline: Mini chamber test validates chlorfenapyr ITN bioefficacy efficiently
articles.title_original: The mini chamber test: a novel bioassay for bioefficacy testing of chlorfenapyr treated nets used in malaria control.
articles.details: A new mini chamber bioassay was developed and optimized for evaluating pyrethroid-chlorfenapyr insecticide-treated nets (ITNs), providing higher throughput and animal-free testing compared to the gold-standard tunnel test. With overnight exposure, the mini chamber showed 95% 72-hour mortality versus 70% in the tunnel test; a 6-hour exposure protocol showed potential for durability monitoring with correlation to HPLC-measured chlorfenapyr content.

articles.headline: P. vivax underdiagnosed in mixed infections; pvmdr1 amplification rising in Roraima
articles.title_original: Hidden Infections, Emerging Resistance: Underdiagnosed Plasmodium vivax and multidrug resistance-1 (pvmdr1) gene Amplification in the Guiana Shield.
articles.details: A 727-case study from Brazilian Amazon states revealed frequent misclassification of P. vivax/P. falciparum mixed infections because microscopy missed P. vivax at higher P. falciparum densities. In Roraima, pvmdr1 copy-number amplification was detected in 60% of isolates, rising to over 70% after 2020, suggesting that inadvertent drug exposure drives selection of reduced-susceptibility strains.

articles.headline: Wealth and urban living associated with reduced P. falciparum infection in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Risk factors and geospatial associations with Plasmodium falciparum malaria infection and IgG seroprevalence: Nigeria, 2018.
articles.details: A nationwide 2018 survey of 89,432 blood samples found 38.3% prevalence of current/recent P. falciparum infection in Nigeria. Children aged 5–14 had highest infection odds; the wealthiest category showed 5-fold lower odds than the poorest, and urban living was associated with 30% reduction in active infection and 60% reduction in exposure.

articles.headline: German university hospital: 58 imported malaria cases, 98% survival, mostly P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Malaria: 11-Year Experience With Imported Cases at a German University Hospital and Epidemiological Trends Amid the COVID-19 Pandemic.
articles.details: A single-center retrospective analysis (2012–2023) of 58 malaria patients found median age 37.5 years, 65% male, with 31.6% requiring intensive care. P. falciparum caused 96% of cases, 98% survived, and only 17% had taken prophylaxis. Thrombocytopenia was the primary laboratory abnormality.

articles.headline: South Africa: climate extremes and migration reshape malaria risk patterns
articles.title_original: Malaria at the margins: climatic extremes, migration, and urban informal settlement risk in South Africa.
articles.authors: Matamanda SH, Musakwa W, Dzomba A
articles.details: Analysis of climate and malaria data (2000–2024) in three South African provinces found minimum-temperature warming and rainfall increasingly dominated by short-duration extremes. Same-year humidity positively associated with malaria in KwaZulu-Natal and Mpumalanga; lag-1 rainfall significant in Limpopo. Post-2022-flood migration increases coincided with malaria tail years, suggesting compound climatic anomalies and mobility reorganize risk.

articles.headline: Avian Plasmodium species persistence mediated by erythrocytic merogony, first Matryoshka virus link
articles.title_original: New experimental insights into the biology of avian malaria parasites.
articles.details: Three avian Plasmodium species transferred to Eurasian siskins persisted up to 185 days; histology and in situ hybridization detected no tissue stages, indicating persistence driven by erythrocytic merogony. One isolate (pCOLL7) showed predominantly gametocyte parasitaemia. Study provides the first association of Matryoshka RNA virus (MaRNAV-9) with avian Plasmodium circumflexum.

articles.headline: HTM investments averted $135 billion in primary healthcare costs globally, 2000–2023
articles.title_original: The benefits of investments to combat HIV, tuberculosis, and malaria for primary healthcare from 2000 to 2023: An economic modeling analysis.
articles.details: Economic modeling of 108 low- and middle-income countries estimated HIV/TB/malaria service scale-up averted 6.9 billion outpatient visits and 3.9 billion inpatient bed-days (US$135 billion in costs) over 2000–2023. Reductions were greatest in sub-Saharan Africa and East Asia, representing median 4.4% of hospital bed capacity and 1.6% of government health spending in 2023.

articles.headline: MosQNet-SA AI model achieves 99.42% accuracy for mosquito species classification
articles.title_original: MosQNet-SA: Explainable convolutional-attention network for mosquito classification with application as a RESTful API for dengue and malaria risk mapping.
articles.details: A convolutional-attention neural network for mosquito classification achieved 99.42% accuracy on 1,000 images across three species (Aedes, Anopheles, Culex) while using 10-fold fewer parameters than existing CNN approaches. The model incorporates explainability methods (Saliency, GradCAM, LIME, Kernel SHAP) and a RESTful API for real-time classification and disease risk mapping.

articles.headline: R561H K13 mutation confers extreme artemisinin resistance in Bangladeshi P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Functional assessment of prevalent kelch13 mutations reveals high-level artemisinin resistance potential in Bangladeshi Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.details: CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing introduced global K13 mutations into Bangladeshi P. falciparum isolates. C580Y and R561H conferred marked artemisinin-resistance increases; R561H showed extreme resistance in a moderately resistant background (ring-stage survival assay: 30.09% ± 1.9%), with asexual fitness advantage (5.1% per generation). This is the first functional evidence from South Asian parasites that local strains are genetically primed to sustain high-level artemisinin resistance.
articles.notable_reason: First functional demonstration of extreme K13-mediated resistance in South Asia

articles.headline: IFN-γ signaling in T cells mediates malaria-associated lung pathology in mice
articles.title_original: Comprehensive immune profiling reveals IFN-γ signaling in T cells mediates parasite phagocytosis in a rodent malaria model.
articles.details: Comprehensive transcriptomics and flow cytometry in a rodent malaria model revealed IFN-γ signaling in T cells plays a key role in lung pathology. Disrupting IFN-γ signaling reduced parasite load in lungs and attenuated pathology by enhancing T cell–monocyte interactions, leading to increased proinflammatory CD8+Ly6C+ monocytes with elevated parasite phagocytic capacity.

articles.headline: Pfk13 and Pfubp1 variants linked to artemisinin-lumefantrine reduced susceptibility in Africa
articles.title_original: Novel Pfk13 and Pfubp1 genotypes in African Plasmodium falciparum isolates exhibiting reduced susceptibility to the antimalarials artemisinin and lumefantrine.
articles.details: Phenotype-genotype analysis of 50 P. falciparum isolates from UK travelers (2012–present) identified novel variants in pfk13 propeller and non-propeller domains, plus variants in pfap2μ, pfcoronin, and pfubp1 associated with reduced in vitro susceptibility to artemisinin and lumefantrine. Multiple African settings (Kenya, Uganda, Zambia, Namibia) showed pfk13-dependent and -independent partial artemisinin resistance.

articles.headline: CSP haplotype baseline in Cameroon: 22 types, 20% vaccine-matched, pre-RTS,S rollout
articles.title_original: Haplotype diversity of Plasmodium falciparum circumsporozoite protein prior to malaria vaccine implementation in Kaelé Health District, Cameroon, 2022-2023.
articles.details: Analysis of 100 P. falciparum samples from Cameroon (2022–2023) identified 22 circumsporozoite protein (CSP) haplotypes (heterozygosity = 0.908). The vaccine-matched RTS,S haplotype was present in 20% of genotyped infections; CSP T cell epitopes showed signs of balancing selection. Study provides a baseline of parasite diversity prior to RTS,S/AS01 vaccine implementation.

articles.headline: Antibody avidity weakly predicts vaccine function or longevity in malaria trials
articles.title_original: Relationship between antibody avidity, Fc-mediated functional activity and longevity of malaria vaccine responses in clinical trials.
articles.details: Analysis of two malaria vaccine trials (phase 1 MSP2, phase 2b RTS,S) found that while IgG magnitude correlated with avidity, avidity was not a major determinant of Fc-mediated functional activities (complement fixation, Fcγ-receptor binding, opsonic phagocytosis). IgG magnitude was a stronger determinant of function than avidity, and avidity showed only weak, antigen-specific association with antibody longevity.

articles.headline: PvDBP gene duplication enables P. vivax immune evasion in natural infections
articles.title_original: PvDBP gene amplification is associated with functional immune evasion by Plasmodium vivax in vivo.
articles.details: Longitudinal cohort study in nine Eastern Cambodian villages (657 participants, ~5%-30% P. vivax prevalence) showed parasites with multiple pvdbp gene copies evade naturally-acquired binding-inhibitory antibodies. Multi-copy parasites comprised 87% of infections in individuals with high anti-PvDBP antibodies vs 38% without (p<0.0001). Multi-copy prevalence ranged from 35% in low-transmission to 47% in high-transmission villages (p=0.0246). Multi-copy parasites more common in asymptomatic than symptomatic individuals, indicating successful immune evasion.

articles.headline: Geographic mapping guides artesunate supply distribution for US severe malaria
articles.title_original: Mapping Hospitals That Treat Severe Malaria Cases in the United States: Evidence to Inform Health Systems Strategies for Intravenous Artesunate Supply.
articles.details: Analysis of 1,466 adjudicated severe malaria cases (81.7% of 1,791 CDC-reported, 2012-2018) showed geographic concentration in New York, Maryland, Texas, DC, and Pennsylvania across 292 of 3,142 US counties (9.3%). Critically, 95.8% of contiguous US cases were hospitalized within 2 hours driving time of a Level 1 trauma center, identified via spatial optimization as ideal artesunate distribution points. Geographic pattern remained consistent year-to-year.

articles.headline: Artesunate treatment of severe malaria in nonendemic countries
articles.title_original: Artesunate Treatment of Severe Malaria in Nonendemic Countries: When, Where, How, and What Now?
articles.details: No abstract available; based on title only, discusses clinical management of severe malaria in settings where malaria is not endemic.

articles.headline: Kenyan pharmacies deliver high-quality malaria care with strong diagnostic-treatment alignment
articles.title_original: Guideline-concordant care and outcomes for pediatric malaria cases: descriptive evidence from pharmacy-based fever management in Kenya.
articles.details: Prospective cohort of 239 febrile children under five at 39 private pharmacies in malaria-endemic Kenya showed 69% received malaria diagnostic testing. Of those tested, 59% were malaria-positive; 78% of malaria-positive children received guideline-concordant antimalarial treatment. Among treated malaria-positive children, 88% had fully recovered at 2-week follow-up. Predictors of testing included caregiver education and younger age; appropriate treatment more likely with older children, male gender, and provider experience.

articles.headline: Malaria nearly eliminated in high-transmission Vietnamese Central Highlands by 2021
articles.title_original: Malaria situation in a highly endemic province in the Central Highlands of Vietnam: a retrospective analysis.
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of 2,147 malaria cases in Dak Lak province, Vietnam, 2017-2022 showed 58.2% indigenous and 39.1% imported cases, with P. falciparum dominant. Cases declined dramatically to near-zero by 2021. Seasonal peaks occurred October-November; temperature and rainfall strongly associated with transmission. Gender, ethnicity, and high-endemicity residence predicted P. falciparum and P. vivax infection. Cold spots identified in southwest Dak Lak.

articles.headline: Renal biomarkers may predict malaria-associated acute kidney injury and mortality
articles.title_original: Kidney and endothelial injury biomarkers as predictors of complications and mortality in patients with malaria: a systematic review.
articles.details: Systematic review (PROSPERO CRD42025642952) of renal and endothelial injury biomarkers as predictors of malaria-associated acute kidney injury and adverse outcomes. Combined evaluation of multiple biomarkers with clinical and laboratory parameters improves characterization of AKI in malaria. Elevated biomarker levels consistently associated with increased risk of renal complications and poor outcomes, supporting their use for early detection and risk stratification.

articles.headline: Mass drug administration achieves 90% coverage but falls short of 80% programmatic target
articles.title_original: Operational performance of a programmatic mass drug administration campaign for malaria in southern Mozambique: a comprehensive mixed-methods evaluation of implementation outcomes.
articles.details: Two rounds of programmatic MDA with dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine in Gaza province, Mozambique, December 2022-February 2023, targeting ~59,271 individuals. Acceptance 96.2% (607/631) among eligible households. Operational coverage (proportion present who received drug) reached 90.2% (569/631); programmatic coverage (all survey participants) 73.9% (569/770). Community (91.3%) and health staff (100%) deemed intervention acceptable and appropriate. Fidelity partial: teams correctly explained purpose in 74.5%, requested consent in 86.6%, emphasized completion in 60.4%.

articles.headline: Dengue fever overlooked in malaria-endemic regions; co-infections significant in Kenya
articles.title_original: Unmasking non-malarial mosquito-borne infections among febrile children in malaria-endemic regions of western Kenya.
articles.details: Screening of 1,004 febrile children under five at health facilities in Busia and Kisumu Counties revealed: 215 (21.4%) P. falciparum alone, 90 (8.9%) dengue fever alone, 75 (7.5%) malaria-dengue co-infections. Busia showed highest P. falciparum-only (23.4%); Kisumu highest dengue-only (12.6%). Dengue identified as major but neglected contributor to non-malarial febrile illness in children, suggesting need for revised diagnostic algorithms.

articles.headline: Novel African genetic variants protect from severe malaria and bacterial infections
articles.title_original: African-specific genetic loci determine iron status and risk of severe malaria and bacteremia in African children.
articles.details: GWAS in 3,928 African children across five sites, replicated in 2,868 African American adults, identified new loci for iron-related biomarkers and severe malaria protection. Variants at GTF3C5 (rs2905094) confer protection against severe malaria and bacteremia. Dantu blood group variants (DUP4 haplotype, rs552439837) associated with soluble transferrin receptor levels and protect against severe malaria and bacteremia. CHCHD7/SDR16C5 variant (rs73596248) increases hepcidin and reduces risk of Klebsiella pneumoniae and Staphylococcus aureus bacteremia.
articles.notable_reason: Novel protective loci identify iron-regulation mechanisms in severe malaria susceptibility

articles.headline: Public awareness education critical for malaria vaccine rollout success in Sudan
articles.title_original: Public awareness and education: key to the success of the malaria vaccine rollout in Sudan.
articles.details: No abstract available; based on title only, discusses importance of community education and awareness for successful malaria vaccine implementation in Sudan.

articles.headline: Biological control methods offer sustainable malaria vector management alternatives
articles.title_original: Exploring the Scope and Status of Biological Interventions in Malaria Control.
articles.details: Narrative review of biological interventions for malaria control including: viruses, fungi, microbial pathogens, and plant-derived compounds (alkaloids, essential oils, larvicidal plants) targeting mosquito vectors; and genetic techniques such as sterile insect technique and genetically modified mosquitoes. Entomopathogenic fungi highlighted as particularly promising for infecting and killing Anopheles vectors. Review addresses limitations of current interventions (insecticide resistance, environmental concerns) for sustainable malaria control.

articles.headline: Malaria and diarrhea impair childhood growth in Tanzania; diarrhea has largest effect
articles.title_original: Associations of Malaria, Diarrhea, and Respiratory Infections with Growth Velocity of Children in Tanzania.
articles.details: Prospective cohort of 2,397 Tanzanian children over 18 months (30,079 monthly visits) assessed infection impacts on growth velocity. Malaria associated with reduced height velocity of 0.14 cm/month (95% CI: -0.14 [-0.23 to -0.05]), not weight. Respiratory tract infections reduced height by 0.10 cm/month (-0.10 [-0.15 to -0.04]) and weight by 37 g/month (-37 [-72 to -2.6]). Diarrhea had strongest effect: 0.26 cm/month height reduction (-0.26 [-0.35 to -0.17]) and 120 g/month weight reduction (-120 [-177 to -62]).

articles.headline: PfCRT chloroquine transport regulated by redox potential
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Chloroquine Resistance Transporter (PfCRT) Is a Redox-Dependent Drug Transporter.
articles.details: Mechanistic study of Plasmodium falciparum chloroquine resistance transporter (PfCRT) using cryo-EM structure analysis and site-specific mutagenesis. Disulfide bonds at cysteine pairs C289/C312 and C301/C309 are required for full chloroquine transport activity. Additional cysteines (C101, C139, C171, C328) modulate transport. Molecular dynamics calculations revealed large conformational changes in loop 7 upon redox-dependent S-S interconversion, indicating redox potential regulates PfCRT-mediated chloroquine transport.

articles.headline: ChAdOx1-MVA LS2 vaccine elicits strong CD8+ response but no clinical protection
articles.title_original: Phase I/IIa study to assess the safety, immunogenicity and efficacy of ChAdOx1-MVA vectored vaccines expressing a novel liver-stage malaria dual antigen LS2 by sporozoite challenge in malaria-naïve adults.
articles.details: Phase I/IIa open-label dose escalation study of ChAdOx1-MVA vectored vaccine expressing novel dual antigen LS2 (combining LSA1 and LSAP2) in malaria-naïve UK adults (n=10 vaccinated, n=6 controls). Well tolerated with no severe adverse events. Prime-boost vaccination induced high immunogenicity with median IFN-γ ELISpot response of 4473 SFC/10^6 PBMC. However, all 10 vaccinated volunteers became infected during controlled human malaria infection challenge (median time to diagnosis 13 days vs 13.25 days in controls), indicating failure to achieve clinical protection.

articles.headline: Multiple insecticide resistance widespread in Odisha malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Mapping the Insecticide Resistance Landscape of Malaria Vectors in Odisha, India (1993-2024): A Scoping Review.
articles.details: Scoping review compiling insecticide resistance data in Anopheles vectors from Odisha, India (1993-2024) from 28 published sources. An. culicifacies, the major malaria vector prevalent in all 30 Odisha districts, showed resistance to DDT (26 districts), malathion (17 districts), and deltamethrin (10 districts) using WHO mortality criteria (susceptible >98%, confirmed resistance <90%). An. fluviatilis, An. annularis, and An. minimus also documented. Findings highlight urgent need for novel insecticides and integrated vector management strategies.

articles.headline: Malaria-HIV co-infection control requires integrated service coordination
articles.title_original: Insights into Malaria and HIV Co-infection: Trends and Impact in Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.details: Narrative review examining epidemiology, risk factors, and health outcomes of malaria-HIV co-infection in sub-Saharan Africa, where both infections pose major public health threats. Reviews diagnostic limitations, treatment interactions, and health system constraints in managing co-infected individuals. Concludes that closer coordination of malaria and HIV services, improved diagnostic capacity, continued antimalarial and antiretroviral advances, and community engagement are needed to reduce dual disease burden and address persistent surveillance gaps.

articles.headline: 2La inversion frequency in Anopheles coluzzii follows Nigeria's climatic gradients
articles.title_original: Spatial eco-climatic gradients drive adaptive 2La chromosomal inversion polymorphism in Anopheles coluzzii, a major malaria vector in Nigeria.
articles.details: Cross-sectional molecular survey of 2La chromosomal inversion polymorphism in An. coluzzii across 12 Nigerian states spanning southern, central, and northern ecological zones. 2La/2La homokaryotype predominant in hot, arid northern Sahelian and Sudan savanna zones; 2La+/2La+ standard arrangement predominant in humid southern forest and mangrove regions; heterokaryotype at intermediate frequencies in central transitional belt. Pronounced latitudinal cline with heterozygosity peaking centrally. Strong alignment between inversion frequency and eco-climatic gradients suggests chromosomal rearrangements promote vector survival and ecological fitness.

articles.headline: Community housing interventions complement malaria and cholera control
articles.title_original: Household and community-based approaches to malaria and cholera prevention: A narrative review with case studies from Uganda and Mozambique.
articles.details: Narrative review and case studies from Uganda and Mozambique examining housing-based interventions for malaria and cholera prevention. Screened windows/doors, sealed eaves, elevated flooring, and insecticide-treated barriers reduce mosquito entry. Improved latrines, proper waste disposal, and hand-washing access reduce waterborne disease spread. Combination of structural housing modifications plus domestic behavior change provides low-cost, community-acceptable, easily implementable prevention complementing existing control programs. Success requires affordability, cultural adaptation, and integration with established strategies.

articles.headline: Avian malaria detected only in peri-urban Culex pipiens
articles.title_original: Urbanization and Vector Species Shape Avian Malaria Prevalence in Mosquitoes.
articles.details: Cross-sectional assessment of avian malaria prevalence in two mosquito species across urban and peri-urban habitats. No avian malaria infections detected in either species in urban areas. Native Culex pipiens from peri-urban sites showed 2.53% prevalence. Findings highlight urbanization's effect on parasite transmission dynamics, with apparent protective effect of urban conditions on avian malaria prevalence.

articles.headline: Ivermectin-induced Loa loa encephalopathy in co-infected patient
articles.title_original: Probable Loa loa-associated encephalopathy following ivermectin administration in a patient with malaria infection: A case report from the Democratic Republic of the Congo.
articles.details: Case report of 37-year-old woman from DRC with probable Loa loa-associated encephalopathy following ivermectin administration for loiasis. Patient had concurrent malaria (Plasmodium trophozoites on blood smear) and intestinal parasitosis. Developed meningeal signs within 24 hours of ivermectin, with cerebrospinal fluid showing Loa loa microfilariae and no bacterial/fungal evidence. Treated with albendazole and supportive care, achieved complete clinical recovery with microfilarial clearance after 19-day hospitalization. Highlights need for microfilarial burden assessment and pharmacovigilance before ivermectin use in Loa loa-endemic settings.

articles.headline: Malaria in pregnancy in DRC conflict zones needs health system strengthening
articles.title_original: Time to Act; Let Us Unite to Eliminate Malaria Among Pregnant Women in War Zone From Eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo: A Review.
articles.details: Narrative literature review (2007-2025) of malaria in pregnancy (MiP) in conflict zones of Eastern DRC, where DRC accounts for 12% of global malaria cases. MiP prevalence rates ranged 17-40% with 26% asymptomatic pooled estimate creating transmission reservoirs. Early-stage parasitemia associated with reduced umbilical artery resistance and fetal growth restriction. Armed conflict destroyed infrastructure causing critical shortages of insecticide-treated nets and intermittent preventive treatment. Emerging artemisinin resistance further complicates control, requiring health system strengthening across six pillars.

articles.headline: Broad protection coverage reduces malaria more than intensive targeting
articles.title_original: Spatio-temporal agent-based modelling of malaria.
articles.details: Stochastic spatiotemporal agent-based model of malaria transmission incorporating mosquito climatic suitability estimates and household location data. Model simulates interactions between human and mosquito agents and impact of spatially distributed interventions. Applied to Vietnam context comparing intervention distribution strategies. Modeling results suggest that providing some level of protection to a wide range of households reduces malaria prevalence more than providing strong protection to a limited number of households.

articles.headline: P. falciparum-Schistosoma coinfection prevalence 10-13% in sub-Saharan children
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum coinfection with Schistosoma haematobium and Schistosoma mansoni among children in sub-Saharan Africa: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis (21 studies) of Plasmodium falciparum-Schistosoma coinfection among children aged 0-17 in sub-Saharan Africa. Pooled prevalence of P. falciparum-S. haematobium coinfection was 10.0% (95% CI 6.0-14.0%, I²=98.0%) with highest prevalence in West Africa (13.0%). P. falciparum-S. mansoni coinfection prevalence was 13.0% (95% CI 6.0-24.0%, I²=99.28%) with highest in East Africa (25.0%). Significant diagnostic method variation noted (PCR+microscopy 13.0% vs PCR+PCR 1.0%), suggesting true prevalence likely underestimated.

articles.headline: Malarial pneumonopathy in cerebral malaria not explained by acidosis
articles.title_original: Malarial pneumonopathy in Malawian children with cerebral malaria.
articles.details: Prospective observational study of 97 Malawian children aged 6 months-12 years with WHO-defined cerebral malaria. 28% (27/97) met WHO respiratory distress criteria. Despite elevated lactate (median 7.0 mmol/L [4.2, 13.3]), median pH was 7.41; metabolic acidosis occurred in only 12%. Primary respiratory alkalosis observed in 33%. Lung ultrasound revealed subcentimeter consolidations in 88% of all participants. Absence of diffuse edema ruled out cardiac/renal failure. Respiratory distress not associated with increased mortality, suggesting mechanisms beyond metabolic acidosis including pulmonary microvascular obstruction and inflammation.

articles.headline: RTS,S vaccine safety monitoring in Ghana, Kenya, Malawi
articles.title_original: Safety monitoring of the RTS,S/AS01(E) malaria vaccine: experiences and lessons from routine pharmacovigilance in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi.
articles.details: Pharmacovigilance monitoring of RTS,S/AS01E vaccine safety through strengthened routine systems in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi. Causality assessment by national expert committees found no new safety concerns or confirmation of Phase 3 signals. Challenges included underreporting of adverse events, reporting delays, and investigation quality issues despite targeted system strengthening efforts.

articles.headline: Spatial hotspot identification using vector and malaria incidence modeling
articles.title_original: Identification of malaria hotspots in southwestern Benin through spatial joint modelling of malaria incidence and vector abundance.
articles.details: Bayesian joint spatial modelling of Anopheles gambiae and An. funestus abundance with malaria incidence in 8 southwestern Benin communes identified contiguous hotspots on Lake Ahémé shores and 4 districts. An. funestus showed stronger spatial correlation with incidence (119.95 km²) compared to An. gambiae (89.90 km²), with 67.29 km² showing synergistic effects. Wind speed, mid-infrared reflectance, leaf area index, and land surface temperature were consistent ecological drivers.

articles.headline: Pediatric malaria in non-endemic Europe: immunity status less predictive than expected
articles.title_original: Pediatric malaria in a non-endemic European setting: clinical patterns, laboratory markers, and the ambiguous role of immunity.
articles.details: Retrospective study of 74 children (59% male, median age 9 years) with malaria in Milan 2004-2024. P. falciparum (62.2%) and P. vivax (27%) predominated; severe malaria occurred in 23% with no significant difference between presumed-immune and non-immune groups (p=0.783). Hyponatremia correlated with shorter hospital stay (ρ=-0.284, p=0.020); elevated CRP correlated with longer fever duration (ρ=0.256, p=0.033). Intravenous artesunate replaced quinine as standard severe malaria treatment post-2015.

articles.headline: Serum uric acid not reliable predictor of complicated malaria outcomes
articles.title_original: Serum Uric Acid as a Biomarker of Disease Severity in Children With Complicated Malaria: A Cross-Sectional Study From Eastern India.
articles.details: Cross-sectional hospital study of 102 Indian children (1-18 years) with P. falciparum complicated malaria, Sept 2022-May 2024. Hyperuricemia in 80.4%, elevated SGOT in 72.5%, SGPT in 83.3%, hyperbilirubinemia in 65.7%. Adverse outcomes in 29.4% of patients. Serum uric acid not significantly associated with outcomes (p=0.115), though abnormal SGOT (p=0.011), SGPT (p=0.004), and bilirubin (p=0.004) were significant.

articles.headline: Malaria global burden, epidemiology, and 21st-century control strategies review
articles.title_original: Malaria in the 21st Century: Global Disease Burden, Epidemiological Insights, and Strategic Control Approaches.
articles.details: Comprehensive review of global malaria epidemiology based on WHO World Malaria Report 2024 and Global Burden of Disease estimates. Covers worldwide trends, regional variations, socio-economic determinants, and control strategies including drug resistance, climate change impacts, and funding limitations. Emphasizes need for multifactorial prevention and management approaches tailored to local environments and policy priorities to meet WHO 2030 elimination goals.

articles.headline: Rwanda malaria prevalence halved despite emerging artemisinin resistance
articles.title_original: Molecular surveillance of Falciparum malaria in Rwanda: Shifts in parasite prevalence and risk factors between the 2014-15 and 2019-20 Rwanda Demographics and Health Surveys.
articles.details: Molecular surveillance of 7,127 adult dried blood spots from 2019-20 Rwanda Demographic and Health Survey using ultra-sensitive qRT-PCR. National P. falciparum prevalence 7.7% (95% CI 6.8–8.7%), predominantly low-density infections (median 7.3 parasites/µL). Prevalence decreased 53% relative to 2014-15 DHS despite reports of increasing artemisinin-resistance-associated K13 mutations. Male sex, lower household wealth, lower education, and lower elevation were significant risk factors.

articles.headline: Double K13 mutations confer ozonide resistance in artemisinin-resistant parasites
articles.title_original: Double Mutations in Plasmodium falciparum Kelch13 drive resistance to next-generation artemisinin derivatives in malaria parasites.
articles.details: Laboratory study of Cambodian K13-mutant P. falciparum subjected to artefenomel (OZ439) pressure identified novel K13 double mutations R539T+A212T as determinants of ozonide resistance, with accelerated parasite recrudescence after OZ439 or OZ277 exposure. Double mutants also included C580Y+A212T. Metabolomic profiling showed increased glutathione, nucleotide, and aspartate-glutamate metabolism but unchanged hemoglobin-derived peptides, suggesting heightened redox regulation capacity rather than altered drug activation.
articles.notable_reason: Double K13 mutations confer ozonide resistance, threatening next-generation drugs

articles.headline: Imported malaria constitutes 89% of cases in Asia Pacific elimination countries
articles.title_original: Estimating the burden of imported malaria in the Asia Pacific Region: A Systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 42 studies (2010–2024) covering 435,454 malaria cases in 9 Asia Pacific countries found pooled imported malaria prevalence of 89.0% (95% CI 75.9–95.5). Imported cases predominantly male (94%), P. falciparum species (57.0%), and originated from Africa (64.3%). Pre-elimination phase countries averaged 84.6%; prevalence ranged from 95% in Sri Lanka and China to 3.2% in South Korea.

articles.headline: Malaria burden in reproductive-age women substantially decreased 1990–2023
articles.title_original: Fatal and non-fatal health outcomes of malaria among reproductive age women in Eastern Sub-Saharan Africa, from 1990 to 2023.
articles.details: GBD 2023 study of fatal and non-fatal malaria outcomes among reproductive-age women in Eastern Sub-Saharan Africa 1990–2023. Mozambique recorded highest 2023 prevalence (19,627 per 100,000 WRA), while Eritrea (842) and Rwanda (1,603) had lowest. Mozambique also had highest mortality, YLLs, and DALYs. Substantial regional reductions from 1990 to 2023 except in Djibouti and Ethiopia, though rates remain heterogeneous and elevated across the region.

articles.headline: Baseline malaria burden and pyrethroid resistance established in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Baseline malaria burden and pyrethroid resistance in Muheza, Tanzania informing a cluster randomized trial of the 3D window screens.
articles.details: Baseline study in 20 hamlets of Muheza District, Tanzania preceding 3D window screen trial. Among 778 children aged 6 months–14 years, malaria prevalence 40.2%, anemia 55.2%. Vector populations: 70.2% An. funestus and 29.8% An. gambiae from 14,263 mosquitoes. Pyrethroid resistance high (56.5% permethrin, 52.8% deltamethrin mortality); kdr-East allele frequency 0.45. Annual entomological inoculation rates: 14.2 (An. funestus), 13.5 (An. gambiae), 27.7 combined.

articles.headline: Risk-based reactive case detection substantially increases infection yield
articles.title_original: Adapting reactive case detection strategies for high-risk populations to increase malaria infection detection in Aceh Province, Indonesia.
articles.details: Comparative evaluation of household-based vs. risk-based reactive case detection in Aceh Province, Indonesia among 47 index malaria cases (28 P. knowlesi). Standard household RACD tested 847 contacts from 34 cases, detecting 1 infection by LAMP/PCR (0.1%; 95% CI 0.0–0.7%). Risk-based RACD targeting non-household forest contacts tested 179 from 32 cases, detecting 8 LAMP-positive infections (4.5%; 95% CI 1.9–8.6%), with 3 PCR-confirmed (1.7%; 95% CI 0.3–4.8%).

articles.headline: Correction to mosquito biting and intervention timing study
articles.title_original: Correction: A matter of timing: Biting by malaria-infected Anopheles mosquitoes and the use of interventions during the night in rural south-eastern Tanzania.
articles.details: Correction notice for previously published research on timing of malaria-infected Anopheles mosquito biting and use of interventions during night hours in rural south-eastern Tanzania.

articles.headline: ITNs reduce child malaria mortality more than temperature in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Assessing the role of interventions and climate on malaria mortality among children under five years of age: insights from two decades of data from the Health Demographic Surveillance System of Nouna, Burkina Faso.
articles.details: Surveillance data from Nouna, Burkina Faso (2002-2021) analyzed for malaria mortality in children <5 using wavelet analysis and Bayesian temporal models (2013-2021). Rainfall was positively associated with mortality (MRR=1.59, 95% BCI=1.18-1.95); land surface temperature decreased mortality (MRR=0.68, 95% BCI=0.52-0.86); insecticide-treated nets reduced mortality (MRR=0.59, 95% BCI=0.42-0.79); artemisinin-based combination therapies showed no significant effect. ITN coverage was more effective than temperature in reducing mortality, though rainfall had greater opposing impact.

articles.headline: Obesity-related inflammation doesn't mediate vitamin A status in non-malnourished populations
articles.title_original: Inflammation Does Not Mediate Relationships between BMI/BMI-for-Age Z-Score and Retinol or Retinol-Binding Protein in Adult Women or Young Children without Underweight, Wasting, or Malaria: An Analysis of 24 Surveys.
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of 24 surveys (BRINDA project): 16,771 women (15-49 years) and 24,707 children (6-59 months), excluding underweight, wasting, and malaria. Examined relationships between BMI/BMI-for-age z-score, inflammatory biomarkers (CRP, AGP), and retinol/RBP via linear regression and structural equation modeling. Among women, BMI was positively associated with retinol/RBP in 5 of 13 surveys; inflammation partially mediated relationships in 3 of 13 surveys. In both populations without undernutrition or malaria, inflammation associated with overweight/obesity did not substantially impact vitamin A assessment via retinol/RBP.

articles.headline: Sub-Saharan African malaria policies lack catastrophic-cost monitoring and accountability
articles.title_original: Assessing the integration of social protection into infectious disease policies: a multi-country analysis of TB, HIV, and malaria strategies in Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.authors: Munyangaju I, Osório D, Nacarapa E
articles.details: Policy document review in Kenya, Malawi, Mozambique, Nigeria, and Zambia covering 111 documents (2015-2025), scored on five domains: social support, financial protection, implementation, targeting/equity, and coordination/accountability (0-3 scale). Overall readiness ranged from 2.6-2.9 (moderate-high), with Malawi (2.9) and Zambia (2.8) highest. Implementation and coordination were strongest (3.0); financial protection weakest (2.1). No country institutionalized catastrophic-cost monitoring, and policies showed significant gaps in financial-risk monitoring, budgeting, and multisectoral accountability despite acknowledging social protection.

articles.headline: Broad IgG responses to PfEMP1 domains protect against childhood malaria
articles.title_original: IgG and IgM responses to PfEMP1 domains associated with semi-immunity to clinical malaria in Burkinabe children under five.
articles.details: Plasma samples from 80 Burkinabe children (0-60 months) collected before and during peak malaria transmission; measured IgG and IgM antibody responses to 271 P. falciparum erythrocyte membrane protein 1 (PfEMP1) domains using AlphaScreen. Pre-season analysis showed 96% of domains IgG-reactive with breadth increasing by age; IgG responses to 132 domains (49%) remained significantly associated with reduced clinical malaria risk after false discovery rate correction. Fc-dependent opsonic phagocytosis activity correlated with protection for only one antigen, suggesting protective mechanisms beyond phagocytosis in young children.

articles.headline: Most P. vivax patients relapse with genetically diverse parasites within 90 days
articles.title_original: Frequent and genetically diverse Plasmodium vivax relapses contribute to malaria transmission in Cambodia.
articles.details: Cohort of 59 Cambodian P. vivax patients treated with artesunate, relocated to malaria-free area, followed every two days for 90 days with qPCR and microscopy; genomes sequenced to assess clonality and transmission potential via membrane-feeding assays. 81% experienced ≥1 relapse within 90 days with up to four consecutive relapses per individual (median time to first relapse: 20 days); relapsing parasites could be genetically identical, siblings, or unrelated to initial infection. Polyclonal infections at baseline had significantly more relapses, and despite low parasitemia, relapse infections were frequently infectious to mosquitoes.
articles.notable_reason: Most P. vivax relapse with transmission potential; radical cure essential

articles.headline: Haemoglobin AA associated with higher asymptomatic malaria in Ghana
articles.title_original: The Role of Haemoglobin Variants and Red Blood Cell Polymorphisms in Asymptomatic Malaria: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study in four endemic Ghanaian communities screening community members without clinical malaria signs for P. falciparum histidine-rich protein 2; performed ABO blood group, RhD, and haemoglobin phenotyping via alkaline electrophoresis; used multivariate logistic regression. Haemoglobin variants were significantly associated with asymptomatic malaria (p=0.021) and red cell polymorphisms (p<0.001); individuals with HbAA had significantly higher odds of asymptomatic infection (AOR=2.1, p=0.02). RhD types alone were not significant (p=0.877), though significant interaction observed when combined with red cell polymorphisms.

articles.headline: Hydroxamate inhibitors show promise against P. falciparum PfA-M1 in silico
articles.title_original: In silico targeting of plasmodium falciparum metallo-aminopeptidases using hydroxamate inhibitors.
articles.details: In silico molecular docking study targeting P. falciparum metallo-aminopeptidase PfA-M1 (zinc-dependent enzyme catalyzing hemoglobin degradation) using cinnamoyl sulfonamide hydroxamate derivatives (NMJ 1-8). Extra-precision docking, MMGBSA binding energy analysis, and induced-fit docking showed favorable interactions (IFD docking scores -1984.16 to -1989.93 kcal/mol); NMJ-2 and NMJ-3 demonstrated strongest binding affinities with stable complexes in molecular dynamics simulations. Both compounds satisfied ADME pharmacokinetic parameters and showed interaction patterns overlapping with reference inhibitor tosedostat.

articles.headline: Duffy-negative individuals with P. vivax infection suggest alternative invasion pathway
articles.title_original: Genetic variation in the Duffy blood group among vivax malaria patients and its impact on disease susceptibility.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study at four Ethiopian sites (January-June 2024) of 485 microscopy-confirmed P. vivax samples; performed Duffy genotyping via TaqMan qPCR and molecular screening via SYBR Green qPCR. 93.8% were mono-P. vivax infections; 75.1% heterozygous Duffy-positive and 24.5% homozygous Duffy-positive, with only 0.4% (2 individuals) Duffy-negative, both presenting with mixed P. vivax/P. falciparum infections and low parasitemia. This rare finding of Duffy-negative individuals with P. vivax infection suggests the existence of alternative invasion pathways beyond the classical Duffy-PvDBP interaction.

articles.headline: Substantial outdoor morning malaria vector biting limits bed nets alone in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Mosquito vector composition and biting behavior of malaria vectors in forest versus humid savanna eco-epidemiological settings of Cameroon.
articles.details: Entomological survey in Mfou (forest) and Tibati (savanna), Cameroon (April-July 2019) collecting 3,651 Anopheles mosquitoes (1,817 and 1,834, respectively) via human landing catches; identified nine species and measured Plasmodium infection by qPCR. In Mfou, An. gambiae s.l. predominated (68.96%); in Tibati, An. funestus s.l. (49.95%). Peak biting occurred outdoors 4-5 hours post-dawn at 2.68 bites/person/hour (Mfou) and 2.31 (Tibati). Entomological inoculation rate higher in Mfou (indoors 1.47 ib/p/n; outdoors 1.00) than Tibati (0.25 and 0.23, respectively), indicating substantial outdoor transmission limits bed nets as sole intervention.

articles.headline: Sickle cell trait protects infants from malaria; modern LLINs cut incidence 80%
articles.title_original: Natural History and the Burden of Malaria During the First Year of Life in the High-Transmission Setting of Uganda.
articles.details: Prospective cohort of 855 infants in Busia District, Uganda (2021-2025, pre-vaccine rollout), enrolled at 4-8 weeks and followed to 1 year with routine microscopy and qPCR every 4 weeks. Over 706.7 person-years, 662 malaria episodes occurred; microscopic parasitemia prevalence 7.9%, combined microscopic/submicroscopic 21.8%. Sickle cell trait (HbAS) conferred 39% protection against symptomatic malaria; modern housing and higher maternal education independently reduced risk. District-wide distribution of long-lasting insecticide-treated nets (alpha-cypermethrin plus chlorfenapyr) in October 2023 was followed by 80% reduction in malaria incidence and significant declines in parasitemia prevalence.
articles.notable_reason: 80% incidence reduction with modern LLINs; infant protection urgently needed

articles.headline: Wavelet and SARIMA models reveal malaria seasonality and forecast Nigerian cases
articles.title_original: Exploring the past and forecasting the future of malaria in selected Nigerian states: A time series modelling approach using wavelet and SARIMA.
articles.details: Time series analysis of confirmed uncomplicated malaria cases from Nasarawa, Kwara, and Zamfara states, Nigeria (January 2015 to December 2024) using wavelet analysis and SARIMA modeling. Nasarawa showed strong annual seasonality with September peaks (70% of years); Kwara exhibited semi-annual and annual cycles with August peaks; Zamfara showed moderate yearly cycles with September peaks (except 2024). Bivariate wavelet analysis revealed near-perfect synchrony between Nasarawa and Zamfara; SARIMA forecasts for 2025-2026: Nasarawa 51,482 cases/month (95% CI: 23,899-116,073), Kwara 20,850 (3,381-202,511), Zamfara 67,463 (27,317-171,418).

articles.headline: Duffy null alleles under selection may explain low P. vivax in Oman
articles.title_original: Adaptive admixture at ACKR1, the Duffy blood group locus, may have shaped Plasmodium vivax prevalence in Oman.
articles.details: Whole-genome sequence analysis of 100 Omani individuals showing 15.6% shared African ancestry (95% CI: 10.8%-20.3%), with 76% African ancestry specifically at the Duffy locus. High frequency of FyES (Duffy null allele) detected; selection coefficient estimated at 0.038 (95% CI: 0.027-0.051) with introduction ≥59 generations ago, older than in other African-admixed populations. Additional rare Duffy null allele identified across Arabian Peninsula populations. Unlike P. falciparum-protective alleles which did not show enriched African ancestry, Duffy null alleles show strong evidence of adaptive admixture, suggesting this selection may have shaped P. vivax prevalence in the region.

articles.headline: Computational framework prioritizes P. falciparum vaccine T cell epitopes
articles.title_original: TEpiNom: A computational framework integrating population data to prioritize Plasmodium falciparum T cell epitopes.
articles.details: TEpiNom integrates P. falciparum sequence diversity from 18 African countries, predicted T cell epitope-HLA binding, and HLA allele frequencies from 24 sub-Saharan populations to identify vaccine candidates. Analysis of 42 vaccine-candidate proteins identified 2265 MHC I and 1992 MHC II conserved epitopes across parasite life stages. Prioritized MHC I epitopes from FabZ, FabG, p36, and PKG achieved 100% predicted inter-locus coverage; epitope-dense regions provided complete coverage, highlighting compact targets for experimental evaluation.

articles.headline: Perceived malaria control paradoxically increases vaccine hesitancy
articles.title_original: Impact of caregivers' perceptions of malaria on their hesitancy toward childhood malaria vaccination.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 2590 adults in 6 Ghanaian regions. Malaria vaccine hesitancy prevalence was 44.8%. Greater perceived control and understanding of malaria was associated with increased hesitancy (aPR = 1.15; 95% CI: 1.09–1.21; p < 0.001). Additional risk factors included region, marital status, ethnicity, housing structure, employment, existing health conditions, and alcohol intake. Familiarity with malaria paradoxically weakens preventive behavior.

articles.headline: Iron-oxide nanoparticles enhance malaria vaccine antibody responses
articles.title_original: Iron-oxide nanoparticles (SPIONs) enhance malaria vaccine antibody response.
articles.details: BALB/c mice (n=8/group) received: SPIONs alone, soluble PfMSP-1 25 μg (days 0/21/42), single-dose SPION-PfMSP-1, or three-dose SPION-PfMSP-1. Soluble antigen alone produced only moderate IgG titers. Single-dose SPION-PfMSP-1 generated delayed but steadily rising response; three-dose SPION-PfMSP-1 produced highest and most durable titers throughout observation. Fe3O4-COOH SPIONs notably improved magnitude and longevity of anti-PfMSP-1 antibodies, supporting SPIONs as effective vaccine-delivery platform.

articles.headline: Plasmodium juxtanucleare hyper-endemic in Thai chickens
articles.title_original: Molecular prevalence and phylogenetic characterization of Plasmodium juxtanucleare in Thai native chickens and fighting cocks across Kalasin Province, Northeast Thailand.
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey (January–April 2025) of 181 blood samples from Thai native chickens (n=112) and fighting cocks (n=69) across 18 districts in Kalasin Province. Overall Plasmodium prevalence 62.98% (114/181); higher in native chickens (67.85%) vs. fighting cocks (55.07%), not significant (p > 0.05). Prevalence varied significantly by district (20%–100%, p < 0.05). All isolates were Plasmodium juxtanucleare (99–100% nucleotide identity). Two haplotypes detected; Haplotype I dominant.

articles.headline: Sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance widespread across India
articles.title_original: Genetic profiling of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance markers in Indian Plasmodium falciparum field isolates.
articles.details: Analysis of 307 isolates collected 1993–2023 from 14 Indian states; 243 Pfdhfr and 241 Pfdhps successfully sequenced. Most frequent mutations: Pfdhfr S108N (77.8%), C59R (61.7%); Pfdhps A437G (31.9%), K540E/N (4.98%). Combined analysis revealed 83% of isolates carried resistant alleles; double and triple mutants predominated. Weak positive selection at Pfdhfr 51 and Pfdhps 436, 540. SP resistance widespread with strong regional differences; gradual rise of higher-order mutants, especially from North-East, raises concern for 2030 elimination target.

articles.headline: Quality assurance essential for malaria rapid diagnostic tests
articles.title_original: Quality assurance in rapid diagnostic tests for accurate diagnosis of malaria.
articles.details: Review emphasizing rigorous quality assurance frameworks for rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) to maintain diagnostic accuracy and support malaria elimination. Inaccurate RDT performance can lead to misdiagnosis, inappropriate treatment, increased mortality, and emergence of drug-resistant strains. Billions of RDTs distributed globally over the past decade, making QA critical for disease surveillance and control.

articles.headline: P. yoelii TRAg7 mediates infected RBC splenic sequestration
articles.title_original: Plasmodium yoelii tryptophan-rich antigen 7 mediates infected erythrocyte adhesion to splenic fibroblasts via binding to vimentin.
articles.details: Experimental study using P. yoelii murine model. PyTRAg7 interacts with vimentin on mouse splenic fibroblasts, activating NF-κB p65 signaling and increasing ICAM-1 and integrin β1 expression. Deletion of PyTRAg7 reduced infected RBC adhesion to splenic fibroblasts, lowered splenic parasite burden, delayed parasitemia onset, and prolonged host survival. Histological analysis showed preserved splenic architecture and reduced hemozoin deposition without PyTRAg7. TRAgs are conserved in human malaria parasites.

articles.headline: Comparative evaluation of P. falciparum synchronization methods
articles.title_original: Comparative evaluation of synchronization methods for Plasmodium falciparum: Efficiency, practicality and experimental applicability.
articles.details: Quantitative comparison of four synchronization methods for P. falciparum 3D7: 5% D-sorbitol, 60% Percoll-Sorbitol, 70%–40% Percoll-Sorbitol, and Plasmion. Efficiency assessed by microscopy and flow cytometry; novel 20 nm polystyrene fluorescent microspheres (PFMs20) used to validate enrichment. Results: sorbitol achieved 88.37% ring-stage purity; 60% PS showed 87.47% PFMs20 binding; 70%–40% PS showed 88.12% binding; Plasmion yielded 73.37%. Provides decision matrix for protocol selection by experimental need.

articles.headline: Opsoclonus myoclonus syndrome following severe malaria in HIV
articles.title_original: Opsoclonus myoclonus syndrome in an HIV patient, following plasmodium falciparum infection.
articles.details: Case report: 47-year-old HIV-positive woman on stable HAART (undetectable viral load) developed opsoclonus-myoclonus syndrome two weeks after treatment for severe P. falciparum malaria. Presented with multidirectional ocular saccades, trunk myoclonus, aggressive behavior, sleep disturbances, and severe ataxia. Initial high-dose IV methylprednisolone produced partial improvement; addition of rituximab led to complete symptom resolution and restoration of functional autonomy. Suggests possible relationship between malaria and OMS in HIV context.

articles.headline: Malaria deaths rising despite vaccine availability
articles.title_original: Vaccines mean malaria deaths should be falling - not rising.
articles.details: Editorial perspective examining the paradox that malaria deaths are not declining as expected despite vaccine rollout.

articles.headline: P. vivax merozoite platform reveals antibody-dependent NK activation
articles.title_original: Accessing invasion-competent Plasmodium vivax merozoites reveals antibody-dependent NK cell activation.
articles.details: Established robust platform for synchronized release and high-yield isolation of invasion-competent P. vivax merozoites from ex vivo cultures using ML10 (cGMP-PK inhibitor) and liposome-based extrusion. Recovery rates exceeded 75% while preserving structural integrity and invasive capacity. Provided direct functional evidence that antibody-opsonized P. vivax merozoites activate human natural killer cells consistent with antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity. Enables direct investigation of P. vivax merozoite invasion biology.

articles.headline: Multidrug-resistant P. falciparum emerging in northern Burundi
articles.title_original: Border Region Surveillance of Malaria Drug Resistance, Northern Burundi, 2023-2024.
articles.details: Study of 423 children <5 years with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria in 8 health facilities across northern Burundi (December 2023–June 2024). After artemether/lumefantrine with first dose directly observed, 4.5% remained parasitemic on day 3. No pfkelch13 mutations detected. However, markers of multidrug resistance were widespread: dhfr triple mutant N51I/C59R/S108N nearly fixed at 92%; dhps double and triple mutants common at 41% and 47%; pfcrt CVIET predominant at 84%. Geographic variation in day-3 positivity and haplotype frequencies noted. Authors warn that delayed clearance and near fixation of multidrug-resistant haplotypes warrant urgently strengthened efficacy monitoring.

articles.headline: Spatial distribution of malaria by bed net use across sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Spatial epidemiology of malaria by bed net utilization in 19 sub-Saharan African countries: a DHS-based study (2013-2023).
articles.details: Cross-sectional DHS study of 126,424 individuals across 11,594 clusters in 19 sub-Saharan African countries. Overall malaria prevalence 24.0% (95% CI 18-30%), varying by net use: no net 32.75%, untreated net 28.91%, ITN users 13.23%. Spatial analysis showed significant clustering (Moran's I = 0.159, p ≤ 0.001) with 34 clusters identified overall. Hotspots in Benin, Burkina Faso, Togo, Uganda, Rwanda, and parts of DRC and Mozambique suggest geographically targeted control interventions.

articles.headline: P. falciparum AMA1 haplotypes not associated with malaria symptoms
articles.title_original: Distribution of Plasmodium falciparum apical membrane antigen-1 cluster one loop haplotypes and their association with malaria symptoms in Bandiagara, Mali.
articles.details: Three-year malaria incidence study analyzing ama1 gene sequences from 100 children in Bandiagara, Mali. Tested whether specific cluster one loop (c1L) haplotypes of apical membrane antigen 1 were associated with clinical malaria (temperature >37.5°C and parasitemia ≥2,500/μL). After adjusting for location, no specific c1L haplotypes were linked to malaria symptoms or disease phenotypes. Authors conclude symptoms may be driven by infections with new alleles rather than previously encountered variants.

articles.headline: African countries adapt malaria control strategies to climate change
articles.title_original: Climate change and malaria control in Africa: country experiences and strategic responses.
articles.details: Synthesis of malaria control responses from six African countries (Chad, Côte d'Ivoire, DRC, Madagascar, Rwanda, Senegal) examining climate-driven epidemiologic changes. Changing rainfall and flooding patterns affect mosquito habitats and transmission. Adaptive strategies include integrating climate-health data, strengthening surveillance, establishing early warning systems, engaging communities, and promoting multisectoral collaboration. Systemic barriers—limited data capacity, infrastructure deficits, behavioral factors, financial constraints—continue to impede implementation.

articles.headline: ZY19489 resistance creates evolutionary trap for artemisinin combination therapy
articles.title_original: Collateral hypersensitivity between ZY19489 and piperaquine neutralizes PfCRT-mediated drug efflux and Plasmodium falciparum resistance.
articles.details: Phase Ib triaminopyrimidine ZY19489 produces novel PfCRT mutation mediating low-grade parasite resistance with substantial fitness cost. ZY19489-resistant parasites lose chloroquine resistance and become hypersusceptible to piperaquine. Mechanistically, ZY19489 blocks mutant PfCRT-mediated piperaquine and chloroquine efflux. Metabolomic studies show reduced intracellular hemoglobin-derived peptides and pyrimidine deoxynucleotide accumulation. This "evolutionary trap" suggests pairing ZY19489 with piperaquine could generate a resistance-refractory combination.

articles.headline: Scoping review protocol on African stakeholder perspectives on gene drive
articles.title_original: Knowledge, perspectives, and risk perceptions on gene drive and genetically modified mosquitoes for malaria control among African stakeholders: a scoping review protocol.
articles.details: Protocol for scoping review examining knowledge, perspectives, and risk perceptions of African stakeholders regarding gene drive and genetically modified mosquitoes for malaria control. Will search PubMed, Embase, Science Direct, Cochrane, and Google Scholar with dual independent screening and thematic analysis. Objectives include understanding governance, acceptance, and risk implications among key African stakeholders to improve community engagement for technology implementation.

articles.headline: Phenotypic screening identifies new nematocidal compounds from malaria library
articles.title_original: High-throughput phenotypic screening of Medicines for Malaria Venture's Hit Generation Library 1 identifies new nematocidal chemotypes.
articles.journal: Int J Parasitol Drugs Drug Resist
articles.details: High-throughput screen of 139,916 compounds from Medicines for Malaria Venture library against Haemonchus contortus larvae. Identified 272 primary hits (0.194%) and confirmed 110 active compounds with reproducible larval motility and development inhibition. 39 compounds showed IC50 <10 μM; 33 caused complete developmental arrest at ≤12.5 μM. Four compounds were non-toxic to HepG2 cells (CC50 ≥20 μM), with favorable physicochemical properties and 16 meeting criteria for medicinal chemistry progression.

articles.headline: Plasmodium vivax gametocytes found in bone marrow with pancytopenia
articles.title_original: A mysterious foe in a case of pancytopenia with splenomegaly: Plasmodium vivax gametocytes in the bone marrow.
articles.details: Case report of a patient presenting with pancytopenia and splenomegaly found to have Plasmodium vivax gametocytes in bone marrow, a rarely documented manifestation of P. vivax infection.

articles.headline: Gut microbiome composition predicts malaria parasitemia levels
articles.title_original: Gut check: converging evidence links microbiome to malaria risk.
articles.details: Research demonstrates pre-infection microbiome composition predicts Plasmodium parasitemia levels in both rhesus macaques and human volunteers, indicating microbiome as underappreciated determinant of malaria burden. Convergent evidence points to Bifidobacterium as potentially protective genus. Findings suggest microbiome-targeted interventions may influence parasitemia and malaria risk.

articles.headline: Var2csa locus mediates transcriptional switching in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Mechanistic insights into coordinated var transcriptional switching in malaria parasites.
articles.details: Mechanistic study reveals transient var2csa locus activation mediates var gene switching, enabling parasites to escape antibody recognition and establish chronic infection. Non-coding RNAs from var2csa repress var2csa transcription and affect switching frequencies. An upstream open reading frame in var2csa 5'-UTR destabilizes mRNA through nonsense-mediated decay pathway, promoting alternative var gene activation. Findings provide molecular insights into coordinated var family transcriptional switching underlying chronic infection.

articles.headline: Single drugs with multiple targets in malaria treatment strategy
articles.title_original: Polypharmacology in malaria treatment: single drugs, multiple mechanisms, greater impact.
articles.details: Review exploring polypharmacology approach to malaria treatment, examining how single drugs targeting multiple parasite mechanisms can achieve combination-therapy-like efficacy while reducing resistance likelihood. Historical examples include natural products like quinine and artemisinin. Discusses opportunities and challenges in developing next-generation antimalarials with multitarget mechanisms. Concludes harnessing polypharmacology could prolong therapeutic durability against drug-resistant Plasmodium species.

articles.headline: ACT resistance markers detected in Brazilian Amazon P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Emergence of artemisinin-based combination therapy resistance markers in Plasmodium falciparum from the Brazilian tri-border region of the Guiana Shield.
articles.details: Deep sequencing of 99 P. falciparum isolates from Roraima State, Brazil (2016-2020) detected pfmdr1-NFD haplotype in 6%, pfcrt-C350R in 7%, pfpm2 amplification 5%, pfpm3 amplification 6%, and pfpm2/3 hybrid in 4%; no validated artemisinin-partial-resistance pfk13 mutations detected. First report of pfpm2/3 hybrid and pfmdr1-NFD in Brazil. These variants, associated with reduced susceptibility to lumefantrine and piperaquine, indicate parasites carry resistance markers prevalent in neighboring countries.

articles.headline: Anopheles microbiome manipulation as complementary malaria control strategy
articles.title_original: Exploring the gut micriobiome in Anopheles mosquitoes: A promising new approach to malaria control.
articles.details: Critical review evaluating Anopheles mosquito microbiome manipulation as complementary malaria control strategy beyond ITNs and IRS. Examines bacterial, fungal, and viral microbiome components and mechanisms including immune modulation, nutrient competition, direct antiparasitic activity, and interference with mosquito physiology. Identifies key limitations: laboratory bias, oversimplification of microbial consortia, context-dependent outcomes, and ecological uncertainties challenging field deployment. Recommends balanced framework for future research and responsible translation.

articles.headline: VAR2CSA vaccine target shows two divergent lineages in Thailand
articles.title_original: Two divergent VAR2CSA lineages with distinct evolutionary signatures circulate in Plasmodium falciparum from Thailand.
articles.details: Analysis of complete var2csa coding sequences (9.1–9.9 kb) from 31 symptomatic P. falciparum isolates from five Thai provinces identified 24 distinct haplotypes (h = 0.97 ± 0.02). Two major lineages co-circulate: a dominant 3D7-type and a distinct Dd2-type. Nucleotide diversity significantly higher in 3' region (π = 0.4253 vs 0.1033, p < 0.0001). Pronounced divergence between lineages (Dxy = 0.506, Da = 0.444) indicates bipartite variation essential for vaccine design.

articles.headline: Bisquinoline analog shows enhanced in vivo efficacy vs drug-resistant malaria
articles.title_original: Designing novel bisquinoline antimalarials from historical 4-aminoquinolines to combat drug-resistant malaria.
articles.details: Structure–activity study of bisquinoline analogs for drug-resistant P. falciparum. Lead compound 25 demonstrated potent in vitro activity (IC50 < 20 nM) against drug-sensitive and multidrug-resistant strains, improved metabolic stability (t1/2 = 121 min), and enhanced in vivo efficacy (ED50 = 0.32 mg/kg/day) with complete curative protection at reduced dose compared to reference ADC-028.

articles.headline: 5-ALA does not enhance artemisinin against malaria parasites
articles.title_original: 5-ALA does not potentiate dihydroartemisinin against Plasmodium falciparum malaria parasites.
articles.authors: Behrens HM, Henshall IG, Spielmann T
articles.details: Correspondence reporting that 5-aminolaevulinic acid (5-ALA), which potentiates artemisinin in cancer, does not potentiate dihydroartemisinin against P. falciparum malaria parasites. Co-administration of artemisinin and 5-ALA is not a promising strategy for malaria treatment improvement.

articles.headline: Digestion genes mapped in neotropical malaria vector Anopheles aquasalis
articles.title_original: Genomic profiling of digestion related enzymes in Anopheles aquasalis a major coastal neotropical malaria vector.
articles.details: Genomic profiling identified 241 digestion-related genes in An. aquasalis: 56 carbohydrate, 51 lipid, 134 protein. Phylogenetic analysis showed close relationships to A. darlingi and A. albimanus with conserved gene clusters. Identified potential vector control targets (alpha-amylase, maltase, serine proteases) and transmission-blocking vaccine targets (aminopeptidase N1, carboxypeptidase B) that may affect Plasmodium development in the midgut.

articles.headline: pfhrp2/3 deletions detected in 3.5% of malaria traveller cohort
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum histidine rich protein 2/3 gene deletions affecting rapid diagnostic test performance in travellers.
articles.details: Among 115 travellers with malaria, pfhrp2/3 deletions occurred in 4 cases (3.5%), with no double deletions. 51.3% of patients had visited countries anticipated to surpass WHO 5% pfhrp2/3 threshold by 2028. Finding emphasizes need for continued monitoring of deletions affecting rapid diagnostic test performance in non-endemic settings.

articles.headline: P. falciparum infections estimated in sub-Saharan African refugee arrivals
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum infections and number needed to screen among refugees arriving from sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.details: Modeling study of 56,248 asylum applicants and quota refugees from sub-Saharan Africa arriving to Sweden 2015–22 estimated 3,930 (7%) asymptomatic P. falciparum infections (1,789 children, 2,141 adults including 851 reproductive-age women). Annual estimate 491 infections (range 356–546), 80% from high and moderate-transmission countries. Number needed to screen estimated at 2–14 in high-moderate transmission populations.

articles.headline: Multi-scale transformer architecture for malaria cell classification
articles.title_original: ISTNet: a multi-scale transformer-based architecture for malaria cell classification.
articles.authors: Wu P, Gao X, Yu H
articles.details: Title indicates development of ISTNet, a multi-scale transformer-based deep learning architecture designed for automated malaria cell classification. No abstract provided; further technical details unavailable.

articles.headline: Correction to endothelial adhesion receptor expression study
articles.title_original: Correction to: Mapping the expression of endothelial adhesion receptors for Plasmodium falciparum-infected erythrocytes in fatal cerebral malaria in Malawian children.
articles.details: Correction to previously published research on endothelial adhesion receptor expression for P. falciparum-infected erythrocytes in fatal cerebral malaria in Malawian children. Specific correction details not provided in available title.

articles.headline: Piperaquine resistance markers tracked over 25 years in Southeast Asia
articles.title_original: Changing genetic profiles of Plasmodium falciparum piperaquine resistance in Southeast Asia over 25 years.
articles.details: Longitudinal analysis of 898 P. falciparum isolates from five Southeast Asian countries (1995–2023) assessed pfcrt mutations and pfplasmepsin2 copy number variation. Regional variation: Cambodia (H97Y 15%, T93S 8%), Thailand (I218F up to 24.44%, G353V 20%), Vietnam (T93S 21%). Over 65% of parasites with pfcrt mutations also carried pfplasmepsin2 amplification.
