articles.headline: Severe malaria predominantly affects HIV+ patients with advanced immunosuppression in Gabon
articles.title_original: Clinical and biological features of complicated and asymptomatic malaria among PLHIV and HIV-negative adults: An observational study in Libreville, Gabon.
articles.details: A cross-sectional study at Centre Hospitalier Universitaire de Libreville (September 2021-October 2022) enrolled 56 PLHIV and 66 HIV-negative individuals with P. falciparum malaria. Asymptomatic malaria was more frequent in HIV-negative patients (60.6% vs 25.0%), while severe malaria significantly predominated in PLHIV (48.2% vs 12.1%, p<0.01). Severe manifestations (impaired consciousness 26.2%, seizures 14.3%, anemia 14.3%) correlated with lower median CD4+ counts (150 vs 304 cells/µL, p=0.018).

articles.headline: Correction to P. vivax population genetics study from Papua New Guinea
articles.title_original: Correction: Population genetics of Plasmodium vivax with transmission decline and rebound in two endemic areas of Papua New Guinea.
articles.details: This is a correction notice for a previously published article on Plasmodium vivax population genetics examining transmission dynamics in Papua New Guinea.

articles.headline: Plant extracts from Rhaponticum and Achillea show potent in vitro antiparasitic activity
articles.title_original: In Vitro Antiparasitic Activity and Phytochemical Profiling of Rhaponticum repens and Achillea millefolium Methanolic Extracts Against Plasmodium Falciparum and Leishmania major.
articles.details: Methanolic extracts of Rhaponticum repens and Achillea millefolium were tested in vitro against P. falciparum and Leishmania major clinical isolates. A. millefolium demonstrated higher activity against P. falciparum (94% inhibition at 2.0 mg/mL, IC50 0.52 mg/mL, selectivity index 26.3), while R. repens was more effective against L. major (81% inhibition, IC50 1.18 mg/mL, SI 10.6). Both extracts showed low cytotoxicity toward mammalian Vero cells, supporting natural product drug development.

articles.headline: Machine learning framework predicts antimalarial activity of Malaria Box compounds
articles.title_original: Hybrid Computational Framework Integrating Ensemble Learning, Molecular Docking, and Dynamics for Predicting Antimalarial Efficacy of Malaria Box Compounds.
articles.details: Researchers integrated ensemble machine learning, molecular docking, and 200-nanosecond molecular dynamics simulations to predict antimalarial efficacy of compounds from the Malaria Box database. The classification model achieved 10-fold cross-validation accuracy of 0.738 and external accuracy of 0.675, while regression models within active classes achieved Q2 values of 0.810-0.793. Molecular docking revealed strong binding affinities to P. falciparum Cytochrome B, with several compounds showing stable ligand-protein complexes.

articles.headline: Helminth co-infection modulates antimalarial antibody responses in endemic populations
articles.title_original: The association of helminth infection against antimalarial antibody responses in malaria infection and vaccination: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.details: A systematic review and meta-analysis of 29 studies examined helminth infection and antimalarial antibody responses. Meta-analysis from eight human studies found that any helminth infection was associated with higher antimalarial IgG responses (SMD -0.44 [95% CI -0.61 to -0.27], p<0.0001), while Schistosoma haematobium showed marginally lower overall IgG (SMD 0.40, p=0.0492) but trends toward better IgG1 and IgG3 responses. Helminth modulation of antibody responses depends on infecting species and antigen type.

articles.headline: Pan-pLDH biomarker identifies severe imported malaria via smartphone video analysis
articles.title_original: pLDH to identify severity in imported malaria: Implementing smartphone video analysis for rapid clinical decision-making.
articles.details: This retrospective cohort study of imported P. falciparum malaria evaluated PfHRP2 and pan-specific lactate dehydrogenase (pan-pLDH) biomarkers using bead-based immunoassays and lateral flow assays with smartphone video-analysis. Pan-pLDH excelled in identifying severe malaria cases, outperforming PfHRP2, while maintaining PfHRP2's perfect diagnostic accuracy. Smartphone-based video-analysis reduced testing time to under 6 minutes, offering rapid clinical decision-making for severity assessment in non-endemic settings.

articles.headline: P. vivax and arboviruses frequently co-circulate on Brazil-French Guiana border
articles.title_original: Co-circulation of Plasmodium vivax and Arboviruses in the Brazil-French Guiana Border.
articles.details: A serological analysis of 300 serum samples (2014-2015) from the Brazil-French Guiana border, including 102 malaria cases and 198 controls, detected high IgG reactivity for dengue and chikungunya viruses with frequent co-reactivity, while Zika virus detection was minimal. These findings highlight the overlapping transmission of P. vivax and arboviruses in this region, emphasizing the need for integrated surveillance strategies to address co-circulation and co-infection risks.

articles.headline: Primaquine plus dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine reduces malaria in simulation
articles.title_original: Agent-based simulation of seasonal malaria chemoprevention strategy in Southern Tanzania: comparing dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine with or without primaquine.
articles.details: Agent-based model of seasonal malaria chemoprevention in Tanzania (2,275 participants) comparing dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP) alone versus DP plus single-dose primaquine (SLDPQ). After three monthly SMC rounds, DP-only group's malaria prevalence declined from 11.5% to 4.1% at day 125, then rebounded to 7.1% at day 180. Addition of SLDPQ further reduced prevalence to 1.4% at day 125 and 3.9% at day 180. Model suggests SLDPQ combined with DP reduces transmission regardless of temperature variations.

articles.headline: Education and socioeconomic factors promote safer sex negotiation
articles.title_original: Safer Sex Negotiation Among Women in Sexual Unions in Tanzania: Analysis of the 2022 Demographic and Health Survey and Malaria Indicator Survey.
articles.details: Analysis of 2022 Tanzania Demographic and Health Survey data found 75.8% of women in sexual unions reported safer sex negotiation. Factors associated with higher negotiation included primary/secondary education (APR 1.16–1.21), middle/rich household status (APR 1.10–1.12), media exposure (APR 1.14), employment (APR 1.06), STI awareness (APR 1.17), and internet use (APR 1.04). Partner education and geographic location also showed significant associations.

articles.headline: Climate change threatens malaria eradication progress
articles.title_original: Climate change could hinder malaria eradication efforts.
articles.details: No abstract available; title indicates climate change may hinder malaria eradication efforts.

articles.headline: Climate forecasting model predicts malaria surges in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Modelling the association of rainfall and temperature with malaria incidence in Adamawa State, Nigeria.
articles.details: Time series analysis of monthly malaria data from Adamawa State, Nigeria (January 2015–April 2024) using SARIMAX modeling with temperature and rainfall as predictors. The model captured annual seasonality and generated forecasts for May 2024–December 2025, projecting approximately 67,052 cases in August 2024 and peak incidence of 80,004 cases in October 2025. Findings highlight climate variability's influence on malaria dynamics and support need for early warning systems.

articles.headline: Author correction on malaria trends and global warming
articles.title_original: Author Correction: Malaria trends in Ethiopian highlands track the 2000 'slowdown' in global warming.
articles.details: Author correction regarding published research on malaria trends in Ethiopian highlands and global warming.

articles.headline: Community engagement builds acceptance for ivermectin malaria control
articles.title_original: Community perceptions and acceptance of ivermectin for malaria control on Sumba Island, Indonesia.
articles.details: Qualitative social science study alongside SLIM trial in four villages on Sumba Island, Indonesia (November 2022–September 2023). Focus group discussions (59 participants) and individual interviews (16 participants) examined community perceptions of ivermectin-treated livestock (ITL) and potential mass drug administration (MDA). Trust-building through village meetings and respectful communication mitigated initial skepticism and led to high acceptance of ITL. Communities expressed interest in future research but raised safety concerns about MDA in humans.

articles.headline: Plasmodium infection reduces gut bacterial diversity in birds
articles.title_original: Microbial community composition variation in relation to malaria infections in Junco hyemalis.
articles.authors: Amaya-Mejia WS, Sehgal RNM, Yeh PJ
articles.details: Comparison of gut microbiota composition in eight wild Oregon Juncos infected with Plasmodium relictum GRW04 versus eight non-infected birds. Alpha diversity was significantly lower in infected birds, with compositional changes primarily among rare bacterial taxa. Infected birds showed depletion of Muribaculaceae, Acidobacteriales, Pseudomonas, and Escherichia-Shingella. Co-occurrence networks in infected birds had fewer bacterial connections and absent hub taxa, suggesting parasite-mediated dysbiosis.

articles.headline: Multi-pronged strategy effectively manages P. vivax outbreak in Suriname
articles.title_original: A six-pronged approach to manage a Plasmodium vivax outbreak in a low- and middle-income country on the road to malaria elimination.
articles.details: Descriptive analysis of Suriname's 2019 P. vivax outbreak response (November 2019–August 2021) involving 179 infections across 153 patients over 22 months. Response employed conventional strategies (1-3-7 surveillance, community education, vector control) plus innovative measures (mass screen-and-treat, mass drug administration, parasite genotyping). Mass drug administration was more effective than mass screen-and-treat in reducing parasitemia. Directly observed treatment achieved 95.1% efficacy by day 180, but relapses sustained transmission until 'Radical Cure' treatment terminated the outbreak.

articles.headline: Low RTS,S vaccine uptake and timeliness limit impact in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Uptake of malaria vaccine (RTS,S/AS01) among children aged 6-24 months: A cross-sectional survey conducted in the Western region of Cameroon, 1 year following the vaccine introduction.
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of RTS,S/AS01 vaccine coverage in Foumbot and Foumban districts, Cameroon, 1 year after introduction (399 children). Coverage was suboptimal: 31.20% for dose 1, 22.61% for dose 2, 17.70% for dose 3, with only 56.49% of dose-1 recipients completing the 3-dose series. Timeliness ranged 50–57% across doses. Parental perception was strongly associated with vaccination uptake (aOR 15.54, p<0.001). Authors conclude performance indicators are below expectations for significant malaria morbidity reduction.

articles.headline: Review finds no gender-responsive studies for TB or malaria
articles.title_original: Gender-responsive and -transformative interventions in HIV, TB and malaria programmes: a review of evidence.
articles.details: Rapid review synthesizing evidence on gender-responsive interventions in HIV, TB, and malaria programs (PubMed/Scopus search, 2004–2024, low- and middle-income countries). From 11,844 identified articles, 42 met inclusion criteria; all 42 focused exclusively on HIV, with zero studies on TB or malaria. Interventions were categorized into health systems (n=6), gender norms change (n=9), social empowerment (n=8), economic empowerment (n=18), and law/policy (n=1). Multi-component interventions showed more sustained improvements.

articles.headline: Colombian P. falciparum shows chloroquine and mefloquine resistance
articles.title_original: Antimalarial drug resistance in Plasmodium falciparum isolates from the Pacific Coast of Colombia.
articles.details: Assessment of antimalarial resistance in Plasmodium falciparum isolates from Colombia's Pacific Coast collected over 23 years using quantitative PCR and sequencing of resistance-associated loci (including PfKelch13). Multiple resistance genotypes and phenotypes persisted for chloroquine and mefloquine, but no artemisinin resistance-associated polymorphisms were detected. Continued chloroquine and mefloquine resistance argues against their use as partner drugs for artemisinin and reflects the unique local epidemiology maintaining this resistance profile.

articles.headline: SP ineffective for pregnancy malaria; DP superior in Uganda trial
articles.title_original: Recurrent Plasmodium falciparum parasitemia and drug resistance mutations during intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy in Uganda.
articles.details: Trial comparing SP, DP, and DP+SP for intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (1,377 samples from pregnant women in Busia, Uganda). 28-day cumulative risk of recurrent parasitemia was 57.8% with SP versus 4.1% with DP or 3.9% with DP+SP; symptomatic malaria was 9.3% versus 1.1%. SP also selected for increased high-level resistance mutations (PfDHFR I164L + PfDHPS A581G from 24.9% to 35.2%). Authors conclude IPTp-SP has poor efficacy and promotes resistance.
articles.notable_reason: SP ineffective; DP superior—guideline-relevant trial

articles.headline: Faster chemiluminescent assay for antimalarial drug screening
articles.title_original: Using Next Generation Chemiluminescent Probes to Improve the Plasmodium falciparum in vitro Parasite Reduction Ratio (PRR) Assay.
articles.details: Optimized PRR assay using lacZ/β-galSENSOR chemiluminescence reduced incubation from 14-28 days to 7 days while maintaining informative pharmacodynamic parameters (lag phase, clearance time, PRR, maximum killing effect). The [3H]-hypoxanthine method failed to detect parasites reliably with shortened protocol. Novel assay enables labs without radioactive facilities to evaluate compounds with greater convenience and higher throughput.

articles.headline: Nanotechnology-based herbal antimalarial therapies: a review
articles.title_original: Nano-Enabled Herbal Remedies for Malaria Treatment: A Review of Recent Advances.
articles.details: Review of nano-formulations of herbal remedies for malaria treatment, synthesizing evidence on nanoparticle-based delivery systems versus conventional treatments (chloroquine, ACT, quinine). Nanoparticles offer advantages including high drug-loading capacity, targeted delivery, improved biocompatibility, and reduced toxicity. No novel experimental data or quantitative results; review concludes nanotechnology-based therapeutics are promising candidates with commercialization potential.

articles.headline: Plasmodium falciparum drug susceptibility across 14 antimalarials in Mali
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum field isolates drug susceptibility in Mali.
articles.details: Ex vivo susceptibility testing of 26 P. falciparum isolates from three endemic sites in Mali against 14 drugs using fluorescence-based assays. Current frontline therapies remain highly potent: dihydroartemisinin (median IC50 <6 nM), lumefantrine (<50 nM), pyronaridine (<10 nM). Next-generation candidates cabamiquine and GNF179 showed strong activity (<10 nM). Authors conclude surveillance needed for early resistance detection despite current drug effectiveness.

articles.headline: Iran's malaria control progress and elimination challenges
articles.title_original: Iran's Journey Through Malaria: From Past Challenges to Future Elimination-A Narrative Review.
articles.details: Narrative review synthesizing WHO and national surveillance data (2000-2025). Iran reduced indigenous cases from thousands in early 2000s to <300 annually by mid-2010s with multiple years of zero transmission. COVID-19 pandemic disrupted progress; 2022 resurgence associated with imported cases and operational disruptions. Ongoing challenges: cross-border transmission, insecticide resistance, Anopheles stephensi spread.

articles.headline: Machine learning identifies kinase inhibitors with dual antimalarial activity
articles.title_original: Small Molecule Kinase Inhibitors with In Vitro β-Hematin Formation and Plasmodium falciparum Protein Kinase G Inhibitory Activity Identified Using Machine Learning.
articles.details: Molecular docking and machine learning screened BioVision kinase library for inhibitors of P. falciparum β-hematin formation and cGMP-dependent protein kinase (PfPKG). Derazantinib showed strongest activity: IC50 88 µM for β-hematin (compared to 22 µM for chloroquine) and 0.160 µM for PfPKG. Pazopanib and afatinib showed more moderate activity against both targets.

articles.headline: Avian malaria parasites in Iberian scavenger birds
articles.title_original: Unraveling Occurrence Patterns and Diversity of Avian Malaria Parasites in Iberian Obligate and Facultative Scavenger Birds.
articles.details: Active monitoring of 383 blood samples from free-living scavenger birds in Spain assessed haemosporidian prevalence and lineage diversity using nested-PCR. Overall prevalence 3.4% (13/383), with Leucocytozoon 2.3% and Plasmodium 1.6%. Black kites showed highest prevalence (8.3%), followed by cinereous vultures (5.3%). Authors report 10 new host-parasite interactions and novel Leucocytozoon lineage infecting European vultures.

articles.headline: SARIMAX model forecasts rising malaria incidence in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Forecasting malaria incidence in a resource-limited urban setting with climate variables as exogenous regressors: time series analysis using a SARIMAX model in Bahir Dar, Ethiopia.
articles.details: SARIMAX (1,2,2)(1,2,2,12) time-series model developed using 90 monthly malaria incidence data points (Jan 2018–June 2025) with weather exogenous regressors. Model shows increasing trend in forecast for 2026-2030. Weather variables not statistically significant predictors (p>0.05). Mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 28.3% indicates reasonable predictive accuracy.

articles.headline: miLab automated malaria detection outperforms microscopy
articles.title_original: Evaluating the diagnostic performance of miLab™ for detection of malaria parasites using nPCR as reference standard.
articles.details: Hospital cross-sectional study of 300 suspected malaria patients in Kumasi, Ghana (Aug 2024–June 2025) evaluated miLab AI-assisted platform versus microscopy (reference: nested-PCR). miLab achieved sensitivity 94.23%, specificity 98.98%, accuracy 97.33% compared to adjudicated microscopy sensitivity 85.58%, specificity 97.96%, accuracy 93.67%. Authors conclude miLab could provide reliable, rapid diagnostic alternative in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Parental health-seeking behaviors for severe childhood malaria in DRC
articles.title_original: Young children with severe malaria in the Kapolowe health zone: an analysis of their parents' health-seeking behaviors and the challenges they face.
articles.details: Qualitative study with 30 focus group discussions in Kapolowe health zone, Democratic Republic of Congo; 9 analyzed in depth using inductive method guided by three-delays model. Mothers frequently delayed seeking care and used self-medication with modern and traditional remedies. Major barriers: geographical distance, impassable roads, lack of facilities, financial costs. Cultural factors (preference for traditional practices and prayer) significantly influenced care-seeking decisions.

articles.headline: SD Bioline Malaria Ag P.f. RDT performance in Nigerian children
articles.title_original: Field performance of the SD Bioline Malaria Ag P.f. rapid diagnostic test among children under five in Nigeria: insights from the 2021 Malaria Indicator Survey.
articles.authors: Olubiyi B, Alabi A, Isiko I
articles.details: Secondary analysis of 2021 Nigerian Malaria Indicator Survey; 9,067 children aged 6–59 months with complete RDT and microscopy results. SD Bioline RDT versus microscopy (reference): sensitivity 88.6%, specificity 75.2%, positive predictive value 49.5%, negative predictive value 96.0%, accuracy 78.1%, Cohen's kappa 0.496. Authors conclude high sensitivity and NPV confirm reliability for ruling out malaria but moderate specificity and low PPV risk overestimating prevalence.

articles.headline: Clinical severity and haematological features of malaria in Pakistan
articles.title_original: Burden of Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium falciparum Malaria: clinical insights from a prospective study in Jacobabad, Sindh, Pakistan.
articles.authors: Noor M, Junaid M, Furqan A
articles.details: Prospective observational study of 260 malaria-admitted patients in Jacobabad, Sindh, Pakistan (2024–2025). Uncomplicated malaria 83.5% (217 cases), severe 16.5% (43 cases). Severe malaria significantly associated with jaundice, bleeding, pallor, respiratory distress, fits, cerebral malaria, anaemia, and thrombocytopenia. Overall case fatality rate 16.2% in pediatric age group; cerebral malaria the most fatal complication.

articles.headline: Cytochrome P450 polymorphisms influence antimalarial pharmacokinetics
articles.title_original: Pharmacogenetic Profiling of Cytochrome P450 Enzymes in Plasmodium vivax Patients Treated with Chloroquine and Primaquine: Implications for Personalized Malaria Therapy.
articles.details: Pharmacogenetic study of 99 Burmese P. vivax patients treated with chloroquine (25 mg/kg) and primaquine (15 mg/kg/day for 14 days). Genotyping identified most frequent alleles: CYP3A5*3 (71.7%) and CYP2D6*10 (30.6%). CYP3A5*3 associated with altered desethylchloroquine levels; CYP2C19*3 with faster parasite clearance. All patients achieved complete parasite clearance and fever resolution without recurrence despite high prevalence of reduced-function variants.

articles.headline: Engineered mosquito bacteria block malaria parasite transmission
articles.title_original: Evaluating paratransgenesis using engineered symbiotic bacteria for Plasmodium inhibition in mosquito vectors: A systematic review.
articles.details: Systematic review (PRISMA) of 10 studies examining paratransgenesis: engineered bacterial and fungal symbionts (Asaia, Serratia, Pantoea, Enterobacter, Aspergillus oryzae) delivering effector proteins (Scorpine, EPIP, Defensin, SM1-2 peptides) into mosquito midguts. Scorpine achieved up to 97.8% inhibition of P. falciparum oocysts; multiple-effector combinations exceeded 89% inhibition. Authors conclude paratransgenesis is potentially important malaria control tool, though ecological safety, microbial stability, and field validation remain obstacles.

articles.headline: Nanopore sequencing identifies mosquito species and detects malaria simultaneously
articles.title_original: A multiplex assay to detect mosquito species, bloodmeal host source and Plasmodium in malaria vectors using Nanopore amplicon sequencing.
articles.details: Study of Oxford Nanopore MinION sequencing to simultaneously identify 150 insectary-reared mosquitoes (9 species) and detect bloodmeals and parasites, plus 150 blood-fed mosquitoes (5 vertebrate hosts) and 40 P. falciparum-infected samples. Platform accurately identified all 9 mosquito species, 5 bloodmeal hosts up to 48 hours post-feed, and P. falciparum with high concordance to Sanger sequencing. Authors conclude this provides cost-effective, high-throughput solution for combined diagnostics.

articles.headline: Geographic and seasonal variation in insecticide resistance genes in Ethiopian malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Spatial and temporal signatures of genomic insecticide resistance in the Anopheles arabiensis mosquito malaria vector from Ethiopia.
articles.details: Whole-genome sequencing of Anopheles arabiensis across Ethiopian regions found high geographic and temporal variability in insecticide resistance genes. Vgsc-L995F prevalent in northern regions; Cyp6aa/p and Coeae2-7g metabolic markers high in west; Gste2 and Cyp9k1 copy number variants frequent in Central Rift Valley. Seasonal shifts observed with increasing frequencies during transmission season. Authors emphasize complex interplay of control, agricultural, and behavioral factors, highlighting need for genomic surveillance integrated with entomological monitoring.

articles.headline: Secondary malaria vectors feed on humans in low-transmission Zambian settings
articles.title_original: Species composition and blood feeding patterns of understudied Anopheles mosquitoes in southern Zambia, an area of low malaria transmission.
articles.details: Cross-sectional entomological survey in southern Zambia collecting 5,697 female anophelines from 12 households. Nine Anopheles species identified: A. rufipes (26.8%) and A. pretoriensis (25.1%) dominant. Blood meal analysis of 401 specimens showed human blood index 0.22, with feeding on goats (62.5%), humans (19.4%), and cows (10.2%). One A. rufipes positive for P. falciparum sporozoites. Authors conclude peri-domestic human activity may sustain residual transmission despite high bed net and IRS coverage.

articles.headline: School-age children harbor majority of transmissible malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Asymptomatic school-age children carry the majority of transmissible Plasmodium falciparum infections.
articles.details: Year-long Malawi cohort using molecular detection of P. falciparum infections and gametocytes. Gametocytes detected in 23% of population; school-age children had significantly higher gametocyte-containing infection incidence and densities. School-age children were key drivers of household gametocyte frequencies. Clearing school-age children's infections in rainy season estimated to decrease population gametocyte abundance by 67%, identifying them as primary transmission reservoir.
articles.notable_reason: Identifies school-age children as primary transmission driver; suggests major intervention target

articles.headline: Photonic fiber sensors detect malaria parasite stages with machine learning
articles.title_original: Machine learning assisted malaria detection using photonic crystal fibre optical sensors.
articles.authors: Abdullah-Al-Shafi M, Sen S, Mubassera M
articles.details: Proof-of-concept study of photonic crystal fiber optical sensor with hollow core and heptagonal cladding for malaria detection via refractive index variations in infected red blood cells. Demonstrated high relative sensitivity: 97.45% (healthy), 96.89% (ring), 96.22% (trophozoite), 95.45% (schizont) stages, with confinement losses approximately 10⁻⁸ dB/m at 2.2 THz. Authors highlight potential as cost-effective, high-performance early detection platform for malaria.

articles.headline: Seasonal migration drives low-transmission malaria hotspots in Sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Rural-Urban Migration and Low-Transmission Endemicity of Malaria in Sub-Saharan Africa: A Statistical Inference Approach to Uncover Possible Mechanisms.
articles.details: Data-driven epidemiological model in Ouagadougou, Burkina Faso examining role of seasonal rural-urban labor migration in sustaining malaria transmission during dry season (3-150 cases per 10,000 person-weeks). Statistical inference compared seasonal, regular, and permanent migration patterns using empirical incidence and mobility data. Seasonal migration most accurately explained low-transmission persistence and rainy-season amplification. Authors conclude incorporating human mobility into transmission models strengthens elimination programs across Sub-Saharan Africa.

articles.headline: Sustained-release artemisinin nanoformulation overcomes drug-resistant malaria
articles.title_original: Development and evaluation of novel zein-based artemisinin sustained-release formulation for treating drug-resistant malaria.
articles.details: Zein (maize protein) nanoparticles encapsulating artemisinin achieved 200-fold enhancement in water solubility and sustained release. In vitro: potent inhibition of artemisinin-resistant P. falciparum in ring survival and recrudescence assays. In vivo (rodent and humanized erythrocyte mouse models): superior efficacy reducing parasitemia and preventing recrudescence compared to free artemisinin, with maintained therapeutic concentrations. Authors conclude this addresses solubility and pharmacokinetic limitations while combating drug-resistant malaria.
articles.notable_reason: Novel approach to overcome artemisinin resistance in preclinical models

articles.headline: Malaria infection impairs Ebola vaccine efficacy in endemic regions
articles.title_original: Acute malaria dysregulates specialized lymph node macrophages to suppress vaccine-elicited protection against the Ebola virus.
articles.details: Study of acute murine Plasmodium infection's impact on rVSV/EBOV (ERVEBO) vaccination. Acute Plasmodium infection at vaccination time reduced vaccine-mediated protection against mouse-adapted EBOV challenge via Plasmodium-induced interferon-gamma suppressing rVSV/EBOV replication in lymph node macrophages, resulting in reduced primary anti-EBOV antibody responses. Higher vaccine doses partially overcame deficits. Authors support maintaining higher-dose rVSV/EBOV protocols in malaria-endemic regions.
articles.notable_reason: Reveals significant malaria-vaccine interaction affecting licensed Ebola vaccine efficacy

articles.headline: Biomarkers accurately diagnose cerebral malaria versus other coma causes
articles.title_original: Specific Biomarkers Differentiate Cerebral Malaria from Other Causes of Coma in African children.
articles.details: Retrospective study of 379 Kenyan children testing Angiopoietin-like 4 and Inhibin-βE as biomarkers for severe malaria. Biomarkers showed high specificity: AUC 0.82 cerebral malaria, 0.86 respiratory distress, 0.85 anemia versus non-malarial coma; combined with platelet levels AUC 0.96. Biomarkers also predicted death by cerebral malaria (AUC 0.85). Authors propose development as diagnostic tool for differentiating cerebral malaria from other coma causes.
articles.notable_reason: Identifies biomarkers with AUC 0.96 for accurate cerebral malaria diagnosis

articles.headline: Ivermectin structure-activity analysis reveals mosquito channel interactions
articles.title_original: Lethal effects of ivermectin structures on malaria vectors and in silico analysis of interactions with their glutamate-gated chloride ion channels.
articles.details: Study of ivermectin lethal effects on Anopheles dirus and A. minimus showing dose-dependent structure-activity: full effect (both sugar rings), partial (monosaccharide), no effect (aglycone). Docking models of Anopheles GluCl protein identified hydrogen bond between ivermectin's second sugar ring (4″-OH) and THR304 of M2-M3 loop, differing from C. elegans (ILE273). Authors conclude improved understanding of mosquito-ivermectin interactions and potential resistance mechanisms.

articles.headline: Host variability and parasite load shape avian malaria distribution
articles.title_original: Variation in pathogen load and the pathogen load-infectiousness relationship broaden avian malaria's distribution.
articles.details: Experimental study in canaries (Serinus canaria) quantifying relationship between Plasmodium relictum parasitemia and infectiousness to Culex quinquefasciatus mosquitoes. Infectiousness increased with parasitemia, temperature, and feeding time. Applied to 17 bird species in 11 Hawaiian communities, finding gradual parasitemia-infectiousness slope and high within-species variability resulted in broad host infectiousness overlap, explaining avian malaria's widespread distribution.

articles.headline: Climate variables predict P. vivax and P. falciparum transmission in Madagascar
articles.title_original: Association of Climate Variables with Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium falciparum Malaria Cases in Mandoto, Madagascar: A Statistical Modeling Study.
articles.details: Over 6 years in Mandoto District, Madagascar, 276,318 RDTs yielded 109,428 malaria cases (39.6% positivity). P. falciparum comprised 71.5% of cases and P. vivax 28.5% after multispecies RDTs became available post-2021. Temperature and precipitation variability effectively revealed malaria seasonality, with peaks in January and April-June. Time-series modeling combined with meteorological data can predict malaria cases and support resource allocation.

articles.headline: IL-10 elevation linked to postpartum malaria susceptibility in primigravid women
articles.title_original: Postpartum cytokine shifts and IL-10-mediated immune suppression in malaria-infected primigravid women.
articles.details: In 33 Burkina Faso women followed from delivery to 3 months postpartum, 48.5% tested malaria-positive by PCR at delivery. Malaria-infected women had significantly elevated IL-10 levels with decreased IL-6:IL-10 ratio (p=0.005), with strong IL-10/infection correlation (σ=0.9, p<0.001). This anti-inflammatory shift persisted postpartum. The authors conclude that modulating cytokine responses could represent a novel therapeutic approach to improving maternal health.

articles.headline: Hemozoin drives ferroptosis-mediated thrombocytopenia in vivax malaria
articles.title_original: Heme and hemozoin induce platelet cell death through UPR-induced apoptosis and ferroptosis in vivax malaria.
articles.details: In vitro study found that heme and hemozoin induce platelet cell death through multiple pathways. Heme activated apoptotic caspases via IRE1α-dependent endoplasmic reticulum stress. Hemozoin triggered stronger necrotic death featuring ferroptosis (lipid peroxidation-dependent, regulated by DGAT1/2 enzymes), which was the predominant pathway for hemozoin-induced thrombocytopenia. These findings identify novel regulated cell death mechanisms associated with malaria-induced thrombocytopenia with potential implications for other hemolytic disorders.

articles.headline: Post-artemisinin delayed hemolysis recognized in pediatric severe falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Severe Plasmodium falciparum Malaria Complicated by Post-artemisinin Delayed Hemolysis in a Non-immune Pediatric Returning Traveler.
articles.details: A 9-year-old with severe P. falciparum malaria from Tanzania presented with multiorgan dysfunction including thrombocytopenia, acute kidney injury, hepatic dysfunction, and pneumonia. After treatment with IV artesunate followed by oral artemether-lumefantrine and achieving parasite clearance, he re-presented 10 days later with hemoglobin decline, indirect hyperbilirubinemia, elevated LDH, reticulocytosis, and low haptoglobin without parasitemia, confirming post-artemisinin delayed hemolysis (PADH). He required transfusion and recovered.

articles.headline: Greece's malaria elimination and re-emergence: lessons for global eradication
articles.title_original: Malaria in Greece: From Ancient Scourge to Eradication.
articles.details: This narrative review traces malaria in Greece from antiquity through endemic periods and successful national eradication via vector control, treatment, environmental interventions, and surveillance. Greece subsequently experienced 21st-century re-emergence of locally acquired cases. The review emphasizes that elimination is achievable through coordinated long-term efforts but requires continuous surveillance and rapid response capacity to prevent re-establishment of transmission. Greece's experience provides highly relevant lessons for contemporary malaria elimination strategies worldwide.

articles.headline: Nigeria's malaria burden in under-5s: regional disparities and intervention gaps
articles.title_original: Burden, challenges, and control of malaria among under-5s in Nigeria.
articles.details: This narrative review of literature since 2010 found persistently high malaria burden among Nigerian children under 5, with notable regional disparities—highest prevalence in northern states and rural areas. Contributing factors include socioeconomic deprivation, climate variability, and suboptimal intervention coverage. Key challenges identified: diagnostic limitations, drug resistance, and inconsistent use of preventive measures. While LLINs, IPT, and IRS demonstrated effectiveness, gaps remain in coverage and implementation, necessitating coordinated multisectoral response with innovative strategies and health system strengthening.

articles.headline: Most malaria interventions in Sub-Saharan Africa remain highly cost-effective
articles.title_original: Strategic resource allocation for malaria elimination in endemic settings: a systematic review of cost-effectiveness evidence.
articles.details: Systematic review of 18 cost-effectiveness studies (2018-2025) on malaria prevention and treatment, primarily in Sub-Saharan Africa. LLINs and IRS were highly cost-effective with costs per DALY averted below national GDP thresholds; combined approaches enhanced effectiveness in high-resistance or high-burden settings. Complex strategies like tafenoquine with G6PD screening showed higher incremental cost-effectiveness ratios due to additional costs and operational challenges. Interventions including LLINs (PBO nets), IRS (Actellic/3GIRS), combined LLIN+IRS, SMC/PDMC, IPTp, and ACT+RDT were highly cost-effective.

articles.headline: G6PD A- variant absent in Southeast Nigeria P. falciparum patients
articles.title_original: No G6PD A- (G202A) variant detected among Plasmodium falciparum-positive patients in Awka, Southeast Nigeria: a hospital-based study.
articles.details: Hospital-based study of 64 P. falciparum-positive patients from Awka, Nigeria screened for G6PD A376G and G202A variants using PCR and Sanger sequencing. The B variant (normal) was predominant at 83%, and no participants had the A- (G202A) variant associated with G6PD deficiency. B and A+ variants showed no significant impact on hemoglobin or parasitemia levels in this cohort. Authors recommend broader genotyping and enzyme activity testing in community-representative samples before informing treatment policy.

articles.headline: Parallelized hmmibd-rs accelerates P. falciparum IBD detection 100-fold
articles.title_original: hmmibd-rs: an enhanced hmmIBD implementation for parallelizable identity-by-descent detection from large-scale Plasmodium genomic data.
articles.details: hmmibd-rs is an enhanced, multi-threaded implementation of hmmIBD for identity-by-descent (IBD) detection in Plasmodium genomic data. Using 128 CPU threads on 220 million simulated P. falciparum-like chromosome pairs reduced computation from 5.2 days to 1.3 hours (~100× speedup), enabling single-day analysis of MalariaGEN Pf7 (~10,000 monoclonal samples). The tool incorporates non-uniform recombination rate maps to improve IBD inference accuracy and reduce false positives in recombination cold spots.

articles.headline: Community-based rectal artesunate for severe malaria in remote Africa
articles.title_original: Severe malaria treatment with rectal artesunate and artemisinin-based combination therapy in remote settings: an effectiveness-implementation hybrid type 3 study protocol (SEMA ReACT).
articles.details: This effectiveness-implementation hybrid Type 3 study (SEMA ReACT) in Zambia and Democratic Republic of Congo assesses feasibility of providing pre-referral rectal artesunate (RAS) to children aged 6 months to 5 years via community health workers. Primary outcomes include time from symptom onset to treatment initiation, 28-day PCR-corrected cure rates following RAS+ACT or RAS+injectable artesunate+ACT, and proportion of population utilizing formal healthcare within 6 months. The study addresses access barriers to injectable artesunate and facility-based care in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence 33% in DRC children 6-59 months; socioeconomic disparities
articles.title_original: Prevalence and factors associated with malaria among children aged 6-59 months in the Democratic Republic of the Congo: a nationwide cross-sectional survey.
articles.details: Nationwide cross-sectional survey of 10,013 children aged 6-59 months from the 2023/24 DRC Demographic and Health Survey found overall malaria prevalence of 33% (95% CI 30.2-35.6), ranging from 5% in Kinshasa to 61% in Bas-Uele province. Associated factors included age 24-59 months (APR=1.25), maternal no/primary education (APR=1.15, 1.13), poor/middle wealth quintiles (APR=1.52, 1.42), no ITN use (APR=1.18), and unimproved housing (APR=1.54-1.27). The authors recommend scaling ITN distribution and multisectoral approaches to address socioeconomic vulnerabilities.

articles.headline: Primaquine-containing regimens prevent P. vivax recurrence better than monotherapies
articles.title_original: Efficacy of artemether lumefantrine vs chloroquine for the treatment of Plasmodium Vivax infection in Pakistan.
articles.details: Prospective cohort study of 354 P. vivax patients in Karachi, Pakistan examined four treatment regimens. By Day 28, recurrence occurred in 10.2% on chloroquine (CQ), 20.2% on artemether-lumefantrine (AL), 1.1% on CQ+primaquine, and 3.8% on AL+primaquine. By 6 months, recurrence increased to 15.3% (CQ), 32.2% (AL), 1.1% (CQ+PQ), and 6.3% (AL+PQ). Cox regression showed AL monotherapy associated with higher recurrence hazard than CQ monotherapy, while primaquine-containing regimens showed significantly lower recurrence hazards than corresponding monotherapies.

articles.headline: Multi-state protocol on malaria transmission drivers in India
articles.title_original: Multicentric longitudinal study on malaria burden, vector bionomics and health system assessment in diverse eco-epidemiological settings in the context of malaria elimination in India: study protocol.
articles.details: Longitudinal multicentric study in 12 Indian districts covering ~25,000 individuals across diverse ecological settings. Baseline survey uses RDTs, microscopy, and PCR for low-density detection; participants followed 1 year with monthly symptomatic screening and subset asymptomatic testing. Evaluates malaria burden, vector bionomics, insecticide resistance, health system performance, and environmental drivers linked to transmission.

articles.headline: Plasmodium falciparum STOML-FtsH complex supports blood-stage development
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum stomatin-like protein forms a putative complex with a metalloprotease in distinct mitochondrial loci.
articles.details: Deletion of P. falciparum STOML caused significant growth defect and slower asexual blood-stage development, with sexual-stage development unaffected. Epitope-tagged STOML localized to punctate mitochondrial sites. Pulldown experiments identified zinc-dependent metalloprotease FtsH as interaction partner. Results suggest STOML-FtsH complex localizes to mitochondrial branching points and facilitates asexual stage development.

articles.headline: No association between loiasis and malaria in rural Gabon
articles.title_original: There Is No Association Between Loiasis and Malaria: Findings from a Secondary Analysis of a Cross-Sectional Survey in Rural Gabon.
articles.details: Secondary analysis of 947 participants (2015-2016) found no association between malaria and loiasis. Crude analysis showed seemingly protective association (OR 0.67, 95% CI 0.45–1.01, p=0.0521) that disappeared after adjustment (OR 1.31, 95% CI 0.81–2.11, p=0.276). Age-dependent differences in disease distribution explained the apparent crude protective effect.

articles.headline: Imported P. falciparum malaria cases increasing in Algeria 2018–2024
articles.title_original: Imported Malaria in a Malaria-Free Region: A Retrospective Analysis of Epidemiological, Clinical, and Therapeutic Patterns in Tizi-Ouzou, Algeria (2018-2024).
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of 16 confirmed imported malaria cases among 106 screened (15.1% positivity) in Algeria during 2018–2024, with notable increase from 2022–2024 reaching 40% positivity in 2024. Cases were predominantly P. falciparum (87.5%), often low parasitemia (<4% in 68.8%), in men (87.5%) aged 20–50 years from West Africa. Uncomplicated malaria 78.6%, severe 21.4%, with anemia (92.8%) and thrombocytopenia (42.8%) common.

articles.headline: Haryana malaria cases resurged to 270 in 2024 after near-elimination
articles.title_original: Malaria Surge in Haryana - an early warning to relook ongoing elimination efforts.
articles.details: Analysis of Haryana malaria data (2018–2024) showed 98.34% decline from 3,149 to 52 cases by 2022, but cases subsequently increased to 270 in 2024. Rising incidence may be related to climate factors affecting mosquito proliferation or health system implementation gaps. Strengthened disease surveillance and vector control needed to sustain 2030 elimination targets.

articles.headline: P. vivax dominates imported malaria in South-West Delhi 2020–2024
articles.title_original: Imported Malaria in South-West Delhi: Five-year Epidemiological Trends (2020-2024) from a Fever Clinic and Implications for Urban Malaria Elimination.
articles.details: Analysis of 7,257 fever cases identified 58 malaria-positive (0.8%), predominantly P. vivax (94.8%) and P. falciparum (3.4%), in 70.7% males aged 18–45 years. Imported cases accounted for 51.7%, increasing from 52.2% (2020) to 66.7% (2024), predominantly from neighboring Uttar Pradesh districts. Findings highlight need for enhanced inter-state surveillance collaboration and targeted vector control.

articles.headline: ITN utilization remains suboptimal in Nigerian children and women
articles.title_original: Insecticide-Treated Net Utilization Among Children and Pregnant Women: 2021 Nigeria Malaria Indicator Survey Analysis.
articles.details: Analysis of 2021 Nigeria Malaria Indicator Survey (13,727 households; 12,742 children <5; 14,476 women 15–49) found 56% of households owned ≥1 ITN but utilization was low: 50% of women and 41.2% of children slept under ITNs. ITN use was higher in rural areas, North West/North East regions, lower wealth quintiles, and among younger children <12 months and women with no/primary education.

articles.headline: Rainfall at 1-month lag predicts malaria in Yumbe district, Uganda
articles.title_original: Climate variability and malaria incidence trends in Yumbe District, West Nile Sub-region of Uganda (2017-2021).
articles.details: Time-series analysis of 2,066,711 malaria cases (2017–2021) in Yumbe district found rainfall positively associated with malaria at 1-month lag (β=0.38, p<0.05), while minimum temperature showed inverse association (β=−0.29, p<0.05). Rainfall strongly led malaria cases by 1 month (r=0.759, p<0.001). Climate integration into surveillance systems could enhance early interventions.

articles.headline: Malaria test-and-treat guideline compliance only 39% in Nigerian hospitals
articles.title_original: Quality of inpatient test-and-treat malaria case-management in public and private hospitals in Kano State, Nigeria.
articles.details: Assessment of 18 Nigerian hospitals reviewing 2,814 suspected malaria admissions found composite test-and-treat compliance of 39.3% (45.7% children vs 26.5% adults; 39.8% public vs 30.8% private). While 73.7% were tested and 90.2% of severe cases received artesunate, only 30% of artesunate-treated patients received follow-on ACT. Microscopy showed 93.2% sensitivity but only 42.5% specificity.

articles.headline: Global P. falciparum drug resistance: rising artemisinin resistance 2005–2025
articles.title_original: The Global Landscape of Plasmodium falciparum Drug Resistance Markers, 2005-2025: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 261 studies from 64 countries (2005–2025) found pooled prevalence of pfdhfr (85.7%), pfcrt (78.0%), pfdhps (73.7%), pfmdr1 (60.5%), and pfk13 (45.0%) with high heterogeneity (I²>95%). Rising pfk13 prevalence since 2012 indicates emerging artemisinin resistance. Persistent pfdhfr/pfdhps mutations demonstrate ongoing sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine pressure and widespread treatment failures.

articles.headline: Correction to transcriptome profiling study
articles.title_original: Correction: Transcriptome profiling of intrahepatocytic Plasmodium and their host hepatocytes based on the infection phase and the zonation of the liver.
articles.details: Correction to article DOI 10.3389/fgene.2025.1548487.

articles.headline: High-throughput tracking reveals artemisinin dormancy-recovery mechanism
articles.title_original: High throughput quantitative tracking of Plasmodium falciparum clonal blood stage parasite growth and applications for antimalarial drug discovery.
articles.details: Developed qTRACE platform with deep learning for single-parasite resolution tracking of P. falciparum growth. After artemisinin exposure, up to 50% of viable parasites recovered from drug-induced dormancy, with recovery rates differing between artemisinin-susceptible and -resistant strains. Confirmed dormant ring-stage viability and mechanistic basis for treatment failure and recrudescence.

articles.headline: Cambodian malaria: geography and occupation shape parasite relatedness
articles.title_original: Genetic relatedness of Cambodian Plasmodium falciparum isolates is driven by geography and occupation.
articles.details: Study of 107 P. falciparum isolates from Cambodian military and farmers (2014-2016) using whole genome sequencing. Mean genome identity-by-descent sharing was 40%, significantly higher in Oddar Meanchey versus Kratie province (p<2.2e-16). Isolates from different occupations shared 7.2% less genome identity than farmer pairs. Results suggest geography and occupation-specific transmission sources should guide targeted interventions.

articles.headline: Central Anhui Province faces highest malaria re-transmission risk
articles.title_original: Assessing the risk of malaria re-transmission in Anhui Province, China: an integration of spatiotemporal scan statistics and ecological niche modeling.
articles.details: Spatial-temporal analysis of imported malaria cases in Anhui Province, China (2017-2023) with vector surveillance data. Persistent clusters identified in central districts including Yaohai, Luyang, and Baohe. Ensemble ecological niche model showed strong predictive performance (AUC 0.943, TSS 0.759) for Anopheles sinensis distribution. Study recommends strengthening surveillance and vector control in epidemic hotspots to prevent re-transmission.

articles.headline: High rate of neurological sequelae in pediatric malaria cases
articles.title_original: Malaria in Children is Associated with a High Rate of Neurological Sequelae and Need for Rehabilitative Therapies in Germany.
articles.details: Retrospective study of 11 pediatric malaria cases (median age 9.5 years) at a German tertiary hospital (2023); 91% were P. falciparum and 72.7% were classified as severe malaria. Despite guideline-appropriate artesunate treatment in 64% of cases, 82% exhibited residual neurological symptoms and 44% had neurocognitive deficits. Three patients developed epilepsies, though 77.8% showed clinical improvement at 12 months.

articles.headline: Out-of-pocket payments hinder malaria treatment access in developing countries
articles.title_original: Implications of out-of-pocket payments for the control of malaria in developing countries: A narrative review.
articles.details: Narrative review synthesizing evidence on economic barriers to malaria control. Out-of-pocket payments significantly hinder timely treatment access, particularly affecting low-income and rural populations, and reduce use of preventive measures and diagnostics. Alternative mechanisms including government subsidies, community health insurance, and donor-funded programs can mitigate financial burden. Study recommends reducing OOP payments through scaled subsidized care and strengthened public health systems.

articles.headline: Diabetes comorbidity increases malaria parasitemia and vaccine antigen responses
articles.title_original: Malaria-diabetes comorbidity is linked to higher parasitaemia and enhanced IgG response to malaria vaccine candidate antigens.
articles.details: Case-control study of 144 Ghanaian participants (25 with diabetes-malaria comorbidity, 41 malaria only, 38 controls). Comorbidity group showed significantly higher parasitemia levels (median 1702 vs 932 parasites/μL, p=0.02) and enhanced IgG responses to GLURP-R2 (1.60-fold, p=0.01), GLURP-R0 (1.43-fold, p=0.03), and MSP1 (1.42-fold, p<0.05). No significant differences observed for MSP3, AMA1, or CSP antibodies.

articles.headline: Seasonal MDA with artemether-lumefantrine prevents malaria in school children
articles.title_original: Impacts of Seasonal Mass Drug Administration in School-Aged Children on Chronic Malaria Infections in Burkina Faso.
articles.details: Prospective study of seasonal mass drug administration in Burkina Faso school-aged children. Uninfected cohort remained parasite-free in 93% of cases; among parasitemic children treated with artemether-lumefantrine, 91% remained negative during follow-up. Persistent infection prevalence was stable at 5-9%, and genotyping revealed low frequencies of drug-resistance mutations. Early MDA at dry-season onset effectively interrupted transmission.

articles.headline: R21 vaccine: high acceptability despite low awareness in Nigeria
articles.title_original: R21/MATRIX malaria vaccine: awareness, perception, acceptability, and willingness to pay among caregivers of under-five children in endemic communities in Lagos, Nigeria.
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 372 caregivers of under-five children in Lagos endemic communities. R21/MATRIX vaccine awareness was low (9.7%), but positive perceptions were high (98.1% believed safe/effective) and acceptability was strong (76.6% would definitely vaccinate). Willingness to pay was reported by 71.2%, with 51.9% willing to pay ₦1,001-₦5,000 per dose. Targeted awareness campaigns recommended to improve vaccine knowledge.

articles.headline: Most Ethiopian health facilities unprepared for pregnant women's malaria care
articles.title_original: Malaria service readiness and associated factors among health facilities that provide antenatal care services in Ethiopia: a cross-sectional study using generalised estimating equation analysis.
articles.details: Nationally representative cross-sectional study of 1,156 health facilities providing antenatal care (2021/2022 Ethiopia Service Provision Assessment). Only 15.7% (95% CI 13.8-18.0) demonstrated full malaria service readiness, with 14.3% having none of the key components. Governance-related factors—client suggestion boxes, recent report submission, and monthly staff meetings—predicted higher readiness (AOR 1.60-2.79). Substantial regional variation observed across Ethiopian regions.

articles.headline: Urban Ethiopian malaria transmission highly heterogeneous by town
articles.title_original: Heterogeneity of malaria transmission in urban settings in Ethiopia: A seroprevalence and risk factor analysis.
articles.details: Community survey of 912 individuals across three urban Ethiopian towns (January-March 2020). RDT prevalence was 0.78% but serology indicated higher past exposure: P. falciparum seroprevalence 19.4%, P. vivax 18%. Substantial heterogeneity existed between towns; Awash Sebat Kilo showed highest seroprevalence (36.5% P. falciparum, 28% P. vivax). Proximity to breeding sites (OR=2.8, p=0.037) and age >15 years were significant risk factors.

articles.headline: Malaria peaks at 25°C and 37 mm rainfall in Colombian municipalities
articles.title_original: Integrating causal inference and machine learning to quantify climate-malaria relationships: Evidence of temperature and rainfall thresholds from Colombian municipalities.
articles.details: Ecological study of 1,075,112 malaria cases across 969 Colombian municipalities (2007-2023) using Targeted Maximum Likelihood Estimation with causal inference guided by directed acyclic graphs. Non-linear relationships found between climate and malaria incidence, with optimal transmission at approximately 25°C temperature and 37 mm monthly rainfall. E-values (1.14-1.48) indicated low-to-moderate tolerance to unmeasured confounding. Findings support integration of specific climate thresholds into early-warning systems.

articles.headline: GMS malaria elimination requires comprehensive health system strengthening
articles.title_original: Strengthening the health systems at national level for malaria elimination in the Greater Mekong Subregion countries: a qualitative study.
articles.details: Multi-country qualitative study with 39 stakeholders across Greater Mekong Subregion countries. Interviews identified that malaria elimination requires inputs from all six WHO health system building blocks: strong political commitment, targeted interventions for high-risk groups, reliable supply chains, skilled workforce, robust quality assurance, and expanded diagnostic/medicine access. Key barriers include lack of targeted interventions in national policies, incomplete private sector reporting, workforce gaps, and declining international donor funding.

articles.headline: P. vivax CyRPA conserved C-terminal region promising vaccine candidate
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity, natural selection, and immunological features of the Plasmodium vivax CyRPA protein: Implications for vaccine development.
articles.details: Analysis of 950 P. vivax CyRPA sequences (including 42 new Colombian isolates) to assess vaccine potential. High nucleotide diversity driven by natural selection and recombination was observed. In-silico epitope mapping identified conserved B- and T-cell epitopes with strong predicted HLA binding affinities; epitopes overlapped with regions known to elicit immune responses. Immune simulations of a C-terminal multiepitope construct predicted robust humoral memory responses.

articles.headline: Gametocytogenesis regulatory mechanisms in Plasmodium
articles.title_original: Molecular mechanisms involved in Plasmodium gametocytogenesis.
articles.details: Review consolidating advances in molecular mechanisms regulating gametocytogenesis in Plasmodium, particularly P. falciparum. Sexual differentiation involves environmental stressors (lysophosphatidylcholine depletion) and sophisticated regulatory networks including transcriptional control via AP2-G master switch regulated by H3K9me3, HP1, and GDV1, and post-transcriptional regulation via PfPuf1/PfPuf2 and epitranscriptomic modifications (m5C and m6A methylation). Understanding these pathways is essential for identifying novel therapeutic targets and transmission-blocking vaccines.

articles.headline: Non-falciparum malaria species driving Zanzibar epidemiology
articles.title_original: Understanding the Epidemiology of Malaria in Zanzibar Through Molecular and Serological Analysis of Samples Collected During Reactive Case Detection.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 1,618 participants from 35 reactive case detection sites in Zanzibar (May 2022–January 2023), measuring serological exposure via MSP1-19kD IgG and active infections via species-specific PCR. Overall malaria exposure: 35.3%, with P. falciparum (29.8%) predominant; non-falciparum species: P. ovale 5.8%, P. malariae 5.9%, P. vivax 5.9%. Active infections: 6.0% overall, predominantly P. falciparum 4.6%. Non-falciparum species showed distinct geographic distribution with higher rural seroprevalences. Current P. falciparum-focused control efforts may inadequately address non-falciparum species exposure.

articles.headline: PfApiAT2 proline transporter essential for P. falciparum transmission
articles.title_original: PfApiAT2 is a proline transporter essential for the transmission of Plasmodium falciparum by the mosquito vector.
articles.details: Experimental study demonstrating PfApiAT2, a proline transporter, is essential for oocyst development in Anopheles gambiae. Halofuginone-resistant pfapiat2-mutant parasites produced stunted oocysts severely defective in sporozoite production; PfApiAT2-knockout parasites showed complete block in sporogony with oocysts that stalled and degenerated. This growth defect was rescued by nutrient supplementation to the mosquito vector. Identifies a critical nutritional dependency of P. falciparum oocysts on the mosquito host, unveiling a potential transmission vulnerability.

articles.headline: L1014F kdr mutation associated with Microsporidia MB in Anopheles
articles.title_original: The L1014F Kdr mutation is associated with a higher prevalence and load of the Plasmodium-blocking symbiont Microsporidia MB In Anopheles Gambiae s.l. In Benin.
articles.details: Field study of 1,040 An. gambiae complex mosquitoes from southern Benin genotyped for L1014F kdr mutation and screened for Microsporidia MB and P. falciparum sporozoites. L1014F kdr frequency: 75.1% in An. coluzzii, 91.3% in An. gambiae s.s. Microsporidia MB absent in kdr-SS, present in 1.9% of combined kdr-RS/RR with higher DNA load in kdr-RR (23.23 ng/µL) vs kdr-RS (10.13 ng/µL). Elevation and soil explained 78% and 20% of Microsporidia MB habitat suitability. Demonstrates association between insecticide-resistance allele and transmission-blocking symbiont prevalence/load.

articles.headline: Malaria epidemiology and management strategies review Africa
articles.title_original: Malaria: a review on its current epidemiological status and management strategies.
articles.details: Comprehensive review of global malaria epidemiology with focus on Africa. Between 2000 and 2022, WHO African Region reported 40% reduction in malaria incidence and 60% decline in mortality. Key advances include LLIN distribution, indoor residual spraying, rapid diagnostics, intermittent preventive treatment, artemisinin-based combination therapies, and newly deployed vaccines (RTS,S/AS01, R21/Matrix-M). Rising insecticide and antimalarial resistance, climate change, and inadequate healthcare infrastructure threaten further progress. Recommendations include cross-border control efforts, expanded seasonal chemoprevention, and scaled vaccine implementation.

articles.headline: E205D CYP6P3 mutation drives pyrethroid resistance Anopheles
articles.title_original: The E205D mutation in CYP6P3 drives pyrethroid insecticide resistance in the African malaria mosquito vector Anopheles gambiae.
articles.details: Experimental and field study identifying E205D point mutation in CYP6P3 as driver of pyrethroid resistance in An. gambiae. In vitro assays showed 205D variant had 3.7-fold higher catalytic efficiency for permethrin. Field data: CYP6P3-E205D correlated with pyrethroid resistance (odds ratio 26.4, p<0.0001) and reduced bed net efficacy. Homozygous resistance genotype showed higher survival on PermaNet 3.0 (OR: 6.1, p=0.011). E205D variant predominant in West/Central Africa. Identifies critical molecular mechanism of resistance to vector control tools.

articles.headline: Study protocol: malaria-dengue transmission Indonesia new capital
articles.title_original: A study protocol for a baseline community-based cross-sectional study to investigate malaria and dengue transmission dynamics in a development area of new capital city, Indonesia.
articles.details: Protocol for community-based cross-sectional study in Ibu Kota Nusantara and surrounding area in East Kalimantan. Approximately 2,000 individuals age >1 year will provide finger-prick blood samples for serological analysis (multiplex bead-based assays for malaria species and dengue virus serotypes) and malaria rapid diagnostic tests. Demographic, clinical, environmental, and geolocation data will be collected. Statistical and geostatistical modeling will assess seroprevalence, spatial patterns, and risk factors for exposure to malaria and dengue.

articles.headline: Severe P. vivax malaria with pulmonary involvement case
articles.title_original: Severe Malaria Due to Plasmodium vivax With Pulmonary Involvement: A Case Report.
articles.details: Case report of 46-year-old woman from India presenting with severe P. vivax malaria with pulmonary involvement. Presented with asthenia, fatigue, fever, hypoxemia, severe anemia, thrombocytopenia, and extensive ground-glass opacification on chest CT. P. vivax parasitemia: 50% with parasite density of 8,645 parasites/µL. Treated with artemether-lumefantrine and doxycycline (IV artesunate unavailable). Parasitemia cleared by day 3 of treatment. Case illustrates that P. vivax is increasingly recognized as causing severe malaria with potential for life-threatening pulmonary complications.

articles.headline: P. vivax population genetics transmission decline-rebound PNG
articles.title_original: Population genetics of Plasmodium vivax with transmission decline and rebound in two endemic areas of Papua New Guinea.
articles.details: Population genetic study of 336 P. vivax isolates from serial cross-sectional surveys over a decade in Papua New Guinea (East Sepik: 2005, 2012, 2016; Madang: 2006, 2010, 2014) using SNP barcode. After LLIN distribution, P. vivax initially declined then resurgent. Madang showed apparent population bottleneck at low transmission followed by rapid recovery; East Sepik showed less dramatic impact with ongoing transmission of multiple lineages. Earlier LLIN implementation and smaller rebound in East Sepik associated with sustained impact. Demonstrates importance of sustained control efforts for lasting P. vivax population impact.

articles.headline: GI disruption in ART-treated SIV P. fragile co-infection
articles.title_original: Gastrointestinal Mucosal Disruptions During ART-Treated SIV/Plasmodium fragile Co-Infection.
articles.details: Experimental study in rhesus macaques with SIV/P. fragile co-infection (n=6). Animals initiated antiretroviral therapy 8 weeks post-SIV infection, then received P. fragile at week 12. Significant CCR5+ CD4+ T cell decline observed in periphery, colon, and duodenum. Duodenum NET-forming granulocyte frequencies positively associated with peripheral SIV burden after P. fragile co-infection but not with parasitemia. Findings suggest P. fragile co-infection during ART may exacerbate mucosal disruption contributing to persistent SIV replication despite therapy.

articles.headline: SR1-interacting proteins in Plasmodium berghei identified
articles.title_original: Identification of serine/arginine-rich splicing factor 1-interacting proteins in Plasmodium berghei.
articles.details: Experimental study using immunoprecipitation and mass spectrometry to identify SR1 (serine/arginine-rich splicing factor 1) interacting proteins in P. berghei. Transgenic parasites expressing PbSR1::GFP showed stronger nuclear signal. IP-MS identified nuclear proteins (RNA-binding proteins, snRNPs) and cytoplasmic proteins including RhopH1, RhopH2, RhopH3. Live-cell imaging showed PbSR1-RhopH2 co-localization in cytoplasm in trophozoites and gametocytes but not in mature schizonts or merozoites. Identifies SR1 interactions with both nuclear and rhoptry proteins with cytoplasmic interactions specific to early developmental stages.

articles.headline: Mossie-GO transfluthrin spatial repellent trial protocol Uganda
articles.title_original: Evaluation of the protective efficacy of a transfluthrin-based spatial repellent product to reduce malaria prevalence in Uganda: study protocol for a cluster-randomised double-blinded control trial-the Mossie-GO trial.
articles.details: Protocol for cluster-randomized double-blinded placebo-controlled trial of Mossie-GO™, a transfluthrin-based spatial repellent, to reduce malaria prevalence in Uganda. 56 clusters randomized 1:1 to intervention or placebo. Primary endpoint: malaria infection prevalence in children ≤5 years (100 children at baseline, 60 per cluster at 6 and 12 months). Secondary endpoints: entomological correlates of transmission, user acceptance, and transfluthrin air concentrations. Study will include continued access to other malaria control tools and collection of household characteristics and malaria exposure behaviors.

articles.headline: High-sensitivity P. falciparum genotyping with contamination detection
articles.title_original: SIMPLseq: a high-sensitivity Plasmodium falciparum genotyping and PCR contamination tracking tool.
articles.details: Describes SIMPLseq, a 6-locus P. falciparum AmpSeq miniplex with well-specific inline barcodes for PCR contamination detection. Achieved 100% average locus detection at ≥0.5 parasites/μl and ≥50% at 0.125 parasites/μl. Sentinel barcoding correctly identified all 24 deliberately introduced and 39 unintentional contaminations in 1420 Malian samples. Integrates contamination tracking without costly pre-amplification, supporting recurrent infection classification and intervention efficacy trials.

articles.headline: Anopheles funestus distribution and transmission in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Updating Anopheles funestus group members in Burkina Faso: species distribution and contribution to malaria transmission.
articles.details: Entomological survey of 67 sites across Burkina Faso (Oct–Dec 2022) identified 617 An. funestus group members. An. funestus s.s. predominated at 95% (589/617), concentrated in Hauts-Bassins (30%) and Sud-Ouest (55%) regions. High zoophilic behavior observed (64.3%). P. falciparum infection detected in 5.76% (33/572) of An. funestus s.l., with 5.6% in An. funestus s.s. and 0.174% in An. rivulorum-like. Emphasizes need to incorporate An. funestus in vector control strategies.

articles.headline: Rivastigmine with arteether improves experimental cerebral malaria outcomes
articles.title_original: Evaluation of clinically approved neuroprotective drugs as adjunct therapies in experimental cerebral malaria.
articles.details: Tested six clinically-approved neuroprotective drugs in P. falciparum screens and Plasmodium berghei ANKA mouse cerebral malaria model. Vinpocetine, cilostazol, and rivastigmine inhibited parasite growth. Early oral treatment with cilostazol, piracetam, or rivastigmine delayed symptom onset and improved survival versus untreated controls. When initiated after neurological signs, rivastigmine combined with α/β-arteether markedly improved survival and parasite clearance compared to either drug alone. Suggests rivastigmine–arteether as practical adjunct cerebral malaria therapy.

articles.headline: Cerebral malaria and severe anemia linked to long-term cognitive impairment
articles.title_original: Cerebral Malaria and Severe Malarial Anemia, but not Other Forms of Severe Malaria, Are Associated With Long-Term Cognitive Impairment.
articles.details: Prospective cohort of 600 Ugandan children (6 months–4 years) with 1 of 5 common severe malaria forms, assessed at 12 months post-discharge. Cerebral malaria and severe malarial anemia associated with lower cognition (CM: –0.66 z-score, P=0.001; SMA: –0.71, P<0.001). Greater proportion with CM (10.6%, 5/47) or SMA (12.1%, 17/140) had cognitive impairment versus community controls (1.9%, 2/104). Respiratory distress, multiple seizures, and prostration showed no significant cognitive difference, indicating specific severe forms cause lasting neurological sequelae.

articles.headline: Not all severe malaria forms impair long-term cognition
articles.title_original: Not All Forms of Severe Malaria Impair Long-term Cognition-More Characterization, More Questions.
articles.details: Editorial commentary examining which forms of severe malaria impair long-term cognitive outcomes in children, building on recent clinical evidence and raising questions for further investigation.

articles.headline: Patient experiences with malaria symptoms in South Africa
articles.title_original: Experiences of people diagnosed with malaria regarding signs and symptoms at a selected village in the Vhembe District, Limpopo province, South Africa.
articles.authors: Kwinda W, Mudzweda AD, Luhalima T
articles.details: Qualitative study in Mhinga Village, Vhembe District, South Africa using semi-structured interviews with malaria patients. Explored patient experiences of malaria signs and symptoms. Two themes emerged: symptom experiences and circumstances prompting healthcare consultation. Different symptoms reported based on affected body systems. Study aimed to increase prevention strategy awareness and reduce transmission burden.

articles.headline: Socioeconomic disparities in malaria outcomes in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Socioeconomic health equity with malaria burden: quantifying slope (Relative) index of inequity in malaria infection outcomes in the Southeastern-coastal Tanzania.
articles.details: Analysis of 2019 baseline survey data from three Tanzanian districts (Rufiji, Kilwa, Kibiti). Higher socioeconomic status associated with lower malaria infection risk (OR=0.9975, 95% CI: 0.9972–0.9978), lower treatment costs, and fewer days absent from work. Significant disparities quantified using Slope and Relative Index of Inequality metrics. Rufiji showed largest equality in infection risk (SII=–12.62%), treatment cost (SII=837.769), and days off (SII=–0.46). Highlights need for equity-focused malaria interventions.

articles.headline: Characteristic MRI pattern in pediatric cerebral malaria brain swelling
articles.title_original: Peri-Rolandic and Occipital Sparing Cortical Edema: A Prevalent MRI Finding in Pediatric Patients with Cerebral Malaria.
articles.details: Retrospective MRI review of 190 Malawian children with clinical cerebral malaria (2013–2019). Cortical edema identified in 163 MRIs. Predominant pattern: diffuse cortical involvement with relative occipital and peri-Rolandic sparing (63.2%, 103/163); occipital-only sparing (22.7%, 37/163); generalized without sparing (14.1%, 23/163). Occipital and peri-Rolandic sparing inversely correlated with brain volume score (β=–0.26, P<0.001) and favorable outcomes (OR 0.3, 95% CI 0.1–0.6, P=0.002).

articles.headline: LLIN modeling shows 80% usage prevents millions of cases
articles.title_original: Modelling the effect of long-lasting insecticidal nets on malaria transmission dynamics in Kebbi State, Nigeria.
articles.details: Deterministic compartmental model of malaria transmission in Kebbi State, Nigeria using confirmed case data (2015–May 2024). Model projected that 80% LLIN usage since 2015 would have averted 5.2 million cases (97.98% reduction). Sustained 80% LLIN usage by May 2029 would avert 3 million additional cases (78.93% reduction). Results underscore importance of scaling long-lasting insecticidal net distribution and usage for malaria control.

articles.headline: PfRIPR vaccine candidate shows high genetic diversity in Senegal
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity of the malaria vaccine candidate PfRIPR in a high transmission region of Senegal.
articles.details: Deep amplicon sequencing of 89 P. falciparum isolates from Kédougou, Senegal identified non-synonymous SNPs in 64/89 (71.9%) samples. Twenty-six SNPs identified total, including 15 novel variants. Seven SNPs predicted to destabilize PfRIPR; two predicted to increase stability. Three SNPs (Q737K, T738K, V840L) located in EGF5-8 domains where neutralizing antibodies bind. High genetic diversity and mutations affecting antibody-binding epitopes may impact vaccine efficacy of Phase 1a RIPR candidate.

articles.headline: P. falciparum invasion preference may explain Duffy negativity distribution
articles.title_original: Invasion preferences suggest a possible role for Plasmodium falciparum parasites in the expansion of Duffy negativity in West and Central Africa.
articles.details: In vitro studies and evolutionary-epidemiological modeling showed P. falciparum preferentially invades Duffy-positive RBCs, with biophysical property differences between Duffy phenotypes. Model incorporating immunity against P. falciparum virulence showed that DARC inhibition increases Duffy-negative (FYBES) allele frequency at low transmission but decreases at high transmission. Suggests P. falciparum together with P. vivax may mechanistically explain Duffy negativity distribution in West and Central Africa.
articles.notable_reason: Mechanistically links P. falciparum to Duffy negativity evolutionary selection

articles.headline: Nigeria malaria burden shifts north despite overall 50% mortality decline
articles.title_original: Spatial and temporal inequalities in malaria incidence and mortality among children aged 0-4 years in Nigeria: a subnational analysis, 2010-2019.
articles.details: Subnational analysis of malaria in Nigeria (2010–2019) using IHME estimates. National incidence declined 27.7% (103 to 74.5 per 1000) and mortality fell 50.2% (477.3 to 237.6 per 100,000 in under-fives). Northern states (Zamfara, Kano, Katsina) remained most affected despite declines, with 2010 incidence >150 per 1000 and mortality >700 per 100,000. Southern states consistently showed lower burden. Subnational inequality metrics narrowed: incidence coefficient of variation fell from 46.3% to 28.1%; mortality from 42.9% to 22.3%.

articles.headline: Faculty enrichment program for applied malaria modeling
articles.title_original: Building contextually-relevant training programs for scientific development: process and lessons learned in implementing two iterations of a faculty enrichment program in applied malaria modeling.
articles.details: Two cohorts at Northwestern University (2022-2023) completed a 4-month faculty enrichment program in applied malaria modeling. Participants valued growth in technical expertise and communication skills; challenges included cross-disciplinary learning and adaptation to new teaching styles. Instructors strengthened their own technical capacity through feedback-driven adjustments.

articles.headline: G6PD deficiency prevalence in Ethiopian malaria patients
articles.title_original: Pooled prevalence of Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency among malaria patients in Ethiopia: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.details: Meta-analysis from PubMed, Cochrane, and Hinari databases found overall G6PDd prevalence of 6% (95% CI 0.04-0.08) in malaria patients in Ethiopia, with regional variation from 1% to 23%. A376G and A376T were the most common mutant variants, followed by G202A with low distribution.

articles.headline: WASH factors and malaria prevalence in Ghanaian children
articles.title_original: Household characteristics, water, sanitation and hygiene (WASH) and malaria prevalence among children aged 6-59 months in Ghana: an analysis of the 2022 Ghana Demographic and Health Survey.
articles.details: Analysis of 3,255 households from 2022 Ghana DHS found malaria prevalence of 3.7% in children aged 6-59 months by RDT. ITN ownership was high (78.9%) but only 51.5% slept under nets; 41.5% were anaemic. Wealth (aOR=0.15), older household heads (aOR=0.22-0.18), anaemia (aOR=2.03), and external toilets (aOR=16.64) significantly predicted malaria risk.

articles.headline: Provider distrust of negative malaria RDTs in DRC
articles.title_original: Positive tests are all alike, every negative test is negative in its own way: lack of confidence in negative malaria rapid diagnostic tests in the Democratic Republic of the Congo.
articles.details: Qualitative study with 64 interviews at 16 health facilities in Kasai Central and Haut Katanga found providers trusted positive RDTs more than negative ones.
