Providers attributed false negatives to prior treatment, early infection stage, missed species, and variable RDT accuracy. HMIS reported higher test positivity rates than Deki reader database, indicating systematic overdiagnosis.

articles.headline: Post-malaria neurological syndrome case report
articles.title_original: Post malaria neurological syndrome and acute disseminated encephalomyelitis: a case highlighting an ongoing debate.
articles.details: Case report of post-malaria neurological syndrome with acute disseminated encephalomyelitis highlighting an ongoing clinical debate.

articles.headline: Nuclear-apicoplast coupling in P. falciparum development
articles.title_original: Independent nuclear and organellar mechanisms determine apicoplast fate in malaria parasites.
articles.details: Live-imaging study tracked P. falciparum apicoplast morphology through the 48-hour intraerythrocytic cycle, identifying four developmental stages. Nuclear DNA replication inhibition blocked early apicoplast biogenesis; apicoplast genome replication inhibition disrupted the 'Crown' stage morphology required for organelle segregation into daughter cells.

articles.headline: Saudi Arabia malaria surveillance 2010-2024
articles.title_original: Malaria: Examining Persistence at the Margins of Endemicity over 15 Years in Saudi Arabia.
articles.details: National surveillance analysis of 52,627 cases from 2010-2024 found malaria burden historically concentrated in 7 endemic regions, later restricted to Aseer and Jazan, with P. falciparum and P. vivax/P. ovale predominating in individuals aged ≥10 years. After 2021, no indigenous transmission occurred; most cases were imported. ARIMA forecasting projected stable trends through 2030.

articles.headline: P. falciparum airport cluster in malaria-free Libya
articles.title_original: Airport Malaria Cluster in Certified Malaria-Free Country, Libya, 2024.
articles.details: November 2024, autochthonous cluster of 4 P. falciparum cases (1 fatal) identified near Benina International Airport, Benghazi. Epidemiologic and entomologic investigation ruled out local transmission, indicating airport malaria origin and exposing the vulnerability of malaria-free regions to imported vectors.

articles.headline: Malaria vector habitat and biting risk modeling 2000-2018
articles.title_original: Spatial-temporal coupling of malaria vector habitat suitability and biting probability.
articles.details: Species distribution modeling (MaxEnt) integrated remotely-sensed data and climate/topographic variables with An. gambiae presence data. Fuzzy logic models incorporated vector presence, human availability, insecticide resistance, and bed net usage to assess spatiotemporal biting probability. Two model scenarios achieved 91% validation accuracy, with increased bed net use correlating to reduced biting probability and P. falciparum prevalence.

articles.headline: Diabetes-malaria comorbidity in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Diabetes Mellitus and Malaria Co-Existence as Deadly Burden in Sub-Saharan Africa: A Review of Clinical Outcomes and Molecular Interactions.
articles.details: Review of clinical and molecular mechanisms of diabetes-malaria coexistence in sub-Saharan Africa. Malaria-related inflammation and oxidative stress disrupt insulin signaling and beta-cell function, increasing hyperglycemia in diabetics. Chronic hyperglycemia damages endothelium and impairs immunity, facilitating parasite survival. TNF-α and insulin signaling pathways link both diseases, associated with higher subclinical infection and prolonged parasitemia in diabetics.

articles.headline: Benzoxaboroles inhibit P. falciparum carbonic anhydrase
articles.title_original: Benzoxaboroles Are Inhibitors of the η-Class Carbonic Anhydrase From Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.details: Benzoxaborole derivatives evaluated for PfaCA inhibition in P. falciparum 3D7 strain. Compounds 13 and 14 were most potent PfaCA inhibitors (KI=0.45 and 0.43 µM respectively) with antiplasmodial activity (IC50=34 and 47 µM). Compound 7 showed strongest antiplasmodial activity (IC50=2.5 µM), with molecular docking supporting favorable binding in the PfaCA active site.

articles.headline: Malaria resurgence in Karen State Myanmar 2020-2024
articles.title_original: Re-emergence of malaria in Karen State, Myanmar: has the battle of Burma been lost?
articles.details: Observational study of malaria clinic network in eastern Karen State (350,000 population) comparing 2020 and 2024 data found P. falciparum cases increased 12-fold and P. vivax 3-fold, with increased transmission: P. falciparum (RR=1.72, 95% CI 1.68-1.76) and P. vivax (RR=1.82, 95% CI 1.80-1.84). Resurgence attributed to disrupted diagnosis and treatment during COVID-19 and military conflict; malaria deaths remained low with declining cases in 2024.

articles.headline: Malaria
articles.title_original: Malaria.
articles.details: Title only; no abstract provided to derive findings or clinical context.

articles.headline: Why whole sporozoite malaria vaccine efficacy declines in field
articles.title_original: The endemic dilemma: why whole sporozoite malaria vaccine efficacy falls in the field.
articles.details: Perspective on why whole sporozoite vaccines (WSVs), the most effective malaria vaccines developed, show sharply reduced durable efficacy in endemic field trials compared to controlled CHMI studies. Proposes two hypotheses: antigen mismatch (limited vaccine genetic diversity vs field parasite diversity reducing immune recognition) and host-context (genetics, infection history, microbiome, nutrition). Calls for future integration of parasite genomics, systems immunology, and animal models reflecting endemic context.

articles.headline: Mixed P. ovale and P. falciparum infections: clinical laboratory findings
articles.title_original: Comparison of routine clinical profiles of patients with imported P. falciparum malaria and co-infection with Plasmodium ovale wallikeri or Plasmodium ovale curtisi.
articles.details: Retrospective multicentre cohort of 28 P. ovale wallikeri/P. falciparum co-infections and 11 P. ovale curtisi/P. falciparum co-infections compared to 117 matched P. falciparum mono-infections. P. ovale wallikeri co-infections: higher leukocyte counts (6.25 vs 4.85 × 10⁹/L; p=0.010), lower parasitemia index (0.6% vs 1.0%; p=0.025). P. ovale curtisi co-infections: lower ALT (18.0 vs 27.3 IU/L; p=0.022), more frequent thrombocytopenia (100% vs 51%; p=0.003). Authors note diagnostic and clinical significance remains uncertain. Predominance of P. ovale wallikeri suggests negative interaction with P. falciparum.

articles.headline: Sex differences in P. falciparum liver-stage infection in controlled studies
articles.title_original: Sex-based differences in Plasmodium infection in the control groups of controlled human malaria infection trials in malaria-naive populations in the USA and the Netherlands: a pooled analysis.
articles.details: Pooled analysis of 102 malaria-naive participants (48 female, 54 male) from 13 CHMI studies (2010–2024) in Seattle and Leiden. Using 18S ribosomal RNA NAAT, mean time to blood-stage positivity was 7.17 days in females vs 7.59 days in males. Adjusted accelerated failure time analysis: time to positivity occurred 8% later in males (time ratio 1.08 [95% CI 1.03–1.16]). Males significantly more likely to have detectable infection after day 7 (35% vs 13%; adjusted OR 5.20 [95% CI 1.52–17.70]). Suggests sex-specific host-pathogen interactions in liver stage.

articles.headline: MAP-X reveals dynamic protein complexes in Plasmodium falciparum
articles.title_original: MAP-X unveils the shapeshifting interactome of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.details: Study introduced MAP-X, a thermal profiling method (meltome-assisted profiling of protein complexes) combined with machine learning to map protein complex dynamics in intact P. falciparum parasites in situ. Reveals a highly dynamic interactome with identification of novel protein complexes and 'moonlighting' proteins (proteins with multiple distinct functions), shifting understanding from static protein lists to temporal networks.

articles.headline: Commercial qPCR assay for P. vivax detection shows high performance
articles.title_original: Promising diagnostic performance of a commercial qPCR assay for Plasmodium vivax detection: Contributing to malaria control and elimination.
articles.details: Study evaluated a commercial qPCR assay for P. vivax detection in 167 samples. Assay demonstrated greater sensitivity than microscopy, detecting three additional positive cases. High specificity observed with no cross-reactivity. Authors conclude it is a reliable tool for malaria diagnosis and surveillance.

articles.headline: Integrated diagnostic and biomarker assessment of malaria in Peshawar, Pakistan
articles.title_original: Integrated diagnostic and host biomarker assessment of plasmodium infections in malaria-endemic Peshawar, Pakistan.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study screening 2500 suspected malaria patients in District Peshawar during peak transmission. PCR (18S rRNA) detected significantly more cases than microscopy (p=0.001). Microscopy showed moderate sensitivity, perfect specificity, 86.4% accuracy (κ=0.692, AUC=0.823). Prevalence peaked in September; higher in males aged 21–30 years (p=0.04). Malaria-positive individuals showed significant hematological abnormalities (reduced hemoglobin, hematocrit, RBC indices) and biochemical alterations (elevated liver enzymes, bilirubin; reduced albumin), with parasite density correlating with these markers.

articles.headline: Beta-thalassemia and a missing malaria landscape
articles.title_original: Beta-thalassemia and a missing malaria landscape.
articles.authors: Elmakhzen B, El Makhzen N, Abriel H
articles.details: Title only; no abstract provided to derive findings or clinical implications.

articles.headline: Wolbachia-based approaches for Plasmodium control in Anopheles mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Wolbachia for malaria control.
articles.details: Review of Wolbachia for malaria vector control. Discusses experimental systems for studying Wolbachia–Anopheles interactions and challenges in generating Wolbachia-transinfected A. gambiae strains. Parasite challenge experiments demonstrate Wolbachia-induced Plasmodium suppression is feasible. Synthesizes mechanisms from successful Wolbachia–Aedes–arborvirus systems and current Anopheles evidence, outlining challenges and opportunities for translating proof-of-concept findings to field application.

articles.headline: Cool roofs and screening reduce heat and malaria vectors in rural Africa
articles.title_original: Housing modifications for heat adaptation, thermal comfort and malaria vector control in rural African settlements.
articles.details: Randomized pilot field study of 40 rural African houses tested cool-roof, cross-ventilation, mat-ceiling, or control modifications. Cool-roof houses achieved lowest Heat Index (daytime −3.3°C, p<0.001; nighttime −2.4°C, p<0.01). Screening of doors, windows, and eaves (intervention vs control) reduced female Anopheles funestus by 77% and Culex mosquitoes by 58%. 85% of households expressed willingness to use resources for housing interventions, indicating feasibility for adoption.

articles.headline: Proteome of sexually committed P. falciparum parasites using MSRP1 marker
articles.title_original: Defining the Proteome of Sexually Committed Parasites in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.details: Study demonstrated MSRP1 is a highly specific marker for sexual commitment in P. falciparum. Generated and validated reporter parasite lines using FACS-based isolation of sexually and asexually committed parasites. Proteomics of isolated parasites revealed distinct protein signatures between populations, including several merozoite surface proteins, indicating functional differences at this crucial transmission-critical juncture in the parasite life cycle.

articles.headline: Delayed hemolytic anemia after oral artemether-lumefantrine in immunosuppressed patient
articles.title_original: Delayed hemolytic anemia after artemether-lumefantrine in Plasmodium falciparum malaria: A case report.
articles.details: Case report of delayed hemolytic anemia (DHA) in a 50-year-old male with severe P. falciparum malaria (parasitemia >10%) on chronic rituximab immunosuppression. DHA developed eight days after artemether-lumefantrine treatment with reticulocytosis and positive direct Coombs test, suggesting possible immune-mediated mechanism distinct from the typical non-immune DHA. Pre-existing immunosuppression, hepatosplenomegaly, and prolonged drug regimen likely contributed. Highlights that DHA can occur after oral artemether-lumefantrine and underscores need for hemoglobin monitoring 2–4 weeks post-treatment in high-risk patients.

articles.headline: TIRAP variant protects against severe malaria but increases P. falciparum risk
articles.title_original: Immunomodulatory effects of the TIRAP/MAL rs8177374 (S180L) variant: Meta-Analytic evidence from severe and Plasmodium falciparum malaria.
articles.details: Meta-analysis of 10 studies (4103 malaria cases, 2460 controls) examining TIRAP/MAL rs8177374 polymorphism. T allele showed protective effect against severe malaria (OR=0.51, 95% CI 0.40-0.65, p<0.001) but increased risk for P. falciparum infection (OR=1.47, 95% CI 1.09-1.98, p=0.012). No association observed with overall malaria susceptibility. Results support modulatory role of TIRAP/MAL in malaria pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Pyrethroid resistance widespread in Tanzania; new insecticides remain effective
articles.title_original: Insecticide Resistance Management in Malaria Vectors in Tanzania Mainland (2015-2024): A Scoping Review.
articles.details: Scoping review of 10 studies on insecticide resistance in Tanzanian malaria vectors (2015-2024). Pyrethroid resistance was consistently reported, driven by kdr mutations and metabolic mechanisms. DDT resistance was common; moderate carbamate resistance appeared in approximately 50% of studies. Organophosphates (pirimiphos-methyl) and new insecticides (clothianidin, chlorfenapyr) remained fully effective with no resistance detected.

articles.headline: Immune signatures distinguish bacterial from malaria acute fever
articles.title_original: Profiling of circulating immune factors and host-derived extracellular vesicles in response to bacterial or malaria acute fever.
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of 52 bacterial and 73 malaria febrile patients examining circulating immune factors and extracellular vesicles. Bacterial infections showed elevated endothelial activation (CD29+, CD62P+, CD9+ EVs) and immune migration markers (MMP-2, MMP-9), while malaria featured antigen recognition (CD8+, CD81+, HLA-DR/DP/DQ+ EVs) and cytokine storm mediators (IFNγ, CXCL10, TNFα). HLA-DR/DP/DQ+ EVs distinguished the two with AUC 0.87.

articles.headline: Fat and vitamin A diet reduces clinical malaria risk in children
articles.title_original: Associations Between Climate-Sensitive Nutrients, Clinical Malaria, and Anaemia Among Young Children in Rural Burkina Faso: An Analysis of Baseline Data From a Cluster-Randomised Controlled Trial.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 452 children aged 6-23 months in Burkina Faso. Clinical malaria occurred in 25%, while anaemia affected 88.5%. Adherence to 'fat and vitamin A' dietary pattern was inversely associated with clinical malaria (OR quintile 3 vs quintile 1: 0.50, 95% CI 0.26-0.96), but showed no association with anaemia. Fibre and micronutrient pattern was neither associated with malaria nor anaemia.

articles.headline: Mixed P. falciparum and P. vivax infections detected in Eastern Uttar Pradesh
articles.title_original: Integrated diagnostic approach for mixed Plasmodium infection among acute febrile patients: rare incidence from Eastern Uttar Pradesh.
articles.details: Investigation of malaria among acute febrile illness patients in Eastern Uttar Pradesh using RDT, microscopy, and PCR. Four cases confirmed by RDT. Upon PCR, three of four samples showed mixed infections of P. falciparum and P. vivax, while one had P. falciparum only. Mixed infections represent a rare occurrence in the region but warrant close monitoring.

articles.headline: Malaria and typhoid coinfection common; antimicrobial resistance emerging
articles.title_original: Malaria and salmonella typhi co- infection and antibiotic susceptibility in febrile patients at the Buea Regional Hospital.
articles.authors: Ebini-Ako TA, Tanih NF, Djakissam W
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 152 febrile patients in Cameroon. Malaria prevalence was 52% (79/152) and typhoid 37.5% (57/152); 22.3% (25/112) of confirmed cases had both infections. Vomiting was statistically associated with typhoid (p=0.008). Salmonella typhi isolates showed variable antibiotic susceptibility, with all isolates resistant to Sulfometoxazole, limiting treatment options.

articles.headline: Triple artemether-lumefantrine-amodiaquine shows no clinically relevant drug interactions
articles.title_original: Population pharmacokinetics of artemether-lumefantrine plus amodiaquine in patients with uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria.
articles.details: Population pharmacokinetic analysis from two RCTs (2015-2020) in Africa and Southeast Asia comparing artemether-lumefantrine alone (n=443) to artemether-lumefantrine plus amodiaquine (n=442) in uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria. Nonlinear mixed-effects models developed for all drugs. Coadministered amodiaquine showed no clinically relevant drug-drug interactions; geometric mean ratios for key pharmacokinetic parameters fell within 0.80-1.25 range.

articles.headline: Malaria fever therapy used in Vienna psychiatry 1951-1969
articles.title_original: In the shadow of the "psychopharmacological revolution": Malaria fever, insulin coma, cardiazol and electroconvulsive therapy at the Vienna Psychiatric University Clinic, 1951-1969.
articles.details: Historical analysis of therapeutic practices at Vienna University Clinic (1951-1969) examining malaria fever therapy alongside electroconvulsive therapy, cardiazol convulsion therapy, and insulin coma therapy. Reviewed 14,919 medical records from adult departments treating neurosyphilis, intellectual disabilities, schizophrenia, and other conditions. Examines extent and justification for highly invasive somatic treatments during transition to new pharmacological era.

articles.headline: MAM01 monoclonal antibody shows protective efficacy in phase 1 trial
articles.title_original: Human monoclonal antibody MAM01 for protection against malaria in adults in the USA: a first-in-human, phase 1, dose-escalation, double-blind, placebo-controlled, adaptive trial.
articles.details: Phase 1 dose-escalation trial of MAM01, a monoclonal antibody targeting Plasmodium falciparum circumsporozoite protein, in 37 U.S. adults aged 18-50 years, doses 1.5-40 mg/kg IV or 5 mg/kg SC. Well tolerated with no treatment-related serious adverse events. After controlled human malaria infection 18-26 weeks post-injection, 6/6 control participants and 18/22 MAM01 recipients developed parasitaemia. Protection observed in highest-dose cohorts.

articles.headline: Novel monoclonal antibody approach to malaria prevention
articles.title_original: A new monoclonal in the arMAMentarium against malaria.
articles.details: Commentary or editorial discussing monoclonal antibody therapeutics for malaria prevention. Title references 'arMAMentarium', playing on MAM01 or similar monoclonal approaches. Discusses emerging role of monoclonal antibodies as alternative prevention strategies.

articles.headline: Postmalaria labyrinthitis ossificans: rare Plasmodium complication
articles.title_original: Postmalaria Labyrinthitis Ossificans: A Rare Complication of Plasmodium Infection.
articles.details: Case report describing labyrinthitis ossificans as a rare postmalaria complication following Plasmodium infection. This represents an unusual inner ear sequela of malaria infection.

articles.headline: Rational design of blood-stage malaria vaccine immunogens
articles.title_original: Erythrocyte invasion in malaria: from molecular mechanisms to rational vaccines.
articles.details: Review of erythrocyte invasion mechanisms in Plasmodium and their application to vaccine design. Discusses how structural investigations of human antibodies targeting invasion machinery have revealed neutralizing epitopes and mechanisms of antibody cooperation. Describes rational, structure-guided protein design approaches enabling development of targeted vaccine components, with first rationally designed blood-stage malaria vaccine immunogen entering clinical trials.

articles.headline: Congenital malaria prevalence 12.7% in Nigerian neonates meta-analysis
articles.title_original: Congenital malaria in Nigeria: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Odeniran PO, Paul-Odeniran KF, Ademola IO
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 35 studies (9,503 neonates) reporting congenital malaria (0–7 days) in Nigeria. Pooled prevalence was 12.7% (95% CI 7.3–19.2) with average parasite density 56.6/μL in infected neonates. Maternal, placental, baby peripheral, and cord blood parasitaemia were 25.5%, 20.3%, 12.9%, and 11.1% respectively. Authors conclude congenital malaria is a significant burden in Nigeria requiring enhanced surveillance and targeted interventions.

articles.headline: TLP surface adhesin modulates sporozoite force generation
articles.title_original: Traction force generation in Plasmodium sporozoites is modulated by a surface adhesin.
articles.details: Traction force microscopy study examining myosin-based force generation in Plasmodium sporozoites during gliding migration. Both wild-type and TLP-lacking (tlp−) sporozoites showed distinct foci of force generation on ventral surfaces, but tlp− sporozoites generated overall lower forces. Authors conclude TLP is an important element of the force-generating machinery for sporozoite gliding motility and skin migration.

articles.headline: 14–18% severe malaria survivors experience renal/neurological sequelae
articles.title_original: Assessment of Long-Term Sequelae After Severe Malaria: A Retrospective Study.
articles.details: Retrospective single-centre cohort of 57 severe P. falciparum malaria cases (2018–2024) treated with IV artesunate. At day 28, 6/41 (14.6%) had renal and/or neurological sequelae; at remote follow-up, 6/33 (18.2%) had sequelae. Risk factors included neurological failure, metabolic acidosis, renal impairment at admission, older age, and day-3 blood smear positivity. At 1 year, 8/14 patients reported persistent symptoms including fatigue and cognitive complaints.
articles.notable_reason: Identifies frequent, persistent sequelae after severe malaria survivorship

articles.headline: MAKI case series in low-endemic region Tanzania
articles.title_original: Malaria-Associated Acute Kidney Injury in a Low-Endemic Region: A Case Series of Five Imported Plasmodium falciparum Infections.
articles.details: Case series of 5 imported P. falciparum infections complicated by malaria-associated acute kidney injury (MAKI) in northeastern Tanzania (December 2023–January 2024). Patients had mean serum creatinine 736 ± 346.4 μmol/L and urea 23.1 ± 14.1 mmol/L at admission. Four patients required hemodialysis; one experienced recrudescence. All five patients survived.

articles.headline: MAKI affects 26.5% of malaria patients; 19.2% mortality
articles.title_original: Clinical characteristics and outcome of malaria-associated acute kidney injury in adult patients: a systematic review and meta analysis.
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 25 studies (2,094 MAKI patients, 2000–2024) predominantly from South Asia. Pooled MAKI incidence was 26.5% (95% CI 19.8–34.4), rising to 77% in severe malaria. P. falciparum was most common, but severe AKI from P. vivax increasingly reported. Dialysis required in 20–90% of cases. Pooled mortality was 19.2% (95% CI 12.9–27.7; range 5–52%), with acute tubular necrosis most common histologically, but thrombotic microangiopathy and acute cortical necrosis strongly associated with P. vivax.
articles.notable_reason: Comprehensive MAKI outcomes synthesis; major mortality driver

articles.headline: Colombian P. falciparum clone causes outbreak in Ecuador
articles.title_original: A previously low prevalence Plasmodium falciparum clone expands in an outbreak in the Pacific Coast of South America.
articles.details: Molecular epidemiology study of 72 P. falciparum samples from northwest Ecuador (2019–2021) and neighboring Colombia using microsatellite markers and whole genome sequencing. All infections were monoclonal. A previously low-frequency clone from the Pacific Coast of Colombia caused new outbreaks in Ecuador through clonal expansion, with high genetic differentiation from previously circulating parasites on the Pacific Coast.
articles.notable_reason: Low-frequency clone expansion threatens malaria elimination efforts

articles.headline: Amodiaquine disrupts P. falciparum glycolysis through three enzymes
articles.title_original: Chemoproteomics-based profiling elucidates the antimalarial effects of amodiaquine through disruption of glycolysis process in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.details: Chemoproteomics study using activity-based protein profiling (ABPP) to identify amodiaquine (AQ) binding targets in P. falciparum. Identified three glycolysis-associated enzymes as antimalarial targets; validation confirmed AQ binds these proteins and disrupts glycolytic processes. Demonstrated complementary effects of artesunate (AS) and AQ on shared molecular targets, providing mechanistic basis for AS–AQ combination therapy efficacy.
articles.notable_reason: Mechanism of action of widely-used artemisinin combination therapy

articles.headline: Evolutionary covariation maps protein interactions in Plasmodium
articles.title_original: Evolutionary rate covariation across malaria parasite species enables inference of protein interactions.
articles.details: Comparative genomics study analyzing evolutionary rate covariation (ERC) across 22 Plasmodium species to infer protein interactions and functions. Known pathways and interacting proteins showed strong ERC signals. Using ERC, identified candidate protein PF3D7_0811600 with high covariation to RhopH2 and RhopH3 rhoptry proteins, which form an ion channel enhancing parasite permeability of infected red blood cells.

articles.headline: Low-density parasitaemia doesn't cause malaria or transmission
articles.title_original: Low-density asymptomatic parasitemia in southern Zambia does not lead to clinical malaria and is not associated with household transmission: results from a two-year cohort study.
articles.details: Intensive longitudinal cohort study of 197 households (1,071 individuals) in pre-elimination Southern Province, Zambia (2018–2020) with monthly qPCR collections and passive surveillance. 144/1,071 (13%) were qPCR-positive at least once; monthly parasite prevalence never exceeded 4% with median density 4 parasites/μL. Clinical malaria incidence was 46.7 cases per 1,000 person-years. No association between low-density asymptomatic parasitaemia and subsequent clinical malaria or household transmission beyond one month.
articles.notable_reason: Questions need to treat asymptomatic parasitemia for elimination

articles.headline: Artificial liver purification rescues severe cerebral malaria case
articles.title_original: Successful treatment of severe cerebral malaria with artificial liver blood purification technology: A case report.
articles.details: Case report of a 43-year-old male with severe P. falciparum cerebral malaria complicated by acute liver/kidney failure, cerebral edema, and anuria. Patient treated with artemether, artificial liver blood purification (ALBP), and continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT). Patient successfully recovered. Authors propose ALBP combined with CRRT as potential adjunctive rescue therapy for severe malaria with multi-organ failure.

articles.headline: Older children 5–14 years account for one-third Niger malaria
articles.title_original: Burden of malaria among children aged 5-10 years in the Sahelian area: do we need to adapt seasonal malaria chemoprophylaxis?
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of routine monthly data from 35 dispensaries across 7 Niger provinces (2018–2021) using SARIMA modeling. Transmission was highly seasonal with peaks July–October. During peak months, children aged 12–59 months accounted for 33.2% of cases, followed by those aged 10–14 years (26.0%) and 2–11 months (22.6%). Older children (5–14 years) together accounted for over one-third of all cases with marked geographic disparities favoring southern irrigated agricultural areas.
articles.notable_reason: Older children bear significant burden; SMC targeting may need expansion

articles.headline: MSCs reduce experimental cerebral malaria via Th17/Treg modulation
articles.title_original: Mesenchymal stem cells alleviate experimental cerebral malaria disease severity by inducing RoRγt(+) Foxp3(+) T regulatory (Tr 17) cells and modulating the dysregulated Th17/Treg axis.
articles.details: Experimental study in Plasmodium berghei ANKA-infected mice (ECM model) demonstrating that mesenchymal stem cell (MSC) infusion reduced parasitemia, increased survival, and improved neurological outcomes including prevention of blood–brain barrier vascular leakage. MSCs modulated the dysregulated Th17/Treg axis by downregulating IL-6, IL-17, and upregulating IL-10. First report that MSCs induced RoRγt+ Foxp3+ dual-phenotype Tr17 effector regulatory T cells suppressing pathogenic Th17 cells.

articles.headline: Feretia apodanthera plant extracts show antiplasmodial activity in vitro and vivo
articles.title_original: Harnessing the potential of medicinal plants in the malaria fighting: In vitro and in vivo antiplasmodial activities of Feretia apodanthera Delille (Rubiaceae).
articles.details: In vitro (SYBR Green assay) and in vivo (Plasmodium berghei NMRI-infected mice) study of leaf, stem bark, and root extracts. Leaf extract most potent: in vitro IC50 0.140-0.620 μg/mL; in vivo dose-dependent suppression 32-76% (250-500 mg/kg); curative efficacy 63-68% at day 5-7; prophylactic efficacy 57-64%. Non-toxic (LD50 >5000 mg/kg). UHPLC-HRMS identified polyphenols, alkaloids, flavonoids, tannins as active compounds. Leaf superior due to higher polyphenol and alkaloid content.

articles.headline: Plasmodium yoelii infection activates antitumor immunity in Lewis lung cancer mice
articles.title_original: Plasmodium infection fully activates the immune system in peripheral blood and tumor microenvironment in a murine Lewis lung cancer model.
articles.details: C57BL/6J mice with subcutaneous Lewis lung cancer treated with intraperitoneal P. yoelii. Flow cytometry revealed increased circulating and tumor-infiltrating CD3+ T cells (CD4+ and CD8+ subsets including Tcm, Tem, Trm, SLEC, MPEC). Infection reduced PD-1 on T cells, decreased polymorphonuclear MDSCs, and increased M1/M2 macrophage ratio. Mechanistically, infection reduced tumor pSTAT3 and TGFβ expression. Conclusion: Plasmodium infection remodels immune system via pSTAT3/TGFβ reduction, offering potential new cancer immunotherapy approach.

articles.headline: CelTOS genetic diversity in P. falciparum from Saudi Arabia shows panmixia
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity and haplotype structure of the CelTOS gene in Plasmodium falciparum from Jazan Province, Saudi Arabia: implications for vaccine development and malaria elimination.
articles.details: Population genetics of CelTOS gene in 202 P. falciparum samples from Jazan Province, Saudi Arabia. Analysis revealed 27 haplotypes (haplotype diversity Hd=0.921, nucleotide diversity π=0.0059), gene-wide ω≈0.36 indicating purifying selection, and low intragenic recombination. Pairwise FST essentially zero with weak regional structure. Dominant haplotypes shared across demographic strata indicating strong gene flow and panmictic population structure. Findings support cross-border control strategies and CelTOS as vaccine development marker.

articles.headline: P. vivax Ripr protein naturally immunogenic with defined B-cell epitopes
articles.title_original: B-cell epitope mapping and characterization of antibody responses to recombinant PvRipr in malaria-exposed individuals.
articles.details: ELISA screening of plasma from naturally malaria-exposed individuals in Brazilian Amazon detected PvRipr-specific antibodies in >60% (IgM or IgG). IgG1 and IgG2 subclasses predominated. Four linear B-cell epitopes identified by in silico prediction; B-PvRipr(879-888) and B-PvRipr(923-958) showed highest seropositivity frequencies. Epitope-specific IgG strongly correlated with total anti-PvRipr IgG and particularly with IgG3 (protective subclass in malaria immunity). Ripr identified as viable vaccine candidate.

articles.headline: Plasmonic biosensor detects P. falciparum LDH with high sensitivity
articles.title_original: Experimental plasmonic sensing of malaria using an aluminum metasurface.
articles.details: Optical biosensor based on surface plasmon resonance using aluminum metasurface with nanohole array detects plasmodium falciparum lactate dehydrogenase (pfLDH), a metabolic enzyme produced during parasite life cycle and present in all Plasmodium species. Sensor operates in visible spectral region with spectral sensitivity 360 nm/RIU and limit of detection 1.3 nM (45.6 ng/mL pfLDH). Achieves label-free detection in phosphate-buffered saline. Offers potential cost-effective, high-sensitivity diagnostic method.

articles.headline: PfHRP2-derived total parasite biomass better reflects malaria severity
articles.title_original: Reevaluating malaria severity metrics: insights from PfHRP2-derived biomass estimates in Ghanaian children.
articles.details: Observational study of 118 Ghanaian children (58 cerebral malaria, 28 severe malarial anemia, 32 uncomplicated malaria) 2012-2016 comparing PfHRP2-derived total parasite burden (PTot) and sequestered burden (PSeq) to peripheral parasitemia (PCir). PTot and PSeq consistently higher than PCir in severe syndromes; PTot-PCir difference most pronounced in cerebral malaria. Higher PSeq associated with coma severity. Prior antimalarial treatment reduced PCir but not PTot. Conclusion: total parasite biomass stronger severity marker than peripheral parasitemia.

articles.headline: Pyrethroid resistance in An. funestus escalated dramatically over four years
articles.title_original: Four-year monitoring of the malaria vector Anopheles funestus in central-west Cameroon reveals an escalation of pyrethroid resistance combined with high malaria transmission.
articles.details: Four-year (2020-2023) surveillance of Anopheles funestus in Mibellon, Cameroon tracking insecticide resistance and malaria transmission. Pyrethroid mortality declined dramatically: permethrin from 77.7% (2015) to 23.2% (2023); deltamethrin from 46.6% (2016) to 8.5% (2023). High-intensity resistance observed across pyrethroids. Sporozoite infection rates 4-21%, predominantly P. falciparum. Rapid selection of G454A-CYP9K1 and 4.3 kb SV resistance alleles. Organophosphates remained fully effective; PBO-based nets maintained efficacy. Artemisinin resistance markers K13 P441L and R561H detected.

articles.headline: An. maculatus identified as potential vector of zoonotic malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Evidence incriminating Anopheles maculatus as a potential vector of Plasmodium knowlesi and Plasmodium inui.
articles.details: Study of Maculatus Subgroup mosquitoes in North Sumatra from July 2022-June 2023 using human landing catches. All 234 specimens morphologically identified as An. maculatus s.l. confirmed genetically as An. maculatus s.s. PCR screening of mosquito heads/thoraces identified 4 specimens (1.7%; 95% CI 0.5-4.3%) positive for Plasmodium DNA; one specimen positive for both Plasmodium knowlesi and Plasmodium inui. First detection of zoonotic malaria parasite DNA in wild-caught An. maculatus, suggesting vector role in P. knowlesi and P. inui transmission.

articles.headline: Pregnant women interested in TB, malaria, GBS, RSV vaccines with safety concerns
articles.title_original: Pregnant women's attitudes and intentions toward tuberculosis, malaria, group B streptococcus, and respiratory syncytial virus vaccines in pregnant: Findings from a cross-sectional study of pregnant women living in Brazil, Ghana, Kenya, and Pakistan.
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 1,603 pregnant women in Brazil, Ghana, Kenya, and Pakistan on attitudes toward tuberculosis, malaria, group B streptococcus, and respiratory syncytial virus vaccines. Vaccine safety for baby ranked most important factor, followed by efficacy for baby and maternal safety. Definite vaccine acceptance intentions: GBS 30%, malaria 26%, RSV 25%, TB 19%. Vaccine hesitancy drivers: 40% believed vaccines unnatural, 38% preferred natural immunity, 19% had delayed recommended vaccines. Study emphasizes importance of understanding vaccine attitudes in designing clinical trials and acceptance programs.

articles.headline: Hepcidin suppressed in moderate malnutrition with asymptomatic malaria
articles.title_original: Hepcidin is low in children with moderate acute malnutrition and asymptomatic malaria: secondary analysis of a 2×2×3 factorial randomised trial in Burkina Faso.
articles.details: Secondary analysis of 1,019 children with moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) in Burkina Faso from 2×2×3 factorial RCT receiving 500 kcal/day supplementation (corn-soy blend or lipid-based) for 12 weeks. Iron deficiency associated with 82% lower serum hepcidin. Symptomatic malaria had 103% higher hepcidin versus afebrile; asymptomatic/recent malaria had 51% lower hepcidin. Supplementation increased hepcidin 61%; lipid-based supplements yielded 22% higher hepcidin than corn-soy. Study documents hepcidin behavior across iron-malaria-nutrition interactions.

articles.headline: Growth-inhibitory epitope mapped in PfRipr5 vaccine candidate
articles.title_original: Identification of a growth-inhibitory epitope in PfRipr5, a malaria vaccine candidate against Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.details: Molecular characterization of PfRipr5 (truncated fragment containing erythrocyte-binding protein Rh5-interacting protein EGF-like domains 5-9), a component of the pentameric PCRCR invasion complex. Western blotting with cysteine-substituted mutants and surface plasmon resonance identified 20-amino-acid cysteine-associated epitope recognized by growth-inhibitory monoclonal antibody 29B11. Epitope recognition dependent on cysteine integrity. Findings provide tractable molecular target for rational subunit vaccine design against blood-stage malaria.

articles.headline: Imported malaria drives transmission in near-elimination southwestern Uganda sites
articles.title_original: Imported malaria predominates in near-elimination settings in Southwestern Uganda.
articles.details: Epidemiologic surveillance with parasite genomics from January 2023-June 2024 at three southwestern Uganda health facilities (Maziba, Muko very low transmission; Kamwezi low-to-moderate transmission). Enrolled microscopy-confirmed malaria cases with travel histories and genotyping using MAD4HatTeR multiplex panel (165 diversity markers, 38 drug resistance loci). Network inference showed Maziba and Muko cases predominantly imported (87-96% with recent travel from high-transmission regions), while Kamwezi exhibited sustained local transmission clusters. Artemisinin partial resistance markers more prevalent in Kamwezi.

articles.headline: Natural product DHFR inhibitors identified via computational screening
articles.title_original: Computational exploration of natural product inhibitors against mutant Plasmodium falciparum DHFR through pharmacophore modeling, docking, and MD simulations.
articles.details: Pharmacophore modeling, virtual screening, and molecular dynamics simulations identified potential pfDHFR inhibitors from natural products targeting drug-resistant P. falciparum. Three lead candidates from TCM database: ZINC70454408 with highest H-bond energy (-14.204 kcal/mol) and Ki 0.04 µM; ZINC04096650 and ZINC85631105 with binding energies -122.64 and -118.92 kcal/mol and Ki < 0.1 µM. Authors recommend future experimental validation.

articles.headline: Nigerian An. coluzzii resistant to pyrethroids and DDT
articles.title_original: Urban evolution of insecticide resistance and susceptibility patterns of Anopheles coluzzii in Southeastern Nigeria: implications for malaria vector control.
articles.details: Cross-sectional susceptibility assessment of An. coluzzii from three Nigerian urban centers (Awka, Onitsha, Nnewi). Deltamethrin mortality 45-63% and DDT mortality 20-56%, indicating resistance; full susceptibility to bendiocarb and pirimiphos-methyl (100%). High kdr 1014F allele frequencies (0.72-0.81) drove pyrethroid resistance; low ace-1 G119S frequencies (0.07-0.12). Findings show need for tailored organophosphate/carbamate interventions.

articles.headline: Community perspectives on monkey malaria risk in Sabah
articles.title_original: What does monkey malaria mean to you? A participatory photovoice study in rural Malaysian Borneo.
articles.details: Participatory photovoice study with 20 participants from three rural Sabah villages (January-May 2024) exploring perceptions of P. knowlesi zoonotic transmission risk. Generated five themes: malaria as embedded everyday risk, community resilience, structural challenges, human-wildlife-environmental health interface, and cultural coping practices. Findings emphasize effective control requires local priorities, cultural practices, and lived experience beyond strictly biomedical models.

articles.headline: Three modeling approaches differ on climate-transmission relationships
articles.title_original: Comparing three approaches to modelling the effects of temperature and rainfall on malaria incidence in different climatic regions.
articles.authors: Madito GT, Brooke BD, Silal SP
articles.details: Vector-host mathematical models compared three approaches incorporating temperature and rainfall effects on malaria transmission across tropical, semi-arid, and subtropical regions at varying vector control coverage. Results differed substantially: first approach sustained seasonal transmission; second projected extended peaks in warm regions; third projected multiple irregular peaks with winter cessation. Conclusion: effective vector control plus larval management can substantially reduce incidence despite climate variation and extreme weather events.

articles.headline: Submicroscopic infections comprise 31% of community malaria burden
articles.title_original: Community reservoirs of malaria parasites and gametocytes in Arba Minch district, southern Rift Valley, Ethiopia: a cross-sectional study.
articles.details: Reactive case detection following 142 PCR-confirmed index cases in Ethiopia (July-October 2022) screening 2,009 individuals. Microscopy-based prevalence 3.6% (72/2009); additional 33 submicroscopic infections identified (10.1% of PCR-tested negatives: P. vivax 4.6%, P. falciparum 4.3%). Submicroscopic infections represented 31% (33/105) of all PCR-confirmed cases. Gametocyte carriage higher in P. vivax reactive cases (69% vs P. falciparum 22%), supporting transmission reservoir role.

articles.headline: Nanobodies against Pfs48/45 reduce P. falciparum transmission
articles.title_original: Pfs48/45 nanobodies block Plasmodium falciparum transmission.
articles.details: Identified nanobodies targeting Pfs48/45 transmission-blocking vaccine candidate that recognize gametocytes and demonstrate strong transmission-reducing activity. Crystal structure reveals nanobody epitope distinct from TB31F monoclonal antibody but similar to RUPA-44 and RUPA-117 epitopes. Findings demonstrate versatility of nanobodies as antibody format for transmission-blocking interventions.

articles.headline: DNA ligase I essential for parasite liver-stage development
articles.title_original: Plasmodium DNA ligase I is essential for parasite blood- and liver-stage development.
articles.details: DNA ligase I (Lig1) from P. falciparum and P. berghei localized primarily to nucleus with organellar presence. Knockout of PbLig1 in sporozoites prevented liver-stage development: parasites invaded hepatocytes but arrested during mid-liver-stage with limited nuclear division and reduced DNA content. Results establish Lig1 as essential enzyme for blood and liver-stage parasite development.

articles.headline: Review of malaria prevention strategies: progress and challenges
articles.title_original: The Evolving Landscape of Malaria Prevention Strategies: A Review of Recent Developments.
articles.details: Comprehensive review synthesizing vector control (ITNs, IRS despite widespread insecticide resistance), chemoprevention (IPTp, SMC with uptake below WHO targets), and vaccines. RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M landmark achievements demonstrating reductions in severe disease and mortality with large-scale rollouts. Novel approaches including monoclonal antibodies and genetically modified mosquitoes show promise. Key challenges: equitable access, sustaining efficacy against evolving parasite/vector biology, health system integration.

articles.headline: Novel sporozoite-specific rhoptry protein identified via proximity labeling
articles.title_original: Identification of a novel rhoptry protein expressed predominantly in Plasmodium sporozoites.
articles.details: Proximity-dependent biotin identification in P. berghei sporozoites identified rhoptry neck protein 12 (RON12) with highest transcript levels in sporozoites. Novel rhoptry protein PBANKA_1363400 identified and localized to sporozoite rhoptries with predominantly sporozoite expression (versus merozoite). Study demonstrates UltraID strategy enables sensitive, species- and stage-specific protein identification in Plasmodium.

articles.headline: Potato carboxypeptidase inhibitor blocks mosquito enzyme in vitro
articles.title_original: Inhibition of carboxypeptidase b1 from Anopheles stephensi by the potato carboxypeptidase inhibitor: a foundational step toward paratransgenesis-based malaria control.
articles.details: In vitro study of Potato Carboxypeptidase Inhibitor (PCI) against carboxypeptidase-B1 (CPBAs1) from Anopheles stephensi. PCI efficiently inhibited CPBAs1 enzymatic activity at low concentrations, comparable to commercial 1,10-phenanthroline inhibitor. CPBAs1 essential for P. falciparum sexual development in mosquito midgut. Authors propose PCI as potential effector molecule for future paratransgenesis-based vector control.

articles.headline: PTRAMP-CSS-Ripr invasion complex conserved across Plasmodium species
articles.title_original: PTRAMP, CSS and Ripr form a conserved complex required for merozoite invasion of Plasmodium species into erythrocytes.
articles.details: Demonstrated PTRAMP, CSS, and Ripr orthologs form disulfide-linked heterodimer complex (PCR complex) across P. falciparum, P. vivax, and P. knowlesi. Cross-reactive antibodies differentially inhibited merozoite invasion. Cryo-EM visualization confirmed predicted models showing core invasion scaffold conserved across species. Findings have implications for multi-species malaria vaccine development.

articles.headline: Weather-informed GAM forecasting for Ghana malaria surveillance
articles.title_original: Malaria incidence, severity and mortality in children under five in Ghana: evidence from generalised additive models.
articles.authors: Bosson-Amedenu S, Eyiah-Bediako F, Anafo AY
articles.details: Negative-binomial generalized additive models on 132 monthly observations (2013-2023) from Tarkwa-Nsuaem, Ghana linked rainfall and temperature to malaria incidence, severity, and deaths. Rainfall-memory model best fit incidence (deviance explained ~80%, R² ~0.88), with 1-month lag and 6-month cumulative rainfall significant (p=0.002). 12-month forecast: ~600-670 monthly incidence cases (95% PI 250-1,350), ~0-1 deaths/month. Transmission reflects recent rainfall history.

articles.headline: Splenic abscess with P. falciparum malaria and sickle cell trait
articles.title_original: Pyogenic Splenic Abscess With Concurrent Active Plasmodium falciparum Malaria and Sickle Cell Trait: A Case Report.
articles.details: Case report of a young West African male with concurrent Plasmodium falciparum malaria, sickle cell trait, and pyogenic splenic abscess. Percutaneous drainage cultured Fusobacterium species. Treatment with antimalarial therapy and broad-spectrum antibiotics resulted in complete recovery, highlighting consideration of anaerobic bacterial pathogens in splenic infections among malaria-endemic populations.

articles.headline: Digital gangrene from severe P. falciparum malaria despite artesunate
articles.title_original: Purpura Fulminans With Digital Gangrene in Severe Plasmodium falciparum Malaria: A Case Presentation.
articles.details: Case report of a 66-year-old woman from Angola with cerebral malaria due to Plasmodium falciparum. Despite early intravenous artesunate, hyperbaric oxygen therapy, and supportive care, she developed purpuric lesions on the feet progressing to gangrene. Systemic recovery was achieved but digital gangrene developed, illustrating a severe complication from microvascular thrombosis and coagulation dysfunction.

articles.headline: Community health workers most effective for promoting bed net use
articles.title_original: Differentiated effects of sources of malaria messages on mosquito bed net use among women of reproductive age in Uganda: a propensity score matched analysis.
articles.details: Secondary analysis of 3,488 women aged 15-49 from Uganda Malaria Indicator Survey (2018-2019) using propensity score matching. Overall 73.2% reported mosquito bed net use. Community health workers showed strongest association with bed net use (ATT=0.322, 95% CI 0.111-0.533), followed by community events (ATT=0.296, 95% CI 0.085-0.507) and social mobilization (ATT=0.185, 95% CI 0.008-0.362). Television exposure was not significantly associated with bed net use.

articles.headline: Gut barrier loss drives microbiota-mediated immunosuppression in malaria
articles.title_original: Intestinal Barrier Loss Enables Microbiota-Mediated Purinergic Suppression During Malaria.
articles.details: In Plasmodium yoelii murine model, intestinal barrier disruption enabled microbiota-derived adenosine signaling leading to immunosuppression and increased disease severity; IgA-coated microbiota fraction alone transferred susceptibility. Barrier reinforcement or adenosine A2A receptor blockade improved germinal centers and reduced severity independent of parasite burden. Ugandan children with severe malaria showed distinct purinergic immune signatures, establishing a microbiota-dependent immunosuppression pathway.
articles.notable_reason: Identifies novel microbiota-purinergic immunosuppression pathway in malaria

articles.headline: Risk factors for malaria in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan
articles.title_original: Determinants of malaria infection across different districts of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan: a cross-sectional study.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 768 participants in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa province identified 24.5% malaria prevalence. Independent risk factors included male gender (AOR 1.8, 95% CI 1.1-2.8), younger/middle age 16-50 (AOR 4.2-8.3), low education (AOR 3.9), low income 10,000-50,000 rupees (AOR 17.8), large family size (AOR 11.3), distance >3 km to healthcare (AOR 3.1), and lack of modern toilets (AOR 4.8).

articles.headline: Long-latency P. malariae infections up to 50 years in China
articles.title_original: Unraveling the origins and features of long-latency Plasmodium malariae infections: a comprehensive epidemiological, clinical, and genetic study of seven cases in Guangdong Province (2014-2022).
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of 7 P. malariae cases in Guangdong Province (2014-2022) with latency periods of 7-50 years, predominantly affecting elderly women (mean age 75.3 years, 85.7% female). 85.7% acquired infection from historical local transmission pre-1990s; one case from Southeast Asian importation. Phylogenetic analysis showed Asian lineage with 95-97% nucleotide homology distinct from African strains. Median diagnostic delay was 4 days.

articles.headline: Stabilized tandem antigen shows potent transmission-blocking activity
articles.title_original: A stabilized tandem antigen chimera that elicits potent malaria transmission-reducing activity.
articles.details: Development of STAC (stabilized tandem antigen chimera) combining Pfs230-D1 and Pfs48/45-D3 epitopes in a single construct with engineered domain disposition. Nanoparticle-based multimerization enhanced functional transmission-reducing activity. Immunizations of female mice with STAC-nanoparticle conjugates elicited antibodies with potent transmission-reducing activity comparable or superior to multimerized individual antigens.

articles.headline: High vaccine awareness but limited knowledge in Northern Uganda
articles.title_original: Caregivers awareness, knowledge, attitudes, and perceptions towards malaria vaccine in Kole and Kwania Districts, Northern Uganda.
articles.details: Mixed methods study of 574 caregivers in Kole and Kwania districts, Northern Uganda regarding R21 malaria vaccine. While 90.1% were aware of the vaccine, only 13.9% demonstrated factual knowledge (16.4% knew number of doses, 21.0% knew dosing intervals, 34.2% knew age eligibility). 57.4% expressed positive attitudes and 57.5% positive perceptions, though 49.7% reported safety concerns.

articles.headline: Malaria positivity increased to 17.7% over five years Ethiopia
articles.title_original: A five-year (2020-2024) malaria surveillance data analysis of Mattu Karl Specialized Comprehensive Hospital, Oromia region, Southwest Ethiopia.
articles.details: Retrospective surveillance from Mattu Karl Hospital, Southwest Ethiopia (2020-2024) of 100,760 tested patients showing 14,303 (14.2%) malaria positivity. Plasmodium vivax predominated at 49.1%, followed by P. falciparum (37.8%) and mixed infections (13.1%). Positivity rate increased from 9.2% in 2020 to 17.7% in 2024, with notably increased mixed infections.

articles.headline: Historical malaria control in Palestine and Arab-Israeli conflict
articles.title_original: Malaria in Palestine: the elephant in the room.
articles.details: Historical examination of malaria's overlooked role in early Arab-Israeli conflict and development of malaria control methods. Discusses how Palestine was historically severely affected by malaria over 100 years ago in many rural areas, making regions nearly uninhabitable. Highlights how boycott movements obscured study of successful historical malaria control efforts.

articles.headline: Malaria-MOI tool improves multiplicity of infection estimation from sequencing
articles.title_original: Malaria-MOI: A flexible and scalable tool for predicting multiplicity of infection in malaria parasites.
articles.details: Development of Malaria-MOI, a Python tool for estimating multiplicity of infection (MOI) from whole genome sequencing data (BAM/VCF formats). Benchmarked on 27 P. falciparum mixed-clone samples with known MOI achieved RMSE 0.38, MAE 0.15, and correlation 0.78. Applied to 8,208 field P. falciparum samples, showed strong negative correlation with FWS (ρ=-0.76), sensitivity 0.99 for monoclonal infections, and superior specificity (0.76) compared to estMOI (0.58).

articles.headline: School-age children highest malaria despite lowest bed net usage
articles.title_original: Disparities in long-lasting insecticidal bed net usage and malaria burden 2 years after a mass distribution campaign in central Côte d'Ivoire: A cross-sectional survey prior to a cluster randomised trial.
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey of 1,672 participants in 33 clusters in Côte d'Ivoire showing LLIN ownership 66.3% and access 27.8%. LLIN usage lowest in females aged 10-14 years (41.0%) compared to under-fives (63.2%). Overall malaria prevalence 41.1%, highest in ages 5-9 years (75.0%, aOR 1.86 vs under-fives). LLIN users had lower infection prevalence (38.5% vs 44.3% in non-users, aOR 0.74).

articles.headline: Expert consensus on P. knowlesi prevention factor priorities
articles.title_original: Exploring influencing factors affecting preventive behaviours of Plasmodium knowlesi malaria among at-risk communities: an application of the nominal group technique.
articles.details: Expert panel used Nominal Group Technique to identify and rank 20 factors affecting P. knowlesi prevention among at-risk Southeast Asian communities, achieving 80% consensus in a single round. Factors categorized into environmental risks, human-related risks, policy and organizational factors, and vector-related risks. Findings provide foundation for refining context-tailored prevention strategies.

articles.headline: Crystal structure of malaria fusogen HAP2 domain revealed
articles.title_original: Crystal structure and nanobodies against domain 3 of the malaria parasite fusogen Plasmodium falciparum HAP2.
articles.details: First crystal structure of Plasmodium falciparum HAP2 domain 3 in complex with nanobodies that bind with nanomolar affinities. Two nanobodies show cross-species reactivity with HAP2 from other Plasmodium species. Provides first structural insights into this transmission-blocking target essential for parasite gamete fusion during mosquito infection.

articles.headline: Novel HDAC inhibitor shows potent antimalarial activity in vivo
articles.title_original: Discovery of γ-Tetrahydrocarboline Derivatives as Plasmodium falciparum Histone Deacetylase Inhibitors for Treatment of Malaria.
articles.details: γ-Tetrahydrocarboline derivative (compound 5ac) identified as PfHDAC1 inhibitor with favorable safety profile (Pf3D7 IC50 = 4.1 nM, selectivity index 358–3573) and potent in vivo antimalarial activity. Compound downregulates invasion-related genes. Addresses urgent need for novel drugs against resistant P. falciparum parasites.

articles.headline: Malaria microparticles carry 1806 proteins; hemoglobin loss pattern identified
articles.title_original: Integrative proteomic profiling of malaria-derived microparticles: A mass spectrometry-based study.
articles.details: Proteomic analysis of plasma microparticles from 43 malaria patients versus 10 healthy controls identified 1729 vs 234 proteins respectively. Malaria microparticles showed significantly higher quantities of hemoglobin subunits. Authors propose hemoglobin loss via microparticles may initiate anemia in P. falciparum infection.

articles.headline: Review: global burden and control of mosquito-borne diseases
articles.title_original: Review of selected mosquito-borne diseases: arboviruses (dengue, chikungunya, Zika, West Nile, Japanese encephalitis, yellow fever) and parasitic diseases (malaria, lymphatic Filariasis).
articles.details: Review covering dengue, chikungunya, Zika, West Nile, Japanese encephalitis, yellow fever, malaria, and lymphatic filariasis. Discusses clinical features, global distribution expanding due to climate change and urbanization, transmission dynamics, and control strategies including vector management, vaccines (R21 for malaria), and chemoprophylaxis. Notes socioeconomic costs exceed $318 billion for Aedes-borne diseases since 1975.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria in blood donors: 0.32% global, 15.89% in Africa
articles.title_original: Global burden of asymptomatic malaria among blood donors: systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 15 studies (January 2018–May 2025) found pooled prevalence of asymptomatic malaria among blood donors was 0.32% globally (95% CI: 0.25–0.39). Regional variation: Africa 15.89%, South America 0.79%, Asia 0.21%. By diagnostic method: microscopy 21.86%, PCR 5.03%, rapid diagnostic test 0.21%.

articles.headline: Proline transporter PfApiAT2 essential for mosquito-stage transmission
articles.title_original: PfApiAT2 is a proline transporter essential for the transmission of Plasmodium falciparum by the mosquito vector.
articles.details: PfApiAT2, a proline-specific amino acid transporter, is essential for oocyst development in Anopheles gambiae. Halofuginone-resistant mutants form stunted oocysts with severely impaired sporozoite production; PfApiAT2-knockout parasites completely blocked in sporogony. Reveals parasites' critical nutritional dependency on mosquito vector, exposing vulnerability targetable for transmission-blocking interventions.
articles.notable_reason: Novel transmission-stage drug target with broad implications

articles.headline: Z. chalybeum root extract achieves 76% parasite clearance in mice
articles.title_original: Antimalarial efficacy of aqueous crude rootbark extracts of Zanthoxylum Chalybeum: An in vivo experimental study in Wistar Albino Mice infected with Plasmodium berghei.
articles.details: In vivo study in Plasmodium berghei–infected Wistar albino mice. Z. chalybeum aqueous rootbark extracts showed dose-dependent parasite clearance (up to 76%), with significant effects at all doses 100–400 mg/kg versus control (p<0.001). Extract also significantly reduced fever (mean temperature difference −1.50 to −1.78°C, p≤0.009). No effect on hemoglobin or hematocrit.

articles.headline: PbHiT: scalable CRISPR-Cas9 system for P. berghei genome editing
articles.title_original: Plasmodium berghei High-Throughput (PbHiT): a CRISPR-Cas9 System to Study Genes at Scale.
articles.details: PbHiT protocol presents scalable CRISPR-Cas9 system for efficient genome editing in rodent malaria parasite P. berghei. Single-step cloning generates vectors with guide RNA physically linked to short (100 bp) homology arms; gRNA serves as barcode for pooled transfections. Enables knockout and tagging strategies for functional studies and high-throughput genetic screens.

articles.headline: Mixed P. vivax–P. falciparum infection in child: diagnostic challenge
articles.title_original: Pediatric Mixed Plasmodium vivax-P. falciparum Infection with Disparate Parasitemias: Diagnostic and Surveillance Challenges.
articles.details: Case report of 9-year-old with mixed Plasmodium vivax (5500 parasites/µL) and P. falciparum (562 parasites/µL) infection. Disparate parasitemias risked underdetecting P. falciparum by conventional microscopy. Treated with artemether-lumefantrine followed by primaquine. Achieved parasite clearance and full recovery without severe malaria complications. Illustrates diagnostic risk in endemic settings.

articles.headline: Afebrile P. falciparum malaria presenting as severe hemolytic anemia
articles.title_original: Afebrile falciparum malaria with severe hemolytic anemia in a returning traveler: a case report.
articles.details: Case report of afebrile Plasmodium falciparum malaria in returning traveler presenting with severe hemolytic anemia (hemoglobin 48 g/L), thrombocytopenia, and intravascular hemolysis. Parasitemia 0.9% (≈1746 parasites/µL) confirmed by blood smear, HRP-2 antigen, and PCR. Treated with intramuscular artesunate followed by oral dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine with full recovery.

articles.headline: Ugandan Anopheles gambiae shows low genetic differentiation across mainland
articles.title_original: Assessing the population genetic structure and demographic history of Anopheles gambiae and Anopheles arabiensis at island and mainland sites in Uganda: implications for testing novel malaria vector control approaches.
articles.details: Genetic analysis of 2918 An. gambiae and 173 An. arabiensis at 6 Ugandan sites (3 island, 3 mainland) using targeted amplicon sequencing of 62 loci. An. gambiae showed low differentiation across sites (FST 0.00054–0.028). Island populations had smaller effective population sizes (146–249) and lower nucleotide diversity than mainland populations (4054–8190). Findings inform design of genetic vector control interventions.

articles.headline: Bacteremia present in 14.5% of severe malaria patients
articles.title_original: Prevalence and factors associated with concomitant bacteremia among adults admitted with severe malaria at Kayunga Regional Referral Hospital, Uganda.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 207 adults with severe malaria at Kayunga Hospital. Blood cultures identified bacteremia in 14.5% of patients. CNS symptoms, low peripheral oxygen saturation, hyperparasitaemia, and leucocytosis were significantly associated with bacteremia (p<0.05). Most common isolates were Salmonella typhi (33.3%), Staphylococcus aureus (30%), and Streptococcus spp. (16.7%).

articles.headline: Pfmdr1 N86Y mutation rare; mostly wild-type alleles
articles.title_original: Molecular surveillance of the Plasmodium falciparum Pfmdr1 N86Y mutation linked to antimalarial drug resistance in Mbouda, Cameroon.
articles.details: Molecular surveillance of 302 patients in Mbouda, Cameroon identified P. falciparum in 28.8%. The Pfmdr1 gene was detected in 95% of isolates. Of analyzed alleles, 94% were wild-type (N86), 1% mixed (N86/Y86), and 0% mutant (Y86). Authors recommend evaluating other Pfmdr1 mutations for assessing antimalarial drug resistance.

articles.headline: Sudanese Copts show adaptive admixture for Duffy-null
articles.title_original: Sudan's complex genetic admixture history drives adaptation to malaria in Sudanese Copts.
articles.journal: Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A
articles.details: Whole-genome sequencing of 125 Sudanese individuals revealed strong adaptive admixture signal on chromosome 1 in Sudanese Copts. SNP rs2814778 in the ACKR1 gene (Duffy-null blood group) showed strong selection coefficient (s=0.0996) providing resistance to P. vivax malaria. This admixture-driven adaptation was introduced 1,000–1,500 years ago via Nilo-Saharan ancestry.

articles.headline: An. farauti travels 73 meters daily in Australia
articles.title_original: Estimating the dispersal of the malaria vector Anopheles farauti through a natural ecosystem in north Queensland, Australia using mark release and recapture experiments.
articles.details: Mark-release-recapture of 19,889 An. farauti across 51 releases (2015-2017) in tropical north Queensland estimated daily survival of 0.68 (wet season) and 0.75 (dry season). Of 19,889 marked mosquitoes, 308 (1.55%) were recaptured. Longest flight distance was 3.51 km with estimated average travel of 73 meters per day, exceeding previously documented distances.

articles.headline: P. falciparum csp diversity baseline before RTS,S vaccine
articles.title_original: Complexity of Infection and Plasmodium falciparum Circumsporozoite Protein Diversity Prior to Malaria Vaccine Implementation in Kaelé Health District, Cameroon, 2022-2023.
articles.details: Analysis of 100 P. falciparum samples from Kaele, Cameroon (2022-2023) before RTS,S/AS01 vaccine implementation. csp was genotyped in 35%, identifying 22 haplotypes (heterozygosity He=0.908, nucleotide diversity π=0.021). The vaccine-matched haplotype was present in 20% of genotyped infections. Complexity of infection varied: ama mean 2.8, sera2 mean 2.1, csp mean 1.3, trap mean 1.2.

articles.headline: Pre-treatment antimalarial levels detected in 24% globally
articles.title_original: Residual antimalarial drug concentrations before treatment in malaria patients in Asia and Africa: A systematic review and meta-analysis depicting its implication for drug pressure.
articles.details: Meta-analysis of 15 studies (3,522 malaria patients) found 24.0% (95% CI 16.0%-34.0%) with pre-treatment antimalarial drug levels. Regional variation: Africa 31.0% (95% CI 19.0%-44.0%) vs Asia 11.0% (95% CI 5.0%-18.0%). By drug type: chloroquine 55.0%, lumefantrine 34.0%, mefloquine 10.0%. Substantial pre-treatment drug levels indicate drug pressure promoting antimalarial resistance.

articles.headline: Severe P. falciparum malaria with complications shows recovery
articles.title_original: Severe falciparum malaria remains a threat in East Kalimantan: A case report.
articles.details: Case report of a 31-year-old male with severe P. falciparum malaria presenting with acute kidney injury, impaired consciousness, jaundice, liver dysfunction, high parasitemia, and severe thrombocytopenia. Treated with artesunate injection and supportive care. Despite poor initial prognosis, the patient achieved full recovery. Early diagnosis and prompt treatment were identified as critical for outcome.

articles.headline: miR-21-5p and miR-3158-3p regulate cerebral malaria pathways
articles.title_original: Functional evidence of hsa-miR-21-5p and hsa-miR-3158-3p in regulating immune pathways relevant to cerebral malaria.
articles.details: Cell line transfection study (MCF7, HepG2) with synthetic miRNA mimics assessed immune gene expression via RT-qPCR after 48 hours. hsa-miR-3158-3p significantly downregulated NF-κB (p<0.05). hsa-miR-21-5p significantly reduced NF-κB, IL-10, and TLR4 (p<0.05). Authors propose these miRNAs may regulate immune pathways implicated in cerebral malaria pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Correction to IPTp acceptability study in Senegal
articles.title_original: Correction: Initiating community intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy in Senegal: qualitative evaluation of stakeholder acceptability and experience.
articles.details: Correction to previously published article on community intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy in Senegal regarding stakeholder acceptability and experience.

articles.headline: PfCyRPA vaccine antigen shows high conservation in Senegal
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity in the Plasmodium falciparum next-generation blood stage vaccine candidate antigen PfCyRPA in Senegal.
articles.details: Deep amplicon sequencing of 93 P. falciparum isolates from Kédougou, Senegal identified 15 SNPs in the PfCyRPA vaccine candidate antigen, with only 5 previously reported. The 3D7 reference allele was predominant. SNP population prevalence ranged from 1.1% to 9.7% (V292F most prevalent). Structure-based modeling predicted most SNPs had minor effects on antibodies, while others altered protein function or PfRH5 interaction.

articles.headline: House sparrows show higher avian Plasmodium prevalence
articles.title_original: Avian malaria and invasion success: Contrasting infection patterns in two introduced Eurasian sparrows in North America (Passer domesticus and Passer montanus).
articles.authors: Trigueros AC, Robertson BQ, Parker PG
articles.details: Sampling of 306 sparrows at 8 sites in Missouri–Illinois (2019-2022) compared avian Plasmodium infection between house sparrows and Eurasian tree sparrows. House sparrows had higher Plasmodium prevalence (24.1%, 95% CI 17.6%-32.0%) vs tree sparrows (11.6%, 95% CI 7.6%-17.2%). Host species was strongest predictor of infection; house sparrows had approximately two-fold higher relative parasitemia. Three lineages were shared, one unique per species.

articles.headline: Host heme metabolism strategy restores artemisinin efficacy
articles.title_original: Targeting Infected Host Cell Heme Metabolism to Kill Malaria Parasites.
articles.details: In vitro study combining proteomics and culture assays demonstrated that 5-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) plus dihydroartemisinin (DHA) synergistically enhanced parasite killing. ALA + DHA achieved complete clearance of artemisinin-resistant P. falciparum isolates from the Greater Mekong Subregion with no recrudescence over three weeks. The strategy exploits selective increased membrane permeability of infected RBCs to enable porphyrin accumulation, augmenting DHA-induced ROS and parasite killing.
articles.notable_reason: Resistance-bypassing strategy restores artemisinin efficacy

articles.headline: AP2-G mutation blocks sexual differentiation in malaria parasites
articles.title_original: A single valine to leucine switch disrupts Plasmodium falciparum AP2-G DNA binding and reveals GDV1's role in ap2-g activation.
articles.details: Discovery of V2163L mutation in AP2-G transcription factor required for P. falciparum gametocytogenesis; mutation prevents binding to ap2-g consensus motif GnGTAC and blocks gene transcription. GDV1-dependent expression of mutant AP2-G demonstrates GDV1's critical role in initial ap2-g activation. V2163 required for MSRP1 expression, serving as marker for early sexually committed schizonts.

articles.headline: Climate, rainfall, and policy shape malaria transmission patterns
articles.title_original: Climatic and governance determinants of malaria transmission in Rivers State, Nigeria.
articles.details: Time series analysis of malaria incidence in Rivers State, Nigeria (2007–2021) using Poisson, quasi-Poisson, and SARIMAX models. SARIMAX(1,1,0)(1,1,1)₁₂ provided best predictive performance, revealing monthly incidence significantly influenced by seasonality, rainfall, and government malaria-control policy periods. Findings support integrating climatic and governance indicators into surveillance and early-warning systems.

articles.headline: S1P receptor expression elevated in fatal cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Expression of sphingosine-1-phosphate receptor 1 in the brain of fatal cerebral malaria.
articles.details: Immunohistochemistry study of archived brain tissues from fatal P. falciparum cerebral malaria (CM) patients. S1PR1 expression intense in cerebral blood vessels and neurons of CM vs control and non-CM groups (all p<0.001). Blood S1P levels decreased in CM and negatively correlated with S1PR1 expression. S1P/S1PR1 signaling pathway implicated in CM pathogenesis, suggesting potential therapeutic targets.

articles.headline: Four Anopheles species identified in Guyana-Venezuela endemic regions
articles.title_original: Anopheles species complexes from Guyana and Venezuela malaria endemic areas using COI sequences.
articles.details: Molecular taxonomy using mtCOI sequencing of Anopheles from malaria-endemic Guyana and Venezuela identified An. albitarsis F, An. goeldii, An. oswaldoi A, and An. oswaldoi B. An. goeldii reported for first time from Guyana and Venezuela; An. oswaldoi A first from Venezuela. An. albitarsis F confirmed as widely distributed important vector in the region.

articles.headline: Afghanistan unlikely to achieve malaria elimination by 2035
articles.title_original: Malaria time series analysis, forecasting, and development of curated intervention strategies for the elimination of malaria from Afghanistan.
articles.details: Time series analysis of malaria in Afghanistan using district 2018–2023 data and national 1990–2023 data. 2018–2023 test positivity rate 15.2%; 96.5% P. vivax, 3.2% P. falciparum; mean annual cases 153,295. Districts along Pakistan border hosted majority of infections. Forecasting analysis under current interventions suggests elimination by 2035 unlikely; 43% of districts ready for sub-national elimination requiring context-specific vector control strategies.

articles.headline: C-terminal CSP antibodies correlate with RTS,S vaccine protection
articles.title_original: Vaccine-Induced Antibodies to a C-Terminal Plasmodium falciparum Circumsporozoite Protein Epitope Associated With Protection Against Malaria Challenge.
articles.details: Sera analysis from controlled human malaria infection trials of rCSP and RTS,S vaccines using peptide arrays. Protected RTS,S vaccinees had broader and more intense antibody responses to junctional, central repeat, and C-terminal CSP regions than unprotected groups and rCSP recipients. Protected group showed higher IgA breadth to C-terminal epitope PNDPNRNV and similar sequences. Findings suggest protection-associated cross-reactive vaccine-induced response providing benefit beyond vaccine-included peptides.

articles.headline: Elevated IL-6 associated with vaccine adverse events
articles.title_original: Cytokines Associated With Moderate and Severe Adverse Events During a Sporozoite Malaria Vaccine Trial With Controlled Human Malaria Infection.
articles.details: Malaria vaccine trial with higher-than-expected adverse event rate with frequent sampling of cytokine profiles. IL-6 elevated on days individuals experienced moderate and severe adverse events. Findings highlight need for further investigation of immune responses correlating with systemic reactogenicity to identify biomarkers and accelerate vaccine development.

articles.headline: CCR5Δ32 allele rare in Amazon malaria-endemic populations
articles.title_original: Updating the frequency of CCR5Δ32 in Brazil: Descriptive analysis of malaria cases and controls from Acre State, Amazon region.
articles.details: Population genetics study of CCR5Δ32 allele (prevents/reduces CCR5 protein expression) in Acre State, Brazilian Amazon. Allele frequency 2.88% in P. vivax-infected women vs 1.04% in uninfected women. Low frequency in Acre compared to other Brazilian regions reflects particular ancestry patterns in Amazonian populations influenced by historical European colonization.

articles.headline: Nigeria shows chloroquine sensitivity, persistent antifolate resistance
articles.title_original: Molecular Surveillance of Malaria in Nigeria Reveals a Low Frequency of Antimalarial Resistance Markers and Clonal Expansion of Chloroquine-Sensitive Infections.
articles.details: Targeted amplicon sequencing of 895 P. falciparum isolates from six Nigerian geopolitical zones (mostly 2017–2021). Wild-type Pfcrt alleles at codons 74–76 observed in 55.1%; chloroquine-resistant CVIET haplotype persisted in 40.9%. Sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance alleles detected in >50% of infections; Pfdhps A437G in 87.3%. Most infections wild-type Pfkelch13, with 12 nonsynonymous propeller domain mutations detected.

articles.headline: Sea surface temperature predicts Amazon malaria months ahead
articles.title_original: A Machine Learning-Based Dynamic SST Index for Long-Lead Malaria Prediction in the Peruvian Amazon.
articles.details: Machine learning methodology leveraging tropical SST variability for Peruvian Amazon malaria prediction. Self-organizing map identified dynamic SST index with superior performance (higher correlation, lower RMSE) compared to traditional ENSO index with lead times exceeding 3 months. Dynamic SST index captures ENSO life cycle evolution and influences malaria by altering local near-surface air temperature and specific humidity.

articles.headline: NLM malaria screening app shows poor diagnostic accuracy
articles.title_original: Diagnostic Accuracy of the National Library of Medicine (NLM) Screener Application in Screening for Malaria Parasites Among Blood Donors at the Korle-Bu Blood Bank, Ghana: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.details: Cross-sectional diagnostic accuracy study of 300 blood donors at Korle-Bu Teaching Hospital, Ghana. PCR identified P. falciparum in 18/300 (6.0%). NLM screener app showed sensitivity 38.9%, specificity 60.6%, PPV 5.9%, NPV 94.0%, κ=−0.001. Applied to donor pool, app would cause 111 unnecessary discards and 11 missed infections, requiring substantial improvement before clinical use in transfusion safety.

articles.headline: Apicortin protein dispensable for parasite survival and development
articles.title_original: Apicortin defines the Plasmodium apical conoid body but is dispensable for the parasite life cycle.
