articles.details: Researchers systematically tested 15 insect-specific viruses to determine whether they might reduce P. falciparum transmission through Anopheles mosquitoes. Six viruses (Flock House, Wallerfield, Agua Salud alphavirus, Herbert herbevirus, Gouléako goukovirus, and invertebrate iridescent virus 6) were selected based on efficient replication in anopheline cells and in vivo in A. stephensi. In coinfection experiments with four non-lethal viruses, neither sporozoite formation nor P. falciparum infection rates differed between virus-infected and control mosquitoes, indicating these viruses do not suppress parasite development.

articles.headline: Framework for assessing malaria surveillance post-elimination capacity
articles.title_original: Building a tool to assess malaria surveillance and response capacity in malaria post-elimination contexts: a modified and dual-weighted Delphi approach.
articles.details: Two systematic reviews and expert consultations (30 epidemiologists and disease control specialists) using a modified two-round Delphi process developed a standardized framework with 34 indicators across 6 domains for assessing malaria surveillance and response capacity in post-elimination settings. Using combined analytic hierarchy process (AHP) and entropy methods, key indicator weights were: surveillance system coverage and performance (0.240), data quality and use (0.3710), information management system (0.0973), resources (0.0375), early diagnosis and treatment (0.1571), and supervision/training (0.0973). Expert authority coefficients were 0.777 and 0.895 in rounds 1 and 2. The framework provides a standardized tool for countries to monitor and improve malaria surveillance systems to sustain elimination.

articles.headline: Novel cytochrome c variant with anomalously low redox potential in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: A Divergent Cytochrome c in Malaria Parasites with an Anomalously Low Redox Potential.
articles.details: Researchers identified a divergent cytochrome c homolog (Pfcyt c-2) in Plasmodium falciparum malaria parasites with pentacoordinate heme structure, unlike the hexacoordinate heme of canonical cytochrome c. Pfcyt c-2 has a redox potential of -278 mV, over 500 mV lower than canonical cytochrome c homologs (+250 mV). The protein shows canonical thermostability and low-level peroxidase activity despite structural heterogeneity. This discovery reveals a new clade of eukaryotic cytochrome c variants with divergent biochemical properties, opening scaffolds for mechanistic discovery and redox engineering.

articles.headline: Bioline mRDT shows high diagnostic accuracy versus microscopy in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Prevalence of malaria and diagnostic performance of malaria rapid diagnostic tests compared to microscopy among febrile patients at Pawe General Hospital, Northwest Ethiopia.
articles.details: A hospital-based cross-sectional study of 422 febrile patients at Pawe General Hospital, Northwest Ethiopia (November-December 2022) evaluated the Bioline™ Malaria Ag Pf/Pv mRDT against Giemsa microscopy. Overall malaria prevalence was 27.0% (114/422): Plasmodium falciparum 84.2%, P. vivax 7.9%, mixed infections 7.9%. The mRDT achieved 99.1% sensitivity, 99.3% specificity, 98.3% positive predictive value, and 99.7% negative predictive value, with near-perfect agreement with microscopy (κ = 0.98). Males were nearly twice as likely to be infected (AOR 1.88; 95% CI 1.16-3.04; p=0.011); households with ≥5 family members had over 2× higher odds (AOR 2.19; 95% CI 1.36-3.54; p=0.001).

articles.headline: Bradford assay validated for R21 malaria vaccine protein quantification
articles.title_original: Systematic Development and Validation of a Bradford-Based Protein Quantification Method for Novel Multi-Dose R21 Malaria Vaccine Formulated with 2-Phenoxy Ethanol (2-PE).
articles.details: A Bradford-based assay was developed and validated for quantifying R21 antigen in multi-dose vaccine formulations containing Matrix-M1 adjuvant and 2-phenoxyethanol preservative. The method achieved a limit of quantitation of 2 µg/mL and demonstrated specificity, accuracy, precision, and repeatability per ICH Q2(R2) guidelines with comparable performance to ELISA. The assay was sensitive enough to detect unintended R21 adsorption to vial surfaces. Protein content quantified by the Bradford method demonstrated robust in vivo immunogenicity in both release and stability studies, establishing a benchmark for quality control of virus-like particle vaccine formulations.

articles.headline: P. vivax and mixed infections challenge Cambodia malaria elimination
articles.title_original: Assessing progress and challenges towards malaria elimination in Kampong Speu, Cambodia: analysis of Plasmodium vivax and mixed infections, 2019-2023.
articles.details: Epidemiological analysis of malaria cases in Kampong Speu Province, Cambodia (2019-2023) from the national Malaria Information System and radical cure treatment records showed substantial progress toward elimination. Annual parasite incidence dropped from 23.8 per 1000 at-risk individuals in 2019 to 0.7 per 1000 in 2023. Radical cure treatment completion rates improved from 78% in 2019 to 98% in 2023. However, males aged 15-49 years, particularly forest-goers in high-risk operational districts, remain the most vulnerable group. Enhanced prevention, increased screening, and 100% radical cure treatment for eligible high-risk populations are recommended.

articles.headline: Common procedural errors reduce malaria RDT accuracy in low parasitaemia
articles.title_original: The effects of introduced procedural errors on malaria rapid diagnostic test performance in a laboratory setting.
articles.authors: Wilson S, Ah Y, Aidoo M
articles.details: Six RDT products were tested against cultured Plasmodium falciparum across parasitaemia ranges with intentional deviations from manufacturer instructions. Increasing sample volume beyond recommended 5 µL impaired performance: 4 of 6 products showed 83.3% (30/36) invalid results due to faulty sample migration. Shortest incubation period (5 minutes) caused most deviations from baseline, while longer periods aligned better. Insufficient buffer volumes caused ≥1 invalid outcome in >50% of products; exceeding buffer reduced invalid results. Higher parasitaemia (1,000/µL) with 5 µL sample achieved 100% agreement with baseline for three products regardless of incubation time and buffer variation.

articles.headline: Housing modification costs $50–116 per house but affordability limits scale-up
articles.title_original: Housing modification to prevent malaria in Uganda: an analysis of costs, willingness to pay, and equity.
articles.details: Economic analysis of screening and eave tube installation in approximately 4,000 homes in Eastern Region, Uganda assessed costs and household willingness to pay (WTP). Screening cost a mean of $116 per house (societal economic; 95% CI $112–120) and eave tubes $50 (95% CI $48–52). Annualized over 5 years: screening $4.22 per person protected per year; eave tubes $3.03. Installation costs were higher in wealthiest versus poorest quintiles (screening $151 vs $69; eave tubes $95 vs $31). Over 75% of respondents were willing to pay, but actual values represented only small fractions of costs (screening 12% vs 7%; eave tubes 18% vs 14% in wealthiest vs poorest quintiles). Equitable scale-up requires additional financing.

articles.headline: Low malaria prevalence in HIV patients on ART and co-trimoxazole
articles.title_original: Characterization of Plasmodium falciparum infections among people living with HIV under antiretroviral and co-trimoxazole chemoprophylaxis in Brazzaville, Republic of Congo.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 683 people living with HIV on antiretroviral therapy and co-trimoxazole prophylaxis in Brazzaville, Republic of Congo (January–September 2022) found low malaria infection rates. By microscopy, 1.2% (8/683) were infected; 9.2% (62/675) carried submicroscopic infections by nested PCR. Plasmodium falciparum mono-infection was most common (98.6%); mean multiplicity of infection was 2.1 ± 1.09 with 68.1% polyclonal. High prevalence of resistance mutations: Pfdhfr 51I (78.2%), 59R (84.2%), 108N (84.0%); Pfdhps 437G (72.0%), 540E (30.8%), 581G (48.5%); Pfcrt 76T (46.9%); Pfmdr1 184F (42.1%). No known artemisinin partial resistance mutations were detected.

articles.headline: IFN-γ and IL-4 correlate with malaria vaccine-induced protection
articles.title_original: IFN-γ and IL-4 correlate with protection induced by an attenuated malaria vaccine.
articles.details: Controlled human malaria infection studies after immunization with radiation-attenuated Plasmodium falciparum sporozoites identified immune correlates of protection. Baseline immune response markers correlating with protection included IFN-γ secretion and HLA-DR high expression. Vaccine-induced immune factors correlating with protection included IL-4 secretion and CD57− γδ T cell frequency.

articles.headline: Substantial asymptomatic P. falciparum burden in rural Gabon adults
articles.title_original: Burden and determinants of asymptomatic malaria among adults living in urban and rural areas in Gabon in 2023: a community-based cross-sectional study.
articles.details: Community-based cross-sectional survey in Bitam (rural), Libreville, and Owendo (urban), Gabon (January–December 2023) among 1,496 asymptomatic adults ≥18 years without recent antimalarial treatment found overall asymptomatic Plasmodium falciparum prevalence of 15.3%, significantly higher in rural areas (22.4%) versus urban settings (4.1%; p<0.01). Independent risk factors included rural residence (aOR 7.1; 95% CI 4.4–9.8), being a worker (aOR 5.2; 95% CI 3.4–7.8), and attending school. ITN use was low (32.7%) and not significantly protective. Findings highlight substantial hidden malaria reservoir in adults, particularly in rural areas, requiring broadened control strategies.

articles.headline: Integrated malaria control linking vectors and community interventions Madagascar
articles.title_original: Integrated Malaria Control in Madagascar: From Vector Dynamics to Community Interventions.
articles.details: No abstract provided; based on title, study examines integrated malaria control approaches in Madagascar that connect vector dynamics to community-level interventions for malaria prevention and control.

articles.headline: Malaria and Chagas disease seroprevalence screening in Italian blood donors
articles.title_original: Prevention of Transfusion-Transmitted Malaria and Chagas Disease in Non-Endemic Countries: An 8-Year Study of Seroprevalence Among Donors at Risk in Tuscany (Central Italy).
articles.details: Eight-year study (2016–2023) of serological screening for transfusion-transmitted malaria and Chagas disease among at-risk blood donors in Tuscany, Italy. Among 3,754 donors tested for anti-Plasmodium antibodies, seroprevalence was 6.8% (95% CI 6.1–7.7%), higher in Sub-Saharan Africa-origin donors (42.9%) and Southeast Asia-origin donors (10.6%). Seroprevalence was 8.2% with DRG ELISA versus 4.8% with Euroimmun ELISA (95% CI 3.8–5.9%). None of 20 seropositive donors tested positive for Plasmodium DNA by LAMP. Among 4,285 tested for anti-Trypanosoma cruzi antibodies, seroprevalence was 0.7% (95% CI 0.5–1.1%); no confirmed chronic Chagas disease cases were identified.

articles.headline: Artemisinin resistance spreads rapidly in East African malaria hotspots
articles.title_original: Mapping the prevalence of molecular markers of Plasmodium falciparum artemisinin partial resistance in Africa: a spatial-temporal modelling study.
articles.details: Spatial-temporal modeling of 182,071 genotyped samples from 3,806 surveys revealed distinct kelch13 (k13) mutations driving artemisinin partial resistance (ART-R) in East Africa: k13 561H in Rwanda, k13 675V in Uganda, and k13 622I in Ethiopia-Eritrea. Kelch13 prevalence increased 3.49% annually in Rwanda and 1.81% annually in Uganda, reaching 39.44% and 26.36% respectively by 2024. This rapid expansion of ART-R into entrenched regional hotspots mirrors early patterns preceding artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT) failure in Southeast Asia.
articles.notable_reason: Rapid multi-centric artemisinin resistance threatens ACT efficacy in Africa

articles.headline: Widespread insecticide resistance documented in Indian malaria vectors
articles.title_original: Spatio-temporal trends of insecticide resistance in Indian malaria vectors (2000-2024): a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.authors: Mullick R, Yadav CP, Singhal R
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 51 studies (2000-2024) found dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane (DDT) resistance across almost all Indian states, with Anopheles culicifacies showing very low susceptibility. Deltamethrin resistance was noted mainly in Chhattisgarh and Madhya Pradesh with average percent mortality of 89% in A. culicifacies, 93% in A. stephensi, and 100% in A. fluviatilis. Malathion resistance was high in A. culicifacies and A. stephensi but complete susceptibility observed in A. fluviatilis. North-eastern vectors remained susceptible, suggesting opportunities for targeted intervention.

articles.headline: COVID-19 pandemic disrupted HIV, TB, and childhood vaccine services in Guinea
articles.title_original: The impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on HIV, malaria, tuberculosis and childhood vaccination services in Guinea: an interrupted time series analysis.
articles.details: Interrupted time series analysis of national surveillance data (2018-2022) in Guinea using Poisson segmented regression found that malaria indicators were generally maintained during COVID-19, with inpatient malaria cases declining 0.8% monthly (incidence rate ratio [IRR] 0.992). However, HIV testing in infants (IRR 0.920), childhood pentavalent vaccination (IRR 0.940), and TB indicators were heavily impacted, with TB total cases declining 15% immediately (IRR 0.850) and TB therapeutic success increasing 0.9% monthly (IRR 1.009) during pandemic.

articles.headline: Metabolic reprogramming and microbiota control P. vivax infection in Anopheles darlingi
articles.title_original: Metabolic reprogramming and gut microbiota ecology drive divergent Plasmodium vivax infection outcomes in Anopheles darlingi.
articles.details: Experimental infections of the Amazon malaria vector Anopheles darlingi with field-derived Plasmodium vivax produced two distinct phenotypes (low and high oocyst burdens). Transcriptomic analysis revealed low-infection mosquitoes mounted sustained responses including activation of detoxification pathways, redox regulation, and aromatic amino acid catabolism, accompanied by reduced bacterial diversity and enrichment of anti-Plasmodium bacteria (Enterobacteriales, Pseudomonadales). High-infection mosquitoes showed limited metabolic reprogramming, expansion of Flavobacteriales, and permissive physiological states. These findings identify metabolic and microbial targets for transmission-blocking strategies.
articles.notable_reason: Novel mechanism linking metabolism, microbiota to vector competence

articles.headline: Bacterial microbiota composition differs between infected and uninfected Anopheles gambiae
articles.title_original: Microbiota diversity of Anopheles gambiae in Bankeng, southern Cameroon, and its association with Plasmodium falciparum infection.
articles.details: Survey of 120 Anopheles gambiae from Cameroon (60 naturally infected with Plasmodium falciparum, 60 uninfected) using 16S ribosomal RNA sequencing detected 99 bacterial taxa, with 97 shared between both groups. Differential abundance analysis revealed 14 bacterial genera more abundant in uninfected mosquitoes and only 2 more abundant in infected ones. Bacterial diversity was significantly higher in uninfected samples during wet season and infected samples during dry season, suggesting associations with infection status though causality cannot be determined.

articles.headline: Engineered bacteria persist in mosquito habitats for paratransgenesis applications
articles.title_original: Environmental persistence and transmission dynamics of Serratia AS1 in mosquito habitats: advancing paratransgenesis for malaria control.
articles.details: Semi-field evaluation of Serratia AS1-mCherry in Anopheles stephensi habitats in Iran demonstrated successful midgut and ovary colonization with persistence in larval breeding water for 14 days and on sugar-soaked cotton pads for 4-6 days. The bacteria transmitted transstadially, venerally, vertically, and through larval habitat acquisition (sipping). Water-based delivery effectively disseminated Serratia AS1 among mosquito populations. These results establish feasibility of using engineered Serratia AS1 with anti-pathogen effector molecules for field-based paratransgenesis malaria control.

articles.headline: Severe malaria complicated by symmetrical peripheral gangrene in adolescent
articles.title_original: Symmetrical lower extremity gangrene in a patient with severe malaria: a case report and literature review.
articles.details: Case report of 14-year-old Ethiopian girl with severe Plasmodium falciparum malaria presenting with fever, vomiting, and diarrhea who developed symmetrical peripheral gangrene (bilateral ischemic damage) of lower limbs despite prompt antimalarial therapy. Clinical examination revealed stable vital signs, preserved organ function, and palpable peripheral pulses. Management was conservative with continued antimalarial treatment, highlighting the importance of early recognition of this rare complication.

articles.headline: Evolutionary framework proposed for P. falciparum population genomic inference
articles.title_original: Towards an evolutionary baseline model of Plasmodium falciparum for population-genomic inference.
articles.details: Commentary discussing the need for a baseline evolutionary model of Plasmodium falciparum that jointly accounts for multiple concurrent processes in natural populations. With access to tens of thousands of genomes through projects like MalariaGEN, population genetics approaches can illuminate selective pressures and demographic history. The authors argue for developing an appropriate null model to improve accuracy of evolutionary parameter inference and identify new drug resistance gene candidates, which would guide targeted molecular surveillance and drug development strategies.

articles.headline: Suboptimal B cell responses explain waning malaria vaccine protective immunity
articles.title_original: Dysfunctional memory B cell responses to the protective repeat region of the Plasmodium circumsporozoite protein are associated with waning humoral immunity.
articles.details: Cross-species analysis of B cell responses to the circumsporozoite protein (CSP) repeat region after Plasmodium infection or immunization revealed two factors predominantly impairing protective memory B cell differentiation: inhibition of germinal center formation by infection and skewed B cell differentiation due to the repetitive structure of the protective CSP region. CSP-based vaccines display limited durability in malaria-naïve individuals yet are even less effective in malaria-experienced individuals, suggesting generation of non-optimal humoral immunity. These findings mechanistically explain CSP vaccine waning.
articles.notable_reason: Explains mechanisms of malaria vaccine waning, guides vaccine improvement

articles.headline: Biliverdin reductase catalytic activity essential for protective jaundice in malaria
articles.title_original: Biliverdin Reductase Catalytic Activity Is Essential for Malaria Resistance.
articles.details: Experimental studies using biliverdin reductase A (BVRA) mutant mice with missense mutations in the NAD(P)H-binding domain (G17A) and reductase motif (E97A) demonstrated that both mutants succumbed to malaria while wild-type mice survived. The protective antimalarial effect depended entirely on BVRA catalytic activity through bilirubin production; mutant strains failed to reach the threshold of circulating unconjugated bilirubin required for parasite control. These findings establish jaundice's enzymatic basis and define a minimal bilirubin concentration threshold for malaria protection.
articles.notable_reason: Identifies enzymatic basis of jaundice-mediated malaria protection

articles.headline: Rosette formation prevents splenic clearance rather than enhancing adhesion
articles.title_original: Rosette margination in blood flow during malaria pathogenesis.
articles.details: Mesoscopic hydrodynamic simulations and microfluidic experiments investigating rosette margination in blood flow found that rosette margination is significantly weaker than margination of single infected red blood cells (iRBCs), contrary to expectations. Poor margination of rosettes arises from the deformability and dynamics of multicellular clusters formed by several healthy RBCs around one iRBC. These results suggest the primary function of rosette formation in malaria is not to enhance cytoadhesion to the endothelium but rather to keep infected RBCs in circulation and avoid splenic clearance.
articles.notable_reason: Reframes understanding of rosette formation function in malaria

articles.headline: High-dose primaquine superior for P. vivax radical cure in children
articles.title_original: Safety, tolerability, and efficacy of high versus low-dose, short versus long-course daily primaquine for the radical cure of uncomplicated Plasmodium vivax malaria in children under 15 years of age: an open-label, non-inferiority, randomized controlled trial (CHILDPRIM).
articles.details: CHILDPRIM randomized controlled trial enrolled 100 children with uncomplicated Plasmodium vivax malaria in Brazil and compared safety, tolerability, and efficacy of three primaquine (PQ) regimens: standard 3.5 mg/kg over 7 days, high-dose 7.0 mg/kg over 14 days, and high-dose 7.0 mg/kg over 7 days. Kaplan-Meier estimates for recurrence-free survival at day 180 were 50% (standard), 82.3% (high-dose 14d), and 79.4% (high-dose 7d) with significant difference (log-rank p=0.0065). High-dose regimens showed more adverse events (methaemoglobinaemia, anaemia, gastrointestinal symptoms) but no serious adverse events, normalizing by day 28.
articles.notable_reason: RCT demonstrates high-dose primaquine superior with comparable safety

articles.headline: Plasmodium vivax Duffy binding protein shows widespread positive selection
articles.title_original: Complete gene sequence diversity, amplification, and natural selection of the Plasmodium vivax Duffy binding protein locus.
articles.details: Analyzed 232 P. vivax isolates from Thailand for complete PvDBP gene sequence diversity and copy number variation. Pvdbp duplication (Cambodian type) detected in 9.48% of isolates (22/232), with nucleotide diversity highest in PvdbpII. Positive selection occurred in the 5' region and PvdbpII, with 26 positively selected codons identified, 22 overlapping predicted B and/or T cell epitopes. Authors conclude positive selection is broadly distributed and likely driven by host immune pressure, with potential implications for vaccine design.

articles.headline: Plasmodium knowlesi gametocytes detected in two-thirds of infected patients
articles.title_original: Plasmodium knowlesi gametocyte carriage and load among malaria patients at Kapit Hospital in Sarawak, Malaysian Borneo.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 295 patients with PCR-confirmed P. knowlesi infection measured gametocyte prevalence via pks25 mRNA. Viable gametocytes present in 67.5% (199/295) of patients, with positive correlation between gametocyte load and total parasitaemia (ρ = 0.32, p = 0.01). Among gametocyte-positive patients, 12.5% (25/199) had elevated levels, including 6% with >100,000 pks25 copies/µL. Findings demonstrate potential for human-to-human transmission via mosquitoes but do not constitute direct evidence of natural transmission.

articles.headline: Dam proximity increases malaria prevalence in Burkina Faso villages
articles.title_original: Epidemiological profile and environmental determinants of Plasmodium falciparum infection in villages surrounding the Soum Agropole, Burkina Faso.
articles.details: Monthly household surveys of 13,786 participants in five Burkinabe villages (March 2022–February 2023) assessed P. falciparum prevalence via blood microscopy. 12-month malaria prevalence was 39.6% (95% CI 38.79–40.42), with highest prevalence (42.1%) in Soum village near the dam and lowest (33.2%) in Séguédin far from the dam. Ages 5–19 showed highest infection rates (43.6–69.4%). Monthly rainfall unexpectedly associated with decreased malaria risk (OR = 0.77, 95% CI 0.66–0.9). Authors conclude dam construction increases malaria risk and recommend integrated health and water management approaches.

articles.headline: Wnt pathway inhibition improves Plasmodium falciparum liver stage development
articles.title_original: Inhibition of Wnt signaling in primary human hepatocytes promotes Plasmodium falciparum liver stage development.
articles.details: In vitro study cultured primary human hepatocytes with signal transduction inhibitors to maintain P. falciparum permissiveness and analyzed transcriptomic profiles of developing liver stages. Inhibition of the Wnt pathway significantly improved size and maturity of developing P. falciparum liver stage schizonts while retaining hepatocyte metabolic activity and epithelial characteristics. The improved in vitro model may facilitate identification and development of novel therapeutic strategies targeting P. falciparum liver stages.

articles.headline: RTS,S vaccine awareness varies between urban and rural Cameroon
articles.title_original: Awareness, perception and influences on uptake of the RTS,S/AS01 malaria vaccine among caregivers for children under 5 years in South West region, Cameroon.
articles.details: Qualitative exploratory study with focus group discussions involving 20 caregivers of children aged 11–30 months in urban and rural South West Cameroon assessed malaria vaccine awareness and perceptions. Urban caregivers showed high but limited understanding; rural caregivers had uneven awareness but strong trust in health workers. Barriers included communication gaps, misinformation, gender dynamics, long waits, and facility distance. Caregivers recommended strengthening community-based communication through churches, town criers, health talks, outreach, and visual materials.

articles.headline: MRI identifies deep gray matter damage in fulminant cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Radiological findings in fulminant cerebral malaria: a rare neuroimaging presentation.
articles.details: Case report of 54-year-old woman with P. falciparum-confirmed cerebral malaria presenting with fever, seizures, shock, and multiorgan failure. CT brain was normal, but MRI revealed bilateral T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in basal ganglia, thalami, and white matter with patchy diffusion restriction suggesting cytotoxic edema. Despite artesunate therapy and organ support, the patient died of refractory multiorgan failure. Authors conclude that recognition of these MRI patterns aids early diagnosis and risk stratification in cerebral malaria.

articles.headline: Serratia bacteria isolated from malaria mosquitoes in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Molecular characterization of potential Plasmodium-Blocking Serratia spp. bacteria in field-caught malaria mosquito in Burkina Faso.
articles.details: Molecular characterization of bacteria isolated from field-caught Anopheles gambiae complex mosquitoes in three Burkinabe sites identified Serratia spp. via PCR targeting luxS gene and 16S rRNA sequencing. Overall prevalence of Serratia was 13.3% among malaria vectors, with prevalence varying by mosquito development stage, locality, and organ dissected. Only one Anopheles coluzzii mosquito was co-infected with Serratia and P. falciparum. Authors conclude that Serratia presence in wild mosquitoes supports its potential use in malaria control.

articles.headline: China's malaria elimination achieved through integrated surveillance and ecology
articles.title_original: China's malaria elimination: One Health lessons for vector-borne disease governance.
articles.authors: Liu J, Liu Y, Cheng G
articles.details: Perspective article interpreting China's WHO-certified malaria elimination (achieved 2021) within a One Health governance framework, drawing on regional case studies in Hainan, Huai River Basin, and Yunnan Province. Identifies four pillars of success: integrated surveillance, regional cooperation, ecological intervention, and technological innovation. Authors emphasize how this pathway offers lessons for sustainable vector control governance and disease prevention amid ecological disruption and climate change.

articles.headline: Geohelminth-malaria co-infection rare but increased in third trimester
articles.title_original: Geohelminthiasis and Malaria Co-Infection in Pregnant Women in Kinshasa: Case of Lisungi and Lukunga Hospitals in Democratic Republic of the Congo.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 336 pregnant women in two Kinshasa hospitals assessed geohelminth-malaria co-infection via thick blood smears and stool examination. Geohelminth-malaria co-infection prevalence was 5.7%, with geohelminthiasis alone at 7.7%; Ascaris lumbricoides was most common (6.3%). Third trimester was significantly associated with increased co-infection risk (ORa = 5.8; 95% CI: 1.36–9.23; p = 0.0077). No significant association was found between co-infection and maternal anemia. Authors recommend systematic screening and integrated management during pregnancy.

articles.headline: Fluorescence assay detects artemisinin-resistant parasite mitochondrial changes
articles.title_original: Fluorescence-based bioassay for the detection of changes in mitochondrial physiology during artemisinin-induced growth arrest of blood-stage Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.details: In vitro study used pH-sensitive fluorochrome Mtphagy to monitor mitochondrial changes during artemisinin exposure in blood-stage P. falciparum. After dihydroartemisinin (DHA) treatment, Mtphagy fluorescence intensity increased in early ring-form trophozoites, indicating pH decrease. Inhibition of mitochondrial fission and PI3K pathway significantly reduced Mtphagy-positive parasites and delayed recrudescence in DHA-treated parasites. Authors conclude that targeting mitochondrial dynamics may offer a strategy for identifying, monitoring, and potentially overcoming artemisinin resistance.

articles.headline: Sudanese healthcare workers lack malaria vaccine knowledge and trust
articles.title_original: Preliminary insights into knowledge and attitudes toward malaria vaccination among Sudanese healthcare workers: an exploratory cross-sectional study.
articles.details: Cross-sectional online survey of 222 Sudanese healthcare workers (January–April 2025) assessed malaria vaccination knowledge and attitudes. Only 9% (20/222) demonstrated adequate knowledge (≥70%), and 21.6% (48/222) displayed positive attitudes. Most were unaware of WHO recommendations (71.2%) or the vaccine's target parasite (76.6%), with 78.4% (174/222) reporting negative attitudes regarding safety, affordability, and trust in authorities. Positive attitudes were more common among males, those with >5 years experience, and private sector workers (p<0.05). Authors conclude targeted training and awareness programs are urgently needed.

articles.headline: Brazilian intervention increased malaria detection but reduced hospitalizations
articles.title_original: What impact did the Brazilian Federal Government's intervention have on malaria cases in the Yanomami Indigenous lands?
articles.authors: de Aguiar Barros J, et al.
articles.details: Descriptive retrospective study analyzed malaria trends in Roraima, Brazil (2021–2024) before and after January 2023 federal government intervention in Yanomami Indigenous Lands. Among 117,214 indigenous cases, intervention was associated with increased case detection (52,210 to 65,004; p=0.0005), decreased hospitalizations (787 to 638; p=0.0304), and steady mortality (111 total; p=0.9089). P. vivax was dominant species. Post-intervention, case detection shifted from adults to vulnerable groups (0–19, 60+ years), and exported cases to other states dropped sharply (95.5–71.5%).

articles.headline: Anopheles species distribution varies across Burkina Faso climatic zones
articles.title_original: Spatial Distribution and Biodiversity of Anopheles Mosquito Species Across Climatic Zones in Burkina Faso: Implications for Malaria Vector Control.
articles.details: Survey of 30,521 Anopheles mosquitoes from 67 health districts across 13 Burkina Faso regions (Sept-Dec 2022) identified An. gambiae s.l. dominating at 94.4%. Molecular analysis of 2,026 An. gambiae s.l. showed marked geographic variation: An. coluzzii predominated in Sahelian (74.9%) and Sudano-Sahelian (71.2%) zones, while An. gambiae s.s. was most frequent in Soudanian zone (53.8%). Biodiversity decreased from humid southern to arid northern areas. Results highlight need for locally adapted vector control strategies.

articles.headline: Periostin has dual roles in malaria-infected cell phagocytosis
articles.title_original: Effect of periostin (OSF-2) on phagocytosis of Plasmodium-infected erythrocytes.
articles.details: In vitro study examined effects of periostin (OSF-2) on phagocytosis of P. falciparum and P. knowlesi-infected RBCs by THP-1 monocytes. OSF-2 increased IRBC phagocytosis when rosetting was prevented, via CD36-dependent mechanism. However, when rosetting was induced by adding uninfected RBCs, OSF-2 reduced phagocytosis rate. These findings highlight complex parasite-host interactions influencing infection pathogenesis.

articles.headline: Antimalarial drug resistance mutations detected across Western Kenya
articles.title_original: Comparative molecular surveillance of polymorphisms in chloroquine resistance transporter, multidrug resistance 1, and Kelch 13 genes associated with antimalarial drug resistance in Plasmodium falciparum isolates from Western Kenya.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study compared P. falciparum from lake-endemic Nyando (Kisumu) and highland Marani (Kisii) regions. Pfcrt mutations: M74I, N75E, K76T each 5.4%; Pfmdr1: N146I 0.9%, Y184F 48.2%, D1246Y 3.6%; Pfk13: S522C 0.9%, R561H 0.9%, A675V 12.8%. Kisii showed higher Pfcrt M74I/N75E/K76T (11.5%) and Pfk13 A675V (27.5%, p<0.05). Novel Pfmdr1 N146I and rare Pfk13 mutations emerged in Kisumu. Artemether-lumefantrine is Kenya's first-line treatment.

articles.headline: Rapid diagnostic test self-testing is feasible in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Feasibility of self-testing for acute malaria using rapid diagnostic tests in three peri-urban sub-Saharan African community settings.
articles.details: Feasibility study of RDT self-testing in 100 households each in Migori (Kenya, n=225), KwaZulu-Natal (South Africa, n=80), and Copperbelt (Zambia, n=163). Test positivity: 11.1% (Kenya), 0% (South Africa), 1.8% (Zambia). Participants interpreted RDTs with 100% sensitivity and 99.7% specificity; over 96% found RDTs easy to use; 92% (Kenya), 86% (Zambia), and 66% (South Africa) felt confident for future self-testing. RDT self-testing is highly acceptable and feasible in SSA.

articles.headline: Biometric fingerprinting improves malaria patient identification in resource-limited settings
articles.title_original: Enhancing unique identification of patients with malaria using biometric fingerprinting in Papua, Indonesia.
articles.details: Before-and-after study at Wania clinic, Timika, Papua (16 months: Phase 1 pre-intervention 3 mo, Phase 2 training 6 mo, Phase 3 consolidation 7 mo). Among 7,471 malaria patients (Phase 1: 1,487; Phase 2: 3,228; Phase 3: 2,756), proportion linked to pre-existing card increased from 44.4% (Phase 1) to 48.9% (Phase 3), difference 4.5% (95% CI 1.4–7.7%). Among fingerprinted patients in Phase 3, 55.8% were linked (difference vs Phase 1: 11.4%, 95% CI 8.1–14.8%).

articles.headline: Poly-alpha microsatellite reveals high genetic diversity in Kenyan P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Characterizing Plasmodium falciparum genetic diversity and complexity of infections in clinical malaria infections in Western and Coastal Kenya using the poly-alpha microsatellite marker.
articles.details: Genotyping study of dried blood spots from Busia 2016 TES (n=93) and Kilifi 2020 monitoring (n=92) using poly-α microsatellite. Busia infections were 80% polyclonal with mean complexity of infection 2.9 (p<0.0001); Kilifi mostly monoclonal (52.5%) with mean COI 1.7. Both regions showed high genetic diversity: expected heterozygosity Busia 0.92, Kilifi 0.90; effective alleles Busia 10.8, Kilifi 9.3. Provides proof of concept for poly-α as molecular correction tool in therapeutic efficacy studies.

articles.headline: Optimized genome amplification enables faster malaria genomic surveillance
articles.title_original: Enhancing whole genome DNA amplification of Plasmodium falciparum for advanced molecular surveillance in malaria control.
articles.details: Study optimized selective whole genome amplification (SWGA) protocol for P. falciparum clinical samples from 13 returning travelers, testing incubation durations of 2, 8, 16 hours and 3 hours with EquiPhi29 polymerase. 8 and 16 hour durations showed comparable yield with >90% SNP concordance. 3 hour protocol achieved >63% P. falciparum genome coverage with 60% lower reagent costs. Drug resistance profiles remained consistent across conditions. Enables faster, cost-effective genomic surveillance.

articles.headline: Deep learning models achieve 98% accuracy for malaria blood smear diagnosis
articles.title_original: Trustworthy deep learning for malaria diagnosis using explainable artificial intelligence.
articles.details: Comparative evaluation of four CNNs and two hybrid architectures on 27,090 Kaggle malaria blood smear images. ResNet18 achieved F1-score 96.33%; Xception and Inception-ResNetV2 achieved approximately 98% accuracy on validation and test sets. Independent validation on Harvard Dataverse dataset with varied staining and imaging conditions confirmed 97–98% accuracy. Three explainable AI techniques (Grad-CAM, LIME, SHAP) provided model interpretability. AI-based diagnostic framework demonstrates high accuracy and generalization for resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Glycolysis regulates ribosomal RNA silencing in malaria parasites
articles.title_original: A metabolism-chromatin axis promotes differential ribosomal RNA transcription in the human malaria parasite.
articles.details: First characterization of epigenetic regulation of rRNA gene transcription in P. falciparum during host-to-vector transmission. rDNA silencing in human host relies on aerobic glycolysis; disruption of NAD+ regeneration during lactate fermentation promotes rDNA de-repression. Identified sirtuin Sir2a as mediator between fluctuating NAD+ levels and transcriptional outcome. rDNA activation couples to parasite metabolic state transition from aerobic glycolysis (human host) to mitochondrial respiration (mosquito vector).

articles.headline: Gut microbiome composition predicts malaria parasitemia risk in humans and primates
articles.title_original: Distinct gastrointestinal microbial signatures predict parasite levels in controlled Plasmodium infections in both rhesus macaques and humans.
articles.details: Experimental study examined associations between pre-infection gastrointestinal microbiome (16S rRNA sequencing) and acute Plasmodium parasitemia in rhesus macaques infected with P. fragile and controlled human malaria infection trial participants infected with P. falciparum. Identified distinct pre-infection microbial signatures associated with above-median parasitemia in both models. Bifidobacterium feature set accurately stratified parasitemia risk. Pre-infection microbiome-parasite dynamics in RM mirrored P. falciparum dynamics in humans, supporting RM model for microbiome-targeting interventions.

articles.headline: Histone lactylation identified as new epigenetic mark in malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Histone lactylation: A new epigenetic mark in the malaria parasite Plasmodium.
articles.details: First characterization of histone lactylation in Plasmodium, identified in P. falciparum, P. knowlesi, and rodent models P. yoelii and P. berghei. Histones were increasingly lactylated in response to elevated lactate levels in vitro and in vivo, positioning this as a metabolic sensor. Mass spectrometry showed multiple histone lysines and non-histone chromatin proteins could be lactylated. P. falciparum showed more abundant and inducible lactylation than P. knowlesi, suggesting species-specific adaptation.

articles.headline: Dengue virus co-infections detected in Niger River Basin, Nigeria febrile patients
articles.title_original: Dengue virus infection amongst malaria and typhoid fever suspected acute febrile patients in the Niger river basin of Nigeria.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 200 febrile patients from three Niger River Basin health facilities. Dengue serology: 38 (19%) had anti-DENV antibodies (IgG 13.5%, IgM 4.0%, NS1 0.5%). Malaria: 104 (52%); Typhoid: 125 (62.5%). Monoinfections: dengue 27 (13.5%), malaria 37 (18.5%), typhoid 58 (29%). Co-infections: malaria-typhoid 58 (29%), dengue-malaria 2 (1%), dengue-typhoid 2 (1%), triple 7 (4%). Risk factors for dengue: age 41–50, farming, married status, environmental factors.

articles.headline: Malaria in pregnancy: 13.4% prevalence; ITN use protective
articles.title_original: Prevalence and associated factors of malaria among pregnant women in Maraki Health Center, North West, Gondar, Ethiopia.
articles.details: Facility-based cross-sectional study of 271 pregnant women in Ethiopia found 13.4% malaria prevalence. Key risk factors: no ITN use (AOR 3.2; 95% CI 1.4-7.5), no IPTp-SP (AOR 2.8; 95% CI 1.1-6.9), <4 ANC visits (AOR 4.6; 95% CI 1.9-11.2), living near stagnant water (AOR 2.5; 95% CI 1.1-5.8), attending ANC in rainy season (AOR 1.9; 95% CI 1.0-3.7). Recommendations include strengthening ITN distribution and IPTp-SP uptake.

articles.headline: Protocol for identifying antigen-specific B cells in rodent malaria
articles.title_original: Protocol to identify and sort antigen-specific B cells from Plasmodium-infected mice.
articles.details: Describes protocol using SpyCage reagents to identify and sort B cells specific to MSP1-19 antigen in Plasmodium-infected mice. Steps include splenic single-cell suspension preparation, B cell enrichment, and staining for live cell sorting enabling downstream single-cell RNA, V(D)J, and ATAC sequencing applications.

articles.headline: Long-read sequencing protocol for P. falciparum antigenic variants
articles.title_original: Protocol for genomic surveillance of Plasmodium falciparum antigens using amplicon-based PacBio long-read sequencing.
articles.details: Protocol using amplicon-based PacBio long-read sequencing identifies and classifies structural variants of msp1, msp2, glurp, and csp in Plasmodium falciparum. Describes PCR barcoding, circular consensus sequencing, in silico variant calling, and Galaxy data analysis. Resolves full-length sequences to measure infection complexity, construct isolate phylogenies, and support vaccine design.

articles.headline: Forest data integration increases malaria surveillance detection three-fold
articles.title_original: Integrating forest data and health facility surveys to optimise risk-based malaria surveillance in the Philippines.
articles.details: Mixed-methods study integrated health facility surveys with environmental data in forested Philippines district to optimize malaria surveillance. Risk-based surveillance incorporating forest data increased probability of detecting malaria foci over three-fold compared to routine surveillance and was highly cost-effective. Routine diagnostic methods showed decreased sensitivity in forest environments.

articles.headline: Correction to Plasmodium falciparum immune response article
articles.title_original: Correction to "Immune Response and Evasion Mechanisms of Plasmodium falciparum Parasites".
articles.details: Erratum correcting previously published article (DOI: 10.1155/2018/6529681) on immune response and evasion mechanisms of Plasmodium falciparum parasites.

articles.headline: Hemozoin directly causes neuronal injury in cerebral malaria
articles.title_original: Hemozoin induces neuronal injury primarily characterized by axon rupture and mitochondrial damage in experimental cerebral malaria.
articles.details: Experimental study in PbA-infected mice and primary cortical neurons demonstrated that hemozoin internalizes into neuronal cytoplasm, causing axon rupture, mitochondrial structural damage, reduced mitochondrial membrane potential, diminished ATP, elevated ROS, upregulated nNOS, and decreased neurotransmitter levels. This is the first evidence that hemozoin directly adheres to and damages neurons, elucidating a potential mechanism underlying neuronal injury in cerebral malaria.
articles.notable_reason: First evidence of direct hemozoin-neuron interaction causing injury

articles.headline: Nigeria drug resistance: low artemisinin, high CQ/SP resistance
articles.title_original: Molecular surveillance of antimalarial drug resistance genes in Nigeria: a systematic review and roadmap to malaria elimination.
articles.details: Systematic review of 26 studies (2010-2024) on antimalarial resistance genes in Nigeria examined Pfcrt, Pfmdr, Pfdhfr, Pfdhps, and Pfk13. Found high prevalence of chloroquine and sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance mutations (especially Pfcrt and Pfdhfr) with regional variation—lower in northern Nigeria. Pfk13 artemisinin resistance mutations were less common. Suggests chloroquine may be reconsidered as ACT alternative only in consistently low-resistance areas pending clinical trials.

articles.headline: 2025 funding cuts threaten malaria control gains in Africa
articles.title_original: African leaders and regional institutions need to take the bull by the horns: a perspective on the impact of the 2025 funding cuts to malaria programmes.
articles.details: Commentary on significant 2025 funding reductions following US termination of global health programs and reduced development aid from multiple countries. Africa carries 94% of global malaria burden (263 million cases in 2023), incurring 1.3% annual GDP reduction. Key program activities impacted: bed net distribution, seasonal chemoprevention, indicator surveys. Calls for increased African domestic funding and public-private partnerships.

articles.headline: Geospatial modeling maps urban malaria risk 0–49% in Ghana
articles.title_original: Fine-scale spatial mapping of urban malaria prevalence for microstratification in an urban area of Ghana.
articles.details: Geospatial analysis combining environmental, climatic, and urban covariates with malaria prevalence data from 12,371 surveyed children (6 months–10 years) in Greater Accra Region, Ghana. Predicted malaria prevalence ranged 0–49%. Satellite-derived data on tasselled cap brightness, enhanced vegetation index, and urban covariates were predictive. Produced probability map showing areas exceeding 10% prevalence to guide targeted interventions.

articles.headline: Molecular testing reveals 1.7% submicroscopic malaria reservoir in Ecuador
articles.title_original: Asymptomatic parasitaemia in a malaria pre-elimination setting in the Putumayo area of the Ecuadorian Amazon basin.
articles.authors: van der Ende J, et al.
articles.details: Repeated cross-sectional survey of 293 asymptomatic individuals in four Putumayo communities (Ecuador–Colombia–Peru border) during 2023 rainy season used tiered diagnostics: RDT, microscopy, and qPCR. RDT detected 0/293; microscopy found 3 P. vivax infections (1.0%; 95% CI 0.2–2.4%); qPCR detected 5 cases (1.7%; 95% CI 0.6–3.9%), revealing 2 submicroscopic infections with very low densities (mean Cq 32.6). Highlights surveillance gap in Ecuador's elimination program.

articles.headline: Infected RBCs impair megakaryocyte differentiation and platelet production
articles.title_original: Interactions between Plasmodium falciparum-infected red blood cells and their extracellular vesicles with megakaryocytes: implications for platelet-like particle formation.
articles.details: In vitro study using MEG-01 cells co-cultured with P. falciparum-infected RBCs (pRBCs), normal RBCs, or extracellular vesicles. pRBCs suppressed megakaryocytic marker expression, downregulated NOTCH3, altered apoptosis/stress genes, and impaired platelet-like particle clotting activity while increasing IL-8, RANTES, and MCP-1. RBC-derived extracellular vesicles induced milder effects. Identifies defective platelet production as mechanism contributing to malaria-associated thrombocytopenia.

articles.headline: Burkitt's lymphoma declined 90% with reduced falciparum malaria in Kenya
articles.title_original: A "Familiar Foe Revisited": examining the relationship between endemic Burkitt's lymphoma and changing malaria admissions in the coastal region of Kenya.
articles.details: Retrospective analysis over 3 decades (1990–2020) of endemic Burkitt's lymphoma in coastal Kenya pediatric admissions. BL incidence declined: epoch 1990–1999 had 29 cases (cumulative incidence 93.1 per 100,000); 2000–2009 had 62 cases (130.3 per 100,000); 2010–2020 had 4 cases (10.2 per 100,000; p<0.0001). Median parasite density fell from 13,966 to 7,224 (p=0.0021). Correlation between BL and P. falciparum infection 0.53 (p=0.0024), suggesting malaria reduction accounts for BL decline.

articles.headline: Organoid models advance liver-stage malaria research
articles.title_original: Experimental models of liver-stage malaria: Progress, gaps, and challenges.
articles.authors: Nitaramorn N, Kulkeaw K, Imwong M
articles.details: Review examining experimental platforms for studying liver-stage Plasmodium parasites, particularly P. vivax hypnozoites and relapse prevention. Discusses limitations of current in vitro models and how three-dimensional organoid technology could address shortcomings to improve development of preventive strategies and radical cures.

articles.headline: Plant extract shows antimalarial activity in mice
articles.title_original: n-Butanol Fraction of Dioscorea dumetorum Tuber Aqueous Extract Exhibited Potent Antimalarial Activity in Plasmodium berghei-infected Mice.
articles.details: The n-butanol fraction (nBF) of Dioscorea dumetorum tubers tested in Plasmodium berghei-infected mice showed profound inhibition of parasite growth by 49.13% (p<0.05). A sub-fraction (CCF1) demonstrated 40.80% inhibition and contained 11 chemical compounds including allantoin and protodioscin. Results suggest promising source for novel antimalarial drug development.

articles.headline: Barriers to malaria treatment persist despite free access
articles.title_original: Predictors and characteristics of malaria treatment-seeking in Malawi: a longitudinal cohort study.
articles.details: Community-based longitudinal cohort study (Apr 2019-May 2020) in rural Malawi with 962 participants contributing 7,293 visits found 82% sought formal treatment but distance and education reduced odds (OR=0.28 for >90 min vs <30 min walking; OR=0.41 for secondary vs no education). Fever prompted health centre visits (OR=1.60), but parasite infection did not. Older age, male sex, and distance reduced health centre attendance.

articles.headline: Submicroscopic malaria prevalent in Peruvian Amazon
articles.title_original: Hidden burden: submicroscopic Plasmodium spp. infections in indigenous populations of the Peruvian Amazon.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study in 926 individuals from 4 indigenous communities in Peru's Río Santiago District found 8.5% (79/926) had submicroscopic P. vivax infections; 74.7% were asymptomatic. Asymptomatic individuals had 2.32-fold higher prevalence (aPR=2.32, 95% CI 1.63-3.29). Household animals were protective (aPR=0.64, 95% CI 0.48-0.85). Caterpiza showed 2.34-fold higher prevalence than Alianza Progreso (aPR=2.34, 95% CI 1.68-3.24).

articles.headline: Anxiety common among guardians of severely ill children
articles.title_original: Prevalence of anxiety and associated factors among guardians of children admitted with severe malaria at Thyolo District Hospital in Malawi.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 187 guardians of children admitted with severe malaria found mild anxiety most common (79.7%), followed by moderate (10.7%) and minimal (9.6%). Older age, guardian type, lower education, rural residence, and uncertain income associated with higher anxiety (p<0.001). Longer hospital stays and repeated admissions associated with increased severity.

articles.headline: Microtubule acetylation not essential for parasite survival
articles.title_original: Luminal acetylation of microtubules is not essential for Plasmodium berghei and Toxoplasma gondii survival.
articles.details: Genetic studies in Plasmodium berghei and Toxoplasma gondii examined role of microtubule acetylation at lysine 40 (K40). Q40K mutation in P. berghei α1-tubulin did not affect parasite development or infectivity. In T. gondii, deletion of α-TAT acetyltransferase had no impact on parasite growth in vitro. Results indicate K40 acetylation not essential for microtubule function in either species.

articles.headline: Clinical breast examination uptake very low in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Factors associated with the uptake of clinical breast examination for early detection of breast cancer: interaction analysis of the 2022 Tanzania Demographic and Health Survey and Malaria Indicator Survey.
articles.details: Secondary analysis of Tanzania DHS 2022 and Malaria Indicator Survey examined factors associated with clinical breast examination (CBE) uptake among women aged 15-49. Overall CBE uptake was very low at 5%. Women with low income, unemployed, or without health insurance had lower odds of CBE. Age effects on CBE differed by residence and education level.

articles.headline: Severe malaria with multiple complications in endemic area
articles.title_original: We Still Have to Fear Malaria: A Case Report of Severe Malaria With Almost all the Listed WHO Complications in a Patient Living in a Sub-Saharan Endemic Area.
articles.details: Case report of 67-year-old woman in sub-Saharan endemic area presenting with malaria-associated confusion and seizures. Findings included hemolytic anemia, severe hypoglycemia, status epilepticus, acute kidney injury (K+=6.24 mmol/L), hepatocellular failure, digital ischemia, and Stage 2 pressure ulcers. Despite improvements in consciousness and metabolic correction, patient died from sepsis. Illustrates severe complications possible in endemic populations.

articles.headline: P. falciparum purine transporters highly conserved globally
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity of Plasmodium falciparum equilibrative nucleoside transporters PfENT1 and PfENT4: Implications for purine-based antimalarial drug development.
articles.details: Analysis of >7,000 P. falciparum isolates examined genetic diversity in PfENT1 and PfENT4 nucleoside transporters. Only SNPs detected; no insertions/deletions or copy number variations. Missense variant frequencies: 4.5% (PfENT1) and 6.2% (PfENT4). Three common variants in PfENT1 (F36S, V129I, L133F; MAF 3.0-4.1%) and four in PfENT4 (I188F, N209I, A260T, S383A; MAF 1.5-38.9%) with similar allele frequencies in rural and urban areas.

articles.headline: Genomic surveillance reveals transmission pattern shifts in Kenya
articles.title_original: Assessing the impact of temporal changes in transmission on Plasmodium falciparum strains in Asembo, western Kenya (1996-2017) using within-host metrics via 24-SNP barcodes.
articles.details: Longitudinal genomic surveillance in Asembo, Kenya (1996-2017) using 24-SNP barcodes tracked P. falciparum multiplicity of infection (MOI) and transmission dynamics. Average MOI declined from 4.32 (1996) to 3.34 (2012), increasing to 3.49 (2017), tracking ITN distribution campaigns. Within-host strain-relatedness inversely correlated with MOI. Novel slope metric suggested transmission shifted toward co-transmission (2001-2012) then returned to superinfection levels by 2017.

articles.headline: Dual-AI nets show sustained vector control in resistant areas
articles.title_original: Three-year trends in malaria transmission parameters under deployment of Interceptor®G1, Interceptor®G2 and PermaNet®3.0 insecticide-treated bed nets in South-Western Burkina Faso.
articles.details: 3-year entomological study (Aug 2019-Feb 2022) in SW Burkina Faso evaluated three ITN types across 73,101 collected Anopheles in pyrethroid-resistant area. Interceptor G2 (dual-AI pyrethroid-chlorfenapyr) in Banfora achieved 88.7% density reduction over 3 years. Pyrethroid-only nets (Gaoua) showed initial decline followed by year-3 resurgence. Sporozoite infection rate and entomological inoculation rate mirrored density trends. Resurgence by year 3 highlighted need for improved durability and complementary strategies.

articles.headline: Malaria incidence declined 96% but persists in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Unraveling the spatiotemporal clustering of malaria incidence and modeling a decade of epidemiological data in Jimma Zone, Oromia, Ethiopia.
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of decade-long data (2012-2021) from 15 districts in Jimma Zone screened 1,718,471 individuals with 359,655 (20.93%) confirmed cases (52.71% P. falciparum, 43.62% P. vivax, 3.67% mixed). Annual parasite incidence declined 96.13%, from 87.97 per 1,000 population in 2012 to 3.40 in 2021. ARIMA modeling predicted stable incidence 2022-2026 with seasonal fluctuations. Chora Botor and Shebe Sonbo remained areas of persistent transmission risk.

articles.headline: Malaria status in Cabo Verde
articles.title_original: "Cabo Verde: is it really malaria-free?".
articles.details: Examines whether Cabo Verde has achieved malaria-free status.

articles.headline: Diagnostic delays in imported malaria Vietnam
articles.title_original: Delayed diagnosis and severe outcomes in imported malaria in Vietnam highlighting systematic gaps in detection.
articles.details: Describes delayed diagnosis and severe outcomes in imported malaria cases in Vietnam, highlighting gaps in detection systems.

articles.headline: Room-temperature stable LAMP for knowlesi diagnosis
articles.title_original: Rapid Detection of Knowlesi Malaria Using Room-Temperature Stable and Ready-to-Use Colorimetric Loop-Mediated Isothermal Amplification Reagents.
articles.details: Dehydrated colorimetric LAMP assay tested on 75 clinical blood samples from Malaysian hospitals, with nested PCR as reference. Achieved 100% sensitivity (95% CI: 92.1–100%) and 100% specificity (95% CI: 88.4–100%). Reagents stable at room temperature for up to 15 days, offering ideal diagnostic for resource-limited settings with knowlesi malaria.

articles.headline: Feasible home tablet neurorehab for cerebral malaria survivors
articles.title_original: A Pilot Feasibility Study of a Home Tablet-Based Neurorehabilitation Program and Serial Brain Vital Sign Monitoring for Survivors of Pediatric Cerebral Malaria.
articles.details: Pilot study of 6-month home tablet-based neurorehabilitation (Dino Island) and brain vital signs monitoring in children aged 3–12 with cerebral malaria history. Low attrition (5%), 100% positive family ratings, and 60% reported positive behavioral changes. Programs demonstrated feasibility, acceptability, and affordability, warranting further development.

articles.headline: Deep learning achieves 89% accuracy for malaria detection
articles.title_original: Malaria Parasite Cell Classification Using Transfer Learning with State-of-the-Art CNN Architectures.
articles.details: Evaluated 8 pretrained CNN architectures (ResNet, VGG, Inception, Xception) for automated malaria parasite classification from blood smear images using transfer learning. ResNet-50 and ResNet-101 achieved ~89% accuracy, Xception ~88%, VGG-16 ~80%. Models fine-tuned on extensive parasitized vs. uninfected cell datasets with precision, recall, and F1-score metrics showing ResNet and Xception superior balanced performance.

articles.headline: Mathematical model evaluates next-generation ITN effectiveness
articles.title_original: Cascades of effectiveness of next-generation insecticide-treated nets against malaria, from entomological trials to real-life conditions.
articles.details: Mathematical model parameterized with entomological data and validated against RCTs to quantify impact of Interceptor®G2 (chlorfenapyr-pyrethroid) and Olyset®Plus (piperonyl butoxide-pyrethroid). Found operational factors—functional survival, ITN use, in-bed exposure—critically determine overall effectiveness beyond entomological factors. Tanzania results extensible via dashboard for setting-specific product selection.

articles.headline: No artemisinin resistance mutations in Iranian P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Absence of Artemisinin Resistance-Associated Mutations in the Plasmodium Falciparum Kelch 13 (K13) Propeller Domain Gene Following Widespread Use of Artemisinin-Based Combination Therapy in Iran.
articles.details: Surveillance of pfk13 mutations in 60 Iranian P. falciparum patients (2022–2023); 50 sequenced with 98% (49/50) wild-type allele and 1 novel nonsynonymous mutation (R513C). No validated artemisinin-resistance-associated mutations detected. Authors conclude artemisinin remains effective for P. falciparum treatment in Iran, warranting continued molecular surveillance.

articles.headline: VAR2CSA antibodies differ by Plasmodium species in pregnancy
articles.title_original: VAR2CSA-reactive IgG in Brazilian women exposed to Plasmodium falciparum or P. vivax infection during pregnancy.
articles.details: Measured VAR2CSA-reactive IgG and Fc-afucosylation in pregnant Brazilian women at delivery using recombinant and native VAR2CSA antigens. FV2CHO-specific IgG levels lower than FV2BIC and restricted to P. falciparum-exposed women; PvDBP-specific IgG higher in P. vivax-exposed women but uncorrelated with VAR2CSA. FV2CHO-specific IgG markedly Fc-afucosylated. Cautions against using insect cell-expressed recombinant proteins to assess pregnancy exposure.

articles.headline: In silico analysis identifies druggable P. knowlesi Hsp90
articles.title_original: Plasmodium knowlesi Heat Shock Protein 90s: In Silico Analysis Reveals Unique Druggable Structural Features.
articles.authors: Daniyan MO, Singh H, Blatch GL
articles.details: In silico analysis of P. knowlesi Hsp90 isoforms revealed key structural differences from human Hsp90, particularly a plasmodium-specific straight glycine-rich helix loop (GHL) motif forming a hydrophobic extension. Virtual screening identified 3 compounds (ZINC22007970, ZINC724661072, ZINC724661078) predicted to bind the GHL pocket strongly while binding weakly to human Hsp90. Provides molecular framework for rational drug design against zoonotic malaria.

articles.headline: Non-falciparum malaria species widely circulating West Africa
articles.title_original: Circulation of non-falciparum malaria species in West Africa: A literature review from 2000 to 2024.
articles.details: Literature review of 31 papers (2000–2024) on non-falciparum malaria in West Africa. P. malariae identified in 80.6% of studies, P. ovale 77.4%, P. vivax 35.5%. Geographic distribution: 29% Senegal, 16.1% Mali, 12.9% Ghana/Nigeria. Majority used RDTs (54.8%), predominantly PfHRP2-based (70.6%). Authors conclude need for more sensitive diagnostic techniques and modified control strategies addressing asymptomatic infections.

articles.headline: Imported malaria epidemiology and diagnostic delays Brazil
articles.title_original: Clinical and epidemiological profiles of confirmed malaria cases in the state of Pernambuco, Brazil, from 2001 to 2022.
articles.details: Descriptive study of 350 malaria cases in Pernambuco (2001–2022): 75.1% male, median age 32, 81.1% urban residents. Median diagnostic delay varied: 10 days Indigenous, 5 days Black, 4 days Mixed/White individuals. Primary exposures: travel and construction; 52% infections from Brazil, 33% Angola. P. vivax 49.7%, P. falciparum 47.5%. Multivariate analysis showed ethnicity/education not significant delay predictors; seasonal pattern evident (p=0.0382).

articles.headline: Free healthcare policy increases visits and reduces malaria
articles.title_original: Evaluating the effects of the free healthcare policy on clinical visits and malaria among children in Burkina Faso: a modeling study of past trends and future forecasts.
articles.details: Analysis of 344,935 clinical visits from 199,664 children across 192 villages (2015–2018) using Differences-in-Differences design evaluating 2016 free healthcare policy. Clinical visits increased 1.1 to 2 per child (82% increase, p<0.001). Malaria prevalence decreased 98% to 80% (p<0.001); severe malaria 6% to 2.2% (p<0.001). Bayesian forecasts predicted further improvements. Access disparities persisted between remote and near-facility areas.

articles.headline: Donor red blood cells drive P. falciparum culture variability significantly
articles.title_original: Improving reproducibility of Plasmodium falciparum culture: a large-language-model-driven literature review and a parasite growth variability assessment of donor red blood cells.
articles.details: Systematic review of 963 PubMed articles (2000-2025) on RBC reporting in malaria culture found only 314 provided explicit RBC source; 61.1% failed to specify donor origin, 72.6% omitted blood group, 6% reported anticoagulant. Experimental study with 69 RBC donors showed parasite growth after 96h varied 6.4-fold (range 3.0-19.3). Low-growth donors had lower hemoglobin (12.3 vs 14.8 g/dL, p=0.03), more anemia history (p=0.01), greater endemic region ancestry (p=0.03). Study proposes STRuBeC reporting standard to improve transparency and reproducibility.

articles.headline: P. vivax mitochondrial antisense transcripts regulate severe malaria infection
articles.title_original: Integrative transcriptomic and machine learning approaches to decipher mitochondrial gene regulation in severe Plasmodium vivax malaria.
articles.details: Integrative transcriptomic and machine learning study decoding mitochondrial gene regulation in severe P. vivax malaria. Found that natural antisense transcripts (NATs) function as potent regulators—not transcriptional by-products—linked to mitochondrial pathways and translational machinery. Authors explicitly note findings are preliminary from limited clinical cohort and should not be interpreted as definitive clinical indicators; validation in larger diverse populations needed. Identify mitochondrial NATs as potential antimalarial drug targets.

articles.headline: Abbott-Bioline malaria RDT dangerously underperforms for P. vivax detection
articles.title_original: The underperforming Abbott-Bioline Malaria Ag P.f/P.v rapid diagnostic test: a whiter shade of pale - where the truth is not plain to see.
articles.details: Comparison of Abbott-Bioline Malaria Ag P.f/P.v RDT versus microscopy in 187 febrile patients during P. vivax outbreak in Myanmar. Of 46 microscopy-confirmed P. vivax cases, RDT detected only 11 (sensitivity 0.24, 95% CI 0.12-0.36; specificity 1.0). Among 28 cases with parasite count ≥200/µL (WHO lower detection limit), RDT detected 11 (sensitivity 0.39, 95% CI 0.21-0.57). Low color band intensity confirmed objectively using ImageJ software. Low sensitivity resulted in dangerous treatment delays despite WHO approval and large-scale deployment.

articles.headline: IgE antibodies bind P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein; trigger immune activation
articles.title_original: Biophysical and functional characterization of IgE interactions with a malaria antigen.
articles.authors: Adam AI, Sutton BJ, McDonnell JM
articles.details: Biophysical characterization of CIS43ε (IgE variant) interactions with P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein (PfCSP) using surface plasmon resonance and functional assays. CIS43ε showed biphasic binding with higher affinity for core PfCSP Peptide 21 than other NANP repeats in bivalent interactions. Multimeric Peptide 21 (not monomers) induced significant degranulation in FcεRI-expressing cells, plateauing at trimeric state. Full-length PfCSP triggered greater degranulation than tetrameric Peptide 21. Findings highlight multivalent interactions and therapeutic potential of IgE antibodies.

articles.headline: Aurora kinase PfArk1 inhibitors block P. falciparum mitotic division; new drug lead
articles.title_original: Targeting Aurora Kinases as Essential Cell-Cycle Regulators to Deliver Multi-Stage Antimalarials Against Plasmodium Falciparum.
articles.details: Evaluated human Aurora kinase inhibitors targeting Aurora-related kinase (Ark) family in P. falciparum. Several AurB inhibitors exhibited multistage potency (<250 nM) against asexual blood stages, liver schizonts, and male gametes with >1000-fold selectivity. Hesperadin, TAE684, and AT83 most potent; hesperadin identified PfArk1 as principal vulnerable Ark family member. Inhibition caused inability to complete mitosis with unsegregated, multi-lobed nuclei and aberrant microtubule organization. PfArk1 identified as main Aurora mitotic kinase with bifunctional AurA/B activity; paves way for drug-discovery campaigns.

articles.headline: PfARMA vaccine candidate antibodies target non-protective disordered region
articles.title_original: The N-terminal region of malaria vaccine candidate Plasmodium falciparum asparagine-rich merozoite antigen is immunodominant and targeted by polyreactive antibodies.
articles.details: Analysis of naturally acquired B cell and antibody responses to P. falciparum asparagine-rich merozoite antigen (PfARMA) in exposed individuals. B cell responses dominated by IgM+ B cells recognizing N-terminal intrinsically disordered region 1 (IDR1); one monoclonal antibody to IDR1 was non-neutralizing, another to folded domain showed weak neutralizing activity. Both plasma IgM and IgG responses predominantly targeted IDR1 and increased with P. falciparum exposure; ~30% of unexposed individuals had polyreactive IgG targeting IDR1. Contrary to prior reports, antibody responses did not correlate with protection in age/exposure-matched children.

articles.headline: Malaria RDT self-testing practical for artisanal gold miners in remote areas
articles.title_original: Practicability of a malaria rapid diagnostic self-test among artisanal gold miners in French Guiana: a cross-sectional study.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 40 artisanal and small-scale gold miners (May-June 2024) in Oiapoque, Brazil assessing Malakit intervention (RDT self-testing and ACT self-treatment). After training, mean knowledge 83.9% [CI 79.6-88.2], practice 84.6% [CI 78.3-90.9] without assistance (100% with guidance), interpretation 77.2% [CI 70.7-83.8]. Correct interpretation: 96.0% positive strips, 70.0% negative strips, 65.8% invalid strips. Overall practicability score 81.9% [CI 78.3-85.6]; 100% valid test results with guidance. Demonstrates feasibility of community-based malaria diagnosis in hard-to-reach populations.

articles.headline: Anopheles seasonality drives malaria transmission patterns in Odisha, India
articles.title_original: Seasonal dynamics and species diversity of Anopheles mosquitoes in malaria endemic districts of Southern Odisha India.
articles.details: Two-year study (March 2021–February 2023) across 11 hyper-endemic districts in southern Odisha collected 10,807 Anopheles identifying 18 species. An. subpictus predominant in summer 2021 (42.99%) and rainy season (46.60%); An. culicifacies (primary vector) consistent with rainy 2021 (31.58%) and rainy 2022 (38.35%); An. varuna peaked in winter (35.56% winter 2021-22, 25.18% winter 2022-23). Ganjam district showed highest species diversity (1.26–2.2). Seasonal variation had statistically significant impact on species diversity (p<0.001), surpassing district-level factors. Emphasizes need for localized, seasonally-adapted vector control.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria prevalent in Ethiopian schoolchildren; key transmission reservoirs
articles.title_original: Asymptomatic Plasmodium infection and predictors among schoolchildren in Bahir Dar Zuria District, Northwest Ethiopia.
articles.details: School-based cross-sectional study (March–May 2024) of 416 schoolchildren in Bahir Dar Zuria, Northwest Ethiopia using RDT, capillary Buffy Coat, and blood film. Composite asymptomatic Plasmodium prevalence 3.4% (95% CI 1.7–6.3); P. falciparum 64.3% of cases. Multilevel logistic regression identified significant predictors: age (AOR 3.53, p=0.001), gender (AOR 2.87, p=0.021), stagnant water near household (AOR 3.76, p=0.031), never used long-lasting insecticidal nets (AOR 2.90, p=0.007), malaria infection in last year (AOR 2.23, p=0.047). All p<0.01. Emphasizes schoolchildren as significant silent transmission reservoirs.

articles.headline: Colombian malaria vector distribution mapped by ecoregion and altitude
articles.title_original: Ecoregional and altitudinal distribution of the principal malaria vectors in Colombia.
articles.details: Database study (1929–2023) of 2,371 distribution records of five principal Colombian malaria vectors: An. albimanus, An. darlingi, An. nuneztovari s.s., An. neivai s.l., and An. calderoni. Coastal species: An. albimanus (Atlantic and Pacific) and An. neivai s.l. (Pacific); inland species: An. darlingi (forest-associated), An. nuneztovari s.s. (Middle Magdalena Valley, Urabá-Bajo Cauca, Atrato River, Catatumbo), and An. calderoni (Cauca River Valley). Most distributed below 500 m elevation; An. albimanus and An. calderoni present above 1,000 m. An. albimanus and An. neivai s.l. show elevation increase through time.

articles.headline: Age, comorbidities predict childhood malaria mortality in South Sudan hospitalized cohort
articles.title_original: A Cox Proportional Hazards Model Approach to Identifying Malaria Risk Factors in Children in South Sudan.
articles.details: Hospital-based retrospective study of 6,410 children with malaria admitted to Al Sabah Hospital, Juba, South Sudan (January–December 2021); 56.08% male, 43.92% female; 4.73% died. Cox proportional hazards model identified: increased age associated with increased hazard of death (HR 1.0223, 95% CI 1.0158–1.0280, p<0.01); higher body weight decreased hazard (HR 0.8513, 95% CI 0.8146–0.8896, p<0.01); quinine treatment lower risk than artesunate (HR 0.7107, 95% CI 0.5501–0.9183, p<0.01); no comorbidity lower risk than comorbidity (HR 0.5530, 95% CI 0.4273–0.7157, p<0.01). Authors note quinine finding contradicts prior studies showing artesunate superiority.

articles.headline: Some malaria vectors seek hosts during daytime; surveillance timing needs adjustment
articles.title_original: Malaria Mosquito Host-Seeking Activity Times in Manhiça District, Rural Mozambique, and the Need to Better Match Entomological Surveillance Strategies to Daylight Cycles.
articles.details: Assessment of daytime host-seeking activity of Anopheles in two villages in Manhiça district, Mozambique. Most abundant species was An. tenebrosus; daytime (06:00–18:00) host-seeking observed only in one An. ziemanni specimen (collected 16:00–18:00), but sunset occurred at 17:06 that day, indicating specimen was collected during actual nighttime. Analysis of sunrise/sunset times across African countries showed seasonal changes exceeding 2 hours depending on longitude and latitude. Authors note entomological surveillance strategies must adjust to match actual daylight cycles and human behavioral components to collect actionable data for malaria control programs.

articles.headline: Malaria burden remains high in Western Tigray despite control efforts
articles.title_original: Malaria Trends, Burden, Seasonal Variation, and Interventions in Western Tigray, Ethiopia.
articles.details: Hospital-based retrospective study (2011–2019) in Ethiopia tracked 36,438 confirmed malaria cases with 50 deaths (0.14% mortality) and 2,016 admissions (5.5%). P. falciparum accounted for 62.1% of cases. Case trends were statistically significant (p=0.001), but mortality trends were not (p=0.62), indicating fluctuating transmission without improvement in case fatality.

articles.headline: Regional heterogeneity in malaria prevalence within endemic Meghalaya
articles.title_original: Malaria risk, treatment-seeking, and prevention in Northeastern India: clinic-based surveillance of persistent transmission.
articles.details: Multi-site facility-based study (2018–2021) across five health centres and one district hospital in three districts of Meghalaya, northeastern India enrolled 1,031 symptomatic patients. Malaria prevalence was 4.4% overall (45/1,031; 60% P. falciparum), ranging from 1.8% to 12.3% across districts. Prior malaria diagnosis (OR=9.2) and outdoor activity during peak mosquito hours were significant risk factors, highlighting local variation in transmission dynamics.

articles.headline: Dried blood spots preserve P. falciparum PfHRP2 reliably up to 120 days
articles.title_original: The reliability of malaria rapid diagnostic test kit for detecting P. falciparum PfHRP2 in dried blood spot samples preserved under different conditions and durations.
articles.details: Study of rapid diagnostic test reliability on dried blood spots (DBS) from 60 patients with uncomplicated malaria (900 DBS total) stored at three temperatures for up to 150 days. PfHRP2 detection remained reproducible from day 30–120 irrespective of temperature. At day 150, positivity fell to 80–90% (p=0.236). Room temperature proved cost-effective and equipment-free for preservation up to 120 days.

articles.headline: First high-quality P. simium reference genome reveals host adaptation signatures
articles.title_original: Novel zoonotic cases of Plasmodium simium from São Paulo with a reference genome of the Brazilian strain.
articles.details: Genomic study generated a complete P. simium reference genome (Ps_SãoPaulo, 21.8 Mb) using Oxford Nanopore and Illumina sequencing. Comparative analysis of 38 Brazilian P. simium isolates identified differences from P. vivax including mitochondrial mutations, deletions in reticulocyte-binding genes (RBP2a, DBP), and variants in blood-stage antigens (msp3.1, msp3.2), revealing potential host-adaptation signatures relevant to zoonotic transmission.
articles.notable_reason: First comprehensive reference genome for zoonotic parasite

articles.headline: Intradermal delivery of sporozoite vaccine reduces CD8+ T cell protection
articles.title_original: Intradermal immunization with Plasmodium berghei late-arresting genetically attenuated sporozoites induces PD-L1 expression on regulatory macrophages and dendritic cells.
articles.details: Rodent study (P. berghei model) compared intradermal (ID) versus intravenous (IV) delivery of late-arresting genetically attenuated sporozoite vaccine. ID immunization resulted in lower splenic CD8+ tissue-resident memory T cells (1.3%, p=0.3) and significantly reduced cytotoxic markers (liver Granzyme B p<0.0001, perforin p<0.0001). ID also induced regulatory myeloid phenotype (low CD86, high PD-L1). These mechanistic findings highlight route-dependent differences in vaccine efficacy.
