High nucleotide diversity (π=0.00359±0.00012) with evidence of adaptive evolution (dN-dS=3.15, p<0.001). Recombinant RII protein showed 84.8% average seropositivity (range 50.0-95.4% across villages); antibody response correlated with age (ρ=0.333, p<0.001). Despite substantial polymorphism, glycan-binding residues remain conserved and antigenicity high across geographic contexts. Authors support continued evaluation as blood-stage vaccine candidate.

articles.headline: System of Rice Intensification may alter malaria vector ecology
articles.title_original: Climate change adapted rice production: does the system of rice intensification impact malaria vector ecology?
articles.details: Review article examining potential impacts of System of Rice Intensification (SRI) on malaria vector bionomics across Africa. SRI reduces water use and agricultural inputs while increasing yields, fundamentally altering rice-growing environment and potentially impacting vector species ecology and local transmission dynamics. As Africa increases SRI adoption under climate change pressures, understanding ecological consequences is critical. No new empirical data presented.

articles.headline: Stakeholder perceptions and barriers to post-discharge malaria chemoprevention
articles.title_original: Assessing stakeholder perceptions and contextual factors in implementing post-discharge malaria chemoprevention in children with severe anaemia in Malawi.
articles.details: Qualitative study with 31 in-depth and key informant interviews across Malawi (May 2024) on WHO-recommended post-discharge malaria chemoprevention (PDMC) for children with severe anemia. Identified barriers: knowledge gaps among health workers, traditional beliefs, financial constraints, logistical challenges. Four themes emerged: dual malaria-anemia burden, healthcare access barriers, adherence strategies, and implementation considerations. Authors emphasize community engagement, supply chain strengthening, and health worker training needed for effective implementation.

articles.headline: AL and DHAPQ show similar parasite clearance in Nigerian children
articles.title_original: Parasite clearance of two commonly administered artemisinin-based combination therapies in under-five children with uncomplicated malaria in a comprehensive healthcare facility in South-Western Nigeria: a randomised controlled trial.
articles.details: Open-label RCT in 122 children aged 6-59 months with uncomplicated malaria in Nigeria (July-December 2020), randomized to artemether-lumefantrine (AL, n=61) or dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DHAPQ, n=61). Day 42 per-protocol: 57 AL, 56 DHAPQ. Fever clearance faster in AL (46.16±3.31h versus 48.83±8.95h, p=0.032); parasite clearance similar (61.30±23.75h versus 63.97±23.42h, p=0.541). Day 42 PCR-uncorrected cure rates: AL 94.7%, DHAPQ 92.9% (p=0.679); both 100% after PCR correction.

articles.headline: AI model achieves 95.61% accuracy for rapid malaria blood smear diagnosis
articles.title_original: Ensemble-Based Early Detection of Malaria via Explainable ViT-CNN Feature Fusion and SHAP.
articles.details: Hybrid AI model combining Vision Transformer (ViT) and EfficientNet CNN for malaria diagnosis from microscopic blood smear images, with SHAP-based feature importance filtering and ensemble training (CatBoost, XGBoost, Logistic Regression). Optimal performance from ViT_Large_16 and EfficientNetB0: 95.61% accuracy with 1.6s processing time (~204× faster than traditional ML). ViT models best captured complex details in blood smears. Authors conclude approach suitable for clinical integration in infectious disease diagnosis.

articles.headline: Correction to experimental cerebral malaria study
articles.title_original: Correction: Anticoagulant therapy and altered tissue factor expression protect against experimental placental and cerebral malaria.
articles.details: Corrigendum to prior article on tissue factor and anticoagulation in experimental malaria models.

articles.headline: Metabolomics identifies metabolic alterations in malaria infection
articles.title_original: Application of a Non-Targeted Metabolomics Study in Plasmodium berghei-Infected Rats: Towards Unravelling Metabolic Alterations During Malaria Infection.
articles.details: Non-targeted metabolomics study in 20 male Sprague Dawley rats (infected versus uninfected controls) using untargeted GC-TOF mass spectrometry. Infected rats showed elevated urea and reduced 1,5-anhydroglucitol, D-(+)-Talose, and arachidonic acid. Pathway analysis revealed enrichment of glucose-alanine cycle, alpha-linolenic acid metabolism, and linoleic acid metabolism. Authors propose metabolomics as potential diagnostic tool for malaria detection and prognosis based on metabolic pathway alterations.

articles.headline: Minimum temperature drives malaria transmission more than mean temperature
articles.title_original: Association between temperature and malaria (1959-2019): a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.details: Meta-analysis of 11 studies (1959-2019) from Africa and Asia examining temperature-malaria associations. Minimum temperature showed strongest correlation with malaria test positivity (r=0.454, 95% CI 0.358-0.549, p<0.001), more consistent than mean temperature (r=0.417, p=0.01) or maximum temperature (r=0.356). Substantial heterogeneity observed (I²>99%). Authors conclude temperature, particularly minimum temperature thresholds, should be integrated into malaria surveillance and early warning systems.

articles.headline: Innovation under pressure: bold ideas for changing malaria landscape
articles.title_original: Innovation under pressure: bold ideas for a changing malaria landscape.
articles.details: Title indicates discussion of innovative approaches to malaria control in response to pressure from changing conditions, but no abstract provided.

articles.headline: Post-artesunate delayed hemolysis diagnostic challenges in malaria
articles.title_original: The usual suspect-malaria? Diagnostic challenges in post-artesunate delayed haemolysis: a clinical laboratory perspective through a case report.
articles.details: Mini-review examining diagnostic challenges of post-artesunate delayed haemolysis (PADH), a rare but severe complication of artemisinin derivatives. Case presentation of travel-associated malaria in Europe highlights difficulty distinguishing PADH from malaria disease manifestations. Emphasizes need for continuous training and quality assurance in medical laboratories.

articles.headline: Insecticide-treated net use among African children at 69.5%
articles.title_original: Utilization of insecticide-treated nets for malaria prevention among children in Africa: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.details: Meta-analysis of 30 studies (2013-2023, n=76,045 children) assessing ITN utilization across Africa. Pooled prevalence: 69.50% (95% CI 64.5-74.5%), with significant regional variation—Central Africa 85.44%, Southern Africa 80.7%, Eastern Africa 69.2%, West Africa 67.0% (I²=100%). Authors conclude ITN utilization is suboptimal and varies by region despite proven effectiveness.

articles.headline: South Africa shows minimal artemisinin-resistant malaria markers
articles.title_original: Very Low Prevalence of Validated kelch13 Mutations and Absence of Histidine-Rich Protein 2/3 Double Gene Deletions in South African Malaria-Eliminating Districts (2022-2024).
articles.details: Molecular surveillance of 4,471 samples (2022-2024) from South African malaria-eliminating districts assessed drug resistance markers (kelch13, crt, mdr1, dhfr, dhps) and diagnostic markers (hrp2/3). Validated artemisinin-resistance mutations extremely rare: P574L in 2 samples (minor allele), P553L in 1 sample. No hrp2/3 deletions detected. Authors conclude recommended treatments and diagnostics remain effective.

articles.headline: SumiShield 50WG maintains 8-month residual mosquito mortality
articles.title_original: Efficacy of the Insecticide Formulation SumiShield® 50WG for Malaria Vector Control in Experimental Huts in Madagascar.
articles.details: Experimental hut study in Madagascar assessing SumiShield 50WG (clothianidin neonicotinoid) for 9 months. Mortality of wild Anopheles remained above WHO 80% threshold for 8 months post-spray but fell below by month 9. No exophily induction or blood-feeding inhibition observed. Authors conclude 8-month residual efficacy suffices for Madagascar's malaria transmission season.

articles.headline: Madagascar ITNs lose effectiveness within 12 months post-distribution
articles.title_original: Early Bio-Efficacy Loss of Nets Mass Distributed for Malaria Vector Control in Madagascar in 2018: Implications for Malaria Prevention.
articles.details: Assessment of DawaPlus 2.0 and PermaNet 2.0 nets distributed in Madagascar (2018) at 0, 12, 24, and 36 months post-distribution. Mortality rates exceeded WHO 80% on arrival (86.4% and 83.6%) but fell to <56% at all subsequent timepoints. Deltamethrin residue declined to <66 mg/m² (DawaPlus) and <36 mg/m² (PermaNet) by month 12. Authors identify urgent need to determine cause and implement corrective measures.

articles.headline: Secondary outdoor-biting vectors drive Madagascar malaria outbreak
articles.title_original: Malaria Outbreak in Farafangana District, Southeast Madagascar, 2018: Are Secondary Vectors a Threat to Current Malaria Control Approaches?
articles.details: Entomological investigation of 2018 malaria outbreak in Farafangana District, Madagascar (6 villages). Of 1,488 Anopheles collected, 25 (1.7%) carried P. falciparum sporozoites; 24 found outdoors with high exophagy rates (59.8%-100% by species). Population survey (n=226) showed 31.4% RDT positivity with 85.9% asymptomatic. Authors conclude outdoor-biting secondary vectors combined with high asymptomatic infection rates undermined indoor-targeted control.

articles.headline: Asymptomatic malaria in migrants missed by RDT but caught by RT-PCR
articles.title_original: Burden of asymptomatic malaria in adult sub-Saharan migrants attending an outpatient clinic in Rome from February 2024 to January 2025.
articles.details: Screening of 87 asymptomatic sub-Saharan African migrants in Rome (Feb 2024-Jan 2025). Malaria prevalence 6% (all male). LAMP/RT-PCR detected 5/87 cases (6.0%) versus RDTs detecting only 2/87 (2.3%). Species: P. falciparum, P. malariae, P. ovale, and one mixed. Longest post-arrival time 181 days; median time since endemic region >600 days. Authors recommend RT-PCR integration into routine screening.

articles.headline: Hierarchical VAR gene switching optimizes parasite immune evasion
articles.title_original: Hierarchical switching pattern in antigenic variation provides survival advantage for malaria parasites under variable host immunity.
articles.details: Theoretical and experimental study establishing mechanistic basis for hierarchical var gene expression in P. falciparum using Markov chain modeling and transcriptomic analysis. Shows switching hierarchy provides survival advantage when host immunity clears PfEMP1 variants at variable rates. Variants cleared efficiently by immune system maintained at higher expression; slowly-cleared variants suppressed. Stable variants show greater IgM binding affinity.

articles.headline: PI4K inhibitor reduces parasitemia 91% in malaria mouse model
articles.title_original: Optimization of 2,8-Diaryl-1,5-naphthyridines as Plasmodium falciparum Phosphatidylinositol 4-Kinase Inhibitors with Improved ADME Profiles and In Vivo Efficacy.
articles.details: Structure-activity relationship optimization of 2,8-diaryl-1,5-naphthyridine phosphatidylinositol 4-kinase (PI4K) inhibitors targeting P. falciparum. Lead compound 27 reduced parasitemia 91% with 4 × 50 mg/kg/day in humanized NOD-scid IL-2Rγnull mouse model. No hERG channel inhibition or mammalian cytotoxicity. Low selectivity against PI3Kα and PI4Kβ but better selectivity against MINK1 and MAP4K4 kinases.

articles.headline: Novel amodiaquine analog achieves single-dose cure in malaria model
articles.title_original: Discovery of Amodiachins, a Novel Class of 4-Aminoquinoline Antimalarials Active against Multidrug-Resistant Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.details: Structure-activity relationship optimization of amodiaquine analog antimalarials (amodiachins) against multidrug-resistant P. falciparum. Compound 43 (ADC-028) exhibited nanomolar activity against drug-sensitive and drug-resistant strains with minimal cytotoxicity and favorable microsomal stability (t1/2=48.2 min). In vivo: nonrecrudescence at 16 mg/kg/d, single-dose cure at 50 mg/kg. Pharmacokinetics: T1/2=84 h, oral bioavailability 76%.

articles.headline: Fluorescent marinoquinoline compounds inhibit P. falciparum and P. vivax
articles.title_original: Fluorescent Marinoquinoline Derivative as Inhibitors of Plasmodium falciparum: SAR Analysis, Mode of Action and In Vivo Studies.
articles.details: Researchers synthesized 20 new marinoquinoline analogs with fluorescence properties. Compound 19 showed IC50 0.28 μM against P. falciparum 3D7, 1.2 μM EC50 ex vivo against P. falciparum, and 0.53 μM EC50 against P. vivax. In mice with P. berghei, a 3 × 50 mg/kg oral dose achieved 96% parasitemia reduction. The compound acts as a protease inhibitor, accumulating in infected erythrocytes and the parasite digest vacuole.

articles.headline: PfAP2-06B transcription factor regulates erythrocyte invasion in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: An ApiAP2 Family Transcriptional Factor PfAP2-06B Regulates Erythrocyte Invasion Indirectly in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.details: Conditional knockdown of the ApiAP2 family transcription factor PfAP2-06B impaired both asexual growth and erythrocyte invasion in P. falciparum. RNA-seq revealed that PfAP2-06B modulates expression of invasion-related genes during the schizont stage, while single-cell RNA-seq showed it influences stochastic cell-to-cell variation in invasion genes. These findings identify PfAP2-06B as a potential novel target for malaria control strategies.

articles.headline: PKCθ inhibition prevents CD8+ T cell-mediated cerebral malaria in mice
articles.title_original: Inhibition of PKCθ Abrogates CD8(+) T Cell-Mediated Neurotoxicity in Murine Cerebral Malaria.
articles.details: In murine cerebral malaria using PbA-infected C57BL/6N mice, PKCθ-deficient mice showed significantly improved survival compared to wild-type without changes in blood or brain parasite burdens. PKCθ deficiency shifted T cell differentiation toward central memory rather than effector memory phenotypes, reducing CD8+ T cell sequestration in the cerebral microvasculature and preventing neurovascular damage and neurological symptoms. These findings suggest potential for repurposing clinical PKCθ inhibitors as host-directed therapy for cerebral malaria.

articles.headline: Global burden and vaccine advances for intracellular parasitic infections
articles.title_original: Intracellular Parasitic Infections Caused by Plasmodium falciparum, Leishmania spp., Toxoplasma gondii, Echinococcus multilocularis, Among Key Pathogens: Global Burden, Transmission Dynamics, and Vaccine Advances-A Narrative Review with Contextual Insights from Armenia.
articles.details: Narrative review examining intracellular parasitic infections including P. falciparum, covering parasite classification, immune evasion mechanisms, diagnostics, and control strategies. The review synthesizes current knowledge on vaccine development including experimental and licensed approaches for intracellular pathogens. Using Armenia as a case study, the review emphasizes the importance of integrated One Health approaches addressing human, animal, and environmental health to control parasitic transmission.

articles.headline: Methods for inducing gametocytogenesis in Plasmodium parasites reviewed
articles.title_original: Induction of gametocytogenesis in human malaria parasites: from stress to genome editing.
articles.details: Mini-review summarizing approaches for inducing gametocytogenesis—the transformation from asexual to sexual-stage gametocytes—in human Plasmodium species. Methods range from conventional environmental stressors to cutting-edge genome editing for precise modifications of sexual conversion-related genes. The review notes substantial progress in induction methods but highlights scalability challenges, particularly for non-falciparum species, and advocates for integrating conventional and genome editing approaches.

articles.headline: Metabolic pathways in mosquito-Plasmodium interactions examined via transcriptomics
articles.title_original: Avian malaria and the overlooked metabolic pathways underlying mosquito-Plasmodium interactions.
articles.details: Review examining metabolic pathways in mosquito-Plasmodium interactions beyond immune responses. Transcriptomic analysis of infected versus uninfected mosquitoes identified differential expression of pathways essential for parasite development, including genes for parasite energetic needs, digestive enzymes for midgut barrier traversal, and salivary enzymes for blood feeding. The findings suggest parasites modulate these pathways to enhance and prolong infection, highlighting potential novel transmission control targets.

articles.headline: Sufficient LLIN ownership reduces malaria infection in Kenyan households
articles.title_original: Long-lasting insecticidal net ownership and malaria infection by socio-economic status: a cross-sectional household study in an area along Lake Victoria, Kenya.
articles.details: Cross-sectional household study in Lake Victoria region, Kenya (June-September 2021) examining LLIN effectiveness across socio-economic status groups via school-based PCR testing. Middle SES households with sufficient LLIN ownership (1 net per ≤2 people) showed significantly lower malaria infection (OR 0.32, 95% CI 0.12-0.92). Combined middle and low SES groups: OR 0.37 (95% CI 0.17-0.87), supporting WHO recommendations for LLIN distribution and compliance strategies.

articles.headline: Malaria RDT results remain stable after one month of storage
articles.title_original: Are malaria rapid diagnostic test results stable over time to support verification of surveillance data?
articles.details: Prospective study across 48 health facilities in Benin, Nigeria, and Uganda (June-September 2023) evaluated RDT stability using digital AI-powered imaging. Of 54,251 RDTs captured, 45,155 met inclusion criteria. At one month, 95.1% of initially positive RDTs remained positive and 95.3% of initially negative remained negative. Positive predictive value was 96.3% (95% CI 96.1-96.5) and negative predictive value was 93.8% (95% CI 93.4-94.1).

articles.headline: Phosphoinositide-binding protein PfPX2 implicated in parasite vesicular trafficking
articles.title_original: Characterization of a phosphoinositide-binding protein containing a PHOX homology domain in the malaria parasite Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.details: Characterized P. falciparum protein PfPX2, a phosphoinositide (PIP)-binding protein with a PX domain that binds all PIP species with preference for PI3P, PI5P, and PI(3,5)P2. Immunofluorescence localized PfPX2 to Golgi apparatus and micronemes in developing schizonts. Proximity labeling identified interactors including PfSortilin, clathrin heavy chain, and PfDyn1, suggesting PfPX2 plays a role in vesicular trafficking between Golgi and micronemes.

articles.headline: Nanopore sequencing reliably distinguishes recrudescence from new malaria infection
articles.title_original: Rapid multiplexed nanopore amplicon sequencing to distinguish Plasmodium falciparum recrudescence from new infection in antimalarial drug trials.
articles.authors: Holzschuh A, Lerch A, Nsanzabana C
articles.details: Developed and validated a multiplexed nanopore AmpSeq assay targeting six microhaplotypes to distinguish P. falciparum recrudescence from new infections in drug trials. The assay achieved high sensitivity (detecting minority clones at 1:100:100:100 ratios), high specificity (false-positive haplotypes <0.01%), and excellent reproducibility (intra-assay 98%, inter-assay 97%). In 20 paired patient samples, it distinguished recrudescence from new infections in 17/20 cases (85%).

articles.headline: Correction to ESCRT-III machinery publication
articles.title_original: Correction: The ESCRT-III machinery participates in the production of extracellular vesicles and protein export during Plasmodium falciparum infection.
articles.details: Correction notice to a previously published article about ESCRT-III machinery's role in P. falciparum extracellular vesicle production and protein export.

articles.headline: Zanzibar malaria epidemic analysis: 23,569 cases, spatiotemporal patterns identified
articles.title_original: The November-2023-March-2024 malaria epidemic in Zanzibar: a spatiotemporal epidemiological analysis.
articles.details: Spatiotemporal epidemiological analysis of 23,569 confirmed malaria cases during November 2023-March 2024 Zanzibar epidemic. Males, individuals aged 15-35, urban residents, and those not sleeping under insecticide-treated nets showed higher malaria risk. One significant space-time cluster identified in urban southwest Unguja. Weekly malaria cases significantly associated with average weekly temperature with an 8-week lag time, indicating setback in Zanzibar's malaria elimination progress.

articles.headline: Antimalarial drugs bind Plasmodium serpentine receptor PfSR10
articles.title_original: Identification of antimalarial drugs and bradykinin as ligands of the Plasmodium membrane protein PfSR10.
articles.details: This study found that antimalarial drugs (chloroquine, dihydroartemisinin, piperaquine-tetraphosphate, primaquine) are agonists of the P. falciparum membrane protein PfSR10, inducing coupling with human Gi/Gq proteins via biochemical reconstitution and cryo-EM analysis. Proteomics identified bradykinin as an endogenous agonist of PfSR10. These findings establish PfSR10 as a receptor for both antimalarials and host peptides, with broad implications for understanding drug action and host-parasite communication.
articles.notable_reason: Direct targets of multiple antimalarials; reveals GPCR-mediated mechanism

articles.headline: Cellular aging biomarkers in malaria host-parasite relationships
articles.title_original: Critical Understanding of the Influence of Cellular Aging Biomarkers on Host-Parasite Relationships Serving as a Key Platform for Malaria Eradication.
articles.details: This comprehensive review examines the interplay between Plasmodium parasites and host immune responses, particularly focusing on how infection mechanisms, parasite evasion strategies, and host immune responses affect cellular aging biomarkers. The review covers P. falciparum, which causes the most severe infection leading to anemia and cerebral malaria, and explores how understanding this dynamic could inform malaria eradication strategies.

articles.headline: DDT and pyrethroid residues in Brazilian Amazon malaria regions
articles.title_original: Potential Risks to Human Health Caused by the Use of Pesticides in Soils of Three Municipalities Impacted by Localized Malaria in the Brazilian Amazon.
articles.details: This study analyzed DDT and alphacypermethrin concentrations in soils from three Brazilian Amazon municipalities (Mazagão, Porto Velho, Cantá) using gas chromatography. DDT levels reached 2.694 mg/kg in Cantá; alphacypermethrin averaged 0.364 mg/kg in Porto Velho. While ecological risks were minimal, health risks were substantial: incremental lifetime cancer risk reached 2.93×10⁻³ for DDT and 1.17×10⁻¹ for alphacypermethrin, with total non-carcinogenic hazard index of 336.61.

articles.headline: Parvovirus B19 coinfection in P. falciparum malaria causes aplasia
articles.title_original: Transient Pure Red Cell Aplasia in a Case of Plasmodium falciparum and Parvovirus B19 Coinfection.
articles.details: A 22-year-old from West Bengal with complicated P. falciparum malaria treated with IV artesunate followed by artemether-lumefantrine developed persistent hypoproliferative anemia despite parasitemia clearance. Bone marrow showed pure red cell aplasia with giant pronormoblasts, and B19V quantitative PCR was 2.1×10³ IU/mL. The patient recovered after intravenous immunoglobulin and prednisone, highlighting that acute B19V coinfection should be considered in malaria patients with persistent anemia.

articles.headline: Long-amplicon sequencing panel for P. falciparum drug resistance surveillance
articles.title_original: Multiplex long-amplicon sequencing for comprehensive molecular surveillance of Plasmodium falciparum resistance to artemisinin and partner drugs in artemisinin-based combination therapies (ACTs).
articles.details: A multiplex long-amplicon PCR panel targeting six genes (Pfk13, Pfcoronin, Pfap2μ, Pfubp1, Pfmdr1, Pfcrt) was developed for comprehensive P. falciparum antimalarial resistance surveillance. Sensitivity thresholds were 50 parasites/μL for dried blood spots and 5 parasites/μL for venous blood, achieving 100% coverage. Cost was $15.60 per sample with 0.25–0.5 GB sequencing data required, making it scalable for monitoring multidrug-resistant strains in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Financial incentives and education for malaria control in Kenya
articles.title_original: The second-round evaluation of financial incentives on malaria prevalence in the Lake Victoria Basin, Kenya: updated study protocol for a cluster-randomized controlled trial.
articles.details: This is an updated protocol for a second-round cluster-randomized controlled trial re-randomizing 92 clusters to conditional cash transfer, lottery incentive, or control arms, with interventions linked to negative malaria test results. Primary outcomes include malaria prevalence assessed by RDT, microscopy, and PCR at 3 and 6 months post-intervention, as well as LLIN usage and knowledge/perception of malaria.

articles.headline: Malaria epidemiology and control outcomes in Pakistan 2020-2024
articles.title_original: Fighting malaria in Shangla District, Pakistan: insight into epidemiology, risk factors, and strategic control measures (2020-2024).
articles.details: A 5-year analysis of 130,401 confirmed malaria cases from 572,696 screened individuals in Shangla District, Pakistan examined epidemiological trends and intervention impacts. Annual parasite incidence rose from 2 to 61 per 1,000 (2020–2023), declining to 46 in 2024. P. vivax comprised 95% of cases (P. falciparum 4.6%). Case declines in 2024 followed deployment of 78 diagnostic centers, IRS expansion, LLIN distribution, and health education.

articles.headline: Caregiver knowledge linked to better malaria prevention in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Malaria knowledge and preventive practices among caregivers of under-five children in Southwest Nigeria.
articles.details: A hospital-based cross-sectional study of 797 caregivers of children under five in Southwest Nigeria found 86.4% had good malaria knowledge. Good knowledge was significantly associated with better prevention practices (OR 2.347, 95% CI 1.251–4.406, p=0.008). Caregivers from Ondo and Ogun states had higher odds of good knowledge than those from Osun State, suggesting targeted public health interventions could improve prevention.

articles.headline: Harmala alkaloids computationally predicted to inhibit P. falciparum PK4
articles.title_original: Computational insights into harmala alkaloids as novel antimalarial candidates against Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.details: In silico analysis evaluated harmala alkaloids (harmine, harmaline) from plants and the bacterium Delftia tsuruhatensis as antimalarial candidates. These compounds are predicted to inhibit P. falciparum protein kinase 4 (PK4), an essential enzyme for parasite survival, while exhibiting lower toxicity and potentially inhibiting both blood-stage growth and transmission. ADME analysis, molecular docking, and MD simulation support their potential, warranting in vitro and in vivo validation.

articles.headline: DANet lightweight neural network for automated malaria diagnosis
articles.title_original: DANet a lightweight dilated attention network for malaria parasite detection.
articles.details: DANet, a lightweight neural network (2.3 million parameters), was validated on 27,558 blood smear images from the NIH Malaria Dataset (150 infected, 50 healthy individuals) for P. falciparum detection. The model achieved F1-score 97.86%, accuracy 97.95%, and AUC-PR 0.98. Its efficiency enables deployment on edge devices like Raspberry Pi 4, providing a practical diagnostic tool for resource-constrained settings.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccine uptake drivers and barriers in Cameroon
articles.title_original: Caregivers' practices and factors associated with malaria vaccine uptake among under-five children in the Tiko Health District, Cameroon: A community based cross-sectional study.
articles.details: A community-based cross-sectional study of 410 caregivers in Tiko Health District, Cameroon assessed RTS,S malaria vaccine uptake among children aged 0–5. Only 32.2% of children were vaccinated; 72.0% completed doses. Factors increasing uptake included female caregivers (aOR 4.16), biological parents (aOR 11.44), income 89–179 USD (aOR 2.76), trust in health workers (aOR 6.12), and healthcare provider information (aOR 7.60). Prior malaria infection decreased uptake (aOR 0.31).

articles.headline: Ceiling-mounted mosquito nets reduce indoor Anopheles in Amazon
articles.title_original: Efficacy of ceiling-mounted mosquito nets for malaria vector control in a Peruvian Amazon riverine community: A stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial.
articles.details: A stepped-wedge cluster-randomized trial of 69 households in the Peruvian Amazon tested ceiling-mounted mosquito nets for vector control. Renovated households showed 55% reduction in indoor Anopheles counts (95% CI 33–74%, p=0.004), 60% reduction in indoor bites per person per night (95% CI 27–78%, p=0.003), and 61% reduction in indoor bites per person per hour (95% CI 15–83%, p=0.018). Peridomestic indices did not differ significantly.

articles.headline: Tfh2 and Tfh1-CCR7+ cells linked to protective malaria antibodies
articles.title_original: Tfh2 and a subset of Tfh1 cells associate with antibody-mediated immunity to malaria.
articles.details: This study mapped CD4+ T follicular helper (Tfh) cell subsets during controlled human malaria infection using single-cell RNA-Seq and VDJ-Seq. Researchers identified distinct Tfh1 subsets differing in CCR7 expression. During infection, Tfh1-CCR7+, Tfh1-CCR7-, and Tfh2 cells all clonally expanded and became activated; however, only Tfh1-CCR7+ and Tfh2 cells positively associated with protective antibody production, revealing greater functional complexity in Tfh-mediated antimalarial immunity.

articles.headline: Nanozyme test detects transmission-competent P. falciparum gametocytes
articles.title_original: Evaluation of Malaria Parasite Transmission Competency Using a Nanozyme-Based Immunodiagnostic Targeting Female Gamete Antigen Release.
articles.details: Researchers developed a nanozyme-based immunoassay detecting PfG377 antigen released during Plasmodium falciparum gametocyte activation. Validation with cultured parasites showed higher PfG377 levels in activated cultures and those treated with transmission-blocking drugs. Patient samples treated with primaquine showed significantly reduced PfG377 signals 2 days post-treatment, suggesting the assay can identify transmission-competent individuals and support malaria elimination strategies.

articles.headline: Sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine inferior to dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine for pregnancy malaria
articles.title_original: Recurrent Plasmodium falciparum parasitemia and drug resistance mutations during intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy in Uganda.
articles.details: This trial compared three intermittent preventive treatment (IPTp) regimens in 771 pregnant Ugandan women: monthly sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP), dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP), or DP+SP. SP showed significantly higher 28-day cumulative risks of recurrent parasitemia (57.8% vs 4.1-3.9%), symptomatic malaria (9.3% vs 1.1-0.3%), and recrudescence (40.1% vs 2.0-0.8%) compared to DP-containing regimens. Additionally, SP use selected for increased prevalence of high-level resistance mutations (PfDHFR I164L and PfDHPS A581G: 24.9% to 35.2%), leading authors to question SP's continued value.
articles.notable_reason: Challenges SP for pregnancy malaria; demonstrates DP superiority

articles.headline: Correction to prior malaria-associated kidney injury study
articles.title_original: Correction: Simião et al. Involvement of Inflammatory Cytokines, Renal NaPi-IIa Cotransporter, and TRAIL Induced-Apoptosis in Experimental Malaria-Associated Acute Kidney Injury. Pathogens 2024, 13, 376.
articles.details: This is a published correction to a prior study on inflammatory cytokines, renal NaPi-IIa cotransporter, and apoptosis in experimental malaria-associated acute kidney injury. No new findings are presented in this correction notice.

articles.headline: Zanzibari communities lack education on acquired immunity, rebound malaria
articles.title_original: Reconsidering rebound malaria: Zanzibari voices on acquired immunity, risk, and history.
articles.details: This qualitative study of 98 Zanzibari residents revealed limited public awareness of acquired immunity and rebound malaria despite 70 years of international interventions since the 1950s. Archival and oral evidence showed no prior researcher communication of these concepts to communities. However, when provided visual education tools and brief five-to-ten-minute conversations, public understanding improved, and participants valued the information. Authors call for ethical malaria interventions with transparent risk disclosure and community autonomy.

articles.headline: Simple strategy to reduce infant malaria infection
articles.title_original: A simple strategy to cut malaria infection rates in babies.
articles.details: No abstract provided. Based on title alone, this paper proposes a strategy to reduce malaria infection rates in infants. Further details cannot be determined without the abstract.

articles.headline: AI and pharmacometrics identify malaria-relevant pharmacogenetic variants for Africa
articles.title_original: Artificial intelligence coupled to pharmacometrics modelling to tailor malaria and tuberculosis treatment in Africa.
articles.details: Researchers developed a computational pipeline combining machine learning, large language models, and physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) modeling to identify pharmacogenetically relevant drug-gene pairs for African populations with malaria and tuberculosis infections. The approach ranks drug-gene pairs using knowledge embedding and LLM refinement, evaluates genetic sensitivity via PBPK analysis, and inspects variants with non-linear mixed effects (NLME) modeling for potential dose adjustments to optimize treatment outcomes in African contexts.

articles.headline: Majority support gene drive mosquitoes despite limited malaria awareness
articles.title_original: "Have Scientists Done Enough?" Community Perspectives on Gene Drive-Modified Mosquitoes for Malaria Control in Northern Uganda: A Mixed-Methods Study.
articles.details: This mixed-methods study surveyed 301 Ugandan community members' awareness and acceptance of gene-drive modified mosquitoes (GDMMs) for malaria control. Only 19.9% were aware of GDMMs, primarily through social media, yet 64.1% (95% CI: 58.4-69.5%) reported acceptance. Independent predictors of acceptance included favorable attitude (aPR 1.25, p=0.013), high subjective norm (aPR 1.28, p=0.004), and high perceived behavioral control (aPR 1.23, p=0.013). Qualitative analysis identified limited awareness as a barrier and perceived benefits/trust as facilitators.

articles.headline: Deep learning improves functional annotation of Plasmodium proteins
articles.title_original: PlasmoFP: leveraging deep learning to predict protein function of uncharacterized proteins across the malaria parasite genus.
articles.details: Researchers developed PlasmoFP, deep learning models trained on phylogenetically relevant SAR supergroup proteins to predict function of uncharacterized Plasmodium species proteins. PlasmoFP estimates epistemic uncertainty, controls false discovery rates, and was validated against manually curated Gene Ontology (GO) terms and experimentally characterized proteins. Across 19 Plasmodium species, PlasmoFP reduced the proportion of unannotated proteins from 15-59% to 3-28% and increased fully annotated proteins from 7-42% to 36-68%.

articles.headline: Avian Plasmodium survey reveals 23 novel lineages in Great Britain
articles.title_original: The lineage diversity, spatiotemporal distribution and pathological significance of Plasmodium and Haemoproteus spp. infection of wild birds in Great Britain.
articles.details: This 15-year surveillance study screened liver and spleen tissues from 857 wild birds for Plasmodium and Haemoproteus species using nested PCR. Overall 13.5% tested positive: 8.9% Plasmodium spp. and 4.7% Haemoproteus spp., with highest rates in Paridae (36.3%) and Turdidae families (34.5%). Researchers identified 30 avian hemosporidian parasite lineages, including 23 not previously reported in Great Britain and four apparently novel lineages. Histopathological examination of Plasmodium-positive blackbirds revealed evidence of exoerythrocytic parasites or malaria-consistent lesions in nine of 13 birds examined.

articles.headline: Mathematical model optimizes age-structured malaria vaccination strategies
articles.title_original: Age-structured vaccination strategies in mathematical modeling of malaria transmission.
articles.authors: Haringo AT, Obsu LL, Bushu FK
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: Researchers developed an age-structured mathematical model for malaria transmission incorporating vaccination with human-mosquito host interactions. The model established two equilibrium points: malaria-free equilibrium (stable when Re < 1) and endemic equilibrium (stable when Re > 1). Sensitivity analysis identified parameters influencing transmission dynamics. Numerical simulations demonstrated that integrated vaccination, treatment, and insecticide vector control could reduce malaria transmission nearly to zero, informing public health planning for sustainable malaria control.

articles.headline: High HRP2/3 gene deletion prevalence threatens malaria diagnostics in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: High Prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum HRP2/3 Gene Deletions in Ethiopia: Implications for Malaria Diagnosis and Treatment-A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
articles.journal: Can J Infect Dis Med Microbiol
articles.details: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 11 studies characterized the pooled prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum HRP2/3 gene deletions in Ethiopia. Overall prevalence was 35.64% (95% CI: 21.43, 49.85%), with PfHRP2 deletions at 8.48% (95% CI: 0.96, 16.01), PfHRP3 deletions at 23.74% (95% CI: 12.16, 35.32), and concurrent deletions at 8.14% (95% CI: 0.67, 15.61%). High deletion prevalence significantly challenges continued use of HRP2/3-based rapid diagnostic tests in Ethiopian malaria control programs.

articles.headline: Platelet distribution width predicts malaria in pregnant women
articles.title_original: Predicting malaria in sudanese pregnant women: reliability of complete blood count.
articles.details: Retrospective study of 174 pregnant Sudanese women (78 with confirmed malaria, 96 controls). Platelet distribution width, hemoglobin, platelet count, and BMI were significantly lower in malaria group (p<0.05). In multivariate analysis, only platelet distribution width remained significant, demonstrating highest diagnostic accuracy (area under curve 0.857). Authors conclude PDW may serve as a reliable, accessible hematological marker for malaria in pregnancy, particularly in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Intermittent larviciding shows no effect on malaria in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Large-scale intermittent larviciding intervention and associations with key malaria epidemiological parameters in Tanga Region, Tanzania.
articles.details: Community-based intermittent larviciding in three councils (>1.1 million population) in Tanga Region, June 2022–April 2024, using Bacillus thuringiensis and B. sphaericus with three application rounds yearly. Clinical malaria incidence showed no significant association: incidence rate ratios were 1.16 (95% CI 0.98–1.37) in high stratum, 0.86 (95% CI 0.72–1.02) in moderate, and 0.96 (95% CI 0.76–1.22) in low stratum. Authors conclude no strong evidence for association between intermittent larviciding and malaria epidemiological indicators.

articles.headline: Ivermectin mosquito killing depends on diet and blood type
articles.title_original: Optimizing endectocide and ectocide compound evaluation in Anopheles malaria vectors.
articles.details: Three in vitro experiments testing ivermectin in Anopheles dirus and A. minimus. Multivitamin diet significantly increased LC50 (lethal concentration) in A. dirus compared to sucrose diet. Ivermectin was more lethal when ingested in blood versus plasma meal for A. dirus (opposite pattern for A. minimus). Approximately 20% loss of ivermectin concentration occurred in mosquito midgut versus blood meal. Authors recommend multivitamin-sucrose mix diet to minimize control mortality without altering ivermectin efficacy.

articles.headline: Framework proposes integrated vector and parasite management strategy
articles.title_original: Integrated vector and parasite management for malaria control in high-endemic areas: pummel and pin strategy to prevent malaria setbacks.
articles.details: Conceptual paper proposing Integrated Vector and Parasite Management (IVPM) with "Pummel and Pin" strategy for high-endemic sub-Saharan Africa based on evidence from Uganda. Framework emphasizes simultaneous reduction of parasite reservoir and vectorial capacity to sustain reductions in endemicity and prevent setbacks. Authors call for operational research on feasibility and cost-effectiveness across different transmission settings.

articles.headline: Nigerian physicians lack knowledge of approved malaria vaccines
articles.title_original: Physicians' knowledge of, and willingness to encourage caregivers to partake in the RTS S/AS01 and R21 matrix M malaria vaccines roll out - a national survey in Nigeria.
articles.details: National cross-sectional survey of Nigerian physicians across six geopolitical zones regarding RTS,S/AS01 and R21 Matrix M vaccines. 68.9% of respondents knew about malaria vaccines; 29.9% did not. 64% correctly identified five years as appropriate vaccination age; 13.8% said 5–10 years; 22.2% did not respond. Majority of physicians very willing to recommend vaccines, with caregivers likely to accept. Authors emphasize need for educational interventions to address physician concerns about efficacy, safety, doses, affordability, and availability.

articles.headline: Fractional-order models simulate malaria transmission dynamics accurately
articles.title_original: Analysis of fractional-order model for the transmission dynamics of malaria via Caputo-Fabrizio and Atangana-Baleanu operators.
articles.details: Mathematical study proposing fractional-order differential equation model for malaria transmission with treatment and vector control. Used Caputo, Caputo-Fabrizio, and Atangana-Baleanu operators; proved existence and uniqueness of solutions via fixed-point theory. Numerical simulations showed increasing fractional order slows malaria spread, reduces peak infections, and prolongs outbreak duration, highlighting memory-dependent nature of disease dynamics.

articles.headline: Saudi Arabia malaria mostly imported; P. falciparum predominant
articles.title_original: Malaria in travelers and local populations: Comprehensive study of incidence patterns and origin-based classification in Saudi Arabia.
articles.details: Ministry of Health data from 13 Saudi administrative regions, January 2022–December 2023. Of 1,453,451 febrile cases examined, 0.7% (10,779) tested positive. 2022: 0.9% (6,460/688,629); 2023: 0.6% (4,319/764,822). Jazan region highest incidence (59%), Makkah (20%), p=0.046. Malaria higher in patients ≥15 years. P. falciparum 63.5%, P. vivax/ovale 33%, P. malariae 0.5%, mixed infections 3% (p=0.002). Authors conclude imported malaria predominant, requiring targeted interventions for travelers.

articles.headline: Hybrid machine-learning models forecast malaria incidence accurately
articles.title_original: Hybrid models combining trend and seasonality components with machine learning algorithms provide accurate forecasting of malaria incidence.
articles.details: Goa, India data (2010–2019) comparing machine-learning (SVM, Random Forest, XGB), time-series (ARIMA, SARIMA, SARIMAX), and hybrid models (RF-ARMA, SVM-ARMA, XGB-ARMA). Machine-learning models: RMSE 13–37 (low accuracy, high precision); time-series: RMSE 5–41 (high accuracy, low precision); hybrid models: RMSE 0.5–15 (high accuracy and precision). Climatic extremes more influential on transmission than average values. Authors recommend hybrid approaches for malaria elimination planning.

articles.headline: Novel probes enable live-cell imaging of Plasmodium DNA synthesis
articles.title_original: Vinyl-DNADetect Probes to Assess DNA Proliferation in Plasmodium Parasites using Inverse-Electron-Demand Diels-Alder Click Reactions.
articles.details: Developed vinyl-DNADetect probes based on 5-vinyl-2'-deoxyuridine (VdU) to detect Plasmodium DNA synthesis. Unlike EdU (standard in mammalian cells), work in P. falciparum despite absence of thymidine kinase. Detected via catalyst-free inverse-electron-demand Diels-Alder click reaction with tetrazine, enabling live-cell imaging without fixation. Demonstrated high-efficiency labeling in P. falciparum-infected erythrocytes and orthogonal pulse-chase double-staining with EdU-based probes.

articles.headline: Anopheles darlingi drives malaria transmission in Yanomami villages
articles.title_original: Bionomics and malaria infection rates in anopheline species in Yanomami villages of the Brazilian Amazon Forest.
articles.details: Longitudinal entomological survey, March 2013–March 2015, in five Yanomami villages, Brazilian Amazon. 1,962 adult Anopheles collected from seven species. A. darlingi predominant (52.5–87.9%); A. nuneztovari s.l. most abundant in one village (80%). A. darlingi infected with P. vivax and P. falciparum (infection rates up to 2.1%). Outdoor entomological inoculation rate 49.3–79.3 in three villages. In Taibrapa 2, A. nuneztovari showed highest nightly human biting rate (359 bites per night extradomestic). Authors recommend village-specific vector control strategies.

articles.headline: Locally acquired malaria in Zanzibar linked to housing and age
articles.title_original: Determinants of locally acquired malaria infections in Zanzibar: a cross-sectional study.
articles.details: Rolling cross-sectional survey, May 2017–October 2018, in five Zanzibar districts, 17,891 surveyed. Among 7,286 non-travelers tested by qPCR, 2.6% (95% CI 2–3%) positive. Risk factors: neighboring/nearby index households lower risk (aOR=0.1, 95% CI 0–0.1); ages 5–15 years higher risk (aOR=3.5, 95% CI 2.2–5.7); open windows (aOR=2.1, 95% CI 1.4–3.3); moderate local index of wetness (aOR=3.4, 95% CI 2.0–5.8). Bed nets protective (aOR=0.5, 95% CI 0.3–0.9). Authors recommend promoting bed nets, housing improvements, and targeting high-risk individuals.

articles.headline: Malaria parasites amplify clag2 gene under nutrient stress
articles.title_original: Nutrient stress dramatically increases malaria parasite clag2 copy number to increase host cell permeability and enable pathogen survival.
articles.details: In vitro study in P. falciparum. CLAG3 knockout parasite unable to expand in physiologically realistic nutrient-limited media but grows normally in standard rich media. In vitro selections yielded mutant capable of expansion under nutrient stress. Whole-genome sequencing revealed dramatically increased clag2 copy number without other relevant changes. qPCR and transfection confirmed increased clag2 production. Authors implicate CLAG2 in nutrient channel formation and demonstrate previously unrecognized genome plasticity enabling parasite adaptation.

articles.headline: Hypnozoite metabolites identified as potential diagnostic markers
articles.title_original: In vitro cultured malaria hypnozoites leave a footprint of specific metabolites.
articles.details: Proof-of-concept study using in vitro P. cynomolgi hypnozoite cultures and metabolomics identified specific hypnozoite-related metabolites in culture supernatant. These metabolites could enable development of rapid diagnostic tests for P. vivax hypnozoite infection, allowing targeted primaquine treatment while sparing those without infection. Further in vivo validation needed in primates and humans.

articles.headline: Malaria control strategies and innovations in DR Congo reviewed
articles.title_original: Malaria in the Democratic Republic of Congo: how support programs consolidate elimination gains and protect at-risk populations from recrudescence.
articles.details: Review article consolidating evidence on malaria in DR Congo with focus on pregnant women and children. Discusses challenges including healthcare access, insecticide resistance, and surveillance gaps, alongside innovations like genomic surveillance, RDTs, and mHealth. Recommends integrated approaches including RTS,S and R21 vaccines, LLINs, community engagement, and strengthened partnerships.

articles.headline: Memory T cells switch from immune suppression to protection
articles.title_original: Memory Regulatory T Cells Reprogram into Protective Tfh-like Effectors in Recurrent Malaria.
articles.details: Longitudinal studies in humans and mice showed memory regulatory T cells (mTregs) undergo antigen-driven expansion and epigenetic reprogramming during P. vivax reinfection, switching from Foxp3+ immunosuppressive cells to Bcl6+ Tfh-like effectors. This transition enhances germinal center responses and parasite-specific antibody production, facilitating parasite control. Precluding mTreg-to-Tfh differentiation abolished protection.

articles.headline: TEP immune proteins compared in malaria and schistosomiasis vectors
articles.title_original: Comparative immunological roles of TEP1 in Anopheles gambiae and Biomphalaria glabrata: implications for malaria and schistosomiasis control.
articles.details: Review article comparing thioester-containing proteins (TEP1) in A. gambiae (malaria vector) and B. glabrata (schistosomiasis vector). Discusses pathogen opsonization, ROS generation, phagocytic elimination, and melanization responses. Explores evolutionary roles in host-parasite coevolution and potential applications for novel vector intervention and disease prevention strategies.

articles.headline: Molecular markers identify imported malaria cases in elimination settings
articles.title_original: Molecular Characterization of Plasmodium Species to Strengthen Malaria Surveillance in Migrant Populations in Honduras.
articles.details: Analysis of 14 molecular markers (6 P. falciparum, 8 P. vivax) in migrant versus local subjects in Honduras nearing elimination. P. falciparum retained wild-type chloroquine-susceptible pfcrt but showed distinct genotypes in pfmdr1, pfama1, pfglurp, pfs47 in migrants. P. vivax showed novel pvcsp haplotypes and first VK247 variant detected in Honduras. Markers effectively inferred geographic origin.

articles.headline: Caregiver knowledge and insurance increase timely malaria treatment uptake
articles.title_original: Determinants of timely malaria treatment among under-five children attending public health facilities in Kisumu East sub-county, Kenya: a health facility-based cross-sectional study.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 434 caregivers in Kenya found that caregivers' ability to recognize malaria symptoms increased odds of timely treatment 2.9-fold (AOR=2.9, p=0.006), and belief in appropriate drugs increased odds 6.9-fold (AOR=6.9, p<0.0001). Health insurance increased odds 2.1-fold (AOR=2.1, p=0.005), while herbalist visits (AOR=0.1) and drug side-effect fears (AOR=0.5) reduced odds. Timely treatment was defined as care within 24 hours of symptom onset.

articles.headline: Single low-dose primaquine safe in G6PD-deficient malaria patients
articles.title_original: Plasma folate dynamics in Plasmodium falciparum-infected African children treated with artemisinin combination therapy and single low-dose primaquine or placebo.
articles.details: Randomized placebo-controlled trial of 408 children (6 months-11 years) in Uganda and Congo treated with single low-dose primaquine (0.25 mg/kg) or placebo plus artemether-lumefantrine or dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine. Plasma folate measured at days 0, 3, 7, 28. Mean baseline folate ~10.9 ng/ml in both groups. SLDPQ and G6PD status did not compromise post-treatment folate concentrations, supporting SLDPQ use without G6PD testing.

articles.headline: Abbott-Bioline RDT critically underperforms; unfit for malaria diagnosis
articles.title_original: Investigating the Abbott-Bioline™ malaria antigen Pf/Pv rapid diagnostic test.
articles.details: Field comparison (October 2024-January 2025) on Thailand-Myanmar border with microscopy and First Response RDT found Abbott-Bioline sensitivity 0.18 for P. falciparum and 0.44 for P. vivax, detecting only 45% of falciparum and 74% of vivax cases. First Response RDT achieved 89-90% sensitivity for P. falciparum and 59-84% for P. vivax. Bioline RDT is not fit for purpose and should be replaced.
articles.notable_reason: Critical RDT quality failure affecting global malaria diagnosis

articles.headline: Artesunate-amodiaquine maintains efficacy in Ghanaian children with malaria
articles.title_original: Efficacy of artesunate-amodiaquine for the treatment of uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria among children aged 2-11 years in the Asante Akim North Municipality, Ghana: a single-arm open trial.
articles.details: Single-arm prospective trial (July 2018-June 2019) of 173 Ghanaian children aged 2-11 years with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria treated with age-dosed artesunate-amodiaquine. All 164 completing treatment achieved parasite clearance by day 3. Adequate clinical and parasitological response (ACPR) 92.07% (mITT) and 100% (Kaplan-Meier) by day 28. No pfk13 mutations detected, confirming continued first-line treatment efficacy.

articles.headline: Plasmodium basal body uses unique microtubule singlet-to-doublet transition
articles.title_original: A basal body microtubule singlet-to-doublet transition in Plasmodium male gametogenesis.
articles.details: High-resolution imaging study (iU-ExM and cryo-ET) of P. yoelii male gametes revealed unique microtubule singlet-to-doublet transition in basal body, distinct from canonical triplet-to-doublet in other organisms. δ-Tubulin and ε-Tubulin expressed in male gametocytes regulate axoneme formation. δ-Tubulin localizes to proximal B-tubule and modulates B-tubule assembly, advancing understanding of parasite gametogenesis mechanisms.

articles.headline: Novel serological biomarkers show promise for malaria diagnosis
articles.title_original: Evaluation of GDH and HDP as novel serological biomarkers for Plasmodium falciparum malaria diagnosis across varying parasitemia and transmission settings in India.
articles.details: ELISA study of 928 plasma samples (886 P. falciparum-positive) evaluated antibody responses to novel biomarkers glutamate dehydrogenase (GDH) and heam detoxification protein (HDP) versus conventional antigens. ROC analysis showed high diagnostic accuracy (>96% AUC) for detecting P. falciparum infection. GDH2 and HRP2 effectively distinguished parasitemia levels; GDH2, HDP2, and LDH distinguished endemicity levels. Further refinement needed.

articles.headline: CYP2D6 enzyme activity shapes P. vivax infection patterns by age
articles.title_original: How CYP2D6 Polymorphism Modulates the Community-Wide Risk of Plasmodium vivax Infection: A Panel Study in Amazonian Brazil.
articles.details: Community-based panel study of ~1300 individuals genotyped for CYP2D6, assessed repeatedly for P. vivax over 4 years using molecular diagnosis found that children with impaired CYP2D6 activity had higher infection frequency, but this pattern reversed in adolescents with normal enzyme activity (p=0.0008). Adults with impaired CYP2D6 had lower parasite densities (p=0.0383). These findings suggest CYP2D6 modulates infection dynamics and immunity development.

articles.headline: mRNA COVID-19 vaccine response unimpaired by P. falciparum parasitemia
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum Parasitemia Does Not Diminish Neutralizing Antibody Responses After mRNA COVID-19 Booster Vaccination in HIV-infected Adults.
articles.details: Asymptomatic HIV-infected adults with and without P. falciparum parasitemia received monovalent or bivalent mRNA-1273 COVID-19 booster vaccines. Both parasitemic and non-parasitemic participants achieved ID50 geometric mean titers >22,000 at 1 month post-boost, well above the 1000 threshold associated with 96% COVID-19 efficacy. Neutralizing antibody responses were unimpaired by concurrent asymptomatic malaria parasitemia.

articles.headline: Novel IgG3 allele associated with fewer P. vivax infections in PNG children
articles.title_original: Association of a Novel IgG3 Allele With Malaria in Children From the Sepik Region of Papua New Guinea.
articles.details: Longitudinal cohort study (N=203) of children aged 1–3 years from East Sepik, Papua New Guinea, investigated IgG1 and IgG3 alleles via PCR and Sanger sequencing. Seventy-eight percent were heterozygous or homozygous for IGHG3*30 (G3m29), a novel IgG3 allele with a long 4-exon hinge region. G3m29 carriers had significantly fewer P. spp infections (β=–1.736, p=0.038), particularly P. vivax asymptomatic infections (1 fewer over 18 months, β=–1.06, p=0.028). G3m29 carriers also had lower IgG levels to P. vivax vaccine candidate proteins.

articles.headline: Malaria-induced neutrophil shift linked to Burkitt lymphoma development
articles.title_original: The Impact of Malaria-Induced Neutrophil Subset Shift and a Link to Burkitt Lymphoma.
articles.details: Multiparameter flow cytometry study of neutrophil subsets in malaria-exposed children, children with Burkitt lymphoma, and malaria-naive controls from Western Kenya. Malaria-exposed children showed increased frequencies of aged neutrophil subsets with reduced mature frequencies compared to naive children. A strong positive correlation (rs=0.7, p<0.0001) was found in immature neutrophil frequency between malaria-exposed healthy and BL children, suggesting malaria-driven expansion of this subset. This neutrophil shift may contribute to the immunopathology associated with Burkitt lymphoma.

articles.headline: CryoEM reveals P. falciparum apicoplast DNA polymerase structural multitasking
articles.title_original: Structural basis of multitasking by the apicoplast DNA polymerase from Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.details: CryoEM structural study solved five pre-chemistry conformational states of P. falciparum apicoplast DNA polymerase (apPol) bound to DNA and nucleotide substrates. The structure revealed that apPol accommodates nascent base pairs with an open-configuration fingers state, facilitating lesion bypass activity, while containing a nascent base pair checkpoint that preferentially selects Watson-Crick pairs for replicative synthesis. These structural features explain how apPol balances both replicative and lesion bypass DNA synthesis in a single protein.

articles.headline: FLAME trial: focal mass drug administration for P. vivax elimination in Peru
articles.title_original: FocaL mass drug administration for Plasmodium vivax malaria elimination (FLAME): study protocol for an open-label cluster randomized controlled trial in Peru.
articles.details: Three-year cluster randomized controlled trial in Loreto, Peru (N=30 low-transmission communities, API <250/1000), comparing focal mass drug administration (fMDA) to control. High-risk individuals within 200 m of prior P. vivax cases received three fMDA cycles over 2 years: age ≥16 received 25 mg/kg chloroquine plus 300 mg tafenoquine; younger ages received 25 mg/kg chloroquine plus 7 days 0.5 mg/kg/day primaquine. Primary outcome is cumulative incidence of symptomatic P. vivax malaria; trial powered to detect ≥68% relative reduction.

articles.headline: Institutional quality and aid flows drive malaria burden in sub-Saharan Africa
articles.title_original: Institutional quality, aid flows, and malaria burden: a geospatial analysis of sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.details: Geospatial analysis of 38 sub-Saharan African countries (2010–2022) using spatial econometric models examined relationships between institutional quality, aid flows, and malaria burden (cases/deaths per 1000 population). Overall malaria burden decreased across the region with significant spatial autocorrelation (Moran's I). Increased health worker density, institutional effectiveness, and aid levels were positively associated with malaria burden. Malaria burden was driven primarily by local conditions with limited spillover effects from neighboring countries.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence in Ghana children exceeds national elimination target
articles.title_original: Prevalence of malaria infection and associated factors among children under five years attending health facilities in the Nandom Municipality, Upper West Region, Ghana.
articles.details: Facility-based cross-sectional study in Nandom Municipality, Ghana (N=404 children with febrile illness), July 2024. Malaria prevalence by microscopy was 26.0% (95% CI 21.9–30.5). Children from Gengenkpe were 4.58 times more likely to test positive (aOR 4.58, 95% CI 1.58–13.27); those not sleeping under ITNs had 2.01 times higher odds (aOR 2.01, 95% CI 1.02–3.93); travel >30 minutes to pharmacy increased odds by 2.19 (aOR 2.19, 95% CI 1.08–4.45). Prevalence exceeds Ghana's 2024 target of 9.7%.

articles.headline: RTS,S malaria vaccine successfully introduced in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi
articles.title_original: POST introduction evaluation (PIE) of the malaria vaccine introduced in three pilot countries (Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi) in 2021.
articles.details: WHO post-introduction evaluation of RTS,S/AS01E malaria vaccine following 2019 introduction in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi. Semi-structured interviews with immunization staff and caregivers, plus observations of vaccination sessions. Malaria vaccine coverage at 24–30 months post-introduction: dose 1 (69–91%), dose 2 (62–82%), dose 3 (58–81%); dose 4 coverage lower due to fewer eligible children. Successful introduction achieved through early stakeholder involvement, staff training, community health workers, defaulter tracing, and sustained social mobilization.

articles.headline: Multiple barriers hinder malaria prevention for pregnant women in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Facilitators and barriers of malaria prevention and treatment services to pregnant women in Ethiopia: a multi-level health system analysis, 2025.
articles.details: Qualitative multi-level study in Ethiopia with 49 stakeholders analyzed facilitators and barriers to malaria services for pregnant women. Facilitators included prioritized bed net distribution, NGO partnerships, expanded antenatal care time, and community engagement. Barriers included chloroquine shortages, fragmented service delivery, staff shortages, and policy gaps including absence of WHO-recommended intermittent preventive treatment (IPTp). Recommendations include strengthening supply chains, adopting integrated one-stop-shop ANC models, piloting IPTp, and improving surveillance systems with pregnancy-specific indicators.

articles.headline: Correction: Anopheles malaria transmission in Colombian gold-mining areas
articles.title_original: Correction: Anopheles species associated with malaria transmission in gold-mining areas of northwest Colombia.
articles.details: Correction to article examining Anopheles species associated with malaria transmission in gold-mining areas of northwest Colombia.

articles.headline: Vitamin E and iron deficiency associated with malaria in Rwanda
articles.title_original: Associations between undernutrition and malaria infection: a case-control study from Rwanda.
articles.details: Case-control study from Rwanda (9 endemic districts, Nov 2021–Dec 2023) comparing malaria cases to malaria-free household controls, assessing nutrient intake and stunting. High micronutrient deficiency risks observed (50–80%): vitamins A, B2, B12, calcium, zinc, and selenium. Vitamin E deficiency (aOR 7.46, 95% CI 4.43–12.58, p<0.001) and iron deficiency (aOR 1.80, 95% CI 1.11–2.93, p=0.017) were independently associated with malaria infection. Age, female sex, and selenium deficiency were protective factors.

articles.headline: Test-treat-track malaria policy tracking adherence remains low in Ghana
articles.title_original: Adherence to the test, treat and track malaria policy among selected health facilities in Ghana: the clients' perspective.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study (N=590 patients, 30 facilities, 3 epidemiological zones, Nov 2019) of WHO test-treat-track policy adherence in Ghana. Southern zone facilities had higher T3 adherence than middle zone (aOR 2.87, 95% CI 1.7–4.8, p<0.001). Males were less likely to return for follow-up or complete home visits (aOR 0.6, 95% CI 0.3–0.9, p=0.018). Testing and treatment coverage were high, but patient tracking was very low across all zones.

articles.headline: Socio-economic factors drive malaria transmission in Indian districts
articles.title_original: Determinants of malaria transmission in Indian districts in 2018: insights from ensemble models.
articles.authors: Sam AK, Khatoon A, Phuleria HC
articles.details: District-wide analysis in India using Random Forest and Poisson regression identified spatial malaria hotspots in central and northeastern districts (API/AFI clusters). Socio-economically marginalized communities with water inaccessibility and no mobile phone access showed higher transmission. Mother's education level positively associated with reduced transmission. Models suggest targeted interventions in clusters of high-burden districts could improve elimination efforts.

articles.headline: Mass malaria testing campaign maintains immunity despite infection reduction
articles.title_original: Increased Plasmodium falciparum EBA175RIII-V antibody titres despite a 13-month malaria mass testing, treatment, and tracking intervention in a high malaria endemic community in Ghana.
articles.details: Longitudinal study of 314 Ghanaians (6 months–90 years) over 13 months with quarterly mass testing/treatment/tracking. P. falciparum infection prevalence decreased from 56.2% to 46.8%; 67.5% of participants infected at least once. Anti-PfEBA175RIII-V IgG antibody titers increased and reached highest levels at end of study, with older age and infection associated with higher titers. Mass testing did not significantly reduce naturally acquired malaria immunity markers.

articles.headline: Elevated ESR marker associated with malaria disease severity
articles.title_original: A systematic review and meta-analysis of Plasmodium infections and erythrocyte sedimentation rate in relation to disease severity.
articles.details: Meta-analysis of 19 studies showed malaria patients have significantly higher erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) than non-malarial controls (SMD 3.00, 95% CI 0.53–5.47, P=0.0171). Severe malaria cases had higher ESR than non-severe cases (SMD 2.26, 95% CI 0.88–3.64, P=0.0014). High heterogeneity (I²=97.3%) and significant publication bias limit confidence. ESR may support malaria diagnosis when combined with other parameters but lacks independent utility.

articles.headline: Correction notice for bacterial infection and malaria marker study
articles.title_original: Correction: Evaluation of CRP as a marker for bacterial infection and malaria in febrile children at the Douala Gyneco-Obstetric and Pediatric Hospital.
articles.details: Correction to prior publication on CRP as a marker for bacterial infection and malaria in febrile children.

articles.headline: Pyronaridine-artesunate shows superior efficacy against P. falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Pyronaridine-artesunate for treating uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria.
articles.details: Cochrane systematic review of 15 RCTs (7,295 participants) comparing new artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT) pyronaridine-artesunate to standard ACTs for uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria. Versus artemether-lumefantrine: pyronaridine-artesunate reduced day-28 PCR-adjusted treatment failures (RR 0.40, 95% CI 0.19–0.85) and unadjusted failures (RR 0.27, 95% CI 0.14–0.52, both moderate certainty). Day-42 unadjusted failures also lower (RR 0.61, 95% CI 0.46–0.82); little difference at day-42 PCR-adjusted.

articles.headline: Liver vessel geometry enables CD8 T cells to find Plasmodium parasites
articles.title_original: Liver sinusoid constraints make random search of CD8 T cells for Plasmodium parasites efficient.
articles.details: Mathematical modeling and live-imaging study showed CD8 T cells confined to liver sinusoids can find Plasmodium-infected hepatocytes via random movement without active attraction. Graph-based simulations showed <10⁵ T cells per liver moving at observed speeds sufficient to detect infection within 24 hours, well below Plasmodium liver stage lifespan in mice. Tissue structure itself enables efficient parasite detection, challenging assumptions that T cell chemotaxis is necessary.
articles.notable_reason: Shows tissue geometry enables parasite detection without T cell attraction

articles.headline: Distinct immune and metabolic responses in P. falciparum vs P. vivax
articles.title_original: Systems Biological Analysis of Immune-Metabolic Host Responses to Distinct Malaria Parasites.
articles.details: Integrated analysis of flow cytometry, transcriptomics, and metabolomics from simian infection models and human malaria comparing P. falciparum (P. coatneyi in rhesus monkeys) and P. vivax (P. cynomolgi). Conserved responses across species: dendritic cell dynamics, HMOX1 upregulation, complement activation. P. vivax/P. cynomolgi showed enriched chemokine responses and T cell differentiation modules. Conserved metabolite regulation included kynurenine and sex hormone-derived metabolites.

articles.headline: Molecular biology techniques accurately detect Plasmodium in Anopheles vectors
articles.title_original: Detection of Plasmodium in Anopheles spp (Diptera: Nematocera: Culicidae) using molecular biology techniques: a systematic review.
articles.journal: Rev Inst Med Trop Sao Paulo
articles.details: Systematic review (PRISMA 2020) of 12 studies (from 484 retrieved) examining molecular methods for Plasmodium species identification in Anopheles mosquitoes. Molecular techniques enable accurate determination of parasitic species in vector populations, contributing to epidemiological surveillance and monitoring of Plasmodium spread in endemic areas despite challenges in applying these methods to field-collected mosquitoes.

articles.headline: Malaria elimination progress threatened by imported case surge in Brazil
articles.title_original: Changes in the malaria transmission profile in a low-endemic area of the Brazilian Amazon: a cause for concern.
articles.journal: Rev Inst Med Trop Sao Paulo
articles.details: Retrospective epidemiological study of malaria in Maranhao state, Brazil (2003–2022); 83,517 cases reported with 67.8% autochthonous and 83.1% in rural areas. API now low-risk in most municipalities; P. falciparum proportion declined from 17.7% (2003) to 6.01% (2022). Epidemiological profile shifted from seasonal to increasingly imported cases; infected individuals predominantly male, aged 15–45 (increased from 59.1% to 70.5%). Risk of resurgence requires strengthened surveillance.

articles.headline: Mathematical models predict recurrent malaria infections in endemic cohorts
articles.title_original: How do recurrent malaria infections occur in clinical cohorts: a mathematical modelling study to support study planning.
articles.details: Five compartmental ordinary differential equation models of increasing complexity (constant risk, immunity/treatment effects, vaccination, heterogeneous transmission, seasonal variation) designed to predict recurrent malaria infections in naive populations. Treatment and acquired immunity substantially reduced recurrent infections; vaccination reduced both first and recurrent infections. High-transmission subpopulations experienced near 100% recurrence rates; seasonal variation produced temporal fluctuations. Models provide equations for estimating expected recurrent events.

articles.headline: Malaria and helminths reduce hemoglobin in Cameroon military forces
articles.title_original: The impact of malaria and intestinal helminths on haemoglobin concentrations among armed forces of the North West and South West Cameroon.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 812 male military personnel in North West and South West Cameroon. Overall malaria prevalence 13.6% (P. falciparum and P. vivax); P. falciparum comprised 86.6% of malaria cases with 86.6% presenting mild malaria. Overall intestinal parasite prevalence 22.8% (5 helminth and 2 protozoan species). Plasmodium falciparum, Trichuris trichiura, and Schistosoma mansoni significantly reduced mean hemoglobin concentration; asymptomatic carriers identified as transmission reservoir.

articles.headline: Wing morphometry reliably distinguishes cryptic Anopheles vector species in Siberia
articles.title_original: Unraveling Wing Shape Variation in Malaria Mosquitoes from the Arctic Edge: A Geometric Morphometric Study in Western Siberia.
articles.details: Geometric morphometric analysis of wing venation patterns in three Anopheles species (An. messeae, An. daciae, An. beklemishevi) and their hybrids from Western Siberia using Procrustes ANOVA, PCA, and CVA. Significant differences in wing shape and size among species; hybrids showed intermediate morphologies. Radial and medial vein landmarks most consistent for species separation. No static allometry detected; wing shape differences independent of size.

articles.headline: Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons in Puerto Rico wetland water
articles.title_original: Occurrence and Distribution of Three Low Molecular Weight PAHs in Caño La Malaria, Cucharillas Marsh (Cataño, Puerto Rico): Spatial and Seasonal Variability, Sources, and Ecological Risk.
articles.authors: Berríos-Rolón PJ, Márquez F, Cotto MC
articles.details: Study measured naphthalene, phenanthrene, and anthracene in Caño La Malaria water at four sites during wet and dry seasons. Naphthalene dominated with concentrations 7.4–2198.8 ng/L (mean 745.79 ng/L wet season, 186.71 ng/L dry). No significant spatial differences but compositional shifts between seasons; diagnostic analyses indicated pyrogenic origin. Ecological risk assessment showed negligible acute toxicity risk (<0.01) but potential chronic risks from benchmark exceedances.

articles.headline: Malaria-soil-transmitted helminth coinfection increases parasitemia
articles.title_original: Integrated assessment of malaria parasite load, anemia, and associated factors in patients with and without soil-transmitted helminthes coinfection in Southern Ethiopia.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 220 Ethiopian patients (110 Plasmodium monoinfected, 110 coinfected with soil-transmitted helminths) found coinfected patients had significantly higher mean parasitemia (40,093.54 parasites/µL). Hookworm and Trichuris trichiura were positively associated with increased parasitemia and lower hemoglobin (hookworm p<0.001, Trichuris p=0.038), while Ascaris showed inverse relationship. Authors recommend integrating deworming into malaria case management.

articles.headline: Environmental and socio-economic determinants of West African malaria
articles.title_original: Environmental and socio-economic determinants of malaria transmission in West Africa: a systematic review.
articles.details: Systematic review of 68 articles from 1989–2025 identified key environmental determinants (air temperature, rainfall, relative humidity, vegetation) and socio-economic factors (education level, residence type, housing structure, poverty, information quality) predicting malaria transmission across West Africa. Results support use of these indicators for targeted malaria elimination and prevention programs.

articles.headline: Multiple first-line artemisinin combinations reduce malaria in Kenya
articles.title_original: Health system challenges and facilitators associated with adaptive cycling deployment of multiple first-line treatment for uncomplicated malaria: a pilot study in a malaria-endemic region of Kenya.
articles.details: Two-year pilot (June 2020–June 2022) in western Kenya compared adaptive cycling of four artemisinin-based combinations (Artemether-Lumefantrine, Dihydroartemisin-Piperaquine, Amodiaquine-Artesunate, Pyronaridine-Artesunate) in Homa Bay versus single-drug control in Migori. Multiple first-line treatment achieved 10.8% malaria positivity versus 23.3% in control (12.5 percentage point difference); cost USD 3 per patient. One minor adverse event reported; major challenges included logistics and diagnostic stock-outs. Authors concluded implementation is feasible for artemisinin resistance mitigation.

articles.headline: Climate change projected to reduce malaria transmission in India
articles.title_original: Changing climate and its impacts on the dynamics of future malaria transmission over certain endemic regions in India.
articles.details: Modeling study used VECTRI model with CMIP6 climate projections under SSP5-8.5 scenario to predict malaria transmission in India through 2080s. Projected 10–30% decrease in Entomological Inoculation Rate across most regions with 4–5°C temperature increase and 10–40% rainfall increase, though some western and peninsular areas showed predicted increases. Temperature and rainfall during monsoon seasons predominantly controlled EIR changes.

articles.headline: LAMP diagnostic detects asymptomatic Plasmodium falciparum with 95% sensitivity
articles.title_original: Sensitive near point-of-care detection of asymptomatic and submicroscopic Plasmodium falciparum infections in African endemic countries.
articles.details: Field evaluation of Pan/Pf LAMP (loop-mediated isothermal amplification) molecular diagnostic with capillary blood in 672 samples from The Gambia and Burkina Faso achieved 95.2% sensitivity (90.4–98.1%), 96.8% specificity (94.9–98.0%), limit of detection 0.6 parasites/μL, and sample-to-result <45 minutes. Detected 94.9% (130/137) asymptomatic and 95.3% (41/43) submicroscopic (<16 parasites/μL) infections, substantially outperforming microscopy and rapid diagnostic tests. Platform meets malERA criteria for community screening.
articles.notable_reason: High-sensitivity POC diagnostic enables asymptomatic/submicroscopic detection

articles.headline: Dengue and malaria coinfection in Nigerian febrile patients
