articles.title_original: Detection of dengue, malaria, and additional causes of acute febrile illness: The need for expanded testing, Bayelsa State, Nigeria.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 443 febrile patients in Bayelsa State, Nigeria found acute dengue seroprevalence 14.5%, malaria 42.4%, and dengue-malaria coinfection 6.5%, with dengue serotype 3 dominant. First documentation of human dengue in the state. Metagenomic sequencing of undiagnosed cases revealed adenovirus, EBV, hepatitis A, Coxsackievirus A5, and mPox clade IIb. Authors recommend routine dengue testing for febrile illness evaluation.

articles.headline: P. vivax and P. ovale rare in Cameroon highland transmission
articles.title_original: Epidemiology of ovale, vivax and falciparum malaria in the highlands of Cameroon: An integrated community survey of human infection and vector abundance.
articles.details: Community survey of 3,661 participants in Dschang, Cameroon found P. vivax 0.1% and P. ovale spp. 0.64% prevalence by qPCR versus 8.1% P. falciparum. P. ovale-falciparum coinfection occurred in 23.1% of P. ovale cases. Using a novel ovale gametocyte assay, gametocytemia was uncommon among qPCR-positive ovale infections. P. vivax and P. ovale concentrated in specific areas with higher Anopheles abundances.

articles.headline: Education and housing linked to malaria in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Prevalence of malaria and associated factors among febrile patients in selected healthcare facilities of Jawi District, northwest Ethiopia.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 420 febrile patients in Jawi District, northwest Ethiopia found malaria prevalence 41.2%, with P. falciparum predominant (64.8%). Risk factors included lack of formal education (AOR=2.452), proximity to mosquito breeding sites (AOR=43.224), and malaria transmission awareness gaps (AOR=1.384). Housing characteristics and inconsistent preventive measure use were also contributors. Authors recommend education programs, housing improvements, and access to diagnosis and treatment.

articles.headline: Glutathione S-Transferase inhibition proposed as novel antimalarial target
articles.title_original: Targeting phase II detoxification enzyme inhibition in Plasmodium for malaria treatment.
articles.details: Review proposing Plasmodium Glutathione S-Transferase (GST) inhibition as novel antimalarial mechanism. GST plays critical role in parasite survival by detoxifying xenobiotics; inhibition offers approach distinct from existing drugs facing widespread resistance. Authors argue GST inhibition in P. falciparum and P. vivax represents promising avenue for antimalarial drug discovery.

articles.headline: Low PfCelTOS genetic diversity in Vietnam supports vaccine development
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity and natural selection of cell-traversal protein for ookinetes and sporozoites (CelTOS) in Plasmodium falciparum isolates from Vietnam.
articles.details: Genetic analysis of 366 Vietnamese P. falciparum isolates (2018–2022) identified 29 polymorphic amino acid changes in cell-traversal protein (CelTOS), with four common substitutions (K100N, S104L, F117V, N119K). Overall genetic diversity was low with geographic variation (Dak Lak higher than other provinces). Biologically significant amino acids were tightly conserved. Authors conclude PfCelTOS's limited diversity and conservation support its potential as vaccine candidate.

articles.headline: TLR7-STAT3 pathway drives macrophage activation against malaria
articles.title_original: Plasmodium yoelii infection regulates M1 macrophage polarization and function through TLR7/STAT3 signaling pathway.
articles.details: Experimental study in mice infected with P. yoelii demonstrated infection triggers M1 macrophage polarization with enhanced phagocytosis through TLR7 upregulation. TLR7 agonist R848 alleviated infection symptoms; STAT3 inhibitors attenuated TLR7-mediated effects, revealing TLR7-STAT3 signaling regulation. Macrophage depletion increased parasitemia and weakened T/B cell responses. Authors conclude Plasmodium infection promotes innate and adaptive immunity through TLR7-STAT3 pathway.

articles.headline: ZY19489 creates evolutionary trap blocking P. falciparum resistance
articles.title_original: Collateral hypersensitivity between ZY19489 and piperaquine neutralizes PfCRT-mediated drug efflux and Plasmodium falciparum resistance.
articles.details: A Phase IIb triaminopyrimidine, ZY19489, induces low-grade resistance via a novel PfCRT mutation that imposes major fitness costs and paradoxically restores parasite susceptibility to piperaquine and chloroquine. ZY19489 blocks mutant PfCRT-mediated drug efflux, interferes with hemoglobin catabolism, and increases pyrimidine deoxynucleotide levels. All three agents act on the same pathway, creating a resistance-refractory scenario where resistance to ZY19489 cannot simultaneously maintain cross-resistance to piperaquine.
articles.notable_reason: Novel resistance mechanism creating evolutionary trap; pairing strategy to combat multi-drug resistance

articles.headline: AI clinical decision support boosts Sudan malaria protocol adherence to 97%
articles.title_original: Enhancing Physicians' Adherence to the 2023 Sudan Malaria Case Management Protocol Using AI as an Intervention Tool.
articles.details: An AI-based clinical decision-support system (CDSS) combined with training improved physician compliance with the 2023 Sudan Malaria Case Management Protocol from 50.7% to 96.8% (95% CI: 92–100; p<0.001) among 50 physicians at Al-Managil Teaching Hospital. Awareness of IV/IM artesunate as first-line for severe malaria rose from 53% to 98% (+45%, p<0.001), and timely treatment initiation within 24–48 hours reached 100% (p<0.001). Residual gaps in microscopy reporting and G6PD testing persisted.

articles.headline: Computational multi-epitope vaccine covers 87% of world population
articles.title_original: Immunoinformatics-Based Multi-Epitope Vaccine Design Against P. falciparum-Causing Malaria: A Computational Approach.
articles.details: Immunoinformatics identified B cell, cytotoxic T, and helper T cell epitopes from five conserved P. falciparum antigens. The resulting multi-epitope subunit vaccine candidate covers an estimated 87.07% of the global population by HLA type, showed stable interactions with TLR4, and induced memory B and CD4+ T cell responses in immune simulations. The construct exhibited appropriate physicochemical properties and solubility.

articles.headline: pfhrp2/3 deletions in Malawi blind HRP2-based diagnostic tests
articles.title_original: First report of pfhrp2 and pfhrp3 gene deletions compromising HRP2-based malaria rapid diagnostic tests in Malawi.
articles.details: A cross-sectional study of 1,582 participants from two Malawi sites (December 2020–June 2021) found dual pfhrp2/3 gene deletions in 15.0% of Lilongwe (n=24) and 10.4% of Mzuzu (n=24) P. falciparum isolates. All dual-deleted samples were false negative by HRP2-based rapid diagnostic tests but positive by microscopy (64.8% sensitivity) and qPCR, compromising RDT diagnostic accuracy in affected regions. RDT sensitivity was 78.5% (95% CI: 74.6–82.1%) overall.
articles.notable_reason: First pfhrp2/3 deletions reported in Malawi; undermines RDT diagnostic strategy

articles.headline: Urban schoolchildren have triple the malaria risk in Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Malaria among schoolchildren in urban, peri-urban and rural settings in Eastern Ethiopia: repeated cross section study.
articles.details: A repeated cross-sectional study in November 2023 and May 2024 among Eastern Ethiopian schoolchildren found malaria prevalence of 5.1% (95% CI: 3.84–6.7) in high transmission and 2.24% (95% CI: 1.48–3.37) in low transmission. Urban areas had 7.3% prevalence vs. 2.9% rural during high transmission (P=0.007), driven by P. falciparum (83% of high transmission cases). Non-use of insecticide-treated nets (AOR 2.2; 95% CI: 1.2–3.99) and presence of under-five children (AOR 1.9; 95% CI: 1.1–3.38) were risk factors.

articles.headline: Chrysosplenetin reverses artemisinin resistance via dual mechanisms
articles.title_original: Chrysosplenetin acts as a homeostasis stabilizer with dual-function in shattering Plasmodium berghei K173 resistance to artemisinin driven by both ABC transporters and heme-ROS/GSH axis.
articles.details: Chrysosplenetin (CHR), a polymethoxy flavonol from Artemisia annua, combined with artemisinin (ART) achieved 100% survival and ~20% parasitemia in drug-resistant P. berghei K173-infected mice, irrespective of Mdr1a status. CHR reshaped resistance-phenotypic disparities in ABC transporter (Mrps, Bcrp) expression (fold-change 1.35–38.03), ROS/GSH redox balance (1.02–10.18-fold), and cytokine profiles (1.14–37.79-fold). CHR acts as a homeostasis stabilizer disrupting ART resistance via both ABC transporters and the heme-ROS/GSH axis.

articles.headline: Malaria drives family financial collapse and lasting disabilities
articles.title_original: Malaria in their words: thematic analysis of online narratives from Sub-Saharan Africa.
articles.details: Thematic analysis of 160 online narratives from Sub-Saharan Africa (121 Kenya, 33 Uganda) revealed malaria's profound socioeconomic impacts: treatment costs forced tradeoffs between healthcare, food security, and children's schooling; concurrent household infections overwhelmed care capacity; and severe infections caused lasting physical disabilities (mobility loss, vision loss, cognitive impairment) leading to social exclusion. Housing structure and occupational settings (agriculture, pre-dawn labor) were key vulnerability factors.

articles.headline: Indigenous and spiritual healers compete with malaria biomedical care
articles.title_original: Indigenous knowledge systems and malaria prevention in Zimbabwe: a market basket analysis.
articles.details: A cross-sectional survey of 480 adults in Manicaland Province, Zimbabwe found 36.7% and 54.0% of individuals and families experienced malaria in three months. Association rule mining revealed strong linkages between visits to church prophets and avoidance of traditional healers (support=0.66, confidence=0.91, lift=1.08). Indigenous knowledge sources included older people (27.7%), informal discussions (7.7%), and observations (5.6%), highlighting complex interactions between spiritual, traditional, and biomedical systems.

articles.headline: Correction: KAP survey of malaria prevention among Chinese expatriates
articles.title_original: Correction: Survey on knowledge, attitudes and practices (KAP) of malaria prevention and control among Chinese expatriates in South Sudan.
articles.details: No abstract provided.

articles.headline: Cluster trials to detect gene-drive mosquito suppression effects
articles.title_original: Requirements for designing cluster randomised control trials to detect suppression of malaria vector population densities.
articles.details: Statistical simulations of cluster randomized control trials (CRCTs) to evaluate modified mosquito interventions (sterilized or genetically modified) used 5-year Anopheles gambiae complex density time series from Burkina Faso. For 50% suppression of all An. gambiae complex species, 21–22 clusters achieved 90% power; 60–66 clusters were needed to detect 50% suppression of An. gambiae alone. For 90% suppression, 9–10 clusters sufficed across all species. Trial sizing depended on target species and suppression magnitude.

articles.headline: Recrudescent severe malaria in non-immune traveler despite guideline treatment
articles.title_original: Recrudescent severe malaria due to Plasmodium falciparum following treatment in a Taiwanese patient: a case report.
articles.details: A 66-year-old Taiwanese man developed P. falciparum severe malaria (20% parasitemia by microscopy, confirmed by qPCR) with multi-organ failure after returning from Central Africa. Initial intravenous artesunate and artemether-lumefantrine achieved parasite clearance; however, recrudescence occurred 4 weeks later with recurrent parasitemia. Revision to artesunate combined with doxycycline and clindamycin, followed by oral artemether-lumefantrine, cleared parasites within 2 weeks. No pfk13 mutations detected.

articles.headline: Uganda healthcare workers lack knowledge, hold negative views on R21 vaccine
articles.title_original: General knowledge and attitude of healthcare professionals and infrastructural readiness prior to implementation of malaria vaccine in selected hospitals in Tororo district, Uganda: a cross-sectional study.
articles.details: A cross-sectional study of 211 healthcare professionals in Tororo district, Uganda (prior to R21 malaria vaccine rollout in April/May 2025) found 70% (147/211, 95% CI: 63–76) held unfavorable attitudes toward R21, with concerns about affordability, accessibility, and effectiveness. Knowledge was notably low: 99% (208/211, 95% CI: 96–100) demonstrated poor knowledge and only 1% (3/211, 95% CI: 0.5–4.4) had good knowledge. Hospital infrastructure readiness for vaccine implementation was limited.

articles.headline: Testing efficacy of Ghanaian herbal antimalarial formulations
articles.title_original: The efficacy of selected Ghanaian herbal antimalarials against laboratory strains and clinical isolates of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.details: Twenty commercial herbal antimalarial formulations from Ghana were tested in vitro against five laboratory strains and two clinical isolates of P. falciparum using growth inhibition assays. Thirty percent (n=6) demonstrated potent activity with IC50 values <50 µg/mL. Most formulations showed poor anti-plasmodial activity, highlighting the need for strengthened regulatory oversight of traditional medicines.

articles.headline: Elevated opsonic phagocytosis linked to higher malaria risk
articles.title_original: A Novel Phagocytosis Assay Reveals the Association between Elevated Opsonic Phagocytosis Levels and an Increased Risk of Clinical Malaria in a Low-Malaria Transmission Area.
articles.details: A nested case-control study of 5,753 individuals from a low-transmission Kenyan highland area (317 cases, 317 controls matched by age and village) evaluated opsonic phagocytosis with a novel assay. OP levels were associated with increased malaria risk in 5–14 year olds (adjusted OR 3.32, 95% CI 1.13–9.77) and ≥15 year olds (adjusted OR 4.85, 95% CI 1.02–23.00), but not <5 year olds, suggesting OP is a marker of malaria exposure rather than protection.

articles.headline: Two-cue sensing optimizes malaria parasite transmission investment
articles.title_original: Malaria parasites can optimize transmission investment by sensing two within-host cues.
articles.authors: Wang A, Greischar MA, Mideo N
articles.details: Mathematical modeling of P. chabaudi transmission investment strategies identified that parasites sensing two cues—log-transformed infected and uninfected red blood cell abundance—can accurately track infection progression and achieve fitness approaching optimal time-varying investment strategies. Parasites detecting two cues exploit hosts more efficiently, resulting in higher virulence, but experience larger fitness declines with environmental fluctuations.

articles.headline: Oxidative stress mechanisms in malaria and typhoid fever
articles.title_original: Oxidative stress in malaria and typhoid fever: A scoping review of the pathogenic mechanisms and therapeutic implications.
articles.details: A scoping review of literature from 2000–2024 examined oxidative stress in malaria and typhoid fever, finding that both diseases elevate reactive oxygen species production, particularly in co-infections. Endogenous antioxidant responses are activated but insufficient to completely neutralize reactive species. Antimalarial and antibacterial drugs influence oxidative-antioxidant dynamics, suggesting potential avenues for adjunctive antioxidant-targeting therapies.

articles.headline: Anopheles species misidentification reveals importance of molecular methods
articles.title_original: Diagnostic puzzle of Anopheles species using morphological vs. molecular approach: a case study in Tripura, a hyperendemic malaria state, Northeast India.
articles.details: A longitudinal entomological survey in Tripura (2019–2024) found that morphological identification misclassified An. minimus as An. fluviatilis in nine samples due to hypermelanization and wing markings. Molecular sequencing confirmed these were An. minimus with three new haplotypes. This misidentification could mislead vector control policies, demonstrating the need for combined morphological and molecular approaches for accurate species identification.

articles.headline: Permethrin baby wraps reduce clinical malaria incidence by 66%
articles.title_original: Permethrin-Treated Baby Wraps for the Prevention of Malaria.
articles.details: A double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial in Uganda enrolled 400 mother-child pairs (6–18 months old): 200 received permethrin-treated wraps and 200 sham-treated wraps (retreated every 4 weeks) alongside pyrethroid long-lasting insecticide-treated nets for 24 weeks. Clinical malaria incidence was 0.73 cases per 100 person-weeks in the intervention group versus 2.14 in controls (incidence rate ratio 0.34, 95% CI 0.23–0.51, p<0.001).
articles.notable_reason: Novel intervention achieves 66% reduction in clinical malaria

articles.headline: Temporal modeling reveals vaccine protection against repeated infections
articles.title_original: Generalized nonparametric temporal modeling of recurrent events with application to a malaria vaccine trial.
articles.details: This paper develops generalized nonparametric temporal models with multiple time scales to analyze recurrent malaria infections, applied to the MAL-094/MAL-095 malaria vaccine efficacy trial. The methods investigate how new infection risk changes over time and how prior infection or vaccination modifies future infection risk, providing insights into protective vaccine effects modified by prior malaria exposure history.

articles.headline: Low artemisinin-resistant markers support continued ACT use
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Molecular Markers of Resistance to Antimalarial Drugs Three Years After Perennial Malaria Chemoprevention in Sierra Leone.
articles.details: A cross-sectional survey in Sierra Leone (June–August 2021) tested 230 P. falciparum-infected children (2–5 years) for drug resistance markers. High prevalence of pfdhfr triple mutant (99.5%), pfdhps A437G (92.1%), and chloroquine-resistance mutations (pfcrt CVIET 36.6%) were detected. No validated pfK13 artemisinin-resistance markers were found, and low prevalence of pfdhfr/dhps quintuple mutants (4.6%) justifies continued use of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine and artemisinin-based therapies.

articles.headline: Circumsporozoite protein diversity challenges vaccine development
articles.title_original: Genetic diversity and natural selection of circumsporozoite surface protein in Vietnam Plasmodium falciparum isolates.
articles.details: Sequencing of 95 pfcsp genes from P. falciparum isolates in five Vietnamese endemic provinces (2018–2022) with comparative analysis of 836 global sequences identified significant polymorphisms including A98G and a 19-amino-acid insertion in the N-terminal region, and notable variation in Th2R and Th3R regions. The central repeat region showed high polymorphism due to varying numbers and types of peptide repeat motifs.

articles.headline: Mitochondrial genome of primate malaria parasite sequenced
articles.title_original: The mitochondrial genome sequence of the Bornean orang-utan (Pongo pygmaeus) malaria parasite, Plasmodium pitheci.
articles.details: The complete mitochondrial genome sequence of Plasmodium pitheci (5,871 bp), a malaria parasite infecting Bornean orang-utans, was determined and used in Bayesian phylogenetic analysis. Previously identified Plasmodium sp. VM, VS, and Pongo clades A and B were confirmed as P. pitheci, though Pongo clade C classification remains unresolved. The genome provides a foundation for developing species-specific molecular diagnostics.

articles.headline: Green nanoparticles show in vitro antimalarial activity
articles.title_original: Antimalarial potential of Matricaria chamomilla-derived MgO nanoparticles against Plasmodium falciparum strains: an experimental study.
articles.details: Green-synthesized magnesium oxide nanoparticles (30–80 nm) derived from Matricaria chamomilla were tested in vitro against P. falciparum 3D7 (chloroquine-sensitive) and K1 (chloroquine-resistant) strains. IC50 values against 3D7 ranged from 0.19–0.22 mg/mL and against K1 from 0.41–0.45 mg/mL. Results support further investigation of green-synthesized MgO nanoparticles as potential antimalarial agents in preclinical models.

articles.headline: Malaria in pregnancy declining but remains high in Ghana
articles.title_original: Trend of malaria test positivity rate among pregnant women, Savannah Region, Ghana, 2018-2022.
articles.details: A retrospective study analyzed malaria in pregnancy trends in Ghana's Savannah Region using DHIMS 2 database (2018–2022). Of 46,734 pregnant women tested (90.8% testing rate), 54.5% tested positive. MiP test positivity declined from 60.4% (2018) to 51.6% (2022). Bole District had the highest prevalence (70.1%), accounting for 40.5% of regional cases, with highest positivity in women <20 years (59.3%).

articles.headline: Liver-stage arresting genetically attenuated parasites as malaria vaccines
articles.title_original: The path from early- to late-liver stage arresting genetically attenuated parasites as a malaria vaccination strategy.
articles.details: This review describes genetically attenuated parasites (GAPs) that arrest during liver stage development as potential malaria vaccines. Late-arresters (6-7 days) demonstrate superior efficacy compared to early-arresters (24-48 hours), highlighting the importance of liver stage immunity for sterile protection. The authors discuss GAPs tested in humans and the preclinical research underlying their development, with focus on safety and efficacy considerations for large-scale vaccine implementation.

articles.headline: Vitamin A integration with SMC increases cost by $0.24 per child
articles.title_original: A cost analysis comparing seasonal malaria chemoprevention with and without Vitamin A supplementation among under-5 children in Nigeria.
articles.details: A cost analysis in Bauchi State, Nigeria compared standalone seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC with SPAQ) to integrated SMC-Vitamin A supplementation (VAS) campaigns covering 168,820 and 170,681 children respectively. SMC-only cost was $0.94 per child (total $158,934), while SMC-VAS cost $1.18 per child (total $186,426), representing an additional cost of $0.24 per child. The authors conclude that integrating VAS with SMC is feasible from a cost perspective given modest incremental costs and potential health benefits from addressing malaria and malnutrition.

articles.headline: Intensive management strategies for severe cerebral malaria in adults
articles.title_original: Management and monitoring strategies for severe cerebral malaria: a guide for the intensivist.
articles.details: This clinical review synthesizes evidence on severe cerebral malaria (CM) management for intensivists. CM, defined by impaired consciousness (GCS <11) caused by P. falciparum, carries 15-25% mortality with long-term neurological sequelae in survivors. Pathophysiology involves parasitized RBC sequestration, immune activation, blood-brain barrier disruption, and cerebral edema. First-line treatment is intravenous artesunate, supplemented by seizure management and targeted interventions for systemic complications. The review emphasizes underutilization of neuromonitoring strategies and gaps in adult-focused research.

articles.headline: Targeting Hsp70-Hsp40 complex arrests P. falciparum DNA replication
articles.title_original: Inhibition of the assembly of Plasmodium Hsp70-1 and Hsp40 complex blocks DNA replication by destabilizing ribonucleotide reductase subunit-2.
articles.details: This molecular study identifies that P. falciparum ribonucleotide reductase subunit 2 (PfR2) requires the PfYdj1-PfHsp70-1 chaperone complex for stability and function. Small molecule 116-9e disrupts this complex, destabilizing PfR2 and reducing dNTP production, resulting in parasite replication arrest. Combination of 116-9e with benzo-hydroxamate, a catalytic inhibitor of PfR2, demonstrates synergistic activity in vitro.

articles.headline: Subcutaneous R21 vaccine with immune complex achieves sterile protection
articles.title_original: Subcutaneous administration of the malaria R21/Matrix M vaccine and immune complex formation with anti-circumsporozoite protein mAb 2A10 elicit protective efficacy in mice.
articles.details: In BALB/c mice, R21/Matrix-M vaccine administered subcutaneously achieved 100% protective efficacy against malaria with only two immunizations, compared to three annual intramuscular doses currently required. A modified R21:2A10 immune complex formulation also induced sterile protection. This represents the first application of immune complex vaccination strategies to parasitic disease, though findings remain preclinical in mice.

articles.headline: Insulin-like peptides regulate egg formation in Anopheles stephensi
articles.title_original: Insulin-like peptides activate egg formation in the Asian malaria mosquito Anopheles stephensi.
articles.authors: Phipps BL, Brown MR, Strand MR
articles.details: Insulin-like peptides AsILP3 and AsILP4 stimulated oocyte vitellogenesis and egg formation in female Anopheles stephensi, whereas ovary ecdysteroidogenic hormone (OEH) showed no stimulatory activity. The study identified differences in gonotrophic cycle regulation compared to Aedes aegypti, including lower nutrient stores and mating-dependent egg production patterns. Nutrient availability limited AsILP3-mediated vitellogenesis in the absence of blood feeding.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccine costs $20-50 per DALY averted; barriers limit delivery
articles.title_original: Costs, delivery strategies and implementation challenges for malaria vaccines: a rapid review of literature.
articles.details: A rapid review of 30 studies on malaria vaccine implementation (2000-2024) found RTS,S/AS01 was the most commonly studied vaccine (n=23, 76.67%) with reported efficacy of 30-75%. Cost-effectiveness estimates ranged from $20-50 per DALY averted, though higher costs were reported in some settings. Routine immunization was the most common delivery strategy (40% of studies). Key implementation barriers included logistical constraints, community acceptance, and healthcare infrastructure limitations.

articles.headline: γδ T-cell exhaustion impairs immunity in chronic malaria infection
articles.title_original: Exploring γδ T-Cell Responses in Malaria and Tuberculosis: Implications for Immunity, Coinfection, and Disease Management.
articles.authors: Mohamud YN, Chaijaroenkul W, Na-Bangchang K
articles.details: This systematic review examines γδ T-cell dynamics in malaria and tuberculosis. In P. falciparum malaria, Vγ9Vδ2+ T cells are rapidly activated upon infection with antiparasitic effects through cytokine production and cytotoxicity. However, chronic or repeated malaria exposure leads to functional exhaustion characterized by reduced cytokine responsiveness and shift toward regulatory phenotypes. In TB, γδ T cells contribute to pathogen containment through IFN-γ and IL-17 secretion and macrophage activation.

articles.headline: Rainfall predicts malaria risk with 2-8 week lag in Uganda
articles.title_original: Quantifying the Lagged Effects of Climate Variables on Malaria Risk in Eastern Uganda.
articles.details: Using a distributed lag nonlinear model on data from July 2018-February 2023 in Eastern Uganda, rainfall exceeding 200 mm/week was associated with increased symptomatic malaria risk after a lag of 2-8 weeks, with peak effect at 2 months. Temperature showed no statistically significant lagged association. School-aged children showed reduced sensitivity to climate variables compared to other age groups. These findings inform timing of preventive interventions.

articles.headline: Plasmodium fragile infection produces malaria pathology in rhesus macaque
articles.title_original: Clinical and Pathological Outcomes of Experimental Plasmodium fragile Infection in a Rhesus Macaque.
articles.details: Experimental infection of a single male rhesus macaque with P. fragile-infected erythrocytes produced clinical signs of malaria including anemia, altered red and white blood cell distributions, RBC morphology changes, and gross and histopathological alterations in liver, lungs, heart, and brain. Parasitized RBCs were observed sequestered in the vasculature of spleen, liver, lung, and brain. Results support P. fragile infection of macaques as a translatable model of human malaria.

articles.headline: Acetyl-CoA synthetase mutations confer modest artemisinin resistance
articles.title_original: Acetyl-CoA synthetase mutations affect the susceptibility of Plasmodium falciparum to antimalarial drugs.
articles.details: CRISPR-engineered P. falciparum lines carrying S868G and V950I mutations in acetyl-CoA synthetase (PfAcAS) displayed reduced susceptibility to artemisinin derivatives (dihydroartemisinin, artesunate, artemether) and chloroquine, though absolute changes in susceptibilities were modest. These mutations were identified by whole-genome sequencing in recrudescent parasites following artesunate and dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine treatment. The findings suggest potential new molecular markers for monitoring drug resistance.

articles.headline: Complex III performs dual roles in parasite mitochondrial function
articles.title_original: Dual Function of Mitochondrial Complex III in Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.details: P. falciparum mitochondrial Complex III performs both electron transport chain ubiquinone regeneration and processing of imported mitochondrial proteins. Mitochondrial processing peptidase alpha (MPPα), a Complex III component, is essential for protein processing even when electron transport is bypassed via cytosolic DHOD. MPPα knockdown increased parasite hypersensitivity to proguanil, which synergizes with mtETC inhibitors. Proteomics revealed MPPα associates with multiple mitochondrially targeted proteins.

articles.headline: Hydropower projects show no direct link to malaria in West Africa
articles.title_original: Hydropower development and malaria transmission: A geospatial econometric study.
articles.details: Geospatial econometric study across Ghana, Côte d'Ivoire, and Gabon assessed P. falciparum malaria incidence (cases per person) and prevalence (parasite rate in children 2-10 years) within 15 km of hydropower sites using Difference-in-Differences regression. No statistically significant increase in transmission following hydropower implementation (p-values 0.089–0.990 across outcomes and countries). Environmental and socioeconomic variables—urbanization, elevation, climate—showed strong associations with transmission (p < 0.01).

articles.headline: Novel anthelmintics identified from malaria-research compound libraries
articles.title_original: Discovery of small molecules with anthelmintic potential in the Medicines for Malaria Venture's COVID and Global Health Priority Boxes using an infrared-based assay for Caenorhabditis elegans motility.
articles.authors: Sun YU, Liu LJ, Caffrey CR
articles.details: Screened 400 compounds from Medicines for Malaria Venture COVID Box and Global Health Priority Box using C. elegans motility assay. Identified 12 hit compounds, including 9 known anthelmintics and 3 novel bioactives: flufenerim, flucofuron, and indomethacin (EC50 values 0.211–23.174 µM). Toxicity testing on HEK293 cells showed EC50 values ranging 0.453 to >100 µM.

articles.headline: Artemether-lumefantrine and artesunate-amodiaquine both effective in Liberia
articles.title_original: Efficacy and safety of artemether-lumefantrine (AL) and artesunate-amodiaquine (ASAQ) for the treatment of uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Liberia, 2022-2023.
articles.details: Therapeutic efficacy study (August 2022–July 2023) among 305 children aged 6–59 months with uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria (2,000–200,000 parasites/μL). 152 received artemether-lumefantrine (AL), 153 received artesunate-amodiaquine (ASAQ). PCR-corrected adequate clinical and parasitological response (ACPR) at day 28: AL 100% (Saclepea) and 95.9% [95% CI 88.0%–98.7%] (Sinje); ASAQ 100% (Saclepea) and 94.4% [95% CI 85.9%–97.9%] (Sinje). No adverse events reported.

articles.headline: Vector Guard ITN outperforms Olyset Plus against resistant mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Efficacy of Vector Guard®, a mosaic alpha-cypermethrin and piperonyl butoxide-treated net, for the control of pyrethroid-resistant malaria vectors: a non-inferiority experimental hut evaluation in Benin.
articles.details: Experimental hut trial in Benin evaluated Vector Guard (alpha-cypermethrin–piperonyl butoxide ITN) against pyrethroid-resistant An. gambiae s.l., comparing to Olyset Plus and Royal Sentry 2.0. Among 6,799 collected females, Vector Guard mortality was 36.4% (unwashed) versus 17.5% for Olyset Plus (p < 0.001), and 17.2% (after 20 washes) versus 8.7% (p < 0.001). Non-inferiority confirmed with mortality odds ratio 2.71 [95% CI 2.26–3.24]. Vector Guard retained 83% piperonyl butoxide and >94% alpha-cypermethrin after washing.

articles.headline: Low health facility use for malaria despite high awareness in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Care-seeking behaviour of caregivers for suspected malaria in under-five children in a Southwestern State of Nigeria.
articles.authors: Akinrinade OT, Isere EE, Ajayi IO
articles.details: Cross-sectional study among 301 caregivers of under-five children in Ondo State, Nigeria. Although 94% suspected malaria within 12 months, only 9.3% sought facility care; 37.4% purchased drugs from vendors or pharmacies, and 25.2% used herbs. Significant predictors of facility visits: female caregivers (adjusted OR 3.32 [95% CI 1.29–8.54]), urban residence (aOR 4.25 [95% CI 1.72–10.48]), proximity within 5 km to facility (aOR 3.38 [95% CI 1.03–11.07]), and good malaria knowledge (aOR 4.16 [95% CI 1.61–10.77]).

articles.headline: Monoclonal antibody targets cerebral malaria-associated PfEMP1 proteins
articles.title_original: A monoclonal antibody selectively recognizing PfEMP1 proteins associated with cerebral malaria.
articles.details: Study characterizes mAb02, a monoclonal antibody recognizing PfEMP1 proteins containing DBLβmotif (found in A and B/A subsets). mAb02 selectively inhibits binding of these proteins to ICAM-1 and EPCR and recognizes infected erythrocytes expressing DBLβmotif-positive PfEMP1 on their surface. The epitope lies in an unfolded linker region of the ICAM-1-binding site. The antibody targets a broadly conserved epitope in PfEMP1 proteins implicated in cerebral malaria pathogenesis.

articles.headline: NFATc1 transcription factor regulates LAG3 in malaria-infected mice
articles.title_original: NFATc1 regulates LAG3+CD8+ T cells in the spleen of mice infected with Plasmodium yoelii NSM.
articles.details: Study in C57BL/6 mice infected with Plasmodium yoelii NSM examined splenic CD8+ T cells using flow cytometry and single-cell RNA sequencing. LAG3 expression significantly increased post-infection, with LAG3+CD8+ T cells showing enhanced activation, proliferation, and cytokine production. Dual-fluorescence reporter gene assays identified NFATc1 as binding to the LAG3 promoter; small interfering RNA knockdown of NFATc1 reduced LAG3 expression.

articles.headline: New compounds inhibit P. falciparum DNA synthesis in resistant strains
articles.title_original: Targeting Plasmodium falciparum Single-Stranded DNA-Binding Protein: Discovery of New Scaffold Compounds Effective against Drug-Sensitive and Artemisinin-Resistant Strains.
articles.details: Identified PPG and 9-HPF as inhibitors of P. falciparum single-stranded DNA-binding protein (PfSSB), targeting DNA replication in the apicoplast. PPG showed IC50 54.95 µM against drug-sensitive P. falciparum (3D7) and resistance index 0.79 against artemisinin-resistant strain (C580Y Kelch13); 9-HPF showed IC50 9.13 µM against 3D7 and resistance index 1.26 against C580Y. Both compounds were nontoxic and structurally distinct from existing antimalarials, reducing cross-resistance risk.

articles.headline: Antioxidant nanoparticles improve survival in severe malaria mouse model
articles.title_original: Self-Assembling Antioxidant Nanoparticles Increase Survival and Reduce Disease Symptoms of Severe Malaria.
articles.details: Study evaluated self-assembling redox nanoparticles (RNP) in Plasmodium berghei-induced malaria in mice. RNP remained in circulation >24 hours after intraperitoneal administration. In infected mice, untreated controls showed dramatic reactive oxygen species (ROS) increase causing lethal damage; low-molecular-weight TEMPOL showed only marginal ROS reduction, while RNP significantly decreased ROS levels, markedly improving disease severity and survival. RNP also suppressed oxidation and fragility of infected erythrocyte membranes.

articles.headline: Economic empowerment associated with lower asymptomatic malaria in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Economic empowerment and various preventive strategies play a role in reducing asymptomatic malaria towards the end of the rainy season.
articles.authors: Okpala C, Umeh I, Anagu LO
articles.details: Community-based cross-sectional study in Ondo State, Nigeria (November 2024) among 130 apparently healthy adults assessed asymptomatic P. falciparum infection using malaria rapid diagnostic test. Prevalence of asymptomatic malaria was 26.15% (34/130). Covering water containers appeared protective (odds ratio 0.29 [95% CI 0.07–1.24]). Participants who agreed that malaria prevention tool costs (insecticide-treated nets, insecticides, repellents) were reasonable showed lower odds of asymptomatic infection (OR 0.27 [95% CI 0.06–1.24]).

articles.headline: Novel purine analogs inhibit P. falciparum HGXPRT enzyme
articles.title_original: C1'-Branched Acyclic Nucleoside Phosphonates as Inhibitors of Plasmodium Falciparum 6-Oxopurine Phosphoribosyltransferase.
articles.details: Study designed and evaluated purine-based C1'-branched acyclic nucleoside phosphonates as inhibitors of P. falciparum hypoxanthine-guanine-xanthine phosphoribosyltransferase (HGXPRT). Lead compounds achieved Ki values of 0.4 µM for P. falciparum enzyme. A phosphonodiamidate prodrug showed IC50 4.3 µM against drug-sensitive P. falciparum (3D7 strain) grown in human erythrocytes, demonstrating in vitro antimalarial activity.

articles.headline: R21/Matrix-M vaccine induces conserved, minimally mutated anti-NANP antibodies
articles.title_original: Repertoire, function, and structure of serological antibodies induced by the R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccine.
articles.details: Proteomic analysis of polyclonal IgG antibodies in 10 malaria-naïve volunteers following R21/Matrix-M vaccination. Vaccine induced polarized anti-NANP repertoires heavily skewed for IGHV3-30/3-33 genes with minimal somatic mutation, remaining static after controlled human malaria infection challenge. Vaccine-generated antibodies cross-reacted with protective N-terminal junction region of circumsporozoite protein (CSP) despite its absence from the R21 construct. NANP-specific monoclonal antibodies blocked sporozoite invasion in vitro and prevented parasitemia in vivo.

articles.headline: Early-evening mosquito transmission drives malaria in Zambia
articles.title_original: Early-evening indoor and outdoor foraging by major malaria vectors in Nchelenge, Zambia.
articles.details: CDC light trap study of 12 anopheline species in 24 households over 6 weeks in Nchelenge District. Anopheles funestus dominated (68.2%) and were primarily indoor (73.4%). P. falciparum-infected mosquitoes were captured before 22:00, with entomological inoculation rate highest in indoor An. funestus at inland locations (18.74 bites/person/6 months). Results indicate malaria transmission occurs before late-night hours in this region despite indoor residual spraying.

articles.headline: Codesign workshops tailor malaria chemoprevention to local contexts
articles.title_original: Tailoring malaria control interventions to suit local context: codesign of perennial malaria chemoprevention (PMC) programmes through the Plus Project.
articles.details: Plus Project applied participatory codesign in Benin, Cameroon, Côte d'Ivoire, and Mozambique to develop country-specific perennial malaria chemoprevention (PMC) models. Three-and-a-half-day workshops engaged stakeholders from national programs, immunization, and development partners. Results yielded four distinct PMC strategies with 4–8 contact points each, adapted to country-specific health system platforms and operational logistics. Collaborative approach increased stakeholder buy-in for implementation.

articles.headline: Anti-epo antibodies not associated with pediatric malarial anemia
articles.title_original: Serum Anti-Erythropoietin Autoantibodies and Their Association With Younger Age in Paediatric Malaria Cases.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 90 children in Ghana: 60 with P. falciparum malaria (30 with anemia, 30 without) and 30 healthy controls. Anti-epo antibodies detected in 5.6% of malaria patients, 0% of controls (not statistically significant). Among malarial anemia cases, 5.0% had antibodies vs 3.37% without anemia (p=0.640). Antibody positivity was significantly associated with younger age and elevated erythropoietin levels but not with malaria infection or anemia.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccine development: current approaches and future directions
articles.title_original: Malaria vaccines: Current developments and immunological insights.
articles.details: Review of malaria vaccine landscape in context of 263 million global infections and emerging drug-resistant parasites. WHO has approved two malaria vaccines. Article discusses prospects of approved vaccines and future candidates targeting different Plasmodium life stages. Addresses genetic parasite complexity and multiple developmental stages as major vaccine development challenges and highlights recent advances in understanding host immune responses.

articles.headline: Circumsporozoite protein vaccine approaches for Plasmodium vivax
articles.title_original: Plasmodium vivax circumsporozoite surface protein (PvCSP) as a vaccine candidate: a brief review.
articles.journal: Rev Inst Med Trop Sao Paulo
articles.details: Review of Plasmodium vivax circumsporozoite protein (PvCSP) as vaccine candidate, addressing disparities in P. vivax vaccine research compared to P. falciparum. Discusses challenges including hypnozoite formation, in vitro culture difficulties, and lack of representative experimental models. PvCSP demonstrates immunogenic potential and partial protection in preclinical studies, but clinical trials have not yet demonstrated consistent protective efficacy. Explores innovative approaches from the past decade to overcome P. vivax-specific challenges.

articles.headline: High malaria burden in pregnant women, southwestern Ethiopia
articles.title_original: Malaria in Pregnancy and Predisposing Factors in Jor District of Gambella Region, Southwest Ethiopia: A Cross-Sectional Study.
articles.details: Facility-based cross-sectional study of 446 pregnant women in Jor District, Gambella region (February–April 2024). Malaria prevalence was 15.2% (95% CI 11.4–17.5): P. falciparum 8.96%, P. vivax 2.7%, comorbidity 3.6%. Independent risk factors included non-ownership of ITNs (AOR=5.74), low altitude (AOR=5.26), stagnant water near residence (AOR=3.61), and lack of malaria knowledge (AOR=2.7). Authors recommend improved ITN access, health education, and environmental modification.

articles.headline: Limited knowledge about P. vivax in Amazonian mining communities
articles.title_original: Malária incubada: a mixed methods analysis on knowledge and experiences on Plasmodium vivax and asymptomatic malaria infections in a hard-to-reach and mobile population in the Amazon.
articles.details: Mixed-methods CUREMA pre-intervention assessment in artisanal and small-scale gold mining communities across Suriname, French Guiana, and Amapá, Brazil. Quantitative survey of 539 participants (73.5% male, median age 38) and qualitative interviews assessed knowledge of asymptomatic P. vivax malaria and radical treatment. Almost all participants recognized mosquito vectors and were aware of asymptomatic carriage and relapse risk, but knowledge about P. vivax characteristics and tafenoquine radical cure was limited, informing context-specific elimination strategies.

articles.headline: Stage-specific dynamics of malaria parasite membrane microdomains
articles.title_original: Dynamics of detergent-resistant membranes of Plasmodium falciparum during blood stage development.
articles.details: Quantitative proteomics and lipid analysis of Plasmodium falciparum detergent-resistant membranes (DRMs) in schizonts and gametocytes. Comparative analysis reveals conserved and stage-specific features in membrane microdomain organization. Following parasite invasion, host DRMs are remodeled with internalization and repositioning of erythrocyte junctional complex components. DRM interactomes show a core of proteins involved in fundamental biological processes maintained between developmental stages, with stage-specific functions occupying peripheral positions.

articles.headline: Major repeat epitope TMV vaccine achieves sterile protection
articles.title_original: Comparison of major, minor and junctional circumsporozoite protein epitopes for malaria vaccine design.
articles.details: Head-to-head comparison of TMV capsid virus-like particles displaying major (NPNA), minor (NPNV), and junctional (NPDP) circumsporozoite protein epitopes in mouse intravenous challenge model. Despite high cross-reactivity and in vitro inhibition, minor repeat candidates did not confer sterile protection. Constructs with major repeats (NPNAx20, NPNAx5) and junctional+minor epitope induced sterile protection. TMV-NPNAx20 achieved liver parasite burden reduction equivalent to RTS,S at lower antibody concentrations and was selected for clinical manufacture.

articles.headline: P. vivax relapse drives malaria illness in Papua New Guinea
articles.title_original: The probability of Plasmodium vivax acute illness following primary infection and relapse in Papua New Guinea.
articles.details: Prospective cohort of 1–3 year old children in Ilaita, Papua New Guinea (16-month follow-up) with fortnightly active/passive case detection and bimonthly parasitology. Bayesian statistical model related observed clinical cases to primary infections and relapses using seasonal patterns. Probability of illness decreased exponentially with cumulative primary infections (acquired immunity). Relapses accounted for approximately 50% of P. vivax illness despite comprising 80% of blood-stage infection force, informing burden estimates and intervention targeting.

articles.headline: Pyramax efficacy compromised by low drug exposure in resistant malaria
articles.title_original: Therapeutic efficacy monitoring of pyronaridine-artesunate (Pyramax®) in treating uncomplicated Plasmodium falciparum malaria in Gia Lai province, Vietnam from 2022 to 2023.
articles.details: Open-label trial of 120 P. falciparum patients in Gia Lai province, Vietnam (March 2022–December 2023), an artemisinin-resistance area. PCR-adjusted adequate clinical and parasitological response at Day 42 was 92.5% (95% CI 85.5–96.2). Median parasite clearance: 84 hours; median clearance half-life: 7.4 hours. 50.4% had parasites detectable at 72 hours (artemisinin resistance marker). Eight recrudescence cases had lower median pyronaridine concentrations (33.3 ng/mL vs 47.6 ng/mL in treatment-free patients), suggesting reduced drug exposure may drive failures.

articles.headline: Computational prediction of high-affinity malaria antibody variants
articles.title_original: A Single Structure-Derived Computational Metric Predicts High-Affinity Antibody Selection Against a Malaria Antigen.
articles.details: Computational study deriving a structural metric from predicted protein structures to identify high-affinity variants of Plasmodium falciparum circumsporozoite protein (PfCSP)-targeting antibodies. Method applied to CIS43 antibody, exploring >3×10^47 variants using germinal center principles to generate high-affinity variants. Framework extended to unrelated L9 antibody by maturing homotypic and antigen-binding interactions. Coupling micro-evolutionarily selected mutations with in silico screening permits rapid identification of high-affinity malaria antibodies for passive immunization.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence low among lymphedema patients in Uganda
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Malaria Among Individuals Living With Lymphedema in Kamwenge District, Western Uganda.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 154 individuals with clinically confirmed lymphedema in Kamwenge District, Uganda (71.4% female, mean age 54.7 years). Plasmodium falciparum detected in 3.3% (n=5) of participants. Study highlights the importance of integrated surveillance in areas with multiple parasitic diseases.

articles.headline: Rwanda adopts region-specific artemisinin combination therapies for emerging resistance
articles.title_original: Strategies for mitigating emerging artemisinin-based antimalarial drug resistance in Rwanda: a promising approach for managing therapies in malaria-endemic countries.
articles.details: Rwanda detected increasing Plasmodium falciparum kelch13 mutations causing artemisinin-based combination therapy resistance. Pfkelch13 A675V predominates in western regions (associated with artemether-lumefantrine failure), while R561H is common in eastern/central areas. The malaria control program now deploys artesunate-piperaquine in western regions, dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine in eastern/central regions, and maintains artemether-lumefantrine only in southern regions with low R561H prevalence.
articles.notable_reason: Emerging artemisinin resistance with Kelch13 mutations driving drug selection

articles.headline: Seasonal malaria chemoprevention costs include substantial indirect productivity losses
articles.title_original: Estimating the opportunity cost of seasonal malaria chemoprevention implementation in Burkina Faso, Mali and Senegal.
articles.details: Multi-country cost analysis of seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC) implementation in Mali, Burkina Faso, and Senegal, involving 1,147 caregivers and 265 community health volunteers during 2022 SMC cycles. Financial costs per SMC dose were $0.99–$1.51 across countries; economic costs including productivity losses were $2.96–$3.14 per dose. Indirect costs constituted 50–66% of total economic costs, highlighting overlooked productivity burdens in malaria prevention programs.

articles.headline: Multiple environmental, cultural, and economic factors drive malaria in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Malaria in pregnancy: a holistic exploration of synergistic association through environmental, sociocultural, and socioeconomic lenses in Pru east municipality, Ghana.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 456 pregnant women in Ghana (April–August 2024) using structural equation modeling to assess synergistic associations of malaria in pregnancy (MiP) determinants. Overall MiP prevalence was 21.5% (95% CI 17.7–25.3%), with P. falciparum at 14.7%, P. malariae at 3.5%, and high parasitemia in 6.4%. Environmental factors (proximity to water/refuse sites), sociocultural factors (outdoor sleeping), and socioeconomic factors (rural residence, delayed antenatal care) were strongly associated with MiP.

articles.headline: High-resolution melting analysis reliably diagnoses Plasmodium species with high sensitivity
articles.title_original: Optimization of the real-time PCR platform method using high-resolution melting analysis and comparison with sequencing and phylogenetic analysis for developing optimal malaria diagnostic methods.
articles.details: Optimization study of real-time PCR with high-resolution melting (HRM) analysis for malaria species identification in 300 suspected malaria cases from southeastern Iran. The HRM method targeted 18S SSU rRNA and achieved 2.73-degree thermal differentiation between P. falciparum and P. vivax. HRM detected 5% P. falciparum and 4.66% P. vivax, with complete agreement with sequencing results and superior performance compared to standard PCR methods.

articles.headline: Artemisinin resistance has emerged in East Africa; mirrors Southeast Asia patterns
articles.title_original: Understanding the global rise of artemisinin resistance: Insights from over 100,000 Plasmodium falciparum samples.
articles.details: Comprehensive review aggregating kelch13 mutation surveillance data from 112,933 P. falciparum samples (1980–2023) globally. Artemisinin partial resistance (ART-R) has spread across Southeast Asia over two decades and, as of 2024, has emerged in East Africa, with concerning parallels to Southeast Asia's trajectory 10–15 years prior. The review examines factors influencing resistance spread, highlighting urgent need for surveillance and mitigation strategies in Africa.
articles.notable_reason: First comprehensive global kelch13 surveillance showing resistance emergence in Africa

articles.headline: Green-synthesized nanoparticles inhibit drug-sensitive and drug-resistant malaria parasites
articles.title_original: Antiplasmodial Activity of Green-Synthesized MgO Nanoparticles Using Achillea millefolium Against Chloroquine-Resistant and-Sensitive Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.details: In vitro evaluation of magnesium oxide nanoparticles green-synthesized using Achillea millefolium against chloroquine-sensitive (3D7) and chloroquine-resistant (K1) P. falciparum strains. The most effective nanoparticle formulation showed IC50 values of 0.17 mg/ml for 3D7 and 0.76 mg/ml for K1 strains, demonstrating potent antiplasmodial activity against both drug-sensitive and drug-resistant parasites.

articles.headline: Macaque proteomic profiles reveal species-specific immune responses to malaria
articles.title_original: Comparative serum proteomics of plasmodium-infected free-living macaques in Thailand: Host immune responses and candidate biomarkers for zoonotic malaria.
articles.details: Serum proteomic analysis of 61 free-living macaques in Thailand naturally infected with zoonotic Plasmodium: 25 M. leonina (20% infected), 32 M. arctoides (50% infected, 34.78% zoonotic), and 4 M. fascicularis (100% infected). Differential proteomics identified 698 differentially expressed proteins from 9,525 total, with 36 forming immune-related networks. Six previously reported malaria biomarkers were consistently expressed across species, supporting their potential as biomarkers for zoonotic malaria.

articles.headline: Arterolane-piperaquine non-inferior to artemether-lumefantrine in falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: A systematic review and meta-analysis of clinical trials comparing arterolane-piperaquine vs. artemether-lumefantrine for the treatment of uncomplicated falciparum malaria.
articles.details: Meta-analysis of 4 randomized controlled trials comparing arterolane-piperaquine versus artemether-lumefantrine for uncomplicated falciparum malaria. At 28 days, PCR-corrected efficacy was equivalent (risk difference 0, 95% CI −0.02 to 0.02); at 42 days, PCR-corrected efficacy remained equivalent (risk difference 0.01, 95% CI −0.01 to 0.03). Adverse effect profiles were similar between groups.

articles.headline: Engineered monoclonal antibody broadens malaria vaccine target recognition
articles.title_original: Anti-Malaria Antibody Engineering Broadens Recognition Motifs and Reveals New Homotypic Interactions that Enhance Protective Breadth.
articles.details: Protein engineering of L9 monoclonal antibody, which provides 6-month protection in African children, via site saturation mutagenesis. The engineered variant L9_yd19 retained high affinity for NVDP minor repeats while acquiring affinity for NANP major repeats on P. falciparum circumsporozoite protein. In vivo challenges showed L9_yd19 protective against transgenic parasites with two widely spaced minor repeats and against parasites expressing only NANP repeats. Cryo-EM revealed novel dual homotypic interface mechanism.
articles.notable_reason: Engineered antibody achieves broader PfCSP recognition with novel mechanism

articles.headline: Splenic macrophages are key targets for novel malaria immunotherapy
articles.title_original: Emerging Role of Splenic Macrophage in Malaria Pathogenesis and Immunity.
articles.authors: Gupta A, Kalkal M, Das J
articles.details: Review synthesizing literature on splenic macrophage roles in malaria pathogenesis and immunity (1955–2025). Splenic macrophages play dual roles in early parasite clearance via phagocytosis and immune modulation. Macrophage polarization during Plasmodium infection exists on a continuum rather than strict M1/M2 dichotomy. Evidence suggests macrophage phenotype influences malaria severity and clinical outcomes, positioning them as key targets for therapeutic strategies.

articles.headline: IV artesunate produces rapid response in severe falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: [Artesunate and Severe Malaria: The Importance of Proper Treatment Steps and Laboratory Monitoring].
articles.authors: Özer D, Çavuş İ, Özbilgin A
articles.details: Case report of a 52-year-old male with P. falciparum high-parasitemia infection from Liberia presenting with severe malaria (metabolic acidosis, acute renal failure, thrombocytopenia, elevated transaminases, hyperbilirubinemia at 8 mg/dL total, elevated creatinine at 1.69 mg/dL). Diagnosed via microscopy, rapid diagnostic test, and real-time PCR. Treated with intravenous artesunate and meropenem, resulting in rapid clinical response and significant parasitemia reduction.

articles.headline: Funding enhances ACT compliance and access in primary healthcare
articles.title_original: Compliance with the Use of Artemisinin-based Combination Therapy in the Treatment of Uncomplicated Malaria amongst Primary Healthcare Workers in Anambra State, Nigeria - The Role of Funding.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study (1,536 case records, 32 PHCs, Anambra State) comparing Global Fund-supported (GFS) versus government-supported (GS) facilities. GFS PHCs showed higher ACT compliance (89.1% vs 76.5%, p=0.00), full ACT stock (56.2% vs 0%), greater staff access to guidelines (48.8% vs 26.8%, p=0.04), and supervision (31.7% vs 2.4%, p=0.00). Conclusion: increased ACT availability and health worker support enhance guideline compliance.

articles.headline: Knowledge-practice gap in malaria prevention for pregnant women
articles.title_original: Knowledge and Utilisation of Malaria Prevention Strategy among Pregnant Women in Some Selected Primary Health Centres in Maiduguri, Borno State, Nigeria.
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey (407 pregnant women, Borno State, Nigeria) found 80.8% had good knowledge of malaria prevention but 79.1% reported poor utilization; 60.9% used long-lasting insecticide-treated nets, 20.6% never used larval source management. Knowledge associated with education (p=0.001), gravidity (p=0.019), and trimester (p=0.0044) but not religion. Study identifies need for health education to bridge knowledge-utilization gap.

articles.headline: Structural and geographic barriers drive retention loss in malaria screening
articles.title_original: Determinants of loss to follow-up among pregnant women living in a high malaria burden setting in Northern Uganda.
articles.details: Retrospective cohort (1,558 pregnant women, Uganda, July 2022-June 2024) found 55.9% lost-to-follow-up; recovery strategies reduced missing data to 29.1%. Protective factors: higher education (aOR=0.75, p=0.0798), primigravida status (aOR=0.73, p=0.0275), and experiencing malaria during pregnancy (aOR=0.61, p<0.0001). Geographic disparity: Aboke Health Center IV had 5-fold higher LTFU than Aber Hospital (aOR=5.57, p<0.0001), highlighting infrastructure barriers.

articles.headline: Novel CPR mechanism escalates pyrethroid resistance in Anopheles funestus
articles.title_original: Genomic Drivers of Pyrethroid Resistance Escalation in the Malaria Vector Anopheles funestus Across Africa.
articles.details: Multi-omics study (RNA-Seq, PoolSeq, 2014-2021) across Africa identified CPR gene mutations (N70I mutation reached 0% to 80% in Malawi by 2021; 5.9 kb promoter duplication in Ghana) that enhance pyrethroid resistance when co-expressed with CYP6P9a, validated in transgenic Drosophila. Also found novel overexpressed genes (V-ATPase, tubulin alpha-1, transposase) and epigenetic regulators suggesting previously uncharacterized resistance mechanisms with application to tracking resistant mosquitoes.
articles.notable_reason: Novel CPR-mediated resistance mechanism; transgenic-validated

articles.headline: Modeling threat of pfhrp2/3 gene deletions to malaria diagnosis
articles.title_original: Modeling the threat of pfhrp2/3 gene deletions for malaria diagnosis.
articles.details: No abstract available; based on title, appears to focus on mathematical modeling of diagnostic threats posed by pfhrp2/3 deletions.

articles.headline: Mathematical model predicts pfhrp2/3 deletion spread across Africa
articles.title_original: Global risk of selection and spread of Plasmodium falciparum histidine-rich protein 2 and 3 gene deletions.
articles.details: Mathematical modeling study examining global spread of pfhrp2/3 deletions (observed in 40 of 47 surveyed countries since 2010) that prevent rapid diagnostic test detection. Model predicts primary southward spread from Horn of Africa through East Africa within 20 years. Four countries consistently classified as high risk across multiple model assumptions despite lacking World Health Organization-prequalified alternative diagnostics at scale, highlighting urgent surveillance and innovation needs.

articles.headline: First US locally acquired malaria in 20 years contained
articles.title_original: Public Health Response to the First Locally Acquired Malaria Outbreaks in the US in 20 Years.
articles.details: Report of 10 locally acquired cases across 4 US states in 2023 (first in 20 years, largest in 35 years); mean age 39.5 years, 70% male, no recent travel. Genetic sequencing showed P. vivax cases (Florida, Texas, Arkansas) of Central/South American origin; single P. vivax strain detected in Florida (3 Anopheles crucians); Maryland P. falciparum of African origin. Intensive public health response including vector control and case investigation rapidly contained all outbreaks to individual counties.

articles.headline: Micro-geographic malaria variation with school-age risk in Tanzania
articles.title_original: Prevalence and risk factors associated with infections caused by Plasmodium parasites at micro-geographic level in three villages of Muheza district in Tanga region, north-eastern Tanzania.
articles.details: Cross-sectional survey (1,134 individuals, 380 households, 3 villages, June 2021, Muheza district) found overall parasite prevalence 19.2% by microscopy and 24.3% by rapid diagnostic test. School children (5-<15 years) had significantly higher prevalence (27.3% and 37.6%) compared to under-fives and adults (p<0.001). Prevalence varied significantly by village (14.7-24.6% by microscopy, p=0.001). Closed eaves protective (aOR=0.91, 95% CI 0.85-0.97). P. falciparum accounted for 97% of single infections.

articles.headline: Super-resistant SP haplotypes emerging in Western Kenya
articles.title_original: Regional variation in sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance genotypes and haplotypes of Plasmodium falciparum dihydrofolate reductase and dihydropteroate synthase genes in Western Kenya.
articles.details: Cross-sectional comparison of sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine resistance genotypes in endemic (Kisumu, n=60) and epidemic (Kisii, n=52) regions found high prevalence of quintuple mutant SGEAA-IRNI (57.89% Kisumu vs 48.94% Kisii) and significantly higher sextuple mutant HGEAA-IRNI in Kisii (44.68% vs 16.31%, p<0.05). Dominance of super-resistant 164L allele in endemic region signals emergence of super-resistance, suggesting need to review SP therapeutic efficacy and continue surveillance.

articles.headline: Malaria transmission hotspot identified in Northern Côte d'Ivoire
articles.title_original: Malaria epidemiology in the Korhogo area, Northern Côte d'Ivoire: baseline assessment prior to a randomized controlled trial.
articles.details: Baseline epidemiology assessment (passive detection from 398,424 person-weeks across 26 villages; 3 active cross-sectional surveys) found mean weekly incidence 0.25 cases per 100 person-weeks (130 per 1,000 person-years); clinical malaria prevalence ranged 13.4% (dry season) to 43.4% (late rainy season) with highest burden in children <5 years; parasite prevalence 85.4-100% in survey subsample; P. falciparum predominant with mixed infections detected.

articles.headline: Dramatic reduction in asymptomatic malaria in Ghanaian schoolchildren
articles.title_original: Reduction in Asymptomatic falciparum malaria infection amongst schoolchildren in three ecological zones of Ghana between 2017 and 2023.
articles.details: Cross-sectional comparison (1,109 participants in 2017 vs 1,154 in 2023) across three ecological zones found asymptomatic P. falciparum by microscopy declined from 16.05% to 1.21%; by PCR from 39.95% to 17.33%. Sahel zone showed 60% reduction. Submicroscopic infections decreased from 24.35% to 16.46%; forest zone remained highest at 47.01% (2017) and 28.28% (2023). Findings indicate sustained effectiveness of malaria control interventions over 6 years.

articles.headline: Fractional-dose RTS,S vaccine maintains sustained efficacy over 50 months
articles.title_original: Sustained efficacy of the RTS,S/AS01(E) malaria vaccine over 50 months of follow-up when used in full-dose or fractional-dose regimens in young children in Ghana and Kenya: final results from an open-label, phase 2b, randomised controlled trial.
articles.details: Phase 2b randomized controlled trial (1,609 children randomized, Ghana and Kenya, 50-month follow-up through November 2022) comparing full-dose and fractional-dose (1/5 dose) RTS,S/AS01E regimens. Vaccine efficacy against all clinical malaria episodes at month 50: R012-14-26-38 (full-dose boosters) 51% (95% CI 37-61), Fx012-14-26-38 (fractional-dose) 43% (28-55), Fx017-20-32 (fractional-dose) 41% (26-53). Cases averted per 1,000 full-dose equivalents: fractional regimens 1,134-1,151 vs full-dose 353-544. Well tolerated; sustained efficacy with additional yearly doses.

articles.headline: Microbiota rhythms may mediate malaria parasite transmission timing
articles.title_original: Do microbes mediate rhythmic malaria parasite-mosquito vector interactions?
articles.authors: Riithi N, Mooney JP, Reece SE
articles.details: Integrative review proposing that mosquito microbiota mediate rhythmic variation in susceptibility to Plasmodium parasites and parasite development, given that gut microbes show daily rhythmicity and modulate vector immunity, metabolism, and direct parasite interactions. Understanding transmission timing is urgently needed as mosquito biting rhythms change.

articles.headline: Droplet digital PCR detects asymptomatic malaria in endemic Thai populations
articles.title_original: High-sensitivity detection of asymptomatic malaria in high-risk Thai populations using droplet digital PCR.
articles.details: Evaluation of ddPCR in 403 endemic Thai and 270 Sudanese workers. ddPCR detected malaria in 0.25% of Thai and 7.41% of Sudanese samples, with superior sensitivity to microscopy especially for low-parasitemia infections. Only 2.59% P. falciparum positive by microscopy, but higher by ddPCR. Demonstrates capacity to detect hidden parasite reservoirs for elimination strategies.

articles.headline: Nanoparticle-encapsulated artesunate improves parasite clearance in mouse malaria
articles.title_original: Effects of artesunate-loaded nanoparticles on Plasmodium berghei treatment in a mouse model.
articles.details: P. berghei infection in mice treated with artesunate in zinc-based metal-organic framework nanoparticles. Artesunate nanoparticles (16 mg/kg) with three injections over 24-hour intervals significantly improved parasite clearance and prevented hyperparasitaemia (≥5%), while 12-hour intervals only delayed it. Dihydroartemisinin and artemisinin nanoparticles also showed stronger clearance than non-encapsulated controls.

articles.headline: LLINs fail to eliminate residual malaria transmission in Burkina Faso
articles.title_original: Residual malaria transmission in Western Burkina Faso: Vector Behavior, insecticide resistance, and the efficacy limits of next-generation LLINs.
articles.details: Entomological surveillance in western Burkina Faso (August-October 2023) across 4 households per district. Seven Anopheles species identified; An. gambiae s.l. complex predominant (An. coluzzii dominant, An. arabiensis secondary). Entomological inoculation rates: 0.875 (August), 0.437 (September), 0.063 (October) infectious bites/person/month. kdr mutations detected across An. gambiae s.l. Despite widespread LLINs and IRS, substantial residual transmission persists.

articles.headline: Triazole amides show potent in vitro activity against P. falciparum
articles.title_original: 1,2,4-Triazole amides: Design, synthesis, characterisation, in vitro and in silico evaluation against blood stage parasites of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.details: Thirty triazole derivatives synthesized and tested for blood-stage activity against P. falciparum (Pf 3D7 strain) and chloroquine-resistant INDO strain. Best compounds: 3,5-dimethyl derivative (IC50 0.87 μg/mL against 3D7; 1.55 μg/mL against INDO); 3-Br (IC50 1.27 and 5.70 μg/mL); 3-Cl (IC50 2.17 and 2.30 μg/mL). No hemolytic or cytotoxic effects. First report of this class as anti-plasmodial agents.

articles.headline: Anti-CSP antibodies confer protection against Plasmodium sporozoites
articles.title_original: Tissue-dependent protection mechanisms of antibodies targeting Plasmodium sporozoites.
articles.details: Review of mechanisms by which antibodies targeting circumsporozoite protein (CSP), the main surface protein of P. sporozoites, provide protection against sporozoite infection. Anti-CSP antibodies (particularly those binding the CSP central repetitive region) have shown protection in animal models and humans, though exact tissue-specific protective mechanisms are not fully understood.

articles.headline: Artemisinin-resistant kelch13 mutations spreading across Africa 2019-2024
articles.title_original: Mapping Plasmodium falciparum mutations in Africa: A critical review of emerging drug resistance and implications for malaria control.
articles.details: Review of P. falciparum genetic diversity in Africa (2019-2024). Validated kelch13 mutations concentrated in east Africa: Eritrea (R622I 68%), Uganda (R561H 52%). Emerging mutations in Rwanda (C469F 36%), Uganda (C469F 59%, P441L 69%), Tanzania (P441L 20%). Partner drug resistance markers (Pfcrt, Pfmdr1, Pfdhfr, Pfdhps) prevalent in eastern (30-38%) and western Africa (44-55%). Contributing factors: drug pressure (23-35%), drug misuse (10-12.5%), cross-border movements (10%).

articles.headline: New malaria vector Nyssorhynchus rondoniensis detected in Venezuela
articles.title_original: First detection of Nyssorhynchus rondoniensis (Diptera: Culicidae) in southern Venezuela and its infection with Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.details: First detection of Ny. rondoniensis in Bolivar state, southern Venezuela, with 88 specimens identified by COI barcode sequencing. One specimen infected with P. falciparum (infection rate 1.14%). Species recently described, previously only from western Brazil. Concerns include anthropophilic behavior, early and late evening biting, presence in peridomestic and forest habitats, and detection in rainy and dry seasons.

articles.headline: Captopril reduces hepatic cytotoxicity in P. chabaudi-infected mice
articles.title_original: Captopril reduces liver cytotoxicity in a murine model of Plasmodium chabaudi infection.
articles.details: P. chabaudi-infected mice treated with chloroquine alone or combined with vasodilators (captopril ACE inhibitor or losartan AT1 blocker). Histopathological analysis evaluated sinusoid congestion, leukocyte infiltration, hemozoin deposition, portal tract inflammation. Chloroquine alone and combined vasodilator treatments reduced histopathological scores. Captopril particularly reduced malaria-induced liver cytotoxicity.

articles.headline: Routine health data can assess malaria intervention effectiveness systematically
articles.title_original: Use of Routine Health Data to Monitor Malaria Intervention Effectiveness: A Scoping Review.
articles.details: Scoping review of studies using routine health management information systems data to monitor malaria intervention effectiveness (2015-2024). PubMed search yielded 957 records; 93 underwent full-text review; 49 met inclusion criteria. Review summarizes studies by geography, outcomes, populations, interventions, and analytical approaches (modelling and non-modelling). Demonstrates routine data can complement and replace surveys and modelling for assessing intervention impact.

articles.headline: Primaquine accelerates gametocyte clearance in artemether-lumefantrine-treated falciparum malaria
articles.title_original: Efficacy and gametocyte carriage in Plasmodium falciparum cases treated with artemether-lumefantrine alone versus with single-dose primaquine in Ethiopia: A randomized controlled study.
articles.details: Randomized controlled trial in Ethiopia with 106 symptomatic P. falciparum patients comparing artemether-lumefantrine (AL) alone versus AL plus single-dose primaquine (PQ). By day 7 PCR: 5.8% parasitemia in AL+PQ vs 18.5% in AL alone (p=0.09); cure rates 98.1% and 100% respectively (p=0.31). Gametocyte clearance: AL+PQ 100% by day 7; AL alone by day 14. Baseline gametocytes 9.4%, increased to 11.3% by day 1 post-treatment.

articles.headline: miRNA profiles differentiate lethal from non-lethal malaria in mice
articles.title_original: Differential expression of miRNAs dictates the susceptibility/resistance during malaria pathogenesis.
articles.details: Small RNA sequencing of splenocytes from mice infected with lethal (Py17XL) vs non-lethal (Py17XNL) P. yoelii strains on day 13 post-infection. Lethal strain: 171 differentially expressed miRNAs; non-lethal: 220 miRNAs (vs controls); 79 miRNAs common, 53 unique to lethal, 86 to non-lethal. Target genes involved in MAPK, mTOR, FoxO, Th17 differentiation, and TGF-β signaling. Distinct miRNA-mediated mechanisms suggest potential biomarkers.

articles.headline: Drug resistance markers in SMC regimens across Mali
articles.title_original: Frequencies of molecular markers of drug resistance in the context of two different Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention (SMC) treatment regimens in the Koulikoro health district, Mali.
articles.details: Study analyzed 564 P. falciparum samples from 2019-2020 for resistance markers to SP+AQ and DHA+PQ in Koulikoro, Mali. Pfdhfr 51I-59R-108N was highly prevalent (>93%), Pfdhps single mutant (437G) ~60%. In 2020, IRNI/IAGKAS and IRN/VAGKGS combined haplotypes detected at 9.6% and 1.2%. Pfcrt wild-type increased significantly from 39% to 52% (p=0.01). PfK13 mutations including Y493H and C580R observed. Authors highlight need for continued surveillance of emerging resistance markers.

articles.headline: RSL3 and Pyrimethamine synergy against P. falciparum in vitro
articles.title_original: Synergistic activity of RSL3 and Pyrimethamine to inhibit the proliferation of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.details: RSL3 synergizes with Pyrimethamine to suppress P. falciparum proliferation in red blood cells. Similar synergy observed with Cycloguanil but not other DHFR inhibitors. Ferrostatin-1 (ferroptosis antagonist) did not rescue parasite growth, suggesting mechanism distinct from canonical ferroptosis. Authors propose RSL3 and related compounds as promising adjuvants to enhance antimalarial efficacy of Pyrimethamine and potentially overcome drug resistance.

articles.headline: Malaria resurgence in Africa: confronting the challenges
articles.title_original: Malaria resurgence in Africa: confronting the challenges.
articles.details: Review article addressing challenges of rising malaria burden in Africa.

articles.headline: Malaria hotspots in India's North East via spatiotemporal mapping
articles.title_original: Identification of malaria vulnerable zones in North East Region of India: Spatiotemporal assessment through geographical information system and self-organizing maps.
articles.details: Retrospective analysis of malaria cases 2011-2019 across North East Region using geospatial techniques and Self-Organizing Maps. Overall malaria incidence decreased consistently 2011-2019. Significant persistent hotspots predominantly concentrated in Meghalaya, Mizoram, and Tripura states. Authors conclude findings provide framework for targeted resource deployment to mitigate malaria burden and move towards elimination goals.

articles.headline: GSDME-dependent astrocyte pyroptosis in cerebral malaria pathogenesis
articles.title_original: GSDME-dependent astrocyte pyroptosis promotes the progression of neuroinflammation in experimental cerebral malaria.
articles.details: Identifies GSDME-dependent pyroptosis in astrocytes during experimental cerebral malaria, triggered by brain-infiltrated CD8+ T cells, amplifying neuroinflammation and exacerbating neuronal injury. In murine CM model, genetic ablation of Gsdme or pharmacological inhibition with mannose significantly attenuated brain inflammation and damage. Authors establish for the first time that GSDME-dependent astrocyte pyroptosis critically exacerbates neuroinflammation in CM, highlighting GSDME as a novel therapeutic target.
articles.notable_reason: First demonstration of mechanism; mannose inhibition reduces severity

articles.headline: Standard operating protocol for malaria diagnosis and treatment
articles.title_original: Standard operating protocol for malaria management.
articles.details: Evidence-based protocol based on WHO recommendations. All suspected cases require parasitological confirmation (peripheral smear or RDT) except suspected severe cases. Treatment: oral artemisinin-based combination therapy for uncomplicated malaria; intravenous artesunate for severe cases including pregnant women (all trimesters) and children. G6PD testing not required for single-dose gametocytocidal primaquine but essential for radical cure with primaquine or tafenoquine.

articles.headline: Antiplasmodial activity of Wrightia species plant extracts
articles.title_original: Phytochemical analysis and evaluation of antiplasmodial and cytotoxic activities of Wrightia dubia and Wrightia pubescens traditionally used for malaria treatment in Southeast Asia.
