articles.details: Three dichloromethane extracts showed significant antiplasmodial activity: W. dubia wood (IC50 1.89 ± 0.06 μg/mL), W. dubia twig (IC50 6.38 ± 0.04 μg/mL), W. pubescens twig (IC50 1.79 ± 0.03 μg/mL). LC-MS identified 38 compounds including new natural product; isolated Tryptanthrin showed activity against 3D7 (IC50 2.50 ± 0.05 μM) and Dd2 (IC50 3.14 ± 0.023 μM). All extracts and compounds non-toxic to HepG2 cells; no hemolysis observed. Authors recommend further in vivo evaluation of Tryptanthrin as lead antimalarial candidate.

articles.headline: Imported malaria in German emergency department: presentation and outcomes
articles.title_original: The role of emergency departments in the treatment of imported severe and uncomplicated malaria - a retrospective monocentric observational study in a German university hospital.
articles.details: Retrospective study of 24 imported malaria patients in ED (2019-2023). 22 P. falciparum, 1 P. ovale, 1 P. vivax infections; 19 uncomplicated, 5 severe cases. 20 patients admitted; no fatalities during hospitalization. Authors conclude EDs play crucial role in malaria diagnosis and management for returning travelers.

articles.headline: CD47 mediates NK cell recruitment in cerebral malaria pathogenesis
articles.title_original: CD47-mediated selective accumulation of mature Ly49h+ NK cells to the brain associates with the pathogenesis of cerebral malaria in mice.
articles.details: Compared P. berghei ANKA infection in wild-type versus CD47-/- mice. CD47 absence conferred significant resistance to experimental cerebral malaria (log-rank P < 0.001) associated with diminished brain sequestration of NK cells, CD4+ T cells, and CD8+ T cells, and lower circulating IL-10 and MIP1β. CD47 critical for maturation and recruitment of brain-sequestered Ly49h+ NK cells mediating pathogenesis. Authors establish previously undefined role for CD47 in NK cell-mediated ECM.
articles.notable_reason: Identifies novel CD47 role in NK-mediated cerebral malaria pathogenesis

articles.headline: Brain injury mechanisms in pediatric cerebral malaria: emerging pathways
articles.title_original: Pathogenesis of brain injury in cerebral malaria: emerging mechanisms and implications for intervention.
articles.details: Review synthesizing recent pathogenesis advances in pediatric cerebral malaria. Describes cascade: blood-brain barrier impairment from hypoxemia and ischemia following infected RBC sequestration; oxidative stress from free heme release; TNF-α, reactive oxygen species, CD8+ T cells cross impaired BBB causing CNS immune activation and neuronal injury. Acute kidney injury and hyperuricemia may worsen outcomes. Discusses promise of neuroimaging, electrophysiology, and in vitro models.

articles.headline: p-Cymene with Quinine achieves 100% malaria clearance in mice
articles.title_original: Therapeutic Effects of Isopropyltoluene (p-Cymene) Alone and in Combination with Quinine Against Malaria Infection Through Modulation of Inflammation and Oxidative Stress.
articles.details: Preclinical study in 108 BALB/c mice infected with P. berghei. p-Cymene 10 mg/kg + Quinine 10 mg/kg achieved 100% survival with 100% parasite growth suppression (p < 0.001). Combination produced most significant reductions in malondialdehyde and nitric oxide, upregulated superoxide dismutase and glutathione peroxidase (>fourfold), IL-10 (>fourfold), while reducing TNF (<1.3-fold) and IL-1β (<1.4-fold). Authors conclude combination effectiveness by modulating oxidative stress and inflammation; clinical trials needed.

articles.headline: Human multi-organ malaria model predicts drug response and toxicity
articles.title_original: Translation of a Human-Based Malaria-on-a-Chip Phenotypic Disease Model for In Vivo Applications.
articles.details: Developed Malaria-on-a-Chip incorporating human liver, spleen, and endothelium with P. falciparum-infected blood, maintained 7 days sustaining all intraerythrocytic stages. Tested chloroquine, lumefantrine, artesunate on 3D7 and W2 strains; all showed dose-dependent clearance. In 3D7, recrudescence with chloroquine/lumefantrine but not artesunate; in W2, chloroquine/lumefantrine stabilized parasitemia while artesunate reduced it further. PK/PD modeling predicted human in vivo efficacy and off-target toxicity parameters.
articles.notable_reason: Novel multi-organ human model for preclinical drug testing and prediction

articles.headline: Plasmodium falciparum heat shock proteins inhibited by quercetin flavonoid
articles.title_original: Comparative Characterization of Plasmodium falciparum Small Heat Shock Proteins and Their Inhibition by Quercetin (3,3',4',5,7-Pentahydroxyflavone).
articles.details: Researchers characterized three small heat shock proteins (PfHsp20a, PfHsp20b, PfHsp20c) in P. falciparum, each containing an α-crystallin domain. All three isoforms demonstrated autonomous chaperone activity with varying efficiency. The plant-derived flavonoid quercetin inhibited their structure and function in a concentration-dependent manner, with PfHsp20c being most sensitive. Quercetin inhibited P. falciparum Nf54 and Dd2 strains in vitro with IC50 values of 5.4 μM and 7.8 μM, establishing sHsps as potential drug targets.

articles.headline: Extracellular vesicles and exosomes in malaria pathogenesis and therapy
articles.title_original: Extracellular vesicles in malaria pathogenesis and Therapy: Emerging insights and future perspectives.
articles.details: This review examines the role of extracellular vesicles (EVs), particularly exosomes derived from the endosomal pathway, in malaria pathogenesis. Exosomes carry bioactive molecules that influence innate and adaptive immune responses and are involved in host-pathogen interactions. The review discusses their immunomodulatory potential, utility as biomarkers for disease diagnosis and prognosis, and the importance of 3D cell culture systems and in vivo models for studying exosome-mediated host-parasite interactions. Exosome-based interventions are proposed as a promising therapeutic paradigm.

articles.headline: Severe malaria in travellers needs specific definition
articles.title_original: Severe malaria in travellers-time to create a specific definition.
articles.authors: Barkati S, Libman M, Schwartz E
articles.details: Article addresses the need for a specific clinical definition of severe malaria in travelers.

articles.headline: Omics studies reveal insecticide resistance mechanisms in sub-Saharan mosquitoes
articles.title_original: Impact of Omics Studies on Understanding Insecticide Resistance Mechanisms in Sub-Saharan Malaria Vectors: A Systematic Review.
articles.details: A systematic review of 22 studies evaluated genomics, transcriptomics, and multi-omics approaches to understand insecticide resistance in Anopheles vectors across sub-Saharan Africa. Genomic and transcriptomic analyses revealed metabolic resistance, target-site mutations, and cuticular changes. Multi-omics studies identified novel resistance markers: CYP450 reductase (CPR), UDP-glycosyltransferases (UGTs), and salivary gland proteins, with species-specific responses indicating localized adaptation. Findings support integration of these approaches into routine surveillance for improved molecular diagnostics and region-specific vector control.

articles.headline: Essential adaptor protein for apicoplast fission discovered in malaria parasites
articles.title_original: An Essential Adaptor for Apicoplast Fission and Inheritance in Malaria Parasites.
articles.details: Researchers identified PfAnchor (Pf3D7_0613600), an essential adaptor protein regulating apicoplast fission in blood-stage P. falciparum. Using Ultrastructure Expansion Microscopy, conditional depletion disrupts fission, causing incomplete cytokinesis and parasite death. Immunoprecipitation revealed PfAnchor interacts with the dynamin-like GTPase PfDyn2, establishing it as the first apicoplast-specific dynamin adaptor protein. These findings identify parasite-specific organelle division as a potential therapeutic vulnerability for antimalarial intervention.

articles.headline: Dengue and P. vivax malaria co-infection in returned traveler
articles.title_original: A Case of Intercurrent Dengue and Probable Relapsing Plasmodium vivax Malaria in a Returned Traveler to India: Case Report and Literature Review.
articles.details: Case report of a returned traveler from India with concurrent dengue and P. vivax infection, with clinical evidence suggesting dengue triggered a P. vivax relapse. The authors note that dengue-malaria co-infection is underestimated due to diagnostic approaches using point-of-care assays (RDTs) that may miss co-infections once one diagnosis is made. The case and literature review examine co-occurrence of dengue and malaria in travelers and explore dengue as a potential precipitant of relapsing malaria.

articles.headline: Antenatal care data can monitor malaria prevalence and intervention coverage
articles.title_original: Antenatal care surveillance for monitoring malaria prevalence and intervention coverage: a multicountry analysis.
articles.details: A multicountry study across six sub-Saharan African countries (Benin, Burkina Faso, Mozambique, Nigeria, Tanzania, Zambia) evaluated whether antenatal care (ANC1) visit data could monitor malaria prevalence and intervention coverage. District-level ANC1 malaria prevalence (by RDT) and children's prevalence were highly correlated (r=0.79, 95% CI 0.65-0.88, p<0.001), with LOOCV mean absolute error of 6.5%. Malaria prevalence and ITN coverage estimates from ANC1 correlate well with household survey estimates and may be useful for monitoring, though treatment-seeking data were not useful.

articles.headline: Mosquito nutritional status shifts malaria vector biting time
articles.title_original: Biting time of day in malaria mosquitoes is modulated by nutritional status.
articles.details: This study examined how nutritional status affects biting behavior in A. gambiae s.l. Female mosquitoes received three feeding treatments: 0.5% sucrose, 10% sucrose, or 10% sucrose plus an additional blood meal. Low-resourced mosquitoes were 2-3-fold more likely to host-seek overall and 5-10-fold more likely to seek hosts at earlier times compared to well-resourced mosquitoes, which predominantly sought hosts in the second half of the night. Nutritional deprivation drives plasticity in biting time, suggesting targeting mosquito nutrition with sugar-baited traps could improve vector control efficacy.

articles.headline: PfHRP2 gene deletions and variability in Angola affect RDT performance
articles.title_original: Analysis of pfhrp2 and pfhrp3 gene deletions, and the structure and variability of PfHRP2 and PfHRP3 proteins: implications for the performance of malaria rapid diagnostic tests in Cubal, Angola.
articles.details: A study in Cubal, Angola analyzed 100 P. falciparum samples for pfhrp2/pfhrp3 deletions and protein variability. Deletions in exon 1-2 were found in 7.29% (pfhrp2) and 11.46% (pfhrp3), with none in exon 2. Epitopes 3A4 and C1-13 were present in all samples, with per-isolate frequencies of 15 and 18 respectively. The low prevalence of deletions and absence of double deletions suggest PfHRP2-RDTs are currently suitable in Cubal, though continued monitoring of deletions is essential and RDTs targeting epitopes 3A4 and C1-13 show potential for improved diagnosis.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence increased sharply in Tanzania rural communities in 2023
articles.title_original: Temporal and spatial trends of the prevalence of infections caused by Plasmodium parasites among rural community members in three regions with varying transmission intensities in Mainland Tanzania.
articles.details: A community survey across three Tanzanian regions tracked malaria prevalence among 8,166 rural residents (aged ≥6) from 2021-2023. Overall prevalence was 23.2%, with significant variation: 22.9% (2021), 20.6% (2022), and 26.9% (2023; p<0.001). Prevalence was significantly higher in school-age children (ages 5-<15), males, and individuals with recent fever (reaching 58.6% in 2023; p<0.001). Risk factors included non-bed-net use, traditional housing, and low socioeconomic status. The sharp 2023 increase and persistent transmission despite 20 years of intensive control interventions indicate need for targeted, adaptive strategies.

articles.headline: High stakeholder acceptance of shorter P. vivax radical cure regimens
articles.title_original: Perceptions of shorter radical cure Plasmodium vivax treatment regimens and their implementation: a qualitative study among stakeholders in Cambodia.
articles.details: A qualitative study among 142 stakeholders in Cambodia assessed acceptance of shorter P. vivax radical cure regimens: 7-day-high-dose primaquine and single-dose tafenoquine. Policymakers and healthcare providers valued these for improved effectiveness and reduced burden. Patients appreciated shorter treatment duration and faster return to work; some stakeholders worried about increased adverse events, but patients were willing to tolerate more side effects for shorter treatment. Acceptance was high in this pre-elimination context, though policymakers needed confidence-building through evidence generation.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence 22.5% in sub-Saharan Africa with distinct geographic hotspots
articles.title_original: Malaria prevalence and its determinants across 19 sub-Saharan African countries: a spatial and geographically weighted regression analysis.
articles.details: A geospatial analysis of DHS data from 19 sub-Saharan African countries (126,424 households) found 22.47% malaria prevalence (95% CI 22.24%-22.70%). Spatial clustering was significant (Moran's I=0.159, p<0.001), with 34 statistically significant clusters in hotspots including Benin, Burkina Faso, Togo, Uganda, Rwanda, and Mozambique. Risk factors for higher prevalence included rural residence, traditional housing, younger household heads, poorest wealth quintile, and lack of bed-net use. Lower prevalence was associated with richest households and female-headed dwellings. Authors recommend targeted interventions including LLIN distribution, IRS, health education, and housing improvement in identified hotspots.

articles.headline: Knowledge, attitudes, practices in Thailand-Cambodia border malaria control
articles.title_original: Estimating malaria risk behaviours and their determinants among at-risk populations in a pre-elimination setting, Sisaket Province, Thailand-Cambodia border.
articles.details: Survey of 402 at-risk individuals in Sisaket Province (late 2022) found 51.5% had good knowledge, 64.7% good attitudes, and 58.2% good practices regarding malaria prevention. Poor attitudes were more common among those with primary education or below (aOR=2.2, 95% CI 1.2–4.0), senior primary (aOR=2.5, 95% CI 1.4–4.8), or low socioeconomic status (aOR=1.8, 95% CI 1.1–3.0). Risk behaviors were significantly more frequent among males (aOR=2.8, 95% CI 1.2–6.8) and those with poor practices (aOR=3.5, 95% CI 1.6–8.1). Authors conclude targeted behavior change strategies should focus on improving attitudes in lower-education groups and reducing risk behaviors in males.

articles.headline: Barriers to malaria prevention services among pregnant women, Uganda
articles.title_original: Barriers to malaria prevention services in pregnancy: a multi-methods study in rural western Uganda.
articles.details: Multi-methods study of 1,644 pregnant women at first antenatal visits in rural western Uganda (February 2021–September 2022). Rapid diagnostic testing found 225/1,644 (13.5%) malaria positive; primigravidas and younger women were at higher risk. Qualitative interviews identified barriers: delayed pregnancy testing, fear of chemoprevention, financial constraints, transportation difficulties, low bed net use, and spousal attitudes. Authors conclude young, primigravid women remain at highest risk and that decentralizing antenatal services into communities may address geographic barriers delaying care initiation.

articles.headline: AI-based malaria RDT reader validated across four West African countries
articles.title_original: Evaluating the performance of an artificial intelligence-based electronic reader for malaria rapid diagnostic tests across Benin, Côte d'Ivoire, Nigeria and Uganda.
articles.details: The HealthPulse smartphone application (AI-based RDT reader) was evaluated on 106,877 RDT images collected across Benin, Côte d'Ivoire, Nigeria, and Uganda (2023). Overall accuracy was 96.8% (95% CI 96.7–96.9%) with F1 score of 96.6 compared to trained human panel reference. Recall and precision exceeded 97% for positive and negative outcomes but were much lower for invalid results (recall 84.8%, precision 42.8%) and uninterpretable tests (recall 0.8%, precision 2.3%). When test lines were faint, AI recall for positive results dropped significantly (aOR=0.02, 95% CI 0.02–0.02). Authors conclude the algorithm shows strong agreement with human interpretation but requires refinement for edge cases and varying RDT products.

articles.headline: Community-based mosquito surveillance via acoustic sensors in Tanzania
articles.title_original: "Fighting against malaria is everyone's concern": a randomized control trial assessing the role of incentives for encouraging local communities to record and upload mosquito sounds using the MozzWear application.
articles.details: Randomized controlled trial in rural Tanzania used HumBug acoustic sensors and the MozzWear app to detect host-seeking mosquitoes by flight sound, with one group receiving airtime incentives and a control group receiving none (four months of weekly recording). The control group (no incentive) performed as well as the incentive group in recording and uploading data. Community members expressed strong intrinsic motivation, viewing malaria control as everyone's responsibility and indicating interest in future surveillance studies. Authors conclude citizens can contribute meaningfully to mosquito surveillance research even without external incentives.

articles.headline: Anopheles breeding habitats and vector density in urban Accra, Ghana
articles.title_original: Larval habitat diversity, physicochemical characteristics and their effect on the larval density of malaria vectors in the city of Accra, Ghana.
articles.details: Larval surveys at 15 locations in Accra (dry and rainy seasons 2022) identified 727 potential mosquito habitats, with 475 (65.34%) positive for Anopheles larvae. Drainage ditches were the most common habitat type (48.21%). Anopheles gambiae sensu lato abundance was highest in irrigated urban farming sites (27.24% of total; 6,244/22,919 larvae), especially during rainy season (77.01%; 17,650/22,919; R²=3.46, p=0.000). Anopheles coluzzii was the predominant vector (54.4%; 368/677). Polluted habitats had elevated ammonium (3.4 vs 1.3 mg/L), lower dissolved oxygen (34% vs 52.9%), and higher conductivity (5,106 vs 2,049 µS/cm) than unpolluted habitats. Invasive Anopheles stephensi was also detected. Authors recommend continuous vector monitoring in urban settings.

articles.headline: Nanopore sequencing for CYP2D6 genotyping in P. vivax elimination
articles.title_original: Enhancing CYP2D6 genotyping with nanopore sequencing to address allele diversity P. vivax malaria elimination.
articles.details: Developed cost-effective nanopore sequencing methodology for CYP2D6 allele profiling to optimize primaquine therapy for P. vivax patients. Genotypic analysis of 90 individuals from Thailand's Greater Mekong Subregion identified complex structural variants and one previously unidentified allele, demonstrating the approach's ability to capture genetic variability often missed by conventional assays. Authors conclude this method offers sensitive, locally relevant CYP2D6 testing that can support real-time informed policy decisions for P. vivax elimination programs.

articles.headline: Epstein-Barr virus reactivation alters cytokine response in malaria pregnancy
articles.title_original: Impact of epstein-barr virus reactivation on cytokine levels in pregnant women with malaria in the west region of cameroon.
articles.details: Analysis of 220 pregnant women from three hospitals in Cameroon's West region with malaria (diagnosed by microscopy and nested PCR) compared cytokine levels in those with EBV reactivation versus latent EBV. Women with reactivated EBV and severe malaria had significantly lower IL-6 (29.86 vs 55.83 pg/mL, p=0.02) and higher TGF-β (17.6 vs 6.1 pg/mL, p=0.01) than those with latent EBV. IL-10 showed strong negative correlation with parasitemia in the reactivated EBV group (r=−0.9762, p=0.0004). Authors propose that EBV reactivation impairs inflammatory control by downregulating IL-6 and elevating TGF-β, potentially exacerbating malaria severity.

articles.headline: Age-dependent inflammatory responses drive malaria disease severity
articles.title_original: Age is an intrinsic driver of inflammatory responses to malaria.
articles.details: Assessment of inflammatory cytokines during malaria in non-immune children and adults, plus in vitro innate cell responses to parasites. Age was associated with elevated inflammatory chemokines (CCL2, CCL3, CXCL8, CXCL9), CRP, and IDO that correlated with symptoms. In naive individuals, classical monocytes and Vδ2+ γδ T cells from adults produced higher inflammatory cytokines and showed greater transcriptional activation after parasite stimulation than children. Adult monocyte responses were dominated by CCL2, whereas children showed increased IL-10 and IL-10 signaling pathway enrichment. Authors identify age-dependent cellular mechanisms as critical drivers of inflammation in malaria.

articles.headline: IgG subclass antibody responses predict malaria control after controlled infection
articles.title_original: IgM, IgG, and IgG Subclass Antibody Responses to Plasmodium falciparum Proteins in Naïve, Malaria-Vaccinated and Semi-Immune Volunteers after Controlled Human Malaria Infection.
articles.details: Controlled human malaria infection (CHMI) via direct venous inoculation of 3,200 P. falciparum sporozoites in three groups: malaria-naive (n=22), PfSPZ-CVac vaccinated naive (n=27), and lifelong malaria-exposed Africans (n=20; 11 normal hemoglobin, 9 sickle trait). PfSPZ-CVac immunized volunteers had higher IgM and IgG to CSP and SSP-2/TRAP. Malaria-experienced Africans exhibited broader antibody responses to blood-stage antigens, longer pre-patent periods, and fewer symptoms. Cytophilic IgG1 and IgG3 to blood-stage antigens were positively associated with longer pre-patent periods, while IgG2 and IgG4 (noncytophilic) were elevated in the semi-immune group. Authors note that elevated noncytophilic antibodies in naturally-exposed individuals may be regulatory and potentially reduce vaccine efficacy.

articles.headline: P. vivax fever paroxysm periodicity from 1950s-60s malaria therapy data
articles.title_original: Clinical and Parasitological Characteristics of Plasmodium vivax Malaria in Malaria-Naïve Patients: A Review of Malaria Fever Therapy in Patients with Schizophrenia and Neurosyphilis during the 1950s and 1960s in Vienna, Austria.
articles.details: Historical analysis of 322 patients (147 with schizophrenia, 175 with neurosyphilis) treated with iatrogenic P. vivax malaria fever therapy in Vienna (1951–1969). Intravenous inoculation dose was 4–8 mL blood. First fever peaks appeared approximately 7 days post-inoculation, with temperature rising over consecutive paroxysms and afebrile intervals shortening from 41 to 31 hours. After a mean of 5–6 fever peaks, all patients received antimalarial therapy (quinine monotherapy or combination). Authors report these data reveal an extra-hepatic P. vivax incubation period of ~7 days and document surprisingly short periodicity between fever paroxysms in untreated infection.

articles.headline: Transfluthrin emanator provides high efficacy against resistant mosquitoes indoor and outdoor
articles.title_original: The BiteBarrier transfluthrin emanator demonstrates significant protection against susceptible and resistant malaria and arbovirus vectors in semi-field trials in Tanzania.
articles.details: Semi-field trial in Tanzania evaluated BiteBarrier, a transfluthrin-based spatial emanator, against five mosquito species over 8 weeks of aging: pyrethroid-susceptible Anopheles gambiae and Aedes aegypti; KDR-bearing An. gambiae; and pyrethroid-resistant An. funestus and Culex quinquefasciatus. Using feeding success as the efficacy endpoint, BiteBarrier provided >93% (95% CI 92–93) indoor and 80% (95% CI 78–81) outdoor protection against mosquito bites, with substantial mortality of 47% (95% CI 43–53) indoors and 26% (95% CI 22–30) outdoors, regardless of mosquito species or resistance status. Authors conclude the emanator offers a scalable complementary tool for reducing human-vector contact in both indoor and outdoor settings.

articles.headline: Epigenetic pathways identified as targets for P. vivax hypnozoite elimination
articles.title_original: A drug repurposing approach reveals targetable epigenetic pathways in Plasmodium vivax hypnozoites.
articles.details: Screening of ReFRAME and epigenetic inhibitor libraries identified DNA methyltransferase inhibitors and histone post-translational modification inhibitors as selectively active against P. vivax and P. cynomolgi hypnozoites. Immunofluorescence showed strong nuclear 5-methylcystosine in liver stage parasites; bisulfite sequencing revealed DNA methylation signals in most coding genes of sporozoites. Methylation levels in proximal promoter regions and first exons were shown to affect gene expression. Validation with MMV019721 (acetyl-CoA synthetase inhibitor affecting histone acetylation) confirmed the importance of epigenetic mechanisms. Authors conclude that epigenetic pathways modulate hypnozoite formation and persistence, offering novel avenues for improved radical cure antimalarials beyond primaquine.
articles.notable_reason: Novel druggable epigenetic targets for hypnozoite elimination could expand P. vivax radical cure options

articles.headline: Microsporidia MB undergoes octosporogony in mosquito germline tissues
articles.title_original: Discovery of sporogeny in the malaria transmission-blocking symbiont of Anopheles mosquitoes.
articles.details: Detailed visualization of Microsporidia MB revealed the symbiont undergoes octosporogony within developing Anopheles oocytes, with spores displaying hallmark microsporidian structures. These findings suggest both vertical and potential horizontal transmission routes, supporting Microsporidia MB's feasibility as a transmission-blocking malaria control tool.

articles.headline: P. falciparum RIPR vaccine candidate shows 71.9% nonsynonymous mutation rate
articles.title_original: Genetic Diversity of the Malaria Vaccine Candidate PfRIPR in a High Transmission Region of Senegal.
articles.details: Deep amplicon sequencing of RIPR in 89 P. falciparum clinical isolates from Kédougou, Senegal (high transmission) identified nonsynonymous SNPs in 71.9% of samples. 26 distinct SNPs were found (15 novel); 7 of 16 structurally modeled SNPs predicted to destabilize RIPR, while 3 SNPs mapped to neutralizing antibody binding domains, suggesting potential immune escape risk.

articles.headline: Tanzania stakeholders propose alternative domestic funding for disease services
articles.title_original: Stakeholders' perspectives on funding malaria, HIV/AIDS and tuberculosis services in Tanzania through domestic resources mobilisation: a qualitative study.
articles.details: Qualitative study interviewed 76 Tanzanian stakeholders to identify domestic funding approaches for malaria, HIV/AIDS, and TB services. Proposed strategies included universal health insurance, disease-specific funds, and product taxation; however, implementation faces barriers including poor awareness, conflicting political promises, and accountability issues.

articles.headline: SERVAL trial: seasonal R21 mass vaccination for malaria elimination
articles.title_original: Seasonal mass vaccination with R21/Matrix-M for malaria elimination (SERVAL): protocol of the cluster randomised trial.
articles.details: Multi-centre cluster-randomised controlled trial (NCT06578572) testing seasonal mass R21/Matrix-M vaccination in The Gambia (low-moderate transmission) and Burkina Faso (intense transmission). 30 villages in Gambia and 24 in Burkina Faso randomised (1:1) to receive R21 in three-monthly rounds (May-July 2024) with booster June 2025, versus control. Primary outcome: malaria prevalence at peak transmission (November 2024).

articles.headline: Correction: P. knowlesi human-to-Anopheles dirus transmission study
articles.title_original: Correction: Human-to-Anopheles dirus mosquito transmission of the anthropozoonotic malaria parasite, Plasmodium knowlesi.
articles.details: Erratum regarding human-to-Anopheles dirus mosquito transmission of Plasmodium knowlesi. No abstract provided.

articles.headline: Malaria and soil-transmitted helminths co-endemic in pregnant women
articles.title_original: Malaria and soil transmitted helminth infection and associated factors among pregnant women attending public health facilities in Dilla town, South Ethiopia: cross sectional study design.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 331 pregnant women in Dilla town, Ethiopia found STH prevalence 18.1% (predominantly Ascaris lumbricoides 13.6%), malaria 8.8%, and co-infection 5.7%. P. vivax (4.8%) and P. falciparum (4.0%) detected. Rural residence, proximity to stagnant water, and non-use of ITNs associated with 2.66–3.67-fold increased malaria odds; integrated antenatal screening recommended.

articles.headline: Azithromycin effect on mortality modulated by SMC timing and coverage
articles.title_original: The role of seasonal malaria chemoprevention in the effect of azithromycin on child mortality: A secondary analysis of the CHAT cluster randomized clinical trial.
articles.details: Secondary analysis of CHAT trial (341 communities, Burkina Faso, twice-yearly azithromycin distribution) examined under-5 mortality reduction by SMC season. Azithromycin mortality rate ratio 0.77 (95% CI 0.60–0.98) during SMC season versus 0.89 (95% CI 0.68–1.15) during non-SMC season. In low SMC coverage areas (<80%), AZ effect RR 0.73 (95% CI 0.56–0.96); in high coverage areas (≥80%), RR 1.0 (95% CI 0.59–1.69). Interaction not statistically significant.

articles.headline: Deep learning model achieves 98% precision detecting P. vivax in blood
articles.title_original: Leveraging MobileNetV2 and deep learning innovation for high accuracy Plasmodium Vivax detection in blood smears.
articles.authors: Prathap VM, Yadav S, Qidwai T
articles.details: YOLOv3 model with modified MobileNetV2 backbone (incorporating Transformed Convolution Layer) developed for P. vivax detection in thin blood smear images. Model performance: accuracy 1.00, precision 0.98, recall 0.98, F1-score 0.97, mean average precision 0.90, addressing limitations of manual microscopy.

articles.headline: Alstonia boonei extract reduces parasitemia and diabetes markers in mice
articles.title_original: Alstonia boonei stem bark aqueous extract ameliorates elevated parasitemia levels, normalises blood glucose, modulates inflammatory biomarkers and enhances antioxidant status in Plasmodium berghei-infected/diabetic mice.
articles.authors: Agunloye OM, Akintoye OF, Oboh G
articles.details: Alstonia boonei stem bark aqueous extract tested in P. berghei-infected diabetic male mice at 40 and 80 mg/kg doses. Extract dose-dependently reduced parasitemia and fasting blood glucose, enhanced antioxidant enzymes (SOD, catalase, GSH), restored erythrocyte and hemoglobin levels, and reduced C-reactive protein, with effects at 80 mg/kg comparable to standard chloroquine-metformin treatment.

articles.headline: Water, sanitation, malaria practices predict diarrhea in Béré, Chad
articles.title_original: Association of Diarrhea Outcomes with Drinking Water Factors, Sanitation, Hygiene, and Malaria Practices in the Population of Béré, Chad.
articles.journal: Int J Environ Res Public Health
articles.details: Survey of 484 residents in Béré, Chad identified drinking water treatment/storage (p<0.001), malaria-related factors (p<0.001), and sanitation/hygiene practices (p<0.001) as significant predictors of adult and childhood diarrhea outcomes. Interventions targeting water quality, sanitation, malaria prevention, and community sensitisation recommended.

articles.headline: Pediatric case: tetanus with cerebral malaria and probable meningitis
articles.title_original: Generalized Tetanus Associated With Malaria and Probable Meningitis in a 4-Year-Old Patient: A Case Report in Limbe Regional Hospital, Cameroon.
articles.details: Case report of unvaccinated 4-year-old boy presenting with febrile seizures and neck stiffness, subsequently diagnosed with generalized tetanus and cerebral malaria with probable enteric fever/bacterial meningitis. Empirically treated with anti-tetanus toxoid, antibiotics, and antimalarials; condition improved within 48 hours in ICU; discharged after 5 additional hospital days.

articles.headline: Lifelong malaria exposure alters baseline B cell phenotypes independent of vaccination
articles.title_original: Effect of controlled human Plasmodium falciparum infection on B cell subsets in individuals with different levels of malaria immunity.
articles.details: Controlled human malaria infection (CHMI) study (NCT01624961, NCT02115516, NCT02237586) in three groups: malaria-naïve, PfSPZ-CVac vaccinated, and semi-immune (lifelong-exposed) volunteers. Semi-immune individuals had increased baseline frequencies of immature and memory B cell subsets compared to naïve; CHMI induced expansion of resting classical memory B cells by day 11 blood-stage. Vaccination and sickle cell trait modulated CHMI-induced B cell responses.

articles.headline: Sicker malaria cases cluster at primary health centres in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Treatment-Seeking for Children with Suspected Severe Malaria Attending Community Health Workers and Primary Health Centres in Adamawa State, Nigeria.
articles.details: Cross-sectional comparison of 589 children under 5 years with suspected severe malaria attending community health workers (N=314) or primary health centres (N=275). Children at primary health centres had higher disease severity, with ≥4 danger signs in 50% versus 39% (p=0.02) and central nervous system involvement in 90% versus 74% (p<0.01). Case fatality rates were 19% at primary health centres versus 6% at community health workers.

articles.headline: Climate change projected to double malaria control needs in Papua New Guinea
articles.title_original: Climate Change Is Expected to Expand Malaria Transmission Range and Population at Risk in Papua New Guinea.
articles.details: Modeling study projecting malaria transmission suitability in Papua New Guinea from 1960 through 2040 using satellite temperature data and climate models. The proportion of the population in transmission-suitable areas increased from 58% (1960) to 61% (2019). Under a conservative climate change model, this is expected to reach 74% by 2040, increasing the population requiring malaria control access from current levels to 13.4 million by 2040, with 2.8 million newly at-risk people attributable to climate change alone.

articles.headline: Education and internet access drive malaria vaccine awareness in Ghana
articles.title_original: Determinants of Malaria Vaccine Awareness Among Women in Ghana: Findings From the 2022 Ghana Malaria Indicator Survey.
articles.details: Cross-sectional analysis of 38,459 Ghanaian women aged 15-49 from the 2022 Ghana Malaria Indicator Survey. Malaria vaccine awareness was significantly higher among women with tertiary education (adjusted odds ratio 2.89), those who used the internet in the past 12 months (adjusted odds ratio 1.85), and residents of Greater Accra (adjusted odds ratio 1.75). Awareness increased with age, peaking in women aged 45-49 years (adjusted odds ratio 2.03).

articles.headline: Vulnerable populations in Ethiopia carry 10% malaria prevalence burden
articles.title_original: Burden of malaria infection among most vulnerable populations in Ethiopia: an umbrella review of systematic reviews and meta-analyses.
articles.details: Umbrella review synthesizing six systematic reviews and meta-analyses of malaria in Ethiopian children, pregnant women, and migrants. Overall pooled prevalence was 10.10% (95% confidence interval 0.36-19.85) with minimal heterogeneity. Key risk factors included not using insecticide-treated nets (pooled odds ratio 5.22, 95% CI 3.93-6.94) and proximity to stagnant water (pooled odds ratio 4.21, 95% CI 2.75-6.46).

articles.headline: Pyrethroid resistance genes confer cross-resistance to novel insecticide
articles.title_original: The duplicated cytochrome P450 CYP6P9a/b confers cross-resistance to a mitochondrial complex I inhibitor in the African malaria vector Anopheles funestus.
articles.details: Study demonstrated that cytochrome P450 genes CYP6P9a/b, which confer pyrethroid resistance in Anopheles funestus, also mediate cross-resistance to a novel mitochondrial complex I inhibitor (Sherlock). Individuals carrying the CYP6P9a_R allele showed significantly higher survival odds when exposed to Sherlock at various concentrations (odds ratio 5.3 at 1x concentration, 18.6 at 5x concentration; p<0.0001). Elevated CYP6P9a expression (24.7-45.6 fold change) was observed in surviving mosquitoes.
articles.notable_reason: Novel cross-resistance pattern threatens new vector control insecticide

articles.headline: Nearly half of under-five malaria cases in Horn of Africa delayed treatment
articles.title_original: Prevalence and associated factors of delay in seeking malaria treatment among under five children in the Horn of Africa: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of treatment delays for malaria in under-five children across the Horn of Africa. The pooled prevalence of delayed treatment seeking was 48% (95% CI 34%-63%). Major risk factors included high transportation costs (odds ratio 4.39), distance greater than 3,000 meters (odds ratio 2.59), and concerns about drug side effects (odds ratio 2.94). Middle-class income status was protective (odds ratio 0.42).

articles.headline: Vegetation near houses increases malaria vector abundance and transmission
articles.title_original: Housing and household characteristics associated with malaria vectors abundance and clinical malaria incidence in a semi-urban and a rural area of Burkina Faso.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 355 houses in Burkina Faso (July-October 2021) using CDC light traps to examine relationships between housing characteristics and malaria transmission. Vegetation around houses was strongly associated with increased mosquito abundance (relative risk 2.5, p<0.001), vector infection (relative risk 2.5, p<0.001), and human malaria cases (relative risk 1.4, p=0.048). Building materials (wall/roof type), presence of breeding sites, and long-lasting insecticidal net use showed no significant associations.

articles.headline: Malaria parasites alter host odor to increase mosquito attractiveness
articles.title_original: [Skin flora, odor and malaria transmission].
articles.journal: Zhongguo Xue Xi Chong Bing Fang Zhi Za Zhi
articles.details: Commentary discussing recent findings that Plasmodium infection induces changes in host skin flora and volatile substances. Studies in mice showed increased ethylbenzene production following Plasmodium infection, which enhanced attractiveness to female Anopheles mosquitoes. These mechanisms underlying altered host odor and increased vector attraction may inform development of novel malaria transmission control strategies.

articles.headline: P. vivax invasion ligand identifies prohibitin-2 as novel receptor
articles.title_original: The reticulocyte restriction: invasion ligand RBP1a of Plasmodium vivax targets human TfR1, prohibitin-2, and basigin.
articles.details: Using proteomic analysis and proximity labeling, researchers identified host cell receptors for Plasmodium vivax invasion ligand PvRBP1a. In addition to known receptors (transferrin receptor TfR1 and basigin), they identified prohibitin-2 (PHB2) as a novel co-receptor involved in parasite invasion of reticulocytes. The study confirmed JK-1 and BEL-A cell lines as effective reticulocyte surrogates for studying P. vivax invasion mechanisms and therapeutic targets.
articles.notable_reason: First identification of prohibitin-2 as P. vivax invasion co-receptor

articles.headline: Plasmodium possesses divergent mitotic machinery targeting potential
articles.title_original: Plasmodium ARK1 regulates spindle formation during atypical mitosis and forms a divergent chromosomal passenger complex.
articles.details: Study identified Aurora-related kinase 1 (ARK1) as a critical regulator of spindle formation and mitotic progression in Plasmodium parasites during both schizogony and gametogony. Conditional ARK1 depletion disrupted spindle biogenesis, kinetochore segregation, and cytokinesis, affecting parasite transmission. Comparative analysis revealed ARK1 is the catalytic core of a unique, non-canonical chromosomal passenger complex with two inner centromere proteins but lacking Survivin and Borealin, representing a parasite-specific structure with potential for multistage antimalarial targeting.
articles.notable_reason: Parasite-specific mitotic mechanism identified; potential drug target

articles.headline: Inflammatory cytokines correlate with parasite density in malaria
articles.title_original: Assessment of Cytokines TNF-alpha, IFN-gamma, TGF-beta-1, and IL-10 in Malaria Patients of the River Nile State , Sudan: A critical study of immune response.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 90 microscopy-confirmed malaria patients in Sudan measuring four cytokines (TNF-α, IFN-γ, TGF-β1, IL-10) by ELISA. Three cytokines were significantly associated with parasite density: TNF-α (r=0.42, p<0.01), IFN-γ (r=0.38, p<0.01), and TGF-β1 (r=0.51, p<0.01). IL-10 levels were significantly higher in P. falciparum infections (560.0 pg/mL) compared to P. vivax (415.6 pg/mL, p=0.019).

articles.headline: Clinical immunity to P. vivax develops rapidly after single infection
articles.title_original: Development of clinical immunity to Plasmodium vivax following repeat controlled human malaria infection.
articles.details: Controlled human malaria infection study with 19 participants undergoing primary Plasmodium vivax infection, 12 returning for secondary homologous challenge, and 6 proceeding to heterologous P. falciparum rechallenge. Clinical immunity to P. vivax developed rapidly after a single infection, protecting against fever and laboratory abnormalities through attenuated inflammatory responses rather than parasite clearance. This immunity was species-specific, providing no protection against heterologous P. falciparum challenge.
articles.notable_reason: Demonstrates clinical immunity mechanism independent of parasite clearance

articles.headline: Malaria surge in Uganda's Elgon region driven by control gaps
articles.title_original: From Endemic to Epidemic: A Qualitative District-Level Assessment of an Increase in Malaria Cases in the Elgon Region of Uganda.
articles.details: This qualitative assessment of 10 districts in Uganda's high-burden Elgon region identified gaps in prevention, detection, and response contributing to malaria incidence increasing from 206 to 271 cases per 1,000 population (2012-2022). Key deficiencies included inadequate insecticide-treated nets (70% of districts), no active indoor residual spraying (30%), limited rapid diagnostic tests (80%), and mean 117-day delays in antimalarial resupply. The study highlights priority intervention areas in eastern Uganda.

articles.headline: Standardizing molecular surveillance data reporting for malaria
articles.title_original: Toward Setting Minimum and Optimal Data to Report for Malaria Molecular Surveillance with Targeted Sequencing: The "What" and "Why".
articles.details: This paper proposes standards for reporting malaria molecular surveillance (MMS) data to enable validation and global comparability in tracking artemisinin resistance and PfHRP2/3 deletions. It recommends reporting individual infection-level polymorphism and microhaplotype data while adhering to local regulatory practices and preventing data colonialism. The framework aims to maximize genomic surveillance impact while ensuring data governance and opportunities for data generators.

articles.headline: Higher temperatures may reduce malaria transmission in Colombia
articles.title_original: Stochastic treatment regimes in climate-health research: Reassessing malaria risk under warming scenarios in Colombia.
articles.details: Using causal machine learning on 2007-2023 data from Colombia's 100 municipalities with highest malaria incidence, this study found malaria transmission peaks between 15-23.5°C and declines at higher temperatures. The Average Treatment Effect for excess malaria cases decreased with warming (ATE -0.007 for +0.5°C; -0.063 for +2.0°C), suggesting further climate warming could constrain transmission in already-warm regions. Effects varied by local temperature profiles and socioeconomic conditions.

articles.headline: Ellagic acid from Mitragyna inermis active against drug-resistant malaria
articles.title_original: A bio-guided investigation of Mitragyna inermis (Willd) O. Kuntze (Rubiaceae) unveils natural product isolates with potent cross-activity against sensitive and multidrug resistant Plasmodium falciparum strains in vitro.
articles.details: Bio-guided investigation of Mitragyna inermis identified ellagic acid with potent activity against both drug-susceptible P. falciparum 3D7 and multidrug-resistant strains (Dd2, INDO, W2, K1), with IC50 0.84-1.62 μg/mL. Methanolic and ethanol/water extracts showed IC50 < 3 μg/mL with selectivity index > 10. Ellagic acid demonstrated favorable pharmacokinetic properties (Lipinski's five rules compliance, good oral bioavailability, high GI absorption), suggesting potential as a drug-discovery starting point.

articles.headline: Herbal extracts reverse testicular damage in malaria-infected mice
articles.title_original: Bitter leaf (Vernonia amygdalina) and siam weed (Chromolaena odorata) aqueous extracts alleviate testicular damage induced by Plasmodium berghei in male mice via modulation of oxidative stress pathways.
articles.details: In 64 male mice infected with chloroquine-sensitive P. berghei NK65, bitter leaf (Vernonia amygdalina) and Siam weed (Chromolaena odorata) aqueous extracts (100-400 mg/kg, 5 days) suppressed parasitemia and significantly reduced reactive oxygen species and TBARS levels while increasing antioxidants in dose-dependent manner (p < 0.05). These treatments reversed testicular oxidative damage induced by malaria infection, implicating oxidative stress modulation mechanisms.

articles.headline: Olfactory pathway genes linked to host-seeking in malaria vector
articles.title_original: Genetic variation in olfactory pathways associated with host-seeking behavior in natural populations of Anopheles minimus, a primary malaria vector in western Thailand.
articles.details: Whole-genome sequencing of wild An. minimus females from western Thailand (collected 2019-2020) identified genetic variants in olfactory pathway genes associated with host-seeking preference. From 12,659,785 variants, filtering yielded 68,975 non-synonymous SNPs, with 2,629-4,369 significant variants per collection group. Gene ontology enrichment highlighted olfaction-related genes; eight candidate nsSNPs were validated by Sanger sequencing, suggesting genetic basis for behavioral variation in this primary malaria vector.

articles.headline: ARIMA model accurately predicts Uganda malaria incidence
articles.title_original: Comparison of ARIMA and FB-Prophet time series models for the prediction of national and regional malaria incidence in Uganda.
articles.details: Time series analysis of Uganda's District Health Information System data (2020-2023) compared ARIMA and FB-Prophet models for predicting national and regional malaria incidence. Malaria cases increased from 200.5 to 265.4 per 1,000 population (2021-2022), with highest burden in West Nile, Acholi, and Busoga regions. ARIMA outperformed FB-Prophet nationally and in 14/15 regions (MAE 0.007, MAPE 31.2% vs. MAE 0.01, MAPE 47.8%), showing potential to guide resource allocation.

articles.headline: Review of genetic variations protecting against malaria
articles.title_original: Human genetic variations conferring resistance to malaria.
articles.details: This review examines human genetic adaptations conferring malaria resistance, including hemoglobinopathies (sickle cell trait, thalassemia), glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency, blood group polymorphisms, and inflammatory/immune variants. Prevalence of these genetic adaptations varies geographically reflecting historical malaria burden. While these variations demonstrate clear epidemiological associations, the precise mechanisms remain incompletely understood, and gene-environment interactions add complexity.

articles.headline: High insecticide resistance widespread in Chinese Anopheles sinensis
articles.title_original: Insecticide resistance and the role of target-site insensitivity mutations among malaria vectors in China: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 30 studies on 30,065 An. sinensis found pooled mortality 61% (95% CI 53-68) across insecticides. Knockdown resistance (kdr) mutations were present in 36% (13% homozygote, 13% heterozygote), and acetylcholinesterase-1 (ace-1) mutations in 78% (42% homozygote, 25% heterozygote). Mortality varied by insecticide class: malathion 81%, fenitrothion 82%, propoxur 69%, but beta-cyfluthrin only 28%, demonstrating widespread resistance to pyrethroids, organochlorines, carbamates, and organophosphates.

articles.headline: ITN use paradoxically lower in educated Nigerians despite awareness
articles.title_original: Socioeconomic and educational influences on malaria prevention and treatment behaviours in rural Nigeria.
articles.details: Analysis of 2021 Malaria Indicator Survey data from rural Nigeria (5,021 households, 9,546 women) found near-universal ITN ownership (97.7%) but lower use (70.3%). Counterintuitively, low-income households had 3.6× higher ITN use odds (95% CI 3.07-4.29). Higher education was associated with reduced net use despite increased prevention awareness (p < 0.001). Media exposure did not significantly influence treatment-seeking patterns, suggesting behavioral and structural factors beyond information access drive malaria control practices.

articles.headline: Adjuvants and boosting enhance P. vivax vaccine epitope responses
articles.title_original: Adjuvants and MHCII modulate the immunogenicity of subdominant epitopes in Plasmodium vivax Duffy binding protein.
articles.details: This immunological study of P. vivax Duffy binding protein demonstrated that antigens with mutations in immunodominant epitopes combined with GLA-SE or alum adjuvants overcame the subdominance of conserved epitopes. However, these effects were largely negated by MHCII restriction. Robust antibody responses to subdominant epitopes required boosting, which altered epitope hierarchy and recruited germinal center B cells. These findings inform subunit vaccine design targeting strain-transcending conserved epitopes.

articles.headline: Suriname first malaria-free country in Amazon region
articles.title_original: Suriname: first malaria-free country in the Amazon region.
articles.details: According to the title, Suriname has achieved malaria-free status in the Amazon region. Without an abstract, specific epidemiological data, timeline, or control strategies cannot be detailed.

articles.headline: Blood donor screening enables real-time malaria surveillance data
articles.title_original: Blood donors as a sentinel population for real-time malaria surveillance using the Sysmex XN-31: a one-year review from the National Blood Transfusion Centre in Burkina Faso.
articles.details: Prospective study screened 53,575 consecutive blood donations (Aug 2023–Aug 2024) at two Burkina Faso centers using Sysmex XN-31 analyzer. Malaria prevalence 5.91% (3,164 positive). Key predictors: age ≤30 years (OR 2.85, p<0.001), male sex (OR 1.47, p<0.001), rural residency (OR 2.40, p<0.001). Strong seasonal variability mirrored rainfall with 3-month lag; only males showed striking seasonal gametocyte carriage variation (1.39% low season vs 4.42% high season, p<0.001). Automated screening provides granular, spatiotemporal malaria burden data unavailable from episodic household surveys.

articles.headline: REL2 pathway controls gut bacteria and affects parasite prevalence
articles.title_original: The major role of the REL2/NF-κB pathway in the regulation of midgut bacterial homeostasis in the malaria vector Anopheles gambiae.
articles.details: Study examined immune responses in Anopheles females to blood feeding and P. falciparum infection. REL2 regulates antimicrobial peptide genes and shapes midgut bacterial composition post-blood meal. REL2 loss causes dysbiosis with Serratia overgrowth and mosquito lethality. Notably, Serratia-induced dysbiosis reduces P. falciparum prevalence in surviving mosquitoes. Findings reveal immune system's critical role in maintaining midgut bacterial homeostasis and complex interactions between mosquito immunity, microbiota, and malaria parasites.

articles.headline: Anopheles stephensi genome sequence assembled and characterized
articles.title_original: Whole genome Shotgun Sequence of Anopheles stephensi, The Host of Malaria parasite, Plasmodium sp.
articles.details: Whole genome shotgun sequence completed for female Anopheles stephensi, a major malaria vector in Asia. Genome assembly 201 Mbp, consisting of 32,280 contigs with N50 of 21.1 kb and GC content 45%.

articles.headline: Drug resistance mechanisms in tuberculosis and malaria reviewed
articles.title_original: Editorial: Mechanisms driving drug resistance in tuberculosis and malaria: genetic, environmental, and evolutionary insights.
articles.details: Editorial examining mechanisms driving drug resistance in tuberculosis and malaria, covering genetic, environmental, and evolutionary insights.

articles.headline: Dual P. vivax and P. falciparum infection with rare exflagellation
articles.title_original: Exflagellation of Plasmodium vivax and Dual Infection Diagnosed on Peripheral Blood Film: A Rare Case Report.
articles.details: Case report of 18-year-old male with dual malaria infection: P. vivax (trophozoites, schizonts, exflagellated microgametes) and P. falciparum (trophozoites) detected on peripheral blood smear. Exflagellation of P. vivax is unusually rare in peripheral blood during human infection. Case demonstrates importance of correct morphological identification for appropriate treatment and preventing parasite transmission.

articles.headline: P. vivax population genetics reveals structured diversity in Mexico
articles.title_original: Population Dynamics of Plasmodium vivax in Mexico Determined by CSP, Pvs25, and SSU 18S rRNA S-Type Polymorphism Analyses.
articles.details: Analysis of parasites from five Mexican malaria foci using circumsporozoite gene (PCR-RFLP), pvs25 mutations, and 18S SSU rRNA variants. VK210 pvcsp type showed greater haplotype diversity (0.860) than VK247 (0.198). Two pvs25 mutations identified three haplotypes. Gene/multilocus haplotype frequencies differed among foci (p<0.001); AMOVA and FST indicated structured P. vivax population. Lacandon region harbored broadest haplotype reservoir; high parasite adaptation in northwestern region presents challenge for malaria elimination.

articles.headline: Guillain-Barré Syndrome follows P. falciparum infection in adolescent
articles.title_original: Guillain-Barré Syndrome Following Falciparum Malaria in an 18-Year Male Adolescent: A Rare Case Report and Literature Review.
articles.details: Case report of 18-year-old male developing GBS after P. falciparum infection with progressive bilateral lower extremity weakness advancing to upper extremities over 1 week, facial drooping, dysphagia, and high-grade fever. CSF analysis revealed elevated protein 172 mg/dL with no cells; nerve conduction studies confirmed acute motor and sensory axonal neuropathy (AMSAN) variant. Treated with intravenous artesunate and supportive care; weakness stabilized by day 14, significant improvement at 1 month, minimal-support ambulation at 2 months.

articles.headline: Estimated-to-reported malaria case ratio gap persists in India
articles.title_original: Time to address the drawbacks in estimation and reporting of malaria cases in India and beyond.
articles.authors: Deora N, Singhal R, Sinha A
articles.details: Analysis from 2010–2022 revealed consistently high estimated-to-reported (ERR) ratio: WHO-SEARO region >6, India >10. Authors highlight unclear WHO assumptions and nearly absent private-sector malaria reporting in India. Contemporary research shows microscopy-positivity 3–55× higher than NMCP reports, indicating substantial surveillance gap. Study calls for WHO clarification of estimation methods and NMCP improvement in private-sector case capture to narrow ERR and support India's 2030 malaria elimination goal.

articles.headline: Method visualizes parasitized erythroblasts in malaria models
articles.title_original: Visual Detection of Malaria Parasite-Parasitized Erythroblasts in Peripheral Blood via Immunization-Based Model.
articles.details: Study developed method to increase parasitized erythroblasts (pEBs) in peripheral blood using immunization model. Mice immunized with live P. yoelii 17XNL then challenged with P. berghei ANKA. Immunized mice were protected from cerebral malaria, survived longer, and pEBs appeared in circulation detectable via Giemsa-stained smears. All blood-stage parasite forms identified within pEBs. Model enables in vivo/ex vivo pEB analysis without bone marrow or spleen isolation, lowering technical and ethical barriers.

articles.headline: Vector survey identifies malaria and arbovirus transmission risk
articles.title_original: Culicidae vector ecology in southern Iran: Integrating Faunistics and molecular analysis of blood-feeding preferences to strengthen malaria surveillance in transition zones.
articles.details: Entomological survey April 2023–January 2024 in Shiraz, southern Iran collected 1,249 adult mosquitoes via pyrethrum spray, CDC light traps, and aspiration. Dominant species: Culex pipiens (39.0%), Cx. quinquefasciatus (23.6%), Aedes caspius (19.6%); Anopheles dthali 80.6%, An. sacharovi 19.4% of Anophelinae. PCR-RFLP of mitochondrial cytochrome b showed Anopheles fed on rodents/dogs (41%) and humans (28%); Cx. pipiens exhibited marked anthropophily (54% human). An. sacharovi clustered in irrigation-rich areas (χ²=12.7, p<0.01). Findings highlight residual malaria risk and arboviruses in transitional zones.

articles.headline: Child age, education, wealth predict malaria in Nigerian under-fives
articles.title_original: Sociodemographic Factors Associated with Malaria Infection among Under-Five Children in Nigeria.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study using 2018 Nigeria Demographic and Health Survey, 10,352 children, analyzed malaria RDT results. Children's age statistically significant (χ²=166.200, p=0.000). Mother's education significant (χ²=762.530, p=0.000). Wealth index significant (χ²=1,020.442, p=0.000). Rural residence increased malaria risk vs urban (OR=1.633, p=0.000). Authors conclude child age, wealth, maternal education, and residence are key predictors for targeted interventions.

articles.headline: SMC awareness high but completion rates remain low in Nigeria
articles.title_original: Knowledge and Uptake of Seasonal Malaria Chemoprevention among Caregivers of Under-five Children in a Tertiary Hospital in Kaduna State, North-western Nigeria.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 234 caregivers of under-five children in Kaduna State tertiary hospital. Of respondents, 182 (76.8%) heard of seasonal malaria chemoprevention (SMC), 156 (65.8%) knew it as prevention drug, 128 (54.0%) correctly identified rainy-season activity; overall 128 (54.0%) had good SMC knowledge. For uptake: 137 (58.4%) received ≥1 SMC dose, but only 65 (27.2%) completed all four doses. Main barriers: not knowing about SMC (54.0%), child absence (20.0%). Factors associated with uptake: education, occupation, ethnicity, religion, knowledge (p<0.05).

articles.headline: Mixed P. falciparum-vivax in G6PD-deficient patient treated successfully
articles.title_original: Navigating Challenges in Mixed Malaria Infections: A Case of Mixed Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax Infection in a Patient With Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase (G6PD) Deficiency.
articles.authors: Lau CKS, Kek XY, Gan WF
articles.details: Case report of imported mixed Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax co-infection in a patient with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency in Malaysia. Diagnosis made by malaria PCR. Patient treated with IV artesunate followed by oral Riamet (artemether/lumefantrine) and primaquine, with clinical improvement after completing full treatment course.

articles.headline: Acanthospermum australe extract inhibits P. falciparum Hsp70 activity
articles.title_original: Acanthospermum australe Extract Inhibits the Chaperone Activity of Plasmodium falciparum Heat Shock Protein 70-1.
articles.details: Study of dichloromethane extract of Acanthospermum australe testing antiplasmodial activity against chloroquine-sensitive P. falciparum strain 3D7. Extract showed IC50 of 1.3 µg/mL and inhibited heat shock protein 70-1 (PfHsp70-1) chaperone activity, increasing protein aggregation and reducing ATPase activity. Results suggest the extract contains compounds that could target parasite Hsp70 functions as potential antimalarial drug approach.

articles.headline: Review: Mitochondrial function as antimalarial drug target in P. falciparum
articles.title_original: Targeting Mitochondrial Function in Plasmodium falciparum: Insight into Antimalarial Drugs and the Emerging Role of Saccharomyces cerevisiae as a Model System.
articles.details: Review article discussing the emerging role of mitochondrial function as a target for novel antimalarial therapies against P. falciparum and the use of Saccharomyces cerevisiae as a model system for drug discovery. Highlights recent advances in developing compounds targeting parasite mitochondrial function, exploiting structural and biochemical differences from human mitochondria. Discusses the model organism's genetic tractability for elucidating drug mechanisms.

articles.headline: Supporting the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis, and Malaria
articles.title_original: Supporting the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis, and Malaria.
articles.details: Article discussing support for the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis, and Malaria.

articles.headline: Need for new tools for malaria prevention in pregnancy
articles.title_original: A need for new tools for prevention of malaria in pregnancy.
articles.details: Perspective article discussing intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp) with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP) as a chemoprevention strategy against malaria. Highlights the need for better understanding of mechanisms by which IPTp-SP reduces low birthweight and for developing novel measures to prevent malaria earlier in gestation.

articles.headline: Controlled human malaria infection studies review and immunological lessons
articles.title_original: Controlled human malaria infection studies: insights into recent advances and key immunological and ethical implementation lessons.
articles.details: Review of controlled human malaria infection (CHMI) studies, which have supported development of malaria vaccines including RTSS/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M. Discusses the CHMI model's utility in predicting vaccine efficacy, advancing drug development, and supporting monoclonal antibody development. Reviews lessons from studies in both naïve and endemic populations and transfer of models to low- and middle-income settings.

articles.headline: Hotspot districts of P. vivax malaria pose elimination challenge in India
articles.title_original: Hotspot districts of persistent Plasmodium vivax malaria pose a formidable challenge to malaria elimination in India.
articles.details: Analysis of persistent Plasmodium vivax malaria hotspots in India representing a significant challenge to malaria elimination efforts.

articles.headline: P. vivax with Duffy-negative adaptation found in Ethiopian populations
articles.title_original: High prevalence of mixed Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax co-infections in Duffy negative individuals in Ethiopia.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 1,154 febrile patients in three Ethiopian regions (2020-2021) found 46% Plasmodium infection: 49% P. falciparum, 26% mixed P. falciparum/P. vivax, 23% P. vivax mono-infection. Among P. vivax infections, 25% occurred in Duffy-negative individuals traditionally thought resistant. PCR testing of 155 samples showed 78% prevalence of PvDBP gene duplication, especially in Gambela region (94%), suggesting evolutionary adaptation of P. vivax to invade Duffy-negative erythrocytes.

articles.headline: CXCR6+ CD127- Tr1 cells regulate malaria immunity and parasite persistence
articles.title_original: CXCR6(+) CD127(-) Tr1 Cells Balance Immunity and Persistence in Plasmodium falciparum Infection.
articles.details: Study of Ugandan children identified CXCR6+ CD127- as phenotypic markers for type 1 regulatory T (Tr1) cells, which comprise ~90% of Plasmodium falciparum-specific CD4+ T cells. These Tr1 cells potently suppressed other CD4+ T cell proliferation via IL-10 secretion. Higher Tr1 frequencies correlated with lower symptom probability despite parasitemia, but also with delayed parasite clearance in untreated asymptomatic children, suggesting dual roles in immunity and persistence.

articles.headline: Genomic diversity and resistance evolution in Anopheles funestus vector
articles.title_original: Genomic diversity of the African malaria vector Anopheles funestus.
articles.details: Genomic study sequencing 656 modern (2014-2018) and 45 historic (1927-1967) Anopheles funestus specimens from 16 African countries found stable but substantial continental population structure, including two genetically distinct ecotypes. Vector control produced strong selection signals with some resistance alleles shared across populations via gene flow while others arose independently. A promising gene drive target identified in Anopheles gambiae showed high conservation in An. funestus.

articles.headline: Phase 3 trial: DHA-PPQ combination not better for pregnancy malaria
articles.title_original: Dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine plus sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine versus either drug alone for intermittent preventive treatment of malaria in pregnancy: A double-blind, randomized, controlled phase 3 trial from Uganda.
articles.details: Double-blind, randomized controlled trial of 2,757 pregnant women in Uganda compared three IPTp regimens: sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (SP) alone, dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DHA-PPQ) alone, or DHA-PPQ plus SP. Primary outcome was composite adverse birth outcome (spontaneous abortion, stillbirth, low birthweight <2,500g, preterm <37 weeks, small-for-gestational-age, neonatal death). DHA-PPQ showed 97% reduction in microscopic parasitemia vs SP (p<0.001), but DHA-PPQ had worse birth outcomes (30.9% vs 26.4%, RR 1.17, p=0.04). Combination therapy showed no improvement over either drug alone.
articles.notable_reason: Large phase-3 RCT challenging DHA-PPQ for IPTp despite antimalarial superiority

articles.headline: Resurgence of malaria in Rwanda despite near-eradication progress
articles.title_original: Investigating the resurgence of malaria in Rwanda: factors contributing to rising cases amidst near-eradication success and strategies for sustained control.
articles.details: Review analyzing reasons for 45.8% increase in malaria cases by 2024 in Rwanda, despite reduction from 4.8 million (2016/17) to 620,000 (2023/24) cases. Major contributing factors include meteorological changes widening Anopheles gambiae range, pyrethroid and artemisinin resistance with K13 mutations, suboptimal non-pyrethroid indoor residual spraying coverage, poverty-related disparities, and human mobility enabling asymptomatic transmission. March 2024 saw 87,000 cases. Recommended strategies include community health worker programs, drone-mediated larviciding, and new drugs (DHA-PPQ and artesunate-pyronaridine).

articles.headline: Malaria parasite-vector-host co-evolutionary arms race and adaptations
articles.title_original: Evolutionary Trajectory of Plasmodium falciparum: From Autonomous Phototroph to Dedicated Parasite.
articles.details: Review synthesizing P. falciparum, Anopheles, and human host co-evolution. Examines genetic polymorphisms governing human immune responses, vector insecticide resistance mechanisms, and parasite genomic streamlining and antigenic variation. Highlights the molecular arms race driving malaria transmission dynamics, parasite drug resistance emergence, and proposes integrated management strategies and next-generation therapeutic targets.

articles.headline: Young primigravida women at high risk for malaria in pregnancy
articles.title_original: Maternal and household risk factors for malaria in pregnancy and low birthweight: a prospective cohort study from Uganda.
articles.details: Prospective cohort of 2,728 pregnant women in Uganda (Dec 2020–July 2024) receiving intermittent preventive treatment (IPTp). Parasitemia at enrollment was 38.1%, declining to 6.5% post-IPTp; 6.4% had high-grade placental malaria, 6.8% delivered low birth weight. Young age and primigravida status showed elevated risk both before (RR=1.62–1.86) and after IPTp (RR=1.72–2.40). Primigravida had substantially elevated risk for high-grade placental malaria (RR=4.20) and low birth weight (RR=2.14).

articles.headline: Climate change shifts malaria transmission patterns in Zimbabwe
articles.title_original: Impact of Climate Change on Malaria Transmission and Management in Zimbabwe: A Scoping Review of the Literature.
articles.details: Scoping review of 22 literature sources on climate change's impact on malaria in Zimbabwe. Studies demonstrated that climate change shifts geographic distribution, timing, and intensity of malaria transmission. However, non-climatic factors—including health system capacity and intervention coverage—also significantly influence transmission dynamics. Review identified key evidence gaps including lack of longitudinal data and limited use of localized predictive modeling.

articles.headline: Protocol for systematic review of uncomplicated-to-severe malaria progression
articles.title_original: Progression from uncomplicated to severe malaria among children in settings receiving different malaria control interventions in sub-Saharan Africa: a systematic review protocol.
articles.details: Systematic review protocol examining progression from uncomplicated to severe malaria in children receiving concurrent malaria control measures in sub-Saharan Africa. Will search PubMed, Web of Science, Embase, Scopus, and Google Scholar. Data will be analyzed using narrative and quantitative synthesis with subgroup analysis assessing impact of different control interventions. Full systematic review expected by December 2025 (PROSPERO: CRD42024619945).

articles.headline: Transmission-blocking vaccines as complementary malaria control strategy
articles.title_original: Mosquito-based transmission-blocking vaccine candidates for malaria: progress, challenges, and innovations.
articles.details: Review of transmission-blocking vaccine (TBV) candidates for malaria. While WHO-recommended vaccines RTS,S/AS01 and R21/Matrix-M show lower efficacy in reducing severe disease, mosquito-based TBVs offer a complementary approach by targeting parasites within mosquitoes and reducing vector survival. Reviews current status of TBV antigen candidates, development challenges, methodologies for evaluating transmission-blocking efficacy, and promising approaches for improving TBV effectiveness.

articles.headline: Controlled malaria infection reveals strain-specific antibody response patterns
articles.title_original: Controlled human malaria infection with NF54 and 7G8 strains elicit differential antibody responses to Plasmodium falciparum peptides.
articles.details: Controlled human malaria infection (CHMI) study in 38 malaria-naïve adults infected with NF54 (n=21) or 7G8 (n=17) P. falciparum strains. Using novel diversity-reflecting peptide microarray with 638,817 unique peptides from 227 proteins, sera at day 28 post-infection showed similar antibody intensity between strain groups but differential breadth; 7G8 elicited higher breadth for 17 of 20 differentially responsive proteins. Six epitopes demonstrated >95% cross-reactivity with variant peptides; ETRAMP10.2 identified as potential vaccine candidate.

articles.headline: El Niño-Southern Oscillation and malaria risk in Amazon
articles.title_original: El Niño-Southern Oscillation and shifting malaria risks in the Amazon.
articles.details: Study examining relationship between El Niño-Southern Oscillation climate patterns and malaria transmission risk in Amazon region. No abstract available; specific methods, populations, and quantitative results cannot be determined from title alone.

articles.headline: Cerebral malaria pathophysiology and emerging biomarker insights
articles.title_original: Neurovascular Pathophysiology and Emerging Biomarkers in Cerebral Malaria: An Integrative Perspective.
articles.details: Integrative review of cerebral malaria (CM) pathophysiology in pediatric cohorts. CM characterized by coma with peripheral parasitemia; pathophysiology involves parasitized erythrocyte cytoadherence in cerebral microvasculature, inflammatory response, endothelial activation, blood-brain barrier disruption, and multifactorial cerebral edema. Recent MRI studies identify characteristic findings (basal ganglia infarcts, brainstem lesions, corpus callosum abnormalities) predicting poor outcomes. Circulating extracellular vesicles emerge as potential diagnostic and prognostic biomarkers. Survivors experience persistent neurocognitive impairments and epilepsy.

articles.headline: Plasmodium vivax causes severe complications in nearly half of cases
articles.title_original: Clinical spectrum and complications of Plasmodium vivax malaria: A retrospective study from Delhi, India.
articles.details: Retrospective study of 361 P. vivax patients from tertiary care in South Delhi (June 2020–May 2024). Complications occurred in 46.3%, with significantly higher rates in females (42% vs. 32%, p=0.039). Common findings: anemia (73.4%), thrombocytopenia (57.9%), leucocytosis. Severity markers: thrombocytopenia (OR=3.20, p<0.001), elevated creatinine (OR=6.07, p=0.001), hyperbilirubinemia (OR=3.71, p<0.001). Strong associations: bleeding-ARDS (r=0.82) and mortality-shock (r=0.74).

articles.headline: Advocacy for increased African representation in Global Fund governance
articles.title_original: It is time to increase Africa's governmental representation on the governing board of the global fund to fight AIDS, tuberculosis and malaria.
articles.details: Opinion piece advocating for increased African governmental representation on the governing board of the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria. Argues for greater African voice in governance of malaria control funding and policy decisions.

articles.headline: Refined diagnostic criteria improve malarial retinopathy specificity
articles.title_original: Re-evaluating malarial retinopathy to improve its diagnostic accuracy in paediatric cerebral malaria: A retrospective study.
articles.details: Retrospective study of 84 children (65 with fatal cerebral malaria diagnosed during life) from Blantyre autopsy cohort (1996-2010). Malarial retinopathy as predictor of cerebral parasite sequestration had sensitivity 89.4% (95% CI 77.6%–95.6%) and specificity 73.0% (95% CI 57.2%–84.8%). In subset with graded retinal assessments, specificity improved to 88.0% (95% CI 70.4%–96.2%) without sacrificing sensitivity (94.3%) by reclassifying patients with fewer than 5 hemorrhages in single eye as retinopathy negative.

articles.headline: HLA-G polymorphism increases malaria severity in iron-fortified children
articles.title_original: HLA-G 14 bp polymorphism: Implications and its associated risk to malaria severity among iron-fortified Ghanaian infants and young children.
articles.details: Secondary analysis of 432 children aged 6-36 months from cluster randomized controlled trial in Bono Region, Ghana. Among iron-fortified children, HLA-G 14 bp insertion/deletion polymorphism carriers (14 bp +/- and 14 bp -/-) showed elevated risk of severe malaria. The HLA-G 14 bp +/- variant was linked to higher risk of anemia among iron-supplemented participants. Findings suggest iron fortification combined with specific HLA-G genetic variants increases malaria severity risk.

articles.headline: Hidden malaria parasite diversity detected in Vietnamese birds
articles.title_original: Hidden Plasmodium diversity revealed in southeastern Asian passerines using next-generation amplicon sequencing.
articles.journal: Curr Res Parasitol Vector Borne Dis
articles.details: Researchers used next-generation amplicon sequencing of cytochrome b gene to investigate Plasmodium infections in three passerine bird species in Cat-Tien National Park, Vietnam. They identified two known and ten novel Plasmodium lineages, with high infection rates in little spiderhunter, white-rumped shama, and blue-winged pitta. Five distinct operational taxonomic units were identified. Species-specific infection patterns reflected differences in habitat use and vector exposure.

articles.headline: P. vivax relapse despite primaquine treatment in traveler
articles.title_original: Recurrent Malaria with Plasmodium vivax: A Case Report and Brief Review of the Literature.
articles.details: Case report of a 41-year-old Romanian man with P. vivax malaria acquired in Southeast Asia without chemoprophylaxis. Initial presentation included fever, chills, myalgia, headache, vomiting, abdominal pain, mild jaundice, and neurological signs, with severe thrombocytopenia and elevated bilirubin. Treated with doxycycline and atovaquone-proguanil, but experienced two confirmed P. vivax relapses despite primaquine radical cure after the first relapse, highlighting treatment challenges.

articles.headline: Correction: Household practices and P. falciparum in Uganda
articles.title_original: Correction: Household practices and infrastructure associated with high Plasmodium falciparum infection rates among children under five years old in Northern Uganda.
articles.details: Correction notice to previously published study on household practices associated with P. falciparum infection in children under five in Northern Uganda.

articles.headline: Seasonal malaria chemoprevention reduces childhood malaria burden
