First documented case in Oman; authors emphasize importance of considering P. vivax as potential cause of severe malaria even in non-endemic regions.

articles.headline: Malaria vaccination as cornerstone strategy for Sub-Saharan Africa elimination
articles.title_original: Mitigation of Malaria in Sub-Saharan Africa through Vaccination: A Budding Road Map for Global Malaria Eradication.
articles.details: Narrative review (2014–2024) of malaria mitigation strategies in Sub-Saharan Africa emphasizing vaccination. RTS,S/AS01 demonstrated 30–55% efficacy in children <5 in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi; R21/Matrix-M showed up to 77% efficacy in trials. Implementation challenges include limited funding, vaccine hesitancy, and infrastructure gaps. Authors conclude vaccination is cost-effective and scalable for eradication when integrated with routine immunization and enhanced community engagement.

articles.headline: Rheumatoid factor prevalence in chronic hepatitis B carriers in malaria-endemic region
articles.title_original: Prevalence of rheumatoid factor in asymptomatic chronic hepatitis B surface carriers in the context of endemic malaria: cross-sectional study at University Hospital of Cocody, Abidjan, Côte d'Ivoire.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 120 asymptomatic chronic hepatitis B surface antigen carriers at a hospital in Abidjan, Côte d'Ivoire (2024). Rheumatoid factor positivity measured by immunoturbidimetry (threshold >14 IU/mL) was 12.5% (95%CI 6.54–18.46). Advanced age ≥30 years was independently associated with rheumatoid factor positivity (aOR=3.32, 95%CI 1.06–10.39, p=0.04). No significant associations found with sex or reason for consultation.

articles.headline: Health education strategies in China's malaria elimination progress
articles.title_original: [Application of health education during the progress towards malaria elimination in China: a review].
articles.journal: Zhongguo Xue Xi Chong Bing Fang Zhi Za Zhi
articles.details: Review of health education's role in malaria control and elimination in China. China certified malaria-free by WHO in 2021 following decades of integrated control efforts. Review covers community-based and school-based health education, health education for entry-exit personnel and healthcare workers, and media propagation strategies for prevention knowledge. No specific quantitative results provided in this review article.

articles.headline: Rapid diagnostic test comparison for P. vivax detection in Korea
articles.title_original: [Comparison of Rapid Diagnostic Tests for the Detection of Plasmodium vivax Malaria].
articles.details: Comparative study of four rapid diagnostic tests (Pf/Pan kits) approved in South Korea for detecting malaria parasites. Sensitivity ranged from 70.9% to 96.5% overall, with substantially lower sensitivity (43.8% to 93.8%) in samples with low parasitemia. All four kits achieved 100% specificity. Results identify significant differences in test effectiveness, particularly for detecting low-level infections.

articles.headline: Anopheles darlingi reference genome assemblies from French Guiana and Peru
articles.title_original: Two chromosomal reference genome sequences for the malaria mosquito, Anopheles ( Nyssorhynchus) darlingi, Root, 1926 from French Guiana and Peru.
articles.details: Two chromosomal reference genome assemblies generated from individual A. darlingi females from French Guiana and Peru. Each genome approximately 180 megabases with three scaffolded chromosomal pseudomolecules and assembled X chromosome. Complete mitochondrial genomes are 15.4 kilobases in length. The two assemblies differ by two inversions in chromosome arm 2R.

articles.headline: Saffron-chloroquine-chitosan nanoparticles active against resistant P. falciparum
articles.title_original: In Vitro Antimalarial Activity of Chloroquine-Crocus Sativus Conjugated to Chitosan Nanocomposits against 3D7 and K1 Strains of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.details: In vitro study of chitosan nanoparticles conjugated with chloroquine and saffron tested against P. falciparum 3D7 (sensitive) and K1 (resistant) strains. Nanoparticle-saffron reduced IC50 from 42.5 μg/ml to 6.24 μg/ml in sensitive strain and from 12.5 μg/ml to 3.12 μg/ml in resistant strain. Combination therapy with chloroquine and saffron nanoparticles showed synergistic effects (p<0.05).

articles.headline: Severe P. falciparum malaria with AKI managed successfully with artesunate
articles.title_original: Managing Acute Kidney Injury in Severe Falciparum Malaria: Insights from a Challenging Case.
articles.details: Case report of 22-year-old male with severe P. falciparum malaria (9.7% parasitemia) complicated by acute kidney injury (serum creatinine 3.52 mg/dL), pulmonary edema, and jaundice. Treated with intravenous artesunate for six days followed by oral dihydroartemisinin/piperaquine for three days, single-dose primaquine, and two hemodialysis sessions. Patient achieved full clinical recovery without sequelae.

articles.headline: Avian Plasmodium species show distinct metabolic and immune effects in siskins
articles.title_original: Dynamics of resting metabolic rate and innate immune response in malaria-infected Eurasian siskins.
articles.details: Experimental study comparing effects of P. relictum (SGS1 lineage, temperate) and P. ashfordi (GRW2 lineage, tropical Africa) on juvenile Eurasian siskins, measuring resting metabolic rate and interleukin-6 levels. SGS1 infection caused reduced resting metabolic rate and IL-6 levels; GRW2 showed transient IL-6 reduction and distinct metabolic dynamics. Results did not conclusively demonstrate that tropical malaria has more severe effects on siskins.

articles.headline: Undiagnosed asymptomatic malaria in pregnancy causes substantial maternal-fetal harm
articles.title_original: Detrimental infant and maternal outcomes of undiagnosed asymptomatic malaria in pregnancy.
articles.details: Brief report on asymptomatic malaria in pregnancy in low-transmission areas. Standard diagnostic tests fail to detect most infections. New evidence demonstrates substantial negative impacts on both maternal and fetal outcomes in undetected asymptomatic infections. Specific quantitative findings not detailed in abstract.

articles.headline: P. falciparum infection varies spatially in Bamako school-aged children
articles.title_original: Prevalence of Plasmodium falciparum infection in school-aged children in urban and peri-urban schools of Bamako, Mali.
articles.details: Cross-sectional prevalence study of 200-230 school children aged 6-13 years in each of four Bamako ecological strata. P. falciparum prevalence ranged from 0% to 15.5% across study sites. Anemia increased infection odds 2.38-fold (95% CI 1.57–3.61); living near Niger River increased odds 3.37-fold (95% CI 2.05–7.45). Authors conclude school children are a major parasite reservoir requiring targeted interventions.

articles.headline: Historical error: DPRK, not ROK, certified malaria-free in 1979
articles.title_original: Misattribution of Korea's malaria elimination status in 1979.
articles.details: Historical source-based review examining the origin of widespread citation that Republic of Korea achieved malaria elimination in 1979. Analysis of WHO records shows this certification actually applied to Democratic People's Republic of Korea (North Korea) as of 1979, not ROK (South Korea). Misinterpretation in US Department of Defense documents led to erroneous attribution that persisted through unverified citations.

articles.headline: Plant extract-silver nanoparticles restore splenic function in P. chabaudi mice
articles.title_original: Histopathologic effect and immunomodulatory of Indigofera oblongifolia with silver nanoparticles against splenic injury by Plasmodium chabaudi infection in mice.
articles.details: Experimental study in 50 female C57BL/6 mice infected with P. chabaudi, comparing treatment with Indigofera oblongifolia leaf extract conjugated with silver nanoparticles (IOLE AgNPs) versus chloroquine phosphate. Histopathological and immunohistochemical analysis showed IOLE AgNPs more effectively restored splenic architecture, reduced inflammatory markers, and improved immune responses compared to chloroquine and control groups.

articles.headline: η-Carbonic anhydrase: novel Plasmodium drug target with preliminary inhibitors
articles.title_original: Plasmodium falciparum η-carbonic anhydrase.
articles.details: Review of P. falciparum carbonic anhydrase (PfCA), identified as a new η-class enzyme in 2014 involved in pyrimidine biosynthesis. PfCA characterized as efficient CO2 hydration catalyst. Inhibition studies identified multiple low-nanomolar compounds, with acetazolamide and an ureido-substituted benzenesulfonamide demonstrating potent P. falciparum growth inhibition in vitro. Authors conclude η-CAs may be innovative drug targets but require further investigation.

articles.headline: P. cynomolgi DHFR/DHPS mutations identified; antifolate drugs likely effective
articles.title_original: Molecular analysis of DHFR and DHPS gene mutations in Plasmodium cynomolgi from humans and macaques in Southeast Asia.
articles.details: Study analyzing DHFR and DHPS mutations in P. cynomolgi from nine humans and 29 macaques in Southeast Asia. Human isolates from Cambodia showed no DHFR mutations and one DHPS mutation (V451I); macaque isolates from Thailand showed DHFR mutations N44T and C49S with two DHPS haplotypes. Molecular docking with eight antifolate drugs showed mutations did not significantly impair drug binding affinity.

articles.headline: AI-enhanced malaria RDT interpretation matches trained reviewers
articles.title_original: Malaria RDT (mRDT) interpretation accuracy by frontline health workers compared to AI in Kano state, Nigeria.
articles.details: Comparative analysis in Nigeria examined mRDT interpretations by frontline healthcare workers, trained expert reviewers, and AI algorithms. AI matched panel readers at 96.38% for positives, 97.12% for negatives; FHWs at 96.82% positives, 94.31% negatives. AI weighted F1 score 96.4 vs FHWs 95.3. AI identified 90.2% of faint positives vs FHWs 76.1%. Conclusion: AI technology enhances mRDT interpretation accuracy, particularly for faint positives, improving diagnosis in resource-limited settings.

articles.headline: Emerging biosensors offer rapid malaria detection at point of care
articles.title_original: Advances in malaria detection.
articles.details: Review of current and emerging malaria diagnostic tools. Malaria causes over 435,000 deaths annually; conventional methods limited by treatment resistance and analytical challenges. Biosensor technology using nanotechnology provides rapid, sensitive, cost-effective alternatives. Devices are user-friendly, require minimal technical expertise, and suit point-of-care testing in remote resource-limited settings, offering potential to revolutionize malaria control and eradication.

articles.headline: IgA mucosal immunity represents underexplored malaria vaccine strategy
articles.title_original: Targeting mucosal immunity in malaria control: the underexplored role of IgA.
articles.details: Review synthesizing literature on Immunoglobulin A (IgA) in malaria. Malaria caused 263 million cases and 597,000 deaths in 2023; current measures show limited mortality reduction. Evidence suggests IgA blocks parasite entry, activates complement, modulates inflammation, though protective efficacy unestablished. Reviews vaccine platforms (oral, nasal, prime-boost) to harness IgA with IgG. Identifies critical gaps in correlating IgA with clinical immunity and need for specialized animal models and human cohorts.

articles.headline: Global malaria burden declining; significant regional disparities remain
articles.title_original: Global, regional, and national burden of neglected tropical diseases and malaria, 1990-2021.
articles.details: Analysis of Global Burden of Disease 2021 data on malaria and NTDs. From 1990–2021, case numbers and age-standardized rates showed recent downward trends. EAPC correlated positively with ASRs and HDI scores. Projections indicate continued decline through 2046. Decomposition analysis showed positive impact of aging and epidemiological shifts on burden reduction. Frontier analysis revealed countries with higher SDI have greater reduction potential.

articles.headline: VK210 most prevalent P. vivax variant; vector susceptibility mapped
articles.title_original: Updated global distribution of Plasmodium vivax circumsporozoite protein variants and their correlation with vector susceptibility: A systematic review and meta-analysis.
articles.details: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 94 studies on Plasmodium vivax Circumsporozoite Protein variants (VK210, VK247, P. vivax-like). VK210 most prevalent globally. Of 94 Anopheles species analyzed, 23.4% susceptible to PvCSP variants: all 22 VK210-susceptible species showed mono-infection susceptibility, 14 species (63.6%) to VK247, 6 species (27.3%) to mixed infections. P. vivax-like identified in Brazil/Guiana in Anopheles oswaldoi and A. daeneorum. Data highlight vector competence importance for regional distribution with vaccine implications.

articles.headline: Ghana malaria programmes lack explicit evidence-based priority-setting
articles.title_original: What are the priority-setting approaches for HIV/AIDS, TB and malaria programmes in Ghana? A qualitative perspective from key informants.
articles.details: Qualitative study with 10 key informants from malaria, HIV/AIDS, TB programmes in Ghana. Identified four priority-setting approaches: health needs identification, stakeholder participation, transparency, contextual factors. Main actors were ministries, development partners, research institutions; public involvement limited. Barriers included inadequate funding and aligning priorities with funders. Conclusion: explicit priority-setting approaches limited in these programmes despite global expansion of such approaches.

articles.headline: Expansion microscopy visualizes malaria parasites within mosquito tissues
articles.title_original: Mosquito Tissue Ultrastructure-Expansion Microscopy (MoTissU-ExM) enables ultrastructural and anatomical analysis of malaria parasites and their mosquito.
articles.details: Developed MoTissU-ExM method for preparing mosquito salivary glands and midguts using ultrastructure expansion microscopy (U-ExM), expanding biological samples ~4.5-fold. Previous U-ExM mostly used on blood-stage parasites in vitro. MoTissU-ExM preserves host and parasite ultrastructure, enabling visualization of oocysts and sporozoites in situ. Validation confirmed expected expansion and provided before/after tissue comparisons. Discusses potential use cases, drawbacks, and future applications.

articles.headline: Ellagic acid inhibits P. falciparum more potently than artemisinin in vitro
articles.title_original: Effects of Ellagic Acid on Hemoglobin Ingestion, Transport, and Digestion in the Digestive Vacuole of Plasmodium falciparum.
articles.authors: Thuduhena AC, Abu-Bakar N, Hamzah SN
articles.details: In vitro study examined ellagic acid against 3D7 (chloroquine-sensitive) and W2 (chloroquine-resistant) P. falciparum using transmission electron microscopy. IC50 values: ellagic acid 1.0 ± 0.7 nM (3D7) and 1.3 ± 0.1 nM (W2), exceeding artemisinin (IC50 3D7 = 2.2 ± 0.2 nM; W2 = 4.3 ± 0.3 nM) (p < 0.001). TEM showed ellagic acid disrupted hemoglobin metabolism in early trophozoite stage. Conclusion: ellagic acid is promising antimalarial targeting hemoglobin processes.

articles.headline: African tick bite fever caused false-positive malaria RDT
articles.title_original: False-Positive Malaria Rapid Diagnostic Test Likely Due to African Tick Bite Fever: A Case Report.
articles.details: Case report of febrile returned traveler from South Africa with positive malaria RDT but confirmed African tick bite fever (rickettsial serology). Subsequent malaria microscopy and additional testing were negative. Patient responded to oral atovaquone-proguanil despite non-malaria diagnosis. Case illustrates diagnostic test performance limitations and risk of premature diagnostic closure in returning travelers.

articles.headline: Reference genome assembled for malaria vector Anopheles nili
articles.title_original: A chromosomal reference genome sequence for the malaria mosquito, Anopheles nili, Theobald, 1904.
articles.details: Genome assembly from wild male Anopheles nili from Cameroon. Genome sequence 195 megabases in span with most assembly scaffolded into three chromosomal pseudomolecules and X chromosome assembled. Complete mitochondrial genome 15.4 kilobases assembled. Provides genomic resource for vector biology research.

articles.headline: Malaria prevalence varies seasonally with rainfall and temperature
articles.title_original: Spatio-temporal characterization of malaria prevalence in a peri-urban resource limited setting of Western Kenya Highlands.
articles.details: Longitudinal study in Mbale, Vihiga County, Kenya (December 2019–November 2020) of 768 malaria-confirmed patients. P. falciparum 98.7%, P. malariae/vivax/ovale 1.3%. Linear regression showed age, gender, location effects on prevalence (R² = 0.7, F = 1.854, p < 0.136). Malaria prevalence varied monthly with rainfall and temperature changes. Conclusion: spatio-temporal patterns and breeding grounds influence prevalence; control strategies should target space and time trends.

articles.headline: Acute kidney injury complicates 16% of severe malaria cases
articles.title_original: Factors associated with acute kidney injury and outcomes in patients with malaria in a district hospital in Rwanda.
articles.details: Retrospective study of 122 severe malaria patient files from district hospital in Rwanda (2016–2017). AKI prevalence 16.3%. Significant associated factors: dehydration (p = 0.01), high-grade fever (p = 0.002), profuse sweating (p = 0.034), vomiting (p = 0.043), diarrhea (p = 0.025). Of 20 AKI cases: 55% completely recovered, 15% died, 30% transferred for hemodialysis. Conclusion: AKI is evident complication; healthcare professionals need better education on diagnosis and management.

articles.headline: Gender differences in malaria-typhoid belief system, Kenya farmers
articles.title_original: The relationship between gender and cultural beliefs of malaria into typhoid progression among rural rice farmers in Central Kenya.
articles.details: Cross-sectional study of 250 Kikuyu rice farmers in Mwea division, Central Kenya, examining cultural beliefs that malaria progresses to typhoid via structured questionnaire. Sixty-two percent of women versus 47% of men held this belief. Women were 2.15 times more likely to believe malaria progresses to typhoid (aOR 2.15, 95% CI 1.21–3.79, p < 0.009). Cultural factors underlie gender disparities in beliefs about disease progression.
